Note from the editor 
 
The root verses up to chapter nine are quoted in their entirety from A Guide to the Bodhisattva’s 
Way of Life , with the permission of the tr anslator, Stephen Batchelor.  
  
The root verses of chapter nine are from the translation by Ven. Joan Nicell, and were used with 
permission from FPMT Education Services.   Root verses are centered, italicized, and marked  by the chapter and verse numbers in square 
brackets. 
 
The outlines are from The Subject Headings from Gyaltsab Je’s  Commentary to Shantideva’s ‘Engaging 
in the Bodhisattva Deeds’, translated by Ven. Losang Sopa (Bob Miller). They were included in the 
transcripts with permission fr om FPMT Education Services.  
  Outlines are indented, in bold, and have been slightly amended by Amitabha Buddhist Centre’s 
interpreter, Ven. Gyurme, for the Basic Program at ABC. The amendments are marked with an 
asterisk. 
 
The transcripts were produced by students and staff of Amitabha Buddhist Centre; the oral commentary was transcribed by Phuah Soon Ek, Vi vien Ng and Angie Xiao and the transcripts 
were proofread and checked by Cecilia Tsong, Fiona O’Shaughnessy and Yap Siew Kee.    Note that in these transcripts ‘angry’ and ‘furio us’ are used interchangeably, and  ‘absorbtion’ 
can generally be taken to mean ‘concentration’. 
 
The transcripts were prepared for FPMT Basic Programs by the editor.

Transcript of the commentary by Geshe Chonyi 
on Shantideva’s  
Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds 
 
PART TWO   Chapter VI-X 
 
The Sixth Chapter: Teaching patience  
Lesson 1   Chapter VI - Introduction 
This new module, which we are starting today, is scheduled to last for about three to four 
months. We will start with an extract from the co llection of advice by the great lama, Gungtang 
Rinpoche1. This advice is aimed at those who want to  study the great treatises. From the onset, 
Gungtang Rinpoche points out that his advice is not directed at Brah ma, the worldly god who 
had the clairvoyance to know past and future li v e s ,  o r   t h e  g o d s  o f  t h e  d e s i r e  r e a l m  w h o  a r e  
endowed with many possessions, great wealth and so on. Rather, Rinpoche said this advice was 
directed at those who had a clear mind and wh o aspired to study the teachings of the Buddha 
correctly. If you are someone who aspires to do th is, then please listen to these instructions: 
 
“All of us are now in the situation where we have  all the favourable conditions for studying. We 
have obtained this precious human life of leisur e and opportunities. Not only that, we have also 
met with the teachings of the Buddha. We have met with teachers who can show us the path. 
Furthermore, we are surrounded by Dharma frie nds who share the same interests and who are 
able to support us in our practice. So, al l the necessary conditions are here now. 
 
It is difficult to meet with such a perfect situ ation again where all the conditions are assembled 
together. Therefore, stop procrastinating in your Dharma studies and practi se. Now is the time to 
take the essence of your life.” 
 With regards to our Dharma studies and practice, mo st of us procrastinate . We always think, “I 
will practise or study tomorrow….Maybe the day after tomorrow, or next year. Or I will study 
after I have concluded this business deal or that project, or maybe when I am 60 or 70 years old. When I retire, then I will start my Dharma practice.” We are all like that. In this particular 
advice, Gungtang Rinpoche is trying to point out that we need to stop this procrastination.  
                                             
 
1 Gungtang Rinpoche: The Path Leading To the Island of Jewels: Advice on the Manner of Studying the Treatises of Sutra 
and Tantra . 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
2  
It is very important that we really try not to miss any classes.  At the beginning of the module 
when you make a very strong determination not to miss classes, then it is going to be of great 
benefit. If you think that it is OK to miss one or  two classes and you start to do so, then it never 
stops. 
 If you are someone who is really interested in st udying, it is important no t to miss classes. Once 
you start missing one class, you will miss a second  class. Then, there will be a third class and a 
fourth class. If you are someone who really want s to study, you should make it a point not to 
miss any classes. But if you are not really committed or interested in these studies, then that is a 
totally different issue.  
We have completed the first fi ve chapters of this text, Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds.  The 
subject matter of this text can be summarised into three main points: 
- To generate the altruistic intention of becoming a buddha for the benefit of all sentient beings.  
- If bodhicitta has been generated, how to stop it from degenerating.  
- On the basis of that, to try to  strengthen and increase the bo dhicitta that has already been 
generated.  The first three chapters of this text de al with the development of bodhicitta.  
 The first chapter talks about the benefits and th e advantages of cultivat ing such an awakening 
mind if one has yet to generate bodhicitta.   
The second chapter, called Disclosure of Wrongdoing, is about the practice of confessing and 
purifying one’s negativities, those obstacles whic h prevent and interfere with the generation of 
bodhicitta.  
 The third chapter then goes on to show how we can assemble all the favourable and necessary 
conditions for cultivating and developing bodhicitta. 
 The second and the third chapters cover the 7-limb  practice. In the second chapter there is the 
practice of making offerings, prostrations, goin g for refuge to the Three Jewels and confessing 
our negativities through the applicatio n of the four opponent powers.   
 The rest of the 7-limbs are covered in the third ch apter, which discusses th e practice of rejoicing 
in virtue, requesting the buddhas to turn the Wh eel of Dharma, supplicating the buddhas not to 
pass into paranirvana and dedicating our merits for the benefit of others.   The practice of mind training – primarily, the pr actice of exchanging oneself for others – follows 
the completion of the 7-limbs in th e third chapter. On the basis of such mind training, we will be 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
3  
able to offer even our bodies for the benefit of others. When we have developed and are able to 
embrace bodhicitta in this stable way, we can th en take on the bodhisattva vows through a ritual. 
 
Chapters four to six cover conscientiousness, gu arding alertness (or vigilance/introspection) and 
patience, which are necessary to prevent the bodh icitta we have developed from degenerating. 
Having taken the bodhisattva vows, we must train in the precepts in order to maintain the vows. 
The fourth chapter shows that the main way to cu ltivate such conscientiousness is to reflect on 
our precious human life of leisure and opportun ities and the faults of  the various negative 
emotions.  If we do not guard or protect our minds, then it will be very difficult to protect the bodhisattva 
vows that we had taken.  In order to do that, we  need to cultivate vigilance/introspection, the 
topic of the fifth chapter, which shows how all un desirable and harmful things originate from the 
mind. But, in the same way, all positive things aris e from the mind as well. This is followed by an 
extensive discussion on how to rely on an d develop mindfulness and vigilance.     
 The chapter on patience also fa lls under the second main point of preventing the degeneration 
of the bodhicitta that we have developed. Anger is an obstacle that causes our bodhicitta to 
deteriorate and the antidote for our anger is the pr actice of patience. We will deal with this in 
the next session.  In order to increase and develop wh atever bodhicitta we have genera ted, we then look at the next 
three chapters of this text. Th ey are the chapters on enthusiasm (or joyous perseverance), 
meditation and wisdom. The final ch apter (ten) is on dedication.  
Lesson 2   Chapter VI - verse 1-2 
As mentioned in the Lamrim Chenmo , when listening to the teaching s, we should cultivate the six 
attitudes as our motivation and abandon the th ree faults of the vessel in our behaviour.  
 It is really important to know the reasons and to  have the correct motivati on for listening to the 
teachings. The reasons have to be virtuous an d correct. The more positive and virtuous our 
motivation, the greater the benefit we receive from  listening to the teachings. When we do not 
have the correct motivation, then no matter how we listen to the teachings, they will not be 
beneficial for our minds even if they are very  good and profound. The teachings of the Buddha 
always emphasise the importance of having the correct reason and motivation for doing 
anything.  The responsibility for this lies with each individual. Therefore, please adjust your 
motivation accordingly when li stening to these teachings. 
 Here is another piece of advice from Guntang Rinpoche: 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
4  
“In these degenerate times, when sentient beings  have very little merit and our minds are also 
weak and degenerate, it is very important to ma ke strong requests to our personal deities for 
blessings. We should work very hard at a ccumulating merit and purifying our minds of 
obscurations. It is important to make offerings, prostrations and engage in the practices of the 7-
limbs. It is extremely important to rely on the power of the meri t field, the buddhas and the holy 
objects that represent the Buddha’s holy body, speech and mind.”  
 Generally speaking, our motivation determines wh ether our actions are virtuous or not. When 
our motivation is virtuous, then our actions are also virtuous.  
 But all actions performed in relation to the merit field or to the holy objects are exceptions to 
this rule. Even when we make offerings or prostrat ions to the merit field or holy objects with an 
incorrect motivation, we still accumulate a great de al of merit. This is due to the power of the 
merit field and the holy objects.   You should therefore grasp the opportunity to rely on holy objects and the merit field to 
accumulate merit and purify your karma, especia lly those of you who really want to learn the 
Buddha’s teachings and complete your studies. It is very difficult to complete your studies, no matter how hard you study, if you do not strive to do this.    This is my own experience from my life in the monastery. There is no guarantee that those who 
are naturally more intelligent or who do well in their studies can complete them. Generally, 
those who work at accumulating merit, purify ing their negative karma, and making whole-
hearted requests to their gurus and special deit ies are the ones who make it in the end and 
successfully complete their studies. Those who are more intelligent tend to  take things easy and 
do not work as hard, whereas those who are not so  intelligent realise that they have to work 
harder.  
3. Explaining the way to train in the remaining four perfections 
A. The way to train in patience 
B. The way to train in joyous effort C. The way to train in the absorptions included in the common and uncommon calm 
abidings 
D. The way to train in wisdom - the nature of insight 
 
After generating the altruistic intention to attain  enlightenment for the benefit of all sentient 
beings, we train in the six perfections. We have completed the first two perfections of generosity 
and ethics. 
 
A. The way to train in patience 
a. Explaining the chapter’s material 
1)  Abandoning hindrances to the accomplishment of abiding by the antidote: 
     devoting oneself to the cultivation of patience 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
5  
a) The disadvantages of anger 
i) The unseen disadvantages 
1’ Anger destroys the roots of virtue 
2’ One should make effort in cultivating patience 
after having understood the faults and qualities of fury and patience 
ii) The visible disadvantages 
b) Reflecting on the benefits of patience 
2)  Engaging the mind in the methods for accomplishing patience 
1’. Anger destroys the roots of virtue
 
[6, 1] 
Whatever wholesome deeds, 
Such as venerating the Buddhas and generosity, 
That have been amassed over a thousand aeons 
Will all be destroyed in one moment of anger.  
 
All the roots of virtue that we have accumulate d through, for example,  generosity and ethics 
over a thousand aeons are destroyed by a single moment of anger.  
There are three forms of generosity: 
the giving of Dharma; 
the giving of material goods; and   bestowing fearlessness on others The roots of virtue that come from the practice of these thre e kinds of generosity over a 
thousand aeons are destroyed in  just one moment of anger. 
 
‘Venerating the Buddhas’  includes offerings to the Dharma and Sangha Jewels and the other 
practices of the 7-limbs, accumulated over a thou sand aeons, which are also destroyed in that 
moment of anger. ‘ Wholesome deeds’  refer to the practice of ethics  such as keeping one’s vows. The 
roots of virtue that comes from the practice of ethics for a thousand aeons are destroyed in just 
one moment of anger. 
 
While the practices of generosity and ethics are explicitly mentioned here, it is not mentioned 
whether the roots of virtue that come from the practice of concentration are similarly wiped out 
by that moment of anger.  
There is some debate over this issue.  Some scholars quote Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the 
Middle Way.  In this text he claims that the roots of virtue accumulated from the activities of 
generosity and ethics over a period of a hund red aeons are destroyed by a moment of anger. 
However, he makes no such reference with regard to the practice of meditation. Using this as a 
scriptural authority, some assert that the roots of virtue generate d by meditation are be destroyed 
by anger.  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
6  
However, in other texts, such as those composed by Arya Asanga, it is asserted that the roots of 
virtue accumulated from activities such as genero sity, ethics and meditation can be destroyed by 
anger.  
 The final conclusion by the great masters and scholars is this: The collection of merit is subject to 
destruction by anger, whereas the collection of wi sdom is not subject to destruction by anger. 
 There are other conditions that a ffect this, such as the subject (i.e. the person who is getting 
angry) not being a bodhisattva  and the object of one’s anger being a bodhisattva. The anger 
referred to here is not mere i rritation but a strong hatred ge nerated towards the bodhisattva, 
without any sense of regret or reliance on any antidotes to purify the negativity that one is 
creating. If we are in that situation, then we have  destroyed whatever roots of virtue that we have 
accumulated over a thousand aeons. This is possibl e just as a small fire arrow can set fire to a 
large heap of hay.  If one is not a bodhisattva and one generates stro ng hatred towards a bodhisattva, one destroys 
the roots of virtue of one’s collection of me rit accumulated over a thousand aeons. One’s 
collection of wisdom, however, is not destroyed. 
 
When a higher level bodhisattva is angered by a lo wer level bodhisattva, then the former destroys 
the roots of virtue from his collection of me rit accumulated over a hundred eons. This is 
mentioned in both the Play of Manjushri Sutra  and Chandrakirti’s Commentary on the “Middle 
Way”. (Refer to Lamrim Chenmo  Vol. 2, pg. 154) 
 When a lower level bodhisattva becomes angry with a higher level bodhisattv a, then he destroys 
roots of virtue from his collection of merit accumulated over one aeon.  For example, a bodhisattva is on  the great path of accumulation
2 and is due to enter the path of 
preparation the next day. On the eve of this ev ent, however, this bodhisattva becomes angry at 
another bodhisattva who is already at the path of  preparation. Instead of entering the path of 
preparation the next day, the realisation of this bo dhisattva is delayed for an aeon and he has to   
retrain for this path all over again.  
 
A non-bodhisattva who gets angry at another non- bodhisattva (e.g. one  ordained person gets 
angry at another ordained person), destroys the amount of merit that comes from making a full 
prostration to a stupa, just from that one moment  of hatred,. (The sutras say that however many 
atoms are covered by the body during such a prostr ation is equal to the nu mber of times that the 
                                              
2 The path of accumulation is divided into 3 levels: small, middling and great. The path of preparation is divided 
into 4 levels: (1) heat (2) peak (3) fo rbearance (4) suprem e mundane qualities.  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
7  
person prostrating  will be reborn as a Wheel Turning King). 
 
It is clear, from the above examples, that we ha ve to be careful with each other since we all 
belong to the family of students of the same teac hers. If we get angry at  one another, then our 
roots of virtue will be destroyed. We should, th erefore, try to reduce or stop our anger from 
arising.  
 
There is no certainty that the roots of virtue will be completely  destroyed by generating anger as 
there are different levels of destruction: great, middling and small. Depending on the strength of 
our anger, there are different fruitional effects of the virtue we have accumulated.  
 When our anger is weak, our roots of virtue are on ly slightly destroyed. The fruitional effect is 
the duration of the pleasure  we enjoy is shortened.  
 When our anger is very strong, then our roots of virtue are more severely destroyed. The 
fruitional effect is that, instead of enjoying an ex tended period of pleasure, we will probably not 
experience any enjoyment at all.    When the strength of our anger is middling, the fruitional effect is not clearly explained in the 
texts. It should fall somewhere between the two situations mentioned above.   The exhaustion of the roots of virtue can be likened to burnt seeds, which cannot produce any 
sprouts and cannot grow into trees.  When we create some negative karma but we engage  in the practice of confession by applying the 
four opponent powers, we  do not experience the re sults of the negative action that we did in the 
past. In a similar way, when we generate very st rong hatred towards another person, it destroys 
the roots of virtue in such a way that we will not experience any positive effects from them.   
 
‘Destroying the roots of virtue’ does not mean that all the roots of virtues are completely 
eradicated but it does mean that the ability of th ose virtuous karmic seeds to issue positive effects 
is considerably weakened.  
 
All bodhisattvas can be the recipients of anger bu t not all bodhisattvas can generate anger.  In 
other words, we may   get angry at anyone, includ ing all the bodhisattvas but it is not possible for 
all bodhisattvas to get angry. 
 The roots of virtue of the bodhisattva who has atta ined the peak stage of the path of preparation 
are not destroyed by anger. However, it is unclea r in the texts whether the roots of virtue of the 
bodhisattva are destroyed by anger before  he achieves this level. It seems that bodhisattvas do not 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
8  
get angry when they have attain ed the peak stage. All bodhisa ttvas, before achieving the peak 
stage of the path of preparation, can get angr y. The superior beings do not get angry.  
 
In general, any virtue that is dedicated toward s enlightenment would not be destroyed. That is 
why we have to dedicate all our merits toward s achieving enlightenment for the benefit of all 
sentient beings. The sutras mention that when we  put a drop of water in to the ocean, until the 
ocean dries up,  our drop of water will remain th ere. In the same way,  any roots of virtue 
dedicated towards enlightenment will never be exhausted.  It is very clear that the roots of virtue of some bodhisattvas are subject to destruction by anger. It 
is also certain that the roots of virtues accumu lated by the bodhisattvas are  always dedicated 
towards enlightenment. This begs the question : Are the roots of virtue dedicated towards 
enlightenment subject to destruction by anger or no t? We should consider this point at the next 
discussion group session.   In Tibetan, anger and hatred has the same meaning. 
2’. One should make effort in cultivating pati ence after having understood the faults and qualities 
of fury and patience.  
(6,2) 
There is no transgression like hatred, 
And no fortitude like patience. 
Thus I should strive in various ways 
To meditate on patience. 
 
Why is there, ‘ no transgression like hatred’ ? This is because when we get angry, we destroy our roots 
of virtue and postpone  our attainment of the higher paths. Anger also  has its own ripening 
result in our future lives in the form of unplea sant experiences and suffering. Therefore, there is 
no negative force that resembles anger.  
 As mentioned in the example given earlier of how anger can postpone the realisation of the 
bodhisattva on the brink of entering the path of preparation, we can infer that if we were to 
generate very strong hatred in our minds fo r even a single second, it will delay our own 
realisations.   
‘And no fortitude like patience’  means there is no better antidote than patience when dealing with 
anger.  
 Since there is no greater negativity than anger and there is no better antidote for anger than 
patience, we must meditate on developing pati ence with great effort. The various ways to 
cultivate patience will be explained in the verses to come. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
9  
 
Chandrakirti mentions that there is no greater negativity than anger in his auto commentary to 
his Commentary on the “Middle Way”.  He uses the example of a big ocean. Just as we are not able 
to count the number of drops of water that make up the ocean, in the same way, the fruitional 
effects of anger have no boundary. There is no greater negative force th an anger. The supreme 
antidote for anger is patience. Therefore we need to familiarise ourselves with patience. 
 
In Gyaltsab Je’s commentary to the Engaging in the Bodhisattvas’ Deeds , he mentions that the roots 
of virtue accumulated through the practice of gene rosity, ethics and even patience are subject to 
destruction by anger. 
 
Question from student:  not heard on recording 
When we talk about virtue, there is virtue create d by the body, speech and mind. Since it is easier 
to create virtue with our bodies and speech, such virtues are also more easily destroyed. As it is 
more difficult to create virtue with our minds, probably the virtue created by the mind is more 
difficult to destroy.  
 The roots of virtues that are included in the cate gory of wisdom are not subject to destruction by 
anger whereas the roots of virtue that arose from meditation are subject to destruction by anger. 
This is mentioned in the root text . But it is not saying that all the roots of virtue from meditation 
are subject to destruction by anger.  
Question from student:  What is the penalty for the arhat who gets angry?  
This would depend on the object with which he is  angry and the level of attainment of the other 
party.   
 
Question:  not heard on recording 
The path of preparation where one is accumulating merit for the 1
st great countless eon is 
divided into four levels:- 
1 Heat -  One overcomes the manifest form of the conceptions of the thorou ghly afflicted class of 
phenomenon as truly existent   
2 Peak - One overcomes the manifest form of th e conceptions of completely purified phenomena 
as truly existent  3 Forbearance - One overcomes the manifest form of  the conceptions of the true existence of the 
apprehender that apprehends the pe rson as substantially existent 
4 Supreme mundane qualities -  One overcomes th e manifest form of conceptions of the true 
existence of the apprehender that appreh ends the person as imputedly existent. 
So there are different objects of abandonmen t at each of these four different stages. 
 The bodhisattva on the path of ac cumulation or the path of prepar ation still has the self-grasping 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
10  
conception holding an “I” to be inherently existe nt. Since that conception has not been stopped, 
there remains an opportunity for the bodhisattva to get angry. 
 
When the bodhisattva has achieved the peak stage of the path of preparation, the bodhisattva has 
abandoned the manifest conception of the appreh ender. This means the bodhisattva has not yet 
abandoned self-grasping but he does not experience  any manifest conception of  grasping at the 
inherently existent “I”. Therefore we can s ay that the bodhisattva does not get angry. 
 This is not the same as saying that the bodhisattva has already abandoned anger as he has not yet 
abandoned the grasping at an “I”. It does no t manifest from that point onwards. While the 
manifest conception of the apprehender remains, be fore achieving  the peak stage of the path of 
preparation, the opportunity for the bodhisattv a to get angry is always there. Once the 
bodhisattva has achieved the peak stage of th e path of preparation,  since that manifest 
conception no longer operates, the bodhisattva does not get angry anymore. (Geshe-la: ‘Maybe 
that is the reason but I am not sure.’) 
Lesson 3   Chapter VI - verse 3-10 
It is important to set the proper motivation fo r listening to the teachi ngs. Think:  ‘For the 
purpose of subduing our unruly, undisciplined minds and our   negative emotions, I am going to 
listen to these teachings on patience from Shantideva’s text, Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds.’   
 
It is important to reflect daily on whatever you have heard and found beneficial from the 
teachings. Use whatever you have  heard and apply it in your daily life. If you do not  use the 
teachings in this way,  you  accomplish  little by merely listening. 
 
This is advice from the teachings of Gungtang Rinpoche called Songs of Expressing Sadness . In one 
of these songs, he laments that although, in real ity, it is the guru who shows us the path and 
protects in this life and all our future lives,  in practice, we make our own decisions without 
relying on him.   
 We may think or even say that there is a need for us to rely on the instru ctions of our teachers  
and recognise  that our guru is the embodiment of  the three refuges. But, in our daily lives, we 
do not pay any heed to his advice or consider our teacher’s instructions to be important.  
 Gungtang Rinpoche goes on to s ay, now that we have attained this precious human rebirth of 
leisure and opportunities that is very difficult to achieve and which give s us the opportunity to 
accomplish so much. We know th is and can even explain it to others. Yet when we examine our 
lives carefully, most of the time, we are spending  it doing meaningless things. When we reflect in 
this way and realise this,  it is a cause for regret and something to be very sad about. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
11  
 
Rinpoche’s advice is  very good and beneficial espe cially if we are able to remember it in our daily 
lives. For example, if we explain to others the be nefits of this perfect human rebirth, but do not 
put it to good use ourselves,  then we are in  fact wasting our own precious human rebirth.  
 
In our daily lives we treasure our possessions and wealth and take great care to protect them  from thieves and robbers. But we are not very careful with our life span, which is  being 
exhausted and stolen every moment by the Lord of De ath. We should be very sad about this also.   
We also pursue and hanker after teachings an d instructions on hidden phenomena, which are 
not perceivable or manifest such as emptiness, bo dhicitta, tantra and so forth. But we do not pay 
any attention to those instructions that deal with  subjects  that are very obvious and accessible, 
such as death and impermanence. Agai n, this is a very sad situation. 
 I am quoting some extracts from this set of advi ce as they are in short sentences. If we can 
remember them in our daily lives, then that  will  be beneficial. The advice is not new and can be 
easily found in the Lamrim .  
 In the last lesson, we looked at the faults an d disadvantages of anger and how one moment of 
anger can potentially destroy the roots of virtue accumulated over a long period of time. On top 
of that, generating hatred or anger will lead to its own unpleasant ripening results in the future. 
By understanding the different faults of anger, we can see why the text says: ‘ There  is no 
transgression like hatred.’   
 
When the object of our anger is  someone who has more qualities than ourselves,  we destroy 
even more roots of virtues e.g. when a non-bodh isattva generates hatred towards a bodhisattva, 
the former destroys a lot of the merit that he accumulated. Since, generally speaking, 
bodhisattvas destroy their roots of virtue when  they are angry with one another, then when 
ordinary beings get angry with each other,  roots of virtue are definitely destroyed.3 
 
As mentioned in the texts, it is not necessary to ascertain from our own side whether the object of our anger is a bodhisattva or not. If, in real ity, that person is a bo dhisattva, regardless of 
whether we have ascertained this or not, once we are angry with him, we destroy our roots of 
virtue even if we have valid reasons for being upse t. It is irrelevant whet her our reasons are valid 
or incorrect. Once we get angry with a  bodhisattv a, we destroy our roots of virtue. We may feel 
our anger can be justified, but this is not the case here and  we have to remember this.  
We looked at the unseen disadvantages of anger in the last lesson. 
                                             
 
3 Regardless of whether the recipient of th e anger is ascertained to be a bodhisattva or whether the perceived faults 
that cause the anger are real, the destruct ion of virtue is said nonetheless to be just as explained above (i.e., the 
virtue accumulated over a hundred eons is lost).  ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 155)  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
12  
 
a) The disadvantages of anger  
i) The unseen disadvantages 
1’ Anger destroys the roots of virtue 
2’ One should make effort in cultivating patience after having 
understood the faults and qualities of fury and patience 
ii) The visible disadvantages 
1’ Anger eliminates the opportunity for physical and mental 
pleasure  
2’ [Anger] causes us to destroy dear friendships and so forth 
iii) A presentation of the disadvantages in brief  
b) Reflecting on the benefits of patience 
1’ Anger eliminates the opportunity fo r physical and mental pleasure  
(6,3) 
My mind will not experience peace 
If it fosters painful thoughts of hatred. 
I shall find no joy or happiness; 
Unable to sleep, I shall feel unsettled. 
 
We experience physical discomfort and our mi nds becomes unsettled with mental unhappiness 
when anger manifests in our minds.  Due to this , we do not experience any peace. Even though 
our minds are usually relaxed, happy and calm, th e moment anger arises, the mind becomes very 
unsettled and unhappy.  
 People around us such as our friends and associ ates may also become unhappy and upset. Even 
though normally we may be an attractive, good - hearted person with a good disposition, we 
suddenly become very unpleasant when anger arises.   Wearing good clothes and ornaments do not help when we are angry because our whole appearance changes. We are unable to sleep, even  on a soft,  comfortabl e bed. Due to mental 
unhappiness, we toss and turn.  We may even have  thoughts of wanting to harm those who have 
been very helpful and kind to us, forgetting the kindness and help we received from them. 
Sometimes, we may even carry out these th oughts and actually harm such people.  
2’ [Anger] causes us to destroy dear friendships and so forth 
This is shown in the next 6 lines:- 
(6,4) 
A master who has hatred 
Is in danger of being killed 
Even by those who, for their wealth and happiness, 
Depend upon the master's kindness. 
 
(6,5)  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
13  
By it, friends and relatives are disheartened; 
Though drawn by my generosity, they will not trust me; 
 
We may be someone who gives money and help. But if we are angry all the time, the people who 
depend on us for help will not remember the acts  of kindness that we show ed them. In fact, they 
may  harm, retaliate or even kill us. If this is the case for those who have benefited from our acts 
of generosity,  it goes without saying that those wh o do not depend on us for help are most  likely 
to be unhappy with us too.  
 If we are a bad tempered person, even our close friends, relatives and loved ones will get 
disheartened with us even if we shower them with our generosity, because deep down in their 
hearts, they will never be happy with us. If those people around us whom we help and support 
through material gifts are never happy with us fr om the depths of their hearts, then people to 
whom we do not give gifts and help  won’t be pleased with us either.
4 
iii) A presentation of the disadvantages in brief 
(6, 5 cd)  
In brief, there is nobody 
Who lives happily with anger. 
 
(6,6)  
Hence the enemy, anger, 
Creates sufferings such as these, 
 
In brief, as soon as anger arises in anyone’s mind, regardless of who that person may be, he or 
she doesn’t experience any happiness.  
 
On top of making us unhappy, anger causes us to  engage in actions that  destroy the roots of 
virtues that we worked so hard to accumulate ov er a long period of time. Anger can potentially 
destroy the roots of virtue accumulated over a th ousand eons. Anger causes us suffering in this 
life, but it does not stop there. We continue to ex perience the faults of anger in our future lives. 
                                              
4 The invisible  faults of anger are  
(a) that it projects its own fruitions,  which are extremely unpleasant, and  
(b) that it prevents the arising of the measureless very pleasant fruitions of  its opposite (virtue). 
 
The faults of anger visible  in this lifetime are that you do not expe rience a peaceful and good mind; the joy and 
happiness that you had previously perish , and you cannot regain them; you ca nnot sleep well; and you weaken the 
stability wherein your mind stays ca lm. When you have great hatred, even  those for whom you formerly cared 
forget your kindness and kill you; even  friends and relatives will get annoyed and leave you; although you gather 
others with your generosity, they will not stay; and so on.  
(Lamrim Chenmo,  Vol. 2, pg. 157) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
14  
Anger is one of the true causes of unhappiness and suffering in this and all our future lives . 
b) Reflecting on the benefits of patience  
(6,6 cd)  
But whoever assiduously overcomes it 
Finds happiness now and hereafter. 
 
On the basis of thoroughly understanding the faults of anger, when we  strive to destroy anger, we  experience only happiness. The practice of patience is, therefore, 
the only cause of happiness not just in this life but also in future lives. The benefits 
of patience are opposite to some of the faults that we have already discussed. Since 
that is the case, we have to strive to cultivate patience.  
 
A. The way to train in patience 
a. Explaining the chapter’s material 
1)  Abandoning hindrances to the accomplishment of abiding by the antidote: 
     devoting oneself to the cultivation of patience 
a) The disadvantages of anger 
b) Reflecting on the benefits of patience 
2) Engaging the mind in the methods for accomplishing patience 
a) Arresting the causes of anger 
i) The nature of the cause along with its disadvantages 
ii) Instructions on making effort in the methods for overcoming anger 
iii) The actual method for overcoming that 
1’ It is unreasonable to nurture displeasure  
2’ The reasons for that  
iv) Having examined in detail the causes that give rise to anger, making effort to abandon them 
b) The benefits of cultivating patience 
i) The nature of the cause along with its disadvantages 
(6,7) 
Having found its fuel of mental unhappiness 
In the prevention of what I wish for, 
And in doing what I do not want, 
Hatred increases and then destroys me. 
 
In order to stop anger, we have to stop its causes. If we do not do this, we will never be able to 
stop anger. Before we can work on the causes of anger, we must first be able to identify them.  
 
When we and those who are close and dear to us experience suffering, we feel  unhappy. When 
we and our loved ones experience happiness, we are pleased. On the other hand, we get angry 
when somebody creates problems and prevents us from being happy or causes us to suffer. When 
somebody prevents us from accomplishing our wishes, we start to be unhappy. This 
unhappiness/ discomfort causes hatred to increase just as food causes the body to grow. So this 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
15  
unhappiness is the cause of hatred.  
 
When this hatred increases in our minds, we experience unhappiness/suffering in this and 
future lives. Once there is hatred, there will always be mental unhappiness and not a single 
moment of peace in the mind.  ‘Then destroys me,’ points to how such hatred  causes us to suffer 
not only in this life but also in our future lives.  
ii) Instructions on making effort in the methods for overcoming anger 
(6,8) 
Therefore, I should totally eradicate 
The fuel of this enemy; 
For this enemy has no other function 
Than that of causing me harm. 
 
Since the fuel of hatred is mental unhappiness, we  must eradicate this mental unhappiness. If we 
want to experience happiness, we must stop hatred . In order to stop hatred, we have to stop its 
cause, which is mental unhappiness.   
Why do we need to look upon hatred as our enemy? ‘For this enemy has no ot her function than that 
of causing me harm. ’ Hatred hurts, harms and causes us problems all the time. It serves no other 
function than that.  
 Our enemies may have a change of heart and offer us  help. But it is never like this with our 
internal enemy, hatred. Hatred harm s us continuously and since this is so, we must treat it as our 
main enemy and make every effort to defeat  it
.     
 
What then is the actual method or way of overcoming anger?  
 
iii) The actual method for overcoming that 
1’ It is unreasonable to nurture displeasure 
2’ The reasons for that 
1’ It is unreasonable to nurture displeasure 
(6,9) 
Whatever befalls me, 
I shall not disturb my mental joy; 
For having been made unhappy, 
I shall not accomplish what I wish, 
And my virtues will decline. 
 
As explained earlier, mental unhappiness arises when we experience problems or when others 
interfere with the fulfilme nt of our wishes. So, ‘ Whatever befalls me, I sha ll not disturb my mental 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
16  
joy,’ means that we must ensure that our mind s remain happy all the time. Maintaining such 
‘mental joy’  means being able to remain continuously in  an undisturbed state. This is something 
that we have to remind ourselves of over and over again.  
 We have to use  whatever method we can to ensu re that we do not become  unhappy with all the 
undesirable things that happen to us. When we keep the mind in this perpetually happy state, 
there will be no opportunity for mental unhappi ness to arise. When mental unhappiness is not 
allowed to arise in the mind, there is no opportunity for ha tred to arise.  
 
Some people may assert that it is not right to remain happy when  things go wrong or when we 
are faced with failure. But, in fact it is  reasonable to remain happy and calm even when 
confronted with problems and hardships.   For example, if someone is making  things difficult for us, our un happiness with this person does 
not make the person disappear. The cause of th e problem is still there while we have to 
experience the suffering of our own unhappiness. On top of that, such mental unhappiness leads 
to the generation of anger and hatred, which se ts in motion all the harmful consequences of 
anger.    In essence, this verse is pointing out that bein g unhappy with the person or situation that is 
causing us difficulties does not make the person or situation go away so we may as well remain 
calm and happy when facing the problem. Furthe rmore, when we are unhappy, we not only do 
not experience happiness in this life but by allo wing the causes of happiness to deteriorate, we 
create the causes for more suffering and unhappiness to come in our future lives.
   
2’ The reasons for that  
(6,10) 
Why be unhappy about something 
If it can be remedied? 
And what is the use of being unhappy about something 
If it cannot be remedied? 
 
When there is a solution to the problem and difficult y we are facing, even if it is life-threatening, 
there is no reason to be unhappy. We should stri ve instead to solve the problem. For example, if 
our pen falls on the ground, there is no reason to  be unhappy since we can pick it up and use it 
again.   If the situation or problem cannot be remedied, again, there is no benefit to being  unhappy about it. For example, if your precious piece of cr ystal falls on the ground and breaks into pieces, 
there is no point in feeling upset about it since you cannot glue it back together. Just buy a new 
one. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
17  
 
It is not enough to just know these reasons on an intellectual  level. When difficulties come, we 
need to remember them and apply them to our problems.  
 
Question from student:  Is it possible to scold  someone without anger? 
This is not going to happen overnight. It all de pends on how well we understand that anger is 
bad. Otherwise, it is going to be difficult.  
 The trick here is to stop the mind from feelin g unhappy. The mind must remain happy all the 
time. When mental unhappiness arises in our minds, we should check what is causing the 
unhappiness. By analysing the causes and conditions that brought about that problem, we can check if there is solution. If the problem can be solved, there is no point in being unhappy. If we 
check and nothing can be done, again  there is no  point in being unhappy since that will not  
change the situation. We have to reflect like this all the time to familiarise ourselves with this way 
of thinking.   
Question from student:  What is the difference between mental unhappiness and unhappiness? 
I think they are the same. In Tibetan, me ntal unhappiness and unhappiness are basically 
referring to the same thing. Mental unhappine ss is the fuel or the cause of hatred. Mental 
unhappiness leads to hatred. But once there is ha tred, there is always mental unhappiness. Once 
hatred is manifest, there is not a single moment of mental peace?  
 
Question from student :  Are bodhisattvas who do not  get angry arya  beings? 
Not necessarily. Question : Do arya beings have clairvoyance to be able to  read minds? 
Yes Question:  If the bodhisattva is clairvoyant, he should  be able to see that I will be getting angry 
soon. Shouldn’t that bodhisattva make himself disappear or leave? Otherwise, where is the 
compassion?  
If the sentient being is nearby, where is the bodhis attva to run to? If the person is far away, yes, 
he can run but if the person is next to him, then where can he run to?  
 It is difficult when anger arises. It is also difficult  to see the future. Even for the bodhisattva, it is 
difficult for him to see when you will get angry.   
Question from student : For a beginner, what is the best way to maintain joy? 
It is not easy at the beginning because there is no  familiarity with the ability to remain happy all 
the time. The point here is to stop all mental un happiness from arising and the way to do that is 
by thinking along those lines and using the reason s  that we have discussed before. We have to 
reflect on and think about those reasons over and over again until we gain a good understanding 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
18  
of them.  Otherwise, it is  going to be difficult.  
 
The better we understand those reasons, the more familiar we will be with applying them; then, 
when difficulties arise, through familiarity with a pplication of the counter-measures, we have less 
mental unhappiness. Less  mental unhappiness me ans more mental happiness, doesn’t it? So we 
improve slowly like that.  
 W i t h o u t  t h i n k i n g  o v e r  w h a t  w e  h a v e  d i s c u s s e d  s o  f a r ,  n o n e  o f  t h a t  i s  g o i n g  t o   h a p p e n .  Therefore, we need patience and we are now st udying  the chapter on patience. When we can 
abide in patience, we will always be happy and undisturbed.  
Lesson 4   Chapter VI - verse 11-16 
W e  a r e  g o i n g  t o  c o n t i n u e  w i t h  m o r e  a d v i c e  f r o m  G u n g t a n g  R i n p o c h e ,  w h i c h  w e  s h o u l d  a l l  
reflect on individually.   
Although we desire liberation, if we examine al l our actions, they are only causes for cyclic 
existence. This is disheartening and some thing we should feel very sad about. 
 
For someone intent on achieving liberation, his ac tions should all become causes to achieve that 
goal and not become causes for samsara. We clai m to have the goal of achieving liberation. But 
when we examine our activities closely, all of them  are actually causes for another cyclic existence. 
When we think about this, it is very sad.   Therefore, from now on, if we are truly intent on achieving liberation, then we must try to 
transform everything that we do to ensure that  it becomes the cause for attaining that goal.  
 
We spend our whole lives bearing the name “a Dh arma practitioner”, someone who is interested 
in practising the teachings of the Buddha. Although we  spend our whole lives with such a name, 
in the next life we still end up as an animal. We have the body of an animal. If that should 
happen again, that would be very sad. 
 Although we bear the name of being “a Dharma practitioner”, when we do not transform all our 
daily actions into Dharma, then it is certain we wi ll end up as an animal in our next life. Bearing 
the name of “a Dharma practitioner” alone will no t guarantee us a good rebirth in the next life. 
 The point of this advice is that it is not sufficien t just to bear the name of a Dharma practitioner. 
That is not enough. We must transfor m whatever we do into the Dharma. 
 This collection of advice is very helpful. From time to time, we sh ould try to remember some of it 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
19  
as it is very beneficial for the mind. In this instance, this advice is about checking to see if we are 
really Dharma practitioners or not. We call ou rselves Dharma practitioners but when we examine 
our daily activities we discover that they are not Dharma. If we spend our whole lives like that 
then, one day, when it is time for the breath to stop and we suddenly find ourselves in an animal 
body, it will be too late.   Therefore, it is our individual responsibility to  transform this act of li stening to the teachings 
into Dharma. This depends on our motivation. We have to set the proper motivation for 
listening to the teachings.   When we listen to the teachings, we need to pay attention. Sometimes we do not pay attention 
when we are in class; sometimes we do not come to class; sometimes we are not 100% interested. 
Then coming to class is not as beneficial as it is if we pay full attention.  The main obstacle to becoming a good-hearted pers on is anger. In order for us to become good-
hearted people, we have to eliminate our anger. Anger is the opposite of the good heart and is 
very harmful. Anger harmed us in the past. It is harming us in the present and it will harm us in 
the future.   We can see how anger harms us in this life. But it  does not stop there. The results of anger will 
also harm us in the future. Anger harms in the pa st because all the roots of virtue accumulated in 
the past are subject to destruction when anger mani fests in the mind. As a result of our anger, we 
accumulate very powerful negative karma, which will propel us into the lower rebirths in our 
future lives. Even in this life, anger disturbs us , causing our loved ones to dislike us and so on.  
 Mental unhappiness causes anger to arise in our minds and it arises:  
(a) when others create problems for our loved ones and ourselves, or  
(b) when we and our loved ones are preven ted from getting what we want, or   
(c) when we see that everything is going well for people whom we dislike.  
 These are the conditions that bring about mental  unhappiness. Is there any point in becoming 
unhappy with these conditions? It is not going to solve the problem or make these conditions 
disappear. On top of that, it makes us miserable. If matters can be changed for the better, there is 
no reason to feel unhappy. If we find that we cannot do anything about the situation, again, we 
h a v e  t o  s t o p  o u r  m i n d s  f r o m  b e c o m i n g  u n h a p p y  s i n c e  t h a t  s e r v e s  n o  p u r p o s e  a t  a l l  b u t  o n l y  generates additional suffering for ourselves. The point here is to keep the mind happy.   For the other party who is creating difficulties for us, our unhappiness does not rid ourselves of this person or make him change his mind towa rds us. It is possible our unhappiness will make 
him happier.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
20  
 
Some of the reasons that we have mentioned can be reflected upon during our meditation. 
Meditation helps us to familiarise ourselves with  these lines of reasoning and there is no better 
meditation than familiarising ourselves with the ways of dealing with anger. Then, when anger 
arises in our minds, we can recall these antido tes and apply them immediately. Of course, we 
may not be so successful in the beginning but if we persevere and become more familiar with 
such thoughts, we will improve. It all depends on how familiar we are with such thoughts.  
  You should do this on your own by spending so me time – maybe 15-20 minutes - thinking about 
these points in stages, sorting them out in your own mind while reflecting on your own 
experiences. ( Geshe-la : I do not know how to lead you in this  meditation so try to do it on your 
own.)  
 
A. The way to train in patience 
a. Explaining the chapter’s material 
1) Abandoning hindrances to the accomplishment of abiding by the antidote: 
    devoting oneself to the cultivation of patience 
2) Engaging the mind in the methods for accomplishing patience 
a) Arresting the causes of anger 
i) The nature of the cause along with its disadvantages 
ii) Instructions on making effort in the methods for overcoming 
anger 
iii) The actual method for overcoming that 
iv)  Having examined in detail the causes that give rise to anger, 
making effort to abandon them 
1’ A general presentation of the divisions [of anger in terms 
of] the objects that give rise to anger 
2’ Arresting fury towards that which brings unwanted 
things 3’ Arresting fury towards that which hinders one’s desires 
b) The benefits of cultivating patience 
1’ A general presentation of the divisions [of anger in terms of] the objects that give rise to anger 
(6,11) 
For myself and for my friends 
I want no suffering, no disrespect, 
No harsh words and nothing unpleasant; 
But for my enemies, it is the opposite. 
 
Regarding the objects that give rise to anger, we  get upset when something happens either to our 
loved ones or ourselves. ‘My friends,’ refers to people whom we consider to be on our side.  
 We do not want ‘the four undesirabl es’ for our loved ones and ourselves: 
(1) suffering  (2) disrespect  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
21  
(3) harsh words (especially criticism) and  
(4) bad reputation. 
  
With regard to our enemies and people we dislike,  ‘the four undesirables’ that we do not want 
for them are:  (5) to have happiness,  (6) to be praised or respected,  (7) to hear pleasant words and  (8) to be well known.   These are the 8 worldly dharmas.  Another commentary refers to the 12 undesirables and 12 desirables, making 24 things that can 
make one unhappy. The 12 undesirables are the: a) 4 things that we do not want for ourselves,  b) 4 things that we do not want  our loved ones to have and  
c) 4 things that we do not wa nt our enemies to experience.  
 The 12 desirables are the:- a) 4 things that we want for ourselves (the oppo site of those 4 things th at we do not want),  
b) 4 things that we would like ou r loved ones to experience and  
c) 4 things that we want our enemies to experien ce ( t he o ppo s it e o f t ho s e thin gs  w e w an t  fo r 
ourselves).   We can also look at these 24 items in terms of th e past, present and future, making a total of 72, 
in the following way: 
a) a set of 24 for the past (this was done to my loved ones and me and this happened to my 
enemy),  
b) a set of 24 for the present and  c) a set of 24 for the future.   The point here is that the causes of mental un happiness are the 8 worldly dharmas in relation to 
our loved ones, ourselves, and our enemies. We get angry when we think about what has 
happened: in the past -  “He harmed me in this way,”  in the present -  “He is harming me now, ” and  in the future - “He will harm me again next time.”              
2’ Arresting fury towards that which brings unwanted things 
a’ Arresting fury towards those who wrong you 
1” Being patient with those who would create suffering for you 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
22  
a” Cultivating the patience that accepts suffering 
(1) Reflecting on the fact contam inated phenomena are not beyond 
being of the nature of suffering 
(2) Reflecting on the benefits of meditating on suffering 
(3) Reflecting on the fact that, if one is familiar [with such 
attitudes] they are not difficult to implement 
(4) The benefits of making effort in abandoning the afflictions 
(5) An extensive explanation of the meditations on suffering 
b” Cultivating the patience that is intent on dharma 
c” Cultivating the patience that pays no heed to those who cause harm 
2” Being patient with those who would belittle you and so forth 
b’ Arresting fury towards those who wrong those dear to oneself 
c’ Arresting fury towards those who do well by one’s enemies 
 
We have identified the objects that give rise to  anger. By depending on those objects, we get 
angry. We are now looking at how to stop anger.  Since there are 3 different kinds of patience, 
there are 3 different methods to generate these different kinds of patience.  
The first kind of patience is being patient with those who create suffering for us.   
a” Cultivating the patience that accepts suffering 
(1) Reflecting on the fact contaminated phenomena are not beyond being of the nature of 
suffering  
(6,12) 
The causes of happiness sometimes occur 
But the causes for suffering are frequent. 
 
Contaminated phenomena or cyclic existence is  in the nature of suffering. The causes of 
suffering are numerous but happiness occurs only ra rely. We have to unders tand that this is the 
very nature of cyclic existence. Samsara does no t and will not transcend suffering. In one of his 
texts, Maitreya Buddha uses the ex ample of excrement. It stinks, by  nature, no matter what we do 
to it or whoever smells it.   
Similarly, samsara is in the nature of suffering. Understanding this helps us to be patient with 
samsaric conditions. When we encounter difficulties or problems, thinking, “Why did this 
happen to me? Why must this happen ?” this only adds to the basic suffering that is already there. 
We are in cyclic existence and the very nature of  cyclic existence is such that it will never 
transcend suffering.  
 Regardless of where we are born in the three realms, as long as we are in cyclic existence, we will 
not be free from suffering - just as the very natu re of excrement is to be smelly. When problems 
or suffering arise, we have to try to bear with  them without making the suffering we are already 
experiencing even worse.   
(2) Reflecting on the benefits of meditating on suffering 
(6,12 cd)    
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
23  
Without suffering, there is no renunciation. 
Therefore, mind, you should stand firm. 
 
(6,13) 
If some ascetics and the people of Karnapa 
Endure the pain of cuts and burns for no reason, 
Then for the sake of liberation 
Why have I no courage? 
 
Some people question the value of thinking abou t suffering. It is appropriate and, indeed, there 
is a need to reflect on samsaric suffering becaus e only then will the wish to be liberated from 
such suffering arise. One then develops renunciation.   It is also important to bear with suffering. When  we are in trouble and think about suffering, it 
moves the mind towards thinking about and engaging  in virtue.  This is quite obvious for those 
of us who practise the Dharma and believe in the teachings.  
 For example, when we are struck with a very se rious illness, our minds start to think about the 
Dharma, “What do I need to practise? I hope I don’t die yet.” But when we are healthy and 
everything is going well, the thought to practise    Dharma does not arise. Therefore, suffering is good since suffering provides the favourable conditions that cause us to prac tise the Dharma. Since renunciation  arises in dependence on the 
presence of suffering and suffering moves our mi nds towards Dharma practice, it is appropriate 
to be patient with suffering. 
 
For example, in India, there are people who worshi p Shiva. In order to please Shiva, they engage 
in their own form of Nyung-nay, fasting and inflic ting pain on themselves by beating, cutting or 
burning themselves, etc. There are also groups of people of Karnapa, in South India, who 
congregate to fight one another.  People of other religious traditions engage in such activities for 
no apparent purpose. We have the greater goal of achieving enlightenment for the benefit of all 
sentient beings. Therefore, we should voluntaril y endure whatever suffering may arise and not be 
afraid of such suffering.   
(3) Reflecting on the fact that, if  one is familiar [with such attit udes] they are not difficult to 
implement 
(a) An extensive explanation 
(i) Establishing that if one has familiarized on eself [with such attitudes] they are easily

(4) The benefits of making effort in abandoning the afflictions

(i) Establishing that if one has familiarized oneself [with such atti tudes] they are easily applied 
(6,14) 
There is nothing whatsoever 
That is not made easier through acquaintance. 
So through becoming acquainted with small harms, 
I should learn to patiently accept greater harms. 
 
It is the very nature of the mind  that things become easier for us through familiarity.  When we 
familiarise our minds with virtue, it becomes easier  and easier to accomplish virtue. Accordingly, 
the more familiar our minds are with the 3 mental  poisons, the easier it becomes for them to 
arise.   We may think, “I can’t do this.” But it will become easier the more familiar we are with enduring 
suffering. By first putting up with small sufferings, gradually, over time, we will be able to tolerate greater hardships. The text suggest s that we should first put up with small harms like heat and 
cold or some unpleasant words. When our minds ar e familiar with such practices, our ability to 
put up with greater harms will increase to such an extent that we will be able to endure even the fires of hell. The Buddha said that for bodhisa ttvas who have achieved a particular form of 
concentration, whatever happens to them, instead of experiencing them as problems, they only 
experience bliss and happiness due to their mental habituation with such practice. 
(ii) Establishing that by means of an analogy 
(6,15) 
Who has not seen this to be so, with trifling sufferings 
Such as the bites of snakes and insects, 
Feelings of hunger and thirst, 
And with such minor things as rashes? 
 It is easier for people who have become familiar with the experience of snake or insect bites or 
feelings of hunger and thirst to put up with such hardships. They are more willing to do so but for those who are not habituated to thes e things, it is much more difficult.  
(iii) The object towards which one is patient 
(6,16) 
I should not be impatient 
With heat and cold, wind and rain, 
Sickness, bondage and beatings; 
For if I am, the harm they cause me will increase. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
25  
 
When our level of forbearance is very small, even a small problem becomes big. But for those 
who have great forbearance, even a big problem becomes small.   
Lesson 5   Chapter VI - verse 17-21 
Listen very carefully for the next five to ten minutes. I am giving homework. I am not sure 
whether you need to take note s. It is not a written assign ment. It is an oral exam ( laughter ). 
 
There are people who question, “What does studying  all these different subjects and texts have to 
do with my daily life?” Some people do not s ee the benefit or how study can be applied at a 
practical level. These teachings can definitely be a pplied in our daily lives but, of course, this is 
not easy. This homework can help you make this happen.    
 
Those who have registered for the Basic Progra m must do the homework . For the others, you 
have a choice to do the homework  or not. It is up to you.  
  W e  h a v e  c o m p l e t e d  f o u r  c l a s s e s .  W h a t  I  a m  g o ing to say now is a summary of what we have 
covered so far. Try to remember what I say and then spend at least 10 to 15 minutes every day 
reflecting on it. Do this co ntinuously for one week.   
 We are looking at the practice of patience, which is the antidote to  anger and hatred. First, recall 
a time when anger or hatred arose in your mind . How did it feel? Did you feel happy or was the 
feeling unpleasant? When you encounter some proble ms and you feel upset and angry, or there is 
a lot of hatred in your mind, examine that f eeling and experience. Was it good or bad?  
  Reflect on this well. It is very obvious when we  look at our own experiences, we can conclude 
that when there is anger in our minds; there is a lot of unhappiness. This unhappiness is 
something that we neither want nor need. That being the case, we have to think of ways and 
means to rid ourselves of this mental unhappiness.   It is very good when we have faith in the Buddha’s teachings and the workings of karma. It is obvious that anger disturbs us and makes us un happy. Worse still, anger destroys the roots of 
virtue that we accumulated in the past for a thousand eons.  It is also obvious that as soon as anger arises in our minds, it blocks and interferes  with the generation of virtuous thoughts and a 
virtuous state of mind, especially when the ange r has been growing in the mind for a long time. 
It becomes very difficult for the mind to be po sitive and virtuous. Anger also harms us in the 
future, by causing us to take rebirth in the lowe r realms. We need to reflect deeply on how anger 
harms us in these different ways.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
26  
We then have to examine the causes of anger arisin g in our minds. What is it that is making us 
upset – a person, an incident or situation? We n eed to sort this out clearly in our minds. The 
conclusion is very clear. As mentioned in the text, when there is mental unhappiness it leads to 
anger/hatred.  
 
We then have to check whether our experiences accord with what is said in the text.  Is it true 
that when we experienced this anger, it was because the mind was unhappy in some way? The 
cause of anger/hatred is mental unhappiness. It becomes clear that first we feel unhappy, then 
that mental unhappiness leads to anger/hatred.   We then have to examine what it is that is caus ing us to feel unhappy. The text points out that 
the main cause of mental unhappiness is the eigh t worldly dharmas. For example, we get angry 
when someone harms us. Is this not so? Think ab out it. We feel unhappy when someone stares 
at us in an unfriendly way, or says something nasty about us or physically hurts us. Because the ‘I’ 
or ‘me’ is harmed, we get angry/unhappy. We co me to this conclusion upon reflection.  
 Let us take as an example the experience of be i n g  v e r b a l l y  i n s u l t e d  b y  s o m e o n e .  W e  b e c o m e  
unhappy, then angry and hatred develops. The co ndition for that mental unhappiness and anger 
is hearing those unpleasant words. We should th en reflect: “The words have already been said. 
Can our anger change what has already taken place? ” We should be able to see that, sooner or 
later, the other party will finish what he has to say.   So think: “Are we able to turn back the clock or change the situation by remaining angry? Can 
the insults be retracted if we continue to be angry?”  When we think like this, it becomes clear that getting angry does not serve any purpose at all. 
The insults have been said. The words cannot be ta ken back. By getting angry, we are unable to 
change the situation so our anger is useless. Furthermore, when we get angry, our own minds 
become disturbed and unhappy.  There would be some purpose to our anger if we could rid ourselves of the condition that 
initially upset us. But we can see that is not so. Our anger also ha s no affect on the other party 
who is insults us. We are the ones who experi ence the mental unhappiness and disturbance. 
 After examining this from every possible angle, the conclusion has to be that anger does not serve 
any beneficial purpose whatsoever. It only  serves to make us unhappy, disturb our minds and 
harm us. Based on this conclusion, we should then be convinced that anger is bad for us and be determined not to give in to it in the future: “I must try to stop my anger from arising.” We must 
remember, “I have to be very careful to take care  of my mind, especially when anger is about to 
arise.” 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
27  
 
We should not think like this ju st occasionally; we should rely on our mindfulness and vigilance 
all the time. When we become more habituated to remembering the conclusion to the decision 
that we made, and when we rely on our mindfuln ess and vigilance, it becomes easier for us to 
stop anger from arising.  
 Now we may think this is all too difficult and ther e is no way we can curb our anger. This may be 
true because we have not familiarised ourselves with applying the antidote to our anger. Over 
time, however, through such mental familiarity, we  will be able to manage our anger and stop it 
quickly even if it does arise. It will not remain in the mind for long. This is definitely achievable. 
 It is very difficult to subdue anger completely. Ev erything that we see or experience seems to exist 
very solidly from its own side.  Anger arises based on that appearance. Although it is difficult for 
us not to get angry, we can still familiarise our minds with the antidotes to anger by meditating 
on some of the points I mentioned earlier. With gr eater familiarity, we will definitely be able to 
stop anger from remaining in the mind for long. When  anger arises, we will be able to stop it and 
not follow it.   When we do not train our minds in this way, we  w i l l  c o n t i n u e  t o  l i v e  w i t h  a n g e r ,  b e c o m i n g  
angrier with each passing day. The days become mo nths, the months become years.  If we do not 
do anything about our anger, we will spend our whole life like that.  It will not take you longer than 10 minutes a day to reflect on some of these points. You should 
reflect on them daily for at least a week. This is not optional. This is something you must do. 
After a week, if you find it beneficial, then, of course, you can continue with the reflection. But 
you must try this out for at least a week. 10 minutes is not a long time. It is now up to you. It is 
your responsibility to put aside those 10 minu tes. When you see its usefulness, you will be 
motivated to do it and you will be able to find th e time. But if you do not want to do it, you will 
not be able to find any time, even if you are not working and free for the whole day.  
 This is homework and something that you have to  do. As I have said, if you do not feel like 
doing this, you will not be able to find these 10 minutes even if you have nothing to do for the 
whole day. But, if you have the interest and desi r e  t o  d o  i t ,  e v e n  i f  y o u  h a v e  t o  w o r k  f o r  t h e  
whole day, you will definitely find that 10 minutes.   
This is something you have to do. It is how Mao Tse Tung worked (laughter)  - you have to do it 
whether you like it or not.  If yo u don’t do it, you will be punished! (laughter)  Sometimes, the 
mind is too wilful and uncontrolled.  What Ma o Tse Tung did was good. Sometimes, our minds 
need that kind of treatment. It must be whacked! (loud laughter and comments!)

Some students may wonder what our studies have to do with their lives. They do not see its 
usefulness or benefit. It is true!  We can study and listen to many teachings but when we do not 
reflect on them and try to appl y them to our daily lives, we will always remain the same. 
Therefore, we have to reflect on whatever we ha ve studied and learnt. Think critically and analyse 
how our studies may be related to our lives. There is no better way to integrate the teachings with 
our minds. If we do not meditate in this way, the teachings will never benefit the mind. This is 
my way of persuading you to do this homework. 
 You may have a lot of daily commitments but it re mains to be seen whether the mind changes as 
a result of merely reciting prayers and doing sadh anas alone. I think it is very difficult for the 
mind to improve if you simply spend all your time doing prayers without doing any reflection. But when you spend just 10 minutes every day, co ntinuously for, say, a month, thinking about 
how to stop anger, you will be able to a ssess its usefulness at the end of the month. 
Whether this happens or not depends on you trying  it out for yourself. You have to accumulate 
the experience. Please do not misunderstand I am not saying that you should discard your 
commitments and stop your daily prayers. Since yo u have made the promise to do so, you have 
to continue with your prayers, but do them happily.   This assignment must be done for one week. Be  prepared for questions. I can ask anyone who 
has signed up to share his/her meditation experi ence. I am serious. I am now like Mao Tse Tung 
(laughter).   This is the homework.
