(4) The benefits of making effort in abandoning the afflictions 
(5) An extensive explanation of the meditations on suffering 
b” Cultivating the patience that is intent on dharma 
c” Cultivating the patience that pays no heed to those who cause harm 
(iv) An example of how the strength of patien ce arises if one familiarizes (oneself with it) 
(6, 17) 
Some, when they se e their own blood, 
Become especially brave and steady, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
29  
But some, when they see the blood of others, 
Faint and fall unconscious. 
 
(6,18) 
These (reactions) come from the mind 
Being either steady or timid 
 
One of the earlier verses discussed how the mi nd can become accustomed to doing anything 
through familiarisation. Similarly, if we grow accu stomed to the practice of patience, our patience 
also increases.   In the course of fighting a war or gang fights, there are injuries and blood is shed. Seeing their 
own blood, some fighters become braver, more de termined and may go on to fight better as a 
result of their extensive fighting  experience. There are also coward s who are afraid of the sight of 
the blood of others. The suffering endured and th e blood shed is the same for these two groups 
of people. The difference lies in the mental cour age of the former group. This is an example of 
how the mind can put up with difficulties when  one’s mind is strong  and courageous.   
(b) Synopsis  
(6,18 cd)  
Therefore, I should disregard harms caused to me, 
And not be affected by suffering. 
 
(6,19)  
Even when the wise are suffering 
Their minds remain very lucid and undefiled; 
 In essence, the greater the famili arity with the practice of patien ce, the stronger our patience is. 
When our minds are strong and courageous, we ar e able to endure difficulties to achieve the 
great purpose of achieving the state of enlightenm ent to benefit other sentient beings. We should 
therefore bear with whatever difficulties may arise.   
The wise  in this context, refers to  the bodhisattvas. The bodhisa ttvas’ minds are never disturbed 
regardless of the suffering they have to experience. They are always very calm and happy. They are 
able to put up with difficulties and suffering due to  their familiarity with the practice of patience 
and their strong, courageous minds. We, on th e other hand, are overwhelmed by even small 
problems, due to our lack of familiarity with such practice.   
(4) The benefits of making effort in abandoning the afflictions 
(6, 19  cd)  
For when war is being waged against the disturbing conceptions, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
30  
Much harm is caused at the time of battle. 
 
(6, 20) 
The victorious warriors are those 
Who, having disregarded all suffering, 
Vanquish the foes of hatred and so forth; 
(Common warriors) slay only corpses. 
 
Soldiers in battle are aware of the difficulties that they will meet in the course of waging war, but 
they carry on fighting until their objective is ac hieved, when the enemy is completely destroyed. 
In the same way, we, as Dharma practitioners, ar e waging war against the affliction of anger. In 
the course of doing so, we will definitely suffe r difficulties and problems. However, like the 
soldier, we must carry on.  
 The supreme hero is the person who wages war ag ainst his own afflictions, such as anger, in 
order to achieve the great purpose of enligh tenment to benefit sentient beings and who 
disregards any suffering that may arise in the course of this war.   Those who enter into battle to conquer an extern al enemy are proclaimed victorious when they 
gain control of the enemy’s country or territory . But such victory only came through killing 
corpses. The external enemy that they kill will de finitely have to die one day.  When we do not 
apply the antidotes to our intern al enemies, the afflictions, th ey will never disappear on their 
own.   Therefore, a person who defeats his external enemy is not the real hero. In fact, the real hero is 
the person who subdues his afflictions, for these af flictions follow us into our future lives if we 
do not counteract them with antidotes.  Therefor e, we should make this pledge, “I must try to 
overcome my afflictions by applying the antidote to them. When am I goin g to start if I do not 
start to do this now?”   
(5) An extensive explanation of the meditations on suffering 
(6,21) 
Furthermore, suffering has good qualities: 
Through being disheartened with it, arrogance is dispelled, 
Compassion arises for those in cyclic existence, 
Negativity is shunned and joy is found in virtue. 
 
Suffering has good qualities:  
When suffering arises, our sadness reduces our arrogance. When someone who is usually puffed 
up with pride is struck down by sickness, we  can see their arrogance naturally subsides.  
Recalling our own past suffering and experiencing suffering in the present become conditions for 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
31  
us to develop immeasurable compassion for those who are suffering.  
 
When we experience suffering and think about its causes, we begin to understand that suffering 
arises from our negative actions. Since we do  not like suffering, we become determined to 
abandon its cause, the negative actions, in order to avoid the result, suffering.  
Since we desire happiness, suffering makes us th ink about creating the causes for happiness by 
engaging in virtuous actions. Suffering thus acts  as a condition for motivating us to practise 
Dharma.  
Lesson 6   Chapter VI - verse 22-26 
Here is more advice from Guntang Rinpoche. Rinp oche points out how we work ourselves to the 
point of exhaustion in our daily lives only to achi eve the goals of this life. Carrying on like this 
brings no benefit at all in our future lives.  Realising this, we become disheartened and sad. 
 
We spend our whole life accumulating wealth and possessions but, at the time of death, we leave 
everything behind and cannot bring it along to the next life. Thinking about this is also 
upsetting.  
 
This advice reminds us that only the holy Dharma  can benefit us in our fu ture lives and that we 
need to give up our attachment to the pleasures an d attractions of this life . Therefore, we have to 
cultivate the patience that is intent on Dharma . We must work hard, actualise the teachings and 
put up with whatever difficulties we may enco unter along the way. In order to bear the 
difficulties that come along with Dharma practi ce, we have to cultivate the three types of 
patience; this is what Gungtang Rinpoch e’s advice actually means. These are: 
- the patience of voluntarily accepting problems or sufferings; - the patience that is willing to undergo whatev er difficulty may arise in the course of one’s 
Dharma practice; and  
- the patience of not retaliating when one is harmed.  
 We have completed discussing the first type of patience – the patience of voluntarily accepting 
suffering.  I think the best way to generate such patience is to understand how all contaminated phenomena 
are in the nature of suffering. For example, we ar e unhappy when we fall ill, grow old or have to 
face death.  Our body is the ba sis upon which we experience the suffering of conditionality (or 
pervasive compounded suffering). Simply having th is body means we have to experience old age. 
It is the very nature of our existence to start aging from the moment we are born.  Thinking in 
this way helps us to be less upset when aging takes place. In the same way, death is inevitable. As 
soon as we are born, it is only a matter of time before we die. We have no choice.  Death is part 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
32  
and parcel of life. Understanding this helps us to deal with the death of loved ones or parents.  
 
For example, if we own a breakable object and it  falls to the ground and breaks, there is no 
reason to be upset if we understand its fragile nature.  In the same way,  when a loved one passes 
away, we should not be excessively upset or sad. Wh y? That person was not meant to live forever.  
 
If that person was immortal and suddenly died , that would be shocking, “What happened – why 
did she die?” But since death is a natural thing,  there is no reason to be so upset.   
 It is helpful to reflect on these points especially when we face difficulties, sickness, old age or the 
fear of death. By voluntarily accepting these sufferings, which cannot be stopped anyway, we 
don’t create additional suffering to what we are already experiencing.   Not cultivating this type of patience means that we give ourselves additional stress, unhappiness 
and problems. This text offers many different re asons to help us develop such patience. Use the 
one you find most beneficial. Since suffering is inev itable for us all, it’s good to apply what we 
have learnt so that we can improve our lives.  As long you are here in the class, I would like to request you all to listen attentively to the 
teachings. Otherwise, coming to class isn’t very be neficial. When you listen carefully, as you try to 
connect what I have said in the past with the current presentation, questions may arise in your 
mind. You may find discrepancies or have qualms and doubts. When you do not listen attentively, you don’t have any questions related to  what we have discussed so far. What I say in 
class is not necessarily correct.   
A. The way to train in patience

    oneself to the cultivation of patience

iii) The actual method for overcoming that

     to abandon them 
1’ A general presentation of the divisions [of anger in terms of] the 
    objects that give rise to anger 
2’ Arresting fury towards that which brings unwanted things 
a’ Arresting fury towards those who wrong you 
1” Being patient with those who would create suffering for you

FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE

b’ Arresting fury towards those who wrong those dear to oneself 
c’ Arresting fury towards those who do well by one’s enemies  
3’ Arresting fury towards that which hinders one’s desires 
b) The benefits of cultivating patience 
 
b” Cultivating the patience that is intent on dharma 
(1) An extensive explanation 
(a) As both fury and the one who is furious, etc. rely upon causes, they are not independent 
(i) Neither fury nor the furious person is independent 
(1’) The reasons it is unreasonable to be furious with an afflicted person 
(2’) Fury is not willingly developed (3’) As all wrongdoing arises from causes and conditions, it is without 
independence 
(ii) Their causes and conditions are also not independent 
(b) Refuting the existence of independent causes (c) The need to overcome fury (anger) 
(2) Synopsis 
(1’) The reasons it is unreasonable to be furious with an afflicted person  
(6, 22) 
As I do not become angry 
With great sources of suffering such as jaundice, 
Then why be angry with animate creatures? 
They, too, are provoked by conditions. 
 
(6, 23)  
Although they are not wished for, 
These sicknesses arise; 
And likewise, although they are not wished for, 
These disturbing conceptions forcibly arise. 
 
We may develop forbearance as a result of havi ng reflected on the patience of voluntarily 
accepting suffering. But we may still think, “I ca n put up with suffering. But why should I not be 
angry at someone who is giving me problems?” Th ese two verses explain why it is inappropriate 
for us to be angry with such a person. 
 According to Tibetan medicine, wind, bile and phle gm are the basis for all the sicknesses that we 
experience. Although we may be un happy when we are sick, we are no t angry at the sickness itself 
in the same way that we are angry with someone who abuses us. There is no reason to be angry 
with our sickness because we can see that it is th e result of certain causes and conditions coming 
together.  Yet we feel it is justifiable to be angry with our enemy, the person who is harming us, thinking, 
“He did that terrible thing to me. I should be angry with him.”   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
34  
For us, getting angry with the enemy and getting angry at our illness are two different matters. 
But, in reality, this is not so. Just as our illnes ses arise due to the coming together of causes and 
conditions and are, therefore, not independent, the abuse (from the person) is also due to the 
coming together of certain causes and conditions. So the two situations are similar in that both 
our enemy and sickness are under the control of  causes and conditions. Our ‘enemy,’ who is 
under the control of his afflictions, has no choice  and is unable to behave independently. It is, 
therefore, inappropriate for us to be angry with him. 
(2’) Fury is not willingly developed  
(6, 24) 
Without thinking, "I shall be angry," 
People become angry with no resistance, 
And without thinking, "I shall produce myself,” 
Likewise, anger itself is produced. 
 
We may reject this rationale by saying, “Yes, thes e two situations may be similar but my sickness 
has no intention of causing me harm, whereas my enemy has every intention of hurting me. 
Therefore, I am justified in being angry with th e enemy”. The first two li nes of Verse 24 refute 
this.  
 
Although we may not have any intention of bein g angry with or harming the other party, anger 
can arise spontaneously, at the slightest provocatio n, or when certain causes and conditions come 
together. The conditions also have no wish to ge nerate anger or harm, but when all the necessary 
conditions come together, anger is the result.   An analogy here may be helpful. The mirror has no intention of reflecting anything on its 
surface, nor does the reflected object have any in tention of being reflecte d.  However, when the 
object and the mirror come together, there is a refl ection of the object in the mirror. Similarly, 
sicknesses and enemies come about through the mere aggregation of causes and conditions. 
Neither have any intention of causing us harm. The enemy may not have the initial thought to 
cause pain and harm but when they are provoked, their anger arises and this leads to the harmful 
actions. 
5 
                                              
5 (a) On analysis of whether the object has self-control, anger is unjustified: Analyze , thinking, “What would be 
reasonable grounds for anger toward harm doers?” Whereupon, you might think, “They first had the thought of 
wanting to harm me, prepared the method, and then ei ther prevented my happiness or inflicted unpleasant 
physical or mental suffering, so my anger is justified.” Ar e you angry because they inflic ted harm while they had the 
self-control not to harm you, or are you angry because they were utterly without any self-control and hurt you while 
helplessly impelled by something else? In the former case, your anger is unjustified because those who inflict harm 
do not have control over themselves, fo r, when the conditions and causes – se eds left by afflictions to which they 
were previously habituated, a nearby object, and erroneou s conceptions – come together, they give rise to the 
thought to harm, even though the harm doers do not thin k, “I will feel malice” ; whereas if those causes and 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
35  
(3’) As all wrongdoing arises from causes and conditions, it is not independent.   
(6, 25) 
All mistakes that occur 
And all the various kinds of wrongdoing 
Arise through the force of conditions; 
They do not govern themselves. 
 
The negative actions that we commit, motivated by  anger and other afflictions, arise from causes 
and conditions. When others direct  negative actions at us, their actions also come about due to 
causes and conditions. Such people are under the co ntrol of their afflictions so there is no reason 
for us to be angry with them. By reflecting on ho w they will have to experience the results of their 
negative actions, we should generate  compassion for them instead.    
 
b” Cultivating the patience that is intent on dharma 
(1) An extensive explanation 
(a) As both fury and the one who is furious, etc. rely upon causes, they are not independent 
(i) Neither fury nor the furious person is independent 
(ii) Their causes and conditions are also not independent 
(b) Refuting the existence of independent causes 
(c) The need to overcome fury (anger) 
(2) Synopsis 
(ii) Their causes and conditions are also not independent  
(6, 26) 
These conditions that assemble together 
Have no intention to produce anything, 
And neither does their product 
Have the intention to be produced. 
                                                                                                                                                           
conditions are not complete, they will never produce the thought to harm, even if the harm doers think, “I will feel 
malice.” These causes and conditions produce the desire to  harm; this in turn produces the work of harming; and 
this produces suffering for someone else , so those harm doers do not have even  the slightest self-control. Moreover, 
they have become like servants of their afflictions, because they are under the control of others, i.e. their 
afflictions. 
In the latter case – you are angry because the harm do ers are utterly without any self-control and, being 
helplessly impelled by something else, they hurt you – then  your anger is totally unjustified. For instance, some 
people who have been possessed by demons and have co me under their control may wish to hurt those who are 
helping them to get free of their demons and thereupon be at them, etc. However, their helpers think, “They do 
this because their demons have eliminated their ability to  control themselves,” and do not have even the slightest 
anger toward them. They then strive to the best of thei r ability to free them from their demons. Likewise, when 
bodhisattvas are hurt by others, they think, “They do th is because the demons of the afflictions have eliminated 
their ability to control themselves.” Without being even th e slightest bit angry with those persons they then must 
generate the spirit of enlightenment, thinking, “I will st rive at the bodhisattva deeds in order to free them from 
these afflictions.” ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 160) 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
36  
 
We may then think, “The enemy harmed me be cause he is under the control of anger and 
certain causes and conditions. But those ca uses and conditions are independent.”  
 
We may be provoked by the sight of  an unpleasant object but if we do not see such a thing, then 
we don’t generate an eye consciousness apprehendi ng such a form. Therefor e, there is no basis 
for anger to arise in relation to such an object.   For anger to arise in relation to a visual form , three conditions must come together: the (1) 
presence of the object, (2) eye sense power (3 ) consciousness. These three conditions don’t 
intend to generate anger in us. Likewise, the a ffliction (anger) doesn’t think, “I shall now be 
produced by these three conditions.”   In summary:  
• The causes and conditions that cause us to be angry are not independent  
• The enemy or person harming us is also not independent as they are under the control of 
their afflictions  
• The afflictions themselves ar e also not independent.  
Lesson 7   Chapter VI - verse 27-30 
We usually begin the lesson with the refuge and bodh icitta prayer.  It is important to think about 
the meaning of this prayer and to try to generate the states of mind mentioned in it. This helps 
us to cultivate the correct motivation for receiving these  teachings. 
 
In the last class, we had complete d the section that dealt  with why it is inappropriate for us to be 
angry with people who are controlled by their afflictions. We have also seen why it is 
inappropriate for us to be upset at the  suffering that we experience. We ma y still think, however, 
“I can put up with the suffering bu t I cannot stand that  person who is making life so difficult for 
me.”   That enemy is harming us due to the convergenc e of causes and condit ions. He may have no 
intention to harm us but, being co ntrolled by his afflictions, when  anger comes, he is unable to 
control himself.  We need to remember this. When we analyse the enemy more deeply, we see 
that when he is behaving so uncontrollably due to his negative emotions, he may also be harming 
others by engaging in negative activities such as  killing, stealing, engaging in sexual misconduct 
a n d  s o  f o r t h .   H e  i s  c r e a t i n g  t h e  c a u s e s  o f  s u f f e r i n g  i n  t h i s  l i f e  a n d  f u t u r e  l i v e s .  W i t h  t h i s  
awareness, we will feel compassion for that  person instead of feeling angry with him. 
 
There are certain Indian non-Buddhist philosophica l systems that assert that there is such a 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
37  
phenomenon as an independent cause leading to harm . To refute this assertion, we need to have 
a good understanding of their positions. In this  text, we are looking at the assertions of the 
Samkhyas (Numerator) and Nyayas (Logician).   
 We had discussed earlier how, for visual objects, anger arises due to the coming together of three 
conditions:-the presence of the object; the ey e sense power perceiving  the object and the 
consciousness or the mind.  In our system, we assert that independent causes  do not exist. When we see how anger arises 
with the coming together of these three cond itions, we realise that  anger does not arise 
independently.  The three conditions are also no t independent since they are the results of their 
own respective causes. This is how we  establ ish that independent causes do not exist.  
 When we assert that a phenomenon  does not exist independently, we are, in effect, saying that 
that phenomenon is a dependent arising (or of de pendent origination) which means it exists in 
dependence on something else and is ‘empty’ of  independence. In a similar way, our enemy is 
also empty of such independence. There is no basi s for anger to arise when we understand this.  
Why is this so? When we are angry at our enemy,  he appears to be independent and solid, but 
when we understand that he is actually not independent but is controlled by certain causes and conditions, our anger will gradually subside.    
b” Cultivating the patience that is intent on dharma 
(1) An extensive explanation 
(a) As both fury and the one who is furious, etc. rely upon causes, they are not 
independent 
(b) Refuting the existence of independent causes 
(i) Refuting the independent self and principal of the Samkhyas 
(1’) Refuting that the principal generates independent expressions 
(2’) Refuting that an aware being autonomously experiences objects 
(ii) Refuting the independent self of the Nyaya’s (Logicians) 
(1’) It is not valid [to posit that] a permanent phenomenon produces an effect 
(2’) It is not valid [for a permanent phenomenon] to rely upon conditions 
(3’) Permanent phenomena are not related to conditions 
(iii) Once one understands all migrators to be like emanations (they see that) it is 
unreasonable to be furious (with them)  
(c) The need to overcome fury (anger) 
(2) Synopsis 
 
When we studied tenets in an earlier module, we learnt how Buddhists refute the existence of a permanent, unitary and independent self.  
 
The Samkhyas, however,  assert that there is a primal independent substance, which is the basis 
for imputing the self. They assert that this  substance exists, independent of causes and 
conditions, and created the world and everything in  it. It is like an independent creator God who 
is capable of producing independent results. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
38  
(1’) Refuting that the principal generates independent expressions 
(6, 27) 6 
That which is asserted as Primal Substance7 
And that which is imputed as a Self,8 
(Since they are unproduced,) do not aris e after having purposefully thought, 
"I shall arise (in order to cause harm)." 
 
(6, 28)9 
If they are unproduced and non-existent, 
Then whatever wish they have to produce (harm will also not exist). 
 
The Samkhyas assert that this independent  prim al substance is capable of creating independent 
harm. As Buddhists, we refute this as an impossibi lity, akin to the horns of a rabbit. The primal 
substance does not exist, as it is not created. Since there is no such thing as an independent 
cause, it cannot produce any effect at all.  
  
The Samkhyas cannot assert that the primal subs tance is created. Creation comes about due to 
causes and conditions. Once they  concede that the primal substance is created by causes and 
conditions, their theory of independent cause collapses.   A created substance is also impermanent. If they co ncede this too, then thei r theory of the primal 
substance being a permanent phenomenon also falls apart.  
                                             
 
6 The non-Buddhist school of the Samkhyas states that all that exists depends on a primal independent substance 
that produces the various phenomena enjoyed by a self, or soul, that is both devoid of parts and permanent, and 
that does not depend on causes and conditions. But since there are no produced phenomena that exist 
without depending on causes and conditions, there cannot exist a substance or a primordial force of that 
sort, nor can there be a permanent self that learns an d takes delight in the various phenomena. This primal 
substance presented by the Samkhyas cannot even possess th e motivation for creating something, and since it is 
not produced itself, it cannot produce in turn, because it is permanent. In fact, any effect can only arise out of 
particular conditions from a substantial cause, which in  turn derives from another cause. (Geshe Yeshe Tobden, 
The Way of Awakening, pg. 154)  
7 According to the non-Buddhist Samkhya school of philosophy, the primal substance is the permanent underlying material 
cause and the nature of all objective phenomena.  
8 This refers to the non-Buddhist concept of an un changing, partless and autonomous self and soul..  
9 Non-Buddhist schools believe in the existence of a permanen t self or "I," whereas Buddhists believe that the self is 
impermanent. By "permanent" we mean something that is not subject to any kind of inner change. 
If there were a soul or a permanent self that had the experience of a particular object, it would have to 
experience that object at all times. 
If we had a permanent self and were enjoying food, we would have to keep  eating without ever stopping, the 
characteristic of immutability being permanent. Instead, we continuously change our activities—we eat, sleep, and 
walk. (Geshe Yeshe Tobden, The Way of Awakening , pg. 154)  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
39  
If the Samkhyas insist that the primal substanc e is not created, it necessarily follows that it 
cannot produce any results. In order for results to  be produced, the cause itself must come from 
its own cause.  Otherwise, it cannot produce any results.    
(2’) Refuting that an aware being autonomously experiences objects 
(6, 28 cd) 
Since (this Self) would perman ently apprehend its object, 
It follows that it woul d never cease to do so. 
 
The Samkhyas also assert that this primal subs tance (or principal) eman ates the pleasant and 
unpleasant objects that we see.  The independent self (an aware being), then interacts with  and 
enjoys these different phenomena. If the indepe ndent self enjoyed looking at a beautiful form, 
which was emanated by the principal, that self wo u l d  h a v e  t o  d o  s o  f o r e v e r .  W h y ?  S i n c e  i t  i s  
permanent, it cannot change.   
(ii) Refuting the independent self of the Nyaya’s 
(1’) It is not valid [to posit that] a permanent phenomenon produces an effect 
(2’) It is not valid [for a permanent phenomenon] to rely upon conditions (3’) Permanent phenomena are not related to conditions 
(1’) It is not valid [to posit that] a permanent phenomenon produces an effect 
(6, 29)10 
Furthermore, if the Self were permanent, 
It would clearly be devoid of action, like space. 
 
While both the Samkhyas and the Nyayas assert an independent,  permanent self, they are 
differentiated by the nature of that self. The Samk hyas assert that the self is the mind while the 
Nyayas assert the self is matter.  
 
The Nyayas assert that the indepe ndent thought of harming is prod uced by this independent and 
permanent self.   Buddhists refute that such a se lf has the ability to function at all. It cannot 
produce any effect since  it is permanent. Such a self is like uncompounded space, which do not 
produce anything at all. 
                                              
10 If a permanent self existed, it would b e  l i k e  s p a c e ,  d e v o i d  o f  a c t i o n .  B u t  t h e r e  i s  n o  s u c h  t h i n g  a s  a  p r i m a l  
substance endowed with volition and able to create, nor does there exist a permanent self. The Samkhyas say that 
the primal substance is permanent, but that it is able to  create by encountering particular circumstances. Now, 
something permanent cannot come into contact with an y circumstance or condition, since nothing can mod-
ify a permanent phenomenon, whose nature is definitively unchanging. 
What influence can any agent have on this hypothetical primal substance whose nature is supposed to be 
permanent? None whatsoever! 
Nothing can modify or benefit a permanent self, becaus e self and object could not relate, and would not be 
connected. (Geshe Yeshe Tobden, The Way of Awakening , pg. 154)

(6, 29 cd) 
So even if it met wi th other conditions, 
How could its unchanging (nature) be affected? 
 
(6, 30)11    
Even if when acted upon (by other conditions) it remains as before, 
Then what could actions do to it? 
(3’) Permanent phenomena are not related to conditions 
(6, 30 cd)   
Thus if I say that this (condition ) acts upon (a permanent Self), 
How could the two ever be (causally) related? 
Lesson 8   Chapter VI - verse 31-40 
The reason it is inappropriate for us to be an gry with our enemy is because he is under the 
control of causes and conditions  that leave him with neither in dependence nor choice in his 
behaviour. Any created phenomenon depends on  a cause. Such causes and conditions are 
themselves subject to their previous causes an d conditions, which means that the continua of 
causes and conditions are beginningless and not independent.  
 
According to the Prasangika Madhyamika tenet sy stem, there is no such phenomenon as an 
independent cause.   
                                              
11 Fire and smoke are of different natures, and yet fire ha s an effect and an influence on smoke. If there were 
a permanent self, nothing could influence it. 
Relationships between various phenomen a can be of two kinds: of the same nature, and cause and effect. "Of 
the same nature" refers to two characteristics of th e same phenomenon, such as being produced and being 
impermanent, or warmth and light, two characteristics of fire. The "of the same nature" argument is invoked 
after denying the possibility of acting on something permanent. The Samkhyas defend their position by stating 
that there is an additional type of relationship by wh ich a permanent self can interact with other objects. In 
reality, however, the only relationships that exist are the two previously described, in which phenomena relate 
mutually. If one phenomenon were permanent, it could not relate with the other!  
In order to speak of causal relation ships between phenomena, there must be an effect. For instance, if we are 
ill and become healthy again after taking medicine, we can say that we were cured as a result of the medicine. 
Since a permanent phenomenon cannot ever change, any ac tion or influence on it will not produce an effect. 
The Samkhyas consider the objects of the world to be im permanent and the self to be  permanent. This permanent 
s e l f  s h o u l d  t h e n  e n j o y  i m p e r m a n e n t  o b j e c t s — b u t  t h i s  t y p e  o f  r e l a t i o n s h i p  i s  c o m p l e t e l y  a b s u r d .  F o r  i f  o n e  
phenomenon is permanent and the others are impermanent,  it is neither possible for a same-nature relationship 
nor a relationship of cause and effect to  exist between them. (Geshe Yeshe Tobden, The Way of Awakening,  pg. 
154)  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
41  
 
There are, however, non-Buddhist philosophical sy stems that assert that there are independent 
causes, which can produce independent results. We  assert that, if such an independent cause 
exists, it exists without depending on other caus es and conditions, which will also mean that it 
cannot produce any effect.

(iii) Once one understands all migrators to be lik e emanations (they see that it is) unreasonable 
to be angry (with them) 
(6, 31) 
Hence everything is governed by other factors 
(which in turn) are governed by (others), 
And in this way noth ing governs itself. 
Having understood this, I should not become angry 
With phenomena, which are like apparitions. 
 
Our enemies are ‘other-powered,’ which means they do not exist independently of causes and 
conditions. The anger generated by our enemies ca me about due to causes and conditions that, 
in turn, arose from their own causes and condit ions, which also arose from their own set of 
preceding causes and conditions and so forth. Therefore, we ca nnot posit a beginning to the 
causes and conditions or find the initial cause th at started the chain of events that brought about 
the anger in the first place. The causes and cond itions themselves are also other-powered and not 
independent.   We need to understand how the enemy is like an illusion or a dream; he appears but he does not 
exist truly. For example, an illusion does not appear suddenly without a reason. It comes about 
due to certain causes and conditions, such as th e presence of the illusionist and the substances 
he uses, etc.   Amongst all the different rationales given, this is sa id to be the best method to deal with anger. If 
we can ascertain that the enemy does not exist independently but as a dependent-arising (due to certain causes and conditions) and therefore has no  control over his actions, we can definitely 
prevent anger from arising in our minds. For ex ample, we would not be angry if our loved one 
who was possessed by spirits harmed us. Since we would know that he was under the control of 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
42  
other causes and conditions, we would only feel  compassion for him.  We should apply that 
same understanding towards our enemies. 
 
b” Cultivating the patience that is intent on dharma  
(1) An extensive explanation 
(a) As both anger and the one who is angry, etc. rely upon causes, they are not 
independent 
(b) Refuting the existence of independent causes  
(c) The need to overcome anger 
(2) Synopsis 
(c) The need to overcome anger 
(6, 32) 
- (If everything is unreal, like an apparition,) then who is there to  
restrain what (anger)? 
Surely (in this case) restraint would be inappropriate- 
It would not be inappropriate, beca use (conventionally) I must maintain 
That in dependence upon restraining (ang er), the stream of suffering is severed. 
 
In response to the previous verse, a qualm may arise, “If all phenomena are like apparitions, 
since they do not exist truly, how can the medita tion on patience be the antidote to anger?”  
 All lower tenet systems assert that phenomena exis t inherently because they do not know how to 
posit the functioning of cause an d effect in any other way.   
 According to the Prasangika-Madhyamika tenet system , we will be able to stop the continuum of 
the effects of anger conventionally  – such as rebirths in the hell realms - through the practice of 
patience. Using the analogy of mistaking a coil of ro pe for a snake, once we understand that it is 
actually a rope, the fear of a snake is eliminated . Similarly, by understanding that our enemy is 
not inherently existent, we will be able to eliminate our anger towards him.   The Prasangika-Madhyamika School refutes the argu ment of the lower tenets in this way: “We 
are only able to abandon the objects of abando nment and to cultivate the relevant antidotes 
because phenomena do not exist inherently. If one asserts that things exis t truly, this assertion 
has to apply to the objects of abandonment as  well. This means that they exist without 
depending on causes and conditions and are, th erefore, not subject to change. That being the 
case, how would we be able to abandon such objects?” 
(2) Synopsis  
(6, 33) 
So when one sees an enemy or even a friend 
Committing an improper action, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
43  
By thinking that such things arise from conditions 
I shall remain in a happy frame of mind. 
 
(6, 34) 
If things were brought into being by choice, 
Then since no one wishes to suffer, 
Suffering would not occur 
To any embodied creature. 
 
By understanding that their behaviour is contro lled by causes and conditions, when our enemies 
or friends commit negative actions, we should be  patient and keep our minds in a happy state. 
 Things do not arise independent of causes and co nditions. If they did, we would have a choice. 
Since it is the wish of all living beings to be ha ppy, we should always be happy. But that is not the 
case. This shows our lack of independence from other factors.  When we are harmed, that situation does not aris e without a cause. When we grasp strongly at 
the enemy as solid, independent and not subject to  other causes and conditions, we continue to 
be angry. For example, when we mistake a coil of rope for a snake, fear arises. Only through 
understanding that there is no snake are we be ab le to overcome our fear. In the same way, by 
understanding how the enemy does not exist inhe rently but exists in dependence on other 
factors, are we able to pacify our anger towa rds the enemy. Understanding and meditating on 
dependent arising, on how phenomena are empty of  inherent existence, is the supreme antidote 
for overcoming any afflictions. It not only stop s manifest afflictions but can also destroy their 
seeds.  
In summary, why is it that we experience suffering  despite our wish to be happy? This shows that 
all phenomena – including our enemies and the harm they inflict on us - do not exist inherently 
or independently of causes and conditions. The more we familiarise ourselves with meditating on 
emptiness, the weaker our anger and other nega tive emotions will be. When we achieve the 
direct realisation of emptiness, we can uproot al l our negative emotions, such as anger, together 
with their seeds.

(a) Some sentient beings cause you harm due to the lack of understanding

Through not being careful, 
People even harm themselves with thorns and other things, 
And for the sake of obtaining women and the like,

Sometimes, people harm themselves due to lack  of conscientiousness. When they are obsessed 
with women (or men), wealth and possessions, etc. , they may even starve  themselves. Controlled 
by strong afflictions, they commit suicide by hanging themselves, leaping from buildings or

(b) If one can kill even oneself due to the lack of understanding, it is not amazing that they can 
cause harm to others 
(6,37) 
If, when under the influence of disturbing conceptions, 
People will even kill their treasured selves, 
How can they be expected not to cause harm 
To the bodies of other living beings? 
 
If they can harm even their own dearly cherishe d bodies, harming others will not be unexpected. 
Understanding this, we develop patience an d keep our minds in a happy state.

 People who harm themselves and others are not ac ting independently because they are controlled 
by their afflictions; in the sa me way that some people are possessed by spirits. Understanding 
this, we develop patience and compassion for them in stead of anger. Even if we are unable to feel 
compassion for them, at the every least,  we should not be angry with them.  
 
(2) Arresting the causes of anger 
(a) It is unreasonable to be angry with a child if it is in his nature (to lack understanding 
and act so) 
(b) It is unreasonable to be furious even if the conditions that brought about harm are adventitious 
(c) By examining the direct and/or indirect causes, [one comes to see that] being furious 
is unreasonable 
(a) It is unreasonable to be angry with a child if it is in his nature (to lack understanding and act 
so) 
(6, 39) 
Even if it were the nature of the childish 
To cause harm to other beings, 
It would still be incorrect  to be angry with them, 
For this would be like begrudging fi re for having the nature to burn. 
 
‘The childish ,’ here means ordinary beings. We need to analyse this: Is it in the nature of ordinary 
beings to harm others? If it is in their nature, it is inappropriate to be angry with them, just as it 
is inappropriate for us to be angry at fire for being hot and burning us when we put our hand in 
it.

FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE

(b) It is unreasonable to be angry even if the c onditions that brought about harm are adventitious 
(6, 40) 
And even if the fault were temporary 
In they who are by nature reliable, 
It would still be incorrect to be angry, 
For this would be like begrudging spac e for allowing smoke to arise in it. 
 
Though it is not in their nature to cause harm, but due to some adventitious conditions, we are 
harmed. It is still inappropriate for us to get angr y. When we are angry, we should be angry with 
the anger or afflictions in the enemy and not the enemy himself because, by nature, he may be a 
good-hearted person. Getting angry with the enemy here would be like getting angry at space when billowing smoke irritates our noses.

Lesson 9   Chapter VI - verse 41-50 
In Gungtang Rinpoche’s collection of advice, he says that we spend 20 years of our lives not 
thinking about the Dharma and another 20 years, thinking, “Oh, I must practise the Dharma,” 
but never doing anything about it. Then we spend another 20 years not being able to practise the 
Dharma, at the end of which we thin k, “Oh, I didn’t get to practise  the Dharma after all.” In this 
way, we waste our entire lives.  
 When we examine our own lives carefully, we ca n see how this advice is applicable to us, 
describing exactly our situation here in this particular module. In th e beginning, we did not 
think of studying. Then we decided we needed  to study, yet we did nothing about it.  
 It is your responsibility to ensure that what  Rinpoche describes does not happen to you. You 
have to make sure that this opportunity does not go to waste. You must pay attention when 
listening to the teachings. Otherwise, even th ough physically, you will have spent 5 years 
attending classes, you will end up knowing nothing. It is important that you try your best to pay 
attention when you are in class.   Sometimes, the mind gets distracted. It is impo rtant that not only the sound of the words is 
going into the ears but the mind must be listenin g as well. If you do not pay attention with your 
mind, at the end of class, if someone asks you,  “What was covered in class today?” your reply 
might be, “Oh, I can’t remember.” A lot of people end up like this. This is not a good thing and 
                                             
 
12 On analysis for either adventitiousness or inherency, an ger is unjustified: The fault of doing harm to others 
either is or is not in the nature of living beings. If it is in  their nature, it is wrong to get angry, just as it is wrong to 
get angry at fire for being hot and burning.  Similarly, if it is adventitious, it is  also wrong to be angry, just as when 
smoke and the like appear in the sky, it is wrong to be angry at the sky on account of these flaws of smoke and so 
forth. Thinking in this way, stop your anger. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg 162)  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
47  
not beneficial.  You must retain something that is  beneficial to the mind.  Please keep this in 
mind.   
 
Most of the points explained in this text need to be understood  and practised through analytical 
meditation, where logic is applied to the reason s given. When the rationale is explained, you 
need to check whether it makes sense or not. If it  does not make sense to you, then you need to 
ask and clarify. Please generate the correct and positive motivation for participating in this 
session. Please listen carefully.  
c” Cultivating the patience that pays no heed to those who cause harm 
(1) Engaging the mind in the method of compassion 
(2) Arresting the causes of fury 
(a) It is unreasonable to be furious with a child if it is in his nature [to lack 
understanding and act so] 
(b) It is unreasonable to be furious even if the conditions that brought about harm are adventitious 
(c) By examining the direct and/or indirect causes, [one comes to see that] being 
furious is unreasonable  
(3) Reflecting on unwanted occurrences being due to one’s own faults 
(c) By examining the direct and/or indirect causes, [one comes to see that] being furious is unreasonable
 
(6, 41) 
If I become angry with the wielder, 
Although I am actually harmed by the stick, 
Then since the perpetrator, too, is secondary, being in turn  
incited by hatred, 
I should be angry with the hatred instead. 
 
If we are physically attacked with a stick or so mebody throws stones at us, most of us will be 
angry with the attacker. Although it is actually the weapon – the stick or stone – that is causing 
pain and hurting the body, we do not get angry at  it. Our reasoning is this: “The stick or stone 
cannot hurt me unless it is used by someone.  Th erefore I should be angry with the attacker and 
not the weapon.”  
 But if we think like this, we should apply the same  line of reasoning to the attacker, who is under 
the complete control of his anger. Therefore, we  should direct our anger towards the anger of 
our attacker and not the attacker himself. The cause of the attack is the hatred in the attacker’s 
mind. He is wedged in between the weapon that is  directly hurting us and the hatred in his own 
mind. By thinking in this way, when we are a ttacked, we can prevent anger from arising towards 
our attacker.  
 
It is said that trying to meditate without listen ing extensively to the teachings is like trying to 
climb a cliff face without fingers. Subduing anger is not easy and requires a lot of effort. This text 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
48  
shows us many ways of doing this.  If we do no t have a wide variety of methods at our disposal, 
we will have nothing to work with even if we have the wish to overco me our anger. The more 
extensively we listen and learn, the wider will be our perspective and the more tools we will have 
to cope with our anger. If we don’t adopt these tools, then we have to rely on only one or two 
antidotes to our anger, which do not really he lp to develop our minds. So, when we hear 
someone saying, “Oh, you don’t need to study in order to meditate,” this is not an acceptable 
position to take.   
(3) Reflecting on unwanted occurrences being due to one’s own faults 
(a) The meaning of the actual 
(i) Reflecting on the harm brought upon you by another as one’s own fault 
(ii) The cause of suffering is a disadvantage of having taken a body 
(iii) [Seeing it as] a disadvantage of havi ng created the causes of suffering in a 
former life 
(iv) As it is one’s own karma that presses others into bringing suffering upon us, 
it is unreasonable to be furious with them 
(v) fury itself is erroneous and thus unreasonable 
(b) Abandoning objections  
(i) Reflecting on the harm brought upon you by another as one’s own fault  
(6, 42) 
Previously, I must have caused similar harm 
To other sentient beings. 
Therefore, it is right for this harm to be returned 
To me, who caused injury to others. 
 
Here is another method to deal with harm directed  at us. All the suffering that we experience in 
life is the fruitional effect of negative action s that we committed in the past. Having harmed 
others, now the harm is returning to us. Reflecti ng on this is one way of curbing our anger and 
developing our patience.  
We discussed the meaning of this verse exte nsively discussed in the previous module, The Wheel 
of Sharp Weapons . Different kinds of negative actions result  in the different kinds of suffering we 
experience in this life.  
 If we can reflect that it is reasonable for onesel f to experience the results of one’s past negative 
actions, we will be able to prevent the mind from  becoming agitated and keep it in a calm and 
peaceful state. 
(ii) The cause of suffering is a disadvantage of having taken a body 
(6, 43) 
Both the weapon and my body 
Are the causes of my suffering. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
49  
Since the other gave rise to the weapon, and I to the body, 
With whom should I be angry? 
 
(6, 44) 
If, in blind attachment, I cling 
To this suffering abscess of a human form 
Which cannot bear to be touched, 
With whom should I be angry when it is hurt? 
 
This is another reason to be patient when we are harmed physically. Both the weapon and our 
bodies play an equal role in our experience of pain when we are attacked. The weapon is the 
cause of the physical pain we feel while our bodi es are the bases for experiencing that pain and 
suffering.   
When we are attacked, there are two causes for th at painful experience: the main (or substantial) 
cause and the contributory cause. The main cause of  our pain is the set of aggregates (body) that 
we have as the result of our karma and delusion s.  The contributory cause of our pain is the 
attacker. Both these causes are equally responsible for our suffering.   
 Why then do we focus our anger only on the attacker and not on our own bodies? It is, 
therefore, inappropriate to be angr y at the attacker. By reflecting  
in this way, we can keep our minds calm and peaceful.   Our bodies, which are made of blood and filth, are unable to bear heat an d cold, causing us great 
agitation. Since this is the natu re of our bodies, which are the result of our own ignorance and 
negativities, then why are we angry when we are ex perience pain and harm? It is unreasonable to 
blame someone else for such physical suffering.   Anger has to be stopped at all times. There ar e people who are angry with and loath their own 
bodies thinking they are too fat/thin/ugl y, etc. This is also inappropriate.  
(iii) [Seeing it as] a disadvanta ge of having created the causes of suffering in a former life 
(6,45) 
It is the fault of the childish that they are hurt, 
For although they do not wish to suffer, 
They are greatly attached to its causes. 
So why should they be angry with others? 
 
(6,46) 
Just like the guardia ns of the hell worlds 
And the forest of razor-sharp leaves, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
50  
So is this (suffering) produced by my actions; 
With whom therefore should I be angry? 
 
“The childish,” refers to us ordinary beings.  Al l the suffering and unpleasant experiences of this 
life are the results of our actions in previous live s. Attached to negative actions such as killing, 
stealing, etc., we have accumulated negative ka rma, which is now ripening in the form of 
suffering. There is no reason, therefore, to blame others for our present suffering. 
 An example is given in verse 46. The experiences of  rebirth in the hell realms – the fearsome hell 
guardians wielding many weapons or being cut by the razor-sharp leaves in the forest of trees – 
are created by and the result of our own negative karma.  (In the previous module, we questioned 
whether the hell guardians were actual beings or only one’s own karmic a ppearances. We need to 
think about this).   Since all our unpleasant experiences and sufferings are the result of our own negative actions, we 
should restrain ourselves from engaging  in these actions again in the future. 
(iv) As it is one’s own karma that presses others into bringing suffering upon us, it is 
unreasonable to be furious with them  
(6,47) 
Having been instigated by my own actions, 
Those who cause me harm come into being. 
If by these (actions) they  should fall into hell, 
Surely isn't it I who am destroying them? 
 
We harmed someone in our previous life. With th at as a cause and the ripening of the karma in 
this life as a condition, our victim is returnin g the harm now.  Our negative karma is the main 
cause that is instigating our enemies to hurt us  now. In that sense, we are responsible for 
‘encouraging’ our enemies to harm us. 
 Since that is so, we also become responsible fo r causing our enemies to create the cause for a 
rebirth in hell through the negative karma they ge nerate by harming us. Reflecting in this way, 
the situation changes dramatically and we can s ee how we are actually harming our enemies.  
 These are excellent tools to help us deal with ou r anger. Combined with our faith of conviction 
in the law of cause and effect (karma) and our co nstant reflection and contemplation, we will be 
able to reach a stage where we will find it hard  to justify our anger in any circumstances and we 
will not become angry anymore.   
When we are angry, our anger is completely focu sed on the enemy. In order to stop our anger, 
we need to stop the mind that always blames the ot her party. The only way to do this is to reflect 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
51  
constantly on the various reasons given. 
(v) Fury itself is erroneous and thus unreasonable  
(6, 48) 
In depending upon them, I purify many transgressions 
By patiently accepting the harms that they cause. 
But in depending upon me, they will fall 
Into hellish pain for a very long time. 
 
(6, 49) 
So since I am causing harm to them 
And they are benefiting me, 
Why, unruly mind, do you become angry 
In such a mistaken manner? 
 
When we engage in the practice of patience, our enemies become the causes for us to purify our 
negativities. By being patient with the enemy and by depending on him, we also accumulate vast 
amounts of merit. When we think like this , the enemy becomes someone who is extremely 
beneficial and helpful instead of someone harmfu l. By depending on the enemy, we can engage 
in the practice of patience, purify our negativiti es and also accumulate merit in the process. On 
the other hand, we become the condition for our enem y to engage in negative actions, as a result 
of which he has to experience the suffering of   rebirth in the hell realms.   
We need to understand why the outline states that, “ Anger itself is erroneous and thus unreasonable.” 
Thinking that the enemy is harmful and not benefi ting us is erroneous. Our enemies provide the 
conditions for us to purify our negativities and accumulate virtue, while we actually provide the 
conditions for our enemies to engage in negativiti es. Therefore, it is inappropriate to be angry 
with the enemy. Instead we should be pleased with him. Patience is cultivated by thinking in this 
way. 
 
c” Cultivating the patience that pays no heed to those who cause harm 
(1) Engaging the mind in the method of compassion 
(2) Arresting the causes of fury 
(3) Reflecting on unwanted occurrences being due to one’s own faults 
(a) The meaning of the actual 
(b) Abandoning objections  
(i) Rejecting [the position that holds] that it is invalid for others to do 
something that benefits us 
(ii) Rejecting [the position that holds] th at it is invalid fo r us to bring harm 
upon others 
(iii) Arresting ingrat itude towards those who act beneficially 
(i) Rejecting [the position that holds ] that it is invalid for others to do something that benefits us 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
52  
(6, 50) 
If my mind has the nobl e quality (of patience), 
I shall not go to hell. 
 
Some people may disagree with verses 48-49, that the enemy is actually benefiting rather than 
harming us. They argue that since we provide th e conditions for the en emy to harm us, as a 
result of which he goes to the lower realms, we too will experience the suffering of going to the 
hell realms for harming our enemy in this way.  Therefore, the enemy is not benefiting us after 
all since he provides the condition for us to go to hell.  
 The refutation to this qualm appears in the fi rst two lines of verse 50. When we develop the 
noble quality of patience, which means we are able  to refrain from retaliating even when we are 
harmed, then we will not go to he ll. This means that the enemy does not necessarily provide the 
condition for us to go to hell.  This is how the enemy benefits, instead of harming us. 
Lesson 10   Chapter VI - verse 50-55 
I gave you some meditation homework a few week s ago. This is another piece of meditation 
homework for you to reflect on for the next couple of weeks. This is revision of what we have 
covered so far, a kind of self-stu dy done with the mind. If you like to study, then you have to do 
this homework. As students, you should follow the in structions of the teacher. Put aside 10 to 15 
minutes every day and reflect on the following points:  
 The points seem quite long. If you can, reflect on them all in the sequence I give you. If you do 
not have the time, then choose and meditate on the points you find most useful.   
 1. Visualise on the crown of your head your medi tational deity and root guru. Your meditational 
deity is inseparable in nature from your root guru.   2. Next, generate the motivation for doing this practice: “In or der to achieve the enlightened 
state of my guru deity, I must perfect my practice of patience.”  3. Visualise you are surrounded by all sentient be ings, including your enemies who are in front 
of you, and make a mental pledge and promise: “Even if all sentient beings rise up as my 
e n e m i e s ,  I  w i l l  n o t  b e  a n g r y  w i t h  t h e m . ”  M a k i n g  t h i s  k i n d  o f  m e n t a l  p l e d g e  i s  p a r t i c u l a r l y  
beneficial and helpful if you can sustain this  mental commitment for the duration of your 
meditation session. You can pledge to keep this  commitment for 10 minutes, a longer period of 
time or even for a whole day, like observing the 8 Mahayana precepts.   
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
53  
4. In dealing with anger, it is imperative to reflec t on the faults and disadvantages of anger.  It is 
mentioned in the text, “ There is no transgression like hatred .”  
 
Anger not only destroys merits accumulated in th e past, present and future ; but also, when anger 
is present in the mind, there is no room for virtuous states of mind to arise.  
 For instance, when we dislike and are angry with someone, we are unable to rejoice even when 
that person is doing a virtuous action. Due to th e presence of anger in our minds, we are unable 
to see the good qualities of that person. It also  becomes difficult to generate positive thoughts 
towards the people who are close to our object of  anger, such as their friends and relatives and 
we are not happy to see them.   It is also said that anger prevents the ge neration of higher spiritual qualities.   
 
The opposite of anger is the practice of pa tience. As mentioned in the text, there is “ no fortitude 
like patience. ” With patience, we are able to generate positive thoughts even towards our enemies 
and one has the mental space for positi ve thoughts to arise in one’s mind. 
 
5. Anger arises when we are harmed in some w ay, be it our bodies, possessions or reputation. 
Focusing on our enemy, we should check whether: it is the nature of that person to harm us; or whether that tendency to harm us is adventitious?   If it is the nature of that person to cause prob lems for us, then there is no reason to be angry 
with them. Such anger would be like begrudging fire for having the nature to burn. When you 
put your hand in the fire, your hand is burnt. It would be foolish to be angry at the fire.   
 Different people have different natures. If the ob ject of our anger is a person who is troublesome 
or who speaks harshly, understanding that that is  his nature, we will be less likely to become 
angry when he behaves badly. Even if anger arises, we  will be able to stop it quickly and not be so 
bothered. We can see this from our own experience.   We may know that person to be good-hearted bu t due to certain causes and conditions coming 
together, that person gets angry at us and harms us . This is an adventitious fault on the part of 
the person. It would be good to find out why that  person is behaving in such a way and not be 
angry or upset with him. 
 
6. Now focus on ourselves. We want happiness and do not want suffering yet we allow ourselves 
to get angry with other people. Our behaviour contradicts our wishes and is completely 
inappropriate.   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
54  
We are unable to bear being harmed in little ways  and become angry.  This is a negative action. 
We are only accumulating the propelling /throw ing karma for ourselves to be reborn in the 
lower realms. We destroy our own roots of virtue . We should realise how extremely foolish we 
are. Why? Because we are choosing to sacrifice small sufferings for the greater suffering of rebirth 
in the lower realms. When we endure small suffe rings (by not becoming angry), then we do not 
have to experience the greater suffering of rebirt h in the hells. So we need to look at ourselves 
and correct our foolish behaviour. 
 7. Next, focus on our bodies which are the bases for experiencing the pain of being harmed by 
weapons and so forth.   Our bodies are as fragile as a water bubble, unab le to bear even the prick of a thorn. When 
attacked, besides the attacker and the weapon h e  u s e s ,  o u r  b o d i e s  a l s o  c o n t r i b u t e  t o  t h e  
experience of pain. The very existence of this bo dy lends itself to suffering. If we did not have 
such a body in the first place, we would not be harmed when attacked. Therefore, both the 
external conditions and our bodies are equal in co ntributing to our experience of pain when we 
are attacked. Since that is so, there is no reason fo r us to be angry at the attacker or his weapon.  
 We tend to place the blame entirely on the a ttacker and never blame our own bodies. We need 
to stop thinking like this by understanding that the mere existenc e of our bodies plays a part in 
our experience of suffering and pain.  We have looked at some of the ways to weaken anger by focusing on: 
our enemies, ourselves, and our bodies. Many re asons are given in the text, but they can be 
condensed into these three points.  
 8. I n  a d d i t i o n ,  i f  y o u  c a n ,  r e f l e c t  o n  h o w  t h e  enemy has no independence or freedom but is 
controlled by other factors. He gets angry and en gages in harmful activities because he has no 
choice.    But this is not how the enemy appears to us. We s ee him as being very solid, existing inherently 
without depending on causes and conditions and we react by getting angry with him. We can 
stop this by reflecting on how he does not exist independently but is also subject to causes and conditions.   We should see the enemy as an apparition or a dream. As long as the causes and conditions 
come together for the production of an event, that event will happen.  
 
Question:  What should we do when anger arises wh en we choose our enemy as the object of 
focus?  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
55  
 
Answer:  Yes, that is possible. When you choose the enemy as the medi tation object, it is possible 
that anger will arise toward that person. But if you leave it unchecked, it will only become worse. 
The point here is to focus on the enemy, apply so me of the reasons we have discussed so far and 
see whether our anger towards that person decreases or not?  
 We also have to focus on ourselves and ask ou rselves what we want. Do we want happiness? 
Using the points discussed so far, there will be benefit when we analyse them correctly.  
c” Cultivating the patience that pays no heed to those who cause harm 
(1) Engaging the mind in the method of compassion 
(2) Arresting the causes of fury 
(3) Reflecting on unwanted occurrences being due to one’s own faults 
(a) The meaning of the actual 
(b) Abandoning objections 
(i) Rejecting [the position that holds] that it is invalid for others to do something 
that benefits us 
(ii) Rejecting [the position that holds] that it is invalid for us to bring harm upon 
others 
(iii) Arresting ingrat itude towards those who act beneficially 
(ii) Rejecting [the position that holds] that it  is invalid for us to bring harm upon others 
(6, 50 cd)  
Although I am protecting myself (in this way), 
How will it be so for them? 
 
Qualm : If our enemies are benefiting us, why will they go to hell?  
 
Reply:  Through our cultivation of patience, we beco me able to protect ourselves even when we 
are harmed. But our enemies have no such protec tion. This shows that we are not benefiting 
them.  
 This  s ho uld be un der s t o o d w it hin  t he co n t ext  o f pra ct is in g pat ien ce.  Here,  w e are able t o  s ee how others benefit us. This helps in our practice of patience.   
(iii) Arresting ingrat itude toward those who act beneficially 
(6, 51) 
Nevertheless, should I return the harm, 
It will not protect them either. 
By doing so my conduct will deteriorate, 
And hence this fortitude will be destroyed. 
 
Qualm:  When others harm me, it benefits me. Since that is the case, if I return the harm, I will 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
56  
be benefiting them as well. Therefore, it is  all right for me to harm them in return.  
 
Reply:  It is incorrect to return the harm because th is causes the anger in the minds of our enemies 
to increase, whereupon they will engage in negative  actions. We will not be able to protect them 
from going to the lower realms. 
 Also such retaliation harms our practi ce of the 4 virtuous dharmas:   
(a) even when scolded,  do not scold in return,  (b) even when someone gets angry at you, do not get angry in return,  
(c) even when hit, do not hit back, and  (d) even when one’s faults are exposed, do not expose others’ faults   
As mentioned in the beginni ng of this chapter, ‘ there is no fortitude like patience’ . By retaliating, 
this fortitude will be destroyed. Furthermore, we  will not be able to protect our enemy from 
going to the lower realms and our practice of the 4 virtuous dharmas will degenerate.  
 
A. The way to train in patience 
a. Explaining the chapter’s material 
1) Abandoning hindrances to the accomplishment of abiding by the antidote: devoting     oneself to the cultivation of patience 
2) Engaging the mind in the methods for accomplishing patience 
a) Arresting the causes of anger 
i) The nature of the cause along with its disadvantages 
ii) Instructions on making effort in the methods for overcoming anger 
iii) The actual method for overcoming that 
iv) Having examined in detail the causes that give rise to anger, making effort 
     to abandon them 
1’ A general presentation of the divisions [of anger in terms of] the     objects that give rise to anger 2’ Arresting fury towards that which brings unwanted things
 
a’ Arresting fury towards those who wrong you 
1” Being patient with those who would create suffering for you 
a” Cultivating the patience that accepts suffering 
b” Cultivating the patience that is intent on dharma  
c” Cultivating the patience that pays no heed to those who 
     cause harm 
2” Being patient with those who would belittle you and so forth 
b’ Arresting fury towards those who wrong those dear to oneself c’ Arresting fury towards those who do well by one’s enemies  
3’ Arresting fury towards that which hinders one’s desires 
b) The benefits of cultivating patience 
 
2” Being patient with those who would belittle you and so forth 
a” Despising and belittling one does not harm one’s body b”  It is unreasonable for one to be furious with a person who is himself in a rage c”  It is unreasonable to be furious because material gain has been obstructed 
d”  It is unreasonable to be furious because others lack faith in you 
a” Despising and belittling one does not harm one’s body
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
57  
(6, 52) 
Since my mind is not physical 
In no way can anyone destroy it, 
But through its being greatly attached to my body 
It is caused harm by  (physical) suffering. 
 
(6, 53) 
Since disrespect, harsh speech 
And unpleasant words 
Do not cause any harm to my body, 
Why, mind, do you become angry? 
 
When someone despises us, puts us down or beli ttles us, we have to check whether those actions 
are harming our bodies or minds. Since the mind is formless, such things cannot harm it in the 
same way the body can be hurt when attacked by  weapons. Harsh speech and the like also cannot 
harm the body. If this is the ca se, then obviously such things ca nnot harm the mind as well. If 
even the atomic bomb cannot destroy the mind , how can harsh words and criticism do so? 
 Due to our attachment to our bodies, we experien ce great suffering when they are harmed. But if 
there is no strong grasping to our bodies, we will  not suffer even when weapons harm them. It is 
said in the teachings that superior bodhisattvas are able to give away their bodies to all living 
beings. They do not experience any pain even when  they cut away their flesh, piece by piece. It 
only causes their bliss and happiness to increase due to their lack of self-cherishing and self-
grasping. By reflecting on these verses, we keep the mind in a peaceful state.
13  
b” It is unreasonable for one to be angry with a person who is himself in a rage  
(6, 54) 
-Because others will dislike me- 
But since it will not devour me, 
Either in this or in another life, 
Why do I not want this (dislike)? 
 Despite having established how unpleasant speec h and the like harm neither our bodies nor 
minds, nevertheless we are unhappy with our critic, thinking, “I do not like those things to be said because other people will not like me  when they hear what you have said.”  
 
                                             
 
13 Prevent your unhappiness, thinking, “Since the mind is not material, it cannot be directly harmed by others. 
While the mind is indirectly harmed by directly harm ing the body, the body canno t be harmed by contempt, 
offensive speech, and unpleasant words. These harm neithe r body nor mind, so I should be delighted.” When you 
prevent your unhappiness,  you do not give rise to hostility. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol.2, pg. 168)  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
58  
The response to this objection is: Their dislike will not affect you in this and future lives.14 
 
c” It is unreasonable to be angry because material gain has been obstructed 
(1) It is unreasonable to be furious at obstructions to material gain because they quickly 
perish 
(2) Arresting the pursuit of wrongdoing 
(3) Establishing, by means of an example, th e unreasonableness of craving material gains 
(4) The reasons craving material gains is unreasonable 
(5) Refuting the reasoning behind spending one’s time in material pursuits 
(1) It is unreasonable to be angry at obstructions to material gain because they quickly perish 
(6, 55) 
-Because it will hinder my worldly gain- 
Even if I do not want this, 
I shall have to leave my worldly gains behind 
And my wrongdoing alone will remain unmoved. 
 
Objection:  When others dislike me, they will lose faith in and respect for me. As a result, it will 
hinder my material gain, so I will be angr y at those who say nasty things about me. 
 
Reply:  Even if you do not meet with any obstacle s and are able to acquire great wealth, you 
cannot bring your wealth and possessions with you to the next life. You have to go alone.   
 
You also have negative karma that comes from ge nerating anger in the process of acquiring this 
wealth. When you committed those negativiti es, you planted imprints in your mental 
continuum, which you will have to carry to your  next life. These imprin ts will continue to 
produce undesirable results in the future. Given a choice between not getting angry and getting 
angry in the process of acquiring material ga in, it is better to choose the first option. 15 
Lesson 11   Chapter VI - verse 56-63 
2” Being patient with those who would belittle you and so forth 
a” Despising and belittling one does not harm one’s body 
b” It is unreasonable for one to be furious with a person who is himself in a rage c” It is unreasonable to be furious because material gain has been obstructed 
                                              
14 Objection : When someone has contempt for me, etc., other peop le will not like me, so this is why I am unhappy 
about it. Reply:  This would have some truth if others’ dislike were to harm you. However, since their dislike does 
nothing to you, give up your unhappiness about others’ contempt.( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 169) 
15 Objection: Indeed I am not harmed by their dislike, bu t in dependence on it I may be hindered in acquiring 
things from them, so I will get angry at those who have co ntempt for me, scorn me, or say unpleasant words to me. 
Reply: Even if you acquire things, you must leave them here , whereas the sin of your anger at them will follow you. 
Hence, of the two choices – dying quickly in destitution or li ving for a long time improperly – the former is better.  
(Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 169)

(1) It is unreasonable to be furious at obstructions to material gain because they
