(2) Arresting the pursuit of wrongdoing
  
(6, 56) 
Thus it is better that I die today 
Than live a long but wicked life; 
For even if people like me  should live a long time, 
There will always be the suffering of death. 
 
W e  h a v e  s e e n  h o w  i n a p p r o p r i a t e  i t  i s  t o  b e  angry with those who prevent us from acquiring 
wealth and possessions. Some may object to this, saying, “I wouldn’t be able to survive if I 
couldn’t acquire life’s necessities. I could even lo se my life and die. So, I am justified in being 
angry at those who stand in my way.”  
 
However, verse 56 says that, “ It is better that I die today/Than li ve a long but wicked life.” “Wicked 
life,” refers to sustaining oneself through wron g livelihoods, of which there are 5 kinds, 
mentioned in the Lamrim16 and The Precious Garland : 
                                              
16 The five wrong livelihoods are: flattery, hinting, giving order to receive, exerting pressure on others, and being 
on one' best behaviour. 
'Flattery' is as follows. You compliment your benefactor, for example, in the hope that you will get something in 
return. If they actually give you something, the act of flattery has become a wrong livelihood. However, if you 
compliment someone sincerely, and without the above motive, it is not a wrong livelihood, even if they 
give you something.  
‘Hinting’ is telling your benefactor things like, 'I have te a and butter moment, but not a great deal of grain,’ in the 
hope of receiving a gift. Or you might say, 'That barley fl our you gave me last year was a big help.' In other words, 
you are hinting that you want grain or barley flour. Ho wever, if you say these things in all honesty without any 
motive of receiving a gift, it is not hinting. 
‘Giving in order to receive' is giving some trifle in order to receive something better. For example, you might 
take a ceremonial scarf or a pot of tea to your benefactor, or anyone else for the matter, so that they will reward 
you or make an offering to your monastery on your behalf. In other words you have gone to the expense of the 
gift as a means of getting something better in return. We call this 'baiting the hook' nowadays. People these days 
are rather keen to commit this wrong livelihood. But if you make a present to your benefactor sincerely and 
without this motive, it is not a wrong livelihood — even if they reward you. 
‘Exerting pressure on others' means pest ering someone for something they do not want to give you, or getting someone 
to give you something they were going to give someone else. Or, you could say things like, 'My benefactor so-and-so 
gave me some really good tea and butt er.' But if you say these things out of sincerity, without any motive of 
receiving gifts as a result, it is not the wrong livelihood of 'exerting pressure.' 
‘Being on one's best behaviour' means doing whatever you like in the privacy of your own room, but acting like a 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
60  
Flattery – in the hope of getting something in return, one flatters the benefactor or sponsor. 
Hinting – wanting to get something but saying so  indirectly to another person, e.g. “I have 
enough to wear but I could do with a little bit more cash.” 
Giving in order to receive – e.g. giving a small gift to the benefactor in the hope of getting 
something big in return. 4) Exerting pressure on others – obtaining some thing through force by harassing the benefactor 
who may not have the wish to give. 5)  Being on one’s best behaviour – e.g. an ordained person acting in a holy way to impress the benefactor, hoping to get something from him.   If we engage in wrong livelihoods, even if we live  a long time, we will accumulate a lot of negative 
karma. Even if someone interferes with our lifespan , it is still inappropriat e to become angry with 
them; this is shown in the la st two lines of verse 56, “ For even if people like me should live a long time, 
/There will always be the suffering of death” . As mentioned in the teac hings of the great Kadampa 
masters, “It is better for evil beings to have short lives.” 
(3) Establishing, by means of an example, th e unreasonableness of craving material gains 
(6, 57) 
Suppose someone should awaken from a dream 
In which they experienced one hundred years of happiness, 
And suppose another should awaken from a dream 
In which they experienced ju st one moment of happiness; 
 
(6, 58) 
For both of these people who have awoken, 
That happiness will never return. 
Similarly, whether my life has been long or short, 
At the time of death it will be finished like this. 
 This is an analogy showing why it  is inappropriate to be attached to material gain. In their 
dreams, one person may experience a hundred years of happiness while another may enjoy 
himself for a very short period of time. When both of them wake up, they will only have 
memories of their dream experiences. They cannot relive those experiences again.  In the same 
w a y ,  a t  t h e  t i m e  o f  d e a t h ,  w h a t e v e r  p o s i t i v e  l i f e  e x p e r i e n c e s  w e  h a v e  b e c o m e  o n l y  m e m o r i e s .  Therefore, there is no difference whether one ha s a long or short life. Understanding this, we 
                                                                                                                                                          
 
good monk who observes all the rules in front of your benefactors in the hope they will give you something because 
of your disciplined behaviour. But my own precious lama, my refuge and protector, told me that if you do not 
have these motives, and instead feel, 'It wouldn't be right to upset this lay person,' and behave like a good monk -
in contrast to the way you behave at h o m e  - you are not 'being on your best behaviour.' ( Liberation in the Palm of Your 
Hand, pg. 142)

61  
should not be angry when someone obstruct s us from gaining wealth and fortune.  
(4) The reasons craving material gains is unreasonable  
(6, 59) 
Although I may live happily for a long time 
Through obtaining a great deal of material wealth, 
I shall go forth empty-handed and destitute 
Just like having been robbed by a thief. 
 
Even if we become very wealthy and have long live s enjoying our material comforts, at the time of 
death, we will have to leave everything behind. It is liked being robbed of all our possessions and 
going on to the next life, empty handed and nake d. Recalling this is another way of preventing 
ourselves from becoming angry when we are hindered from acquiring more money and 
possessions.  
(5) Refuting the reasoning behind spending one’s time in material pursuits 
(6, 60) 
-Surely material wealth will enable me to live, 
And then I shall be able to co nsume transgressions and do good- 
But if I am angry on account of it 
Will not my merit be consumed  and transgressions increase? 
 
(6, 61) 
And what use will be the life 
Of one who only commits wrongdoing, 
If, for the sake of material gain, 
One causes (the merits needed  for) life to degenerate? 
 W e  m a y  t h i n k ,  “ I f  I  h a v e  m o n e y ,  I  c a n  d o  s o  many wonderful things with it - I can go on 
pilgrimages, make offerings to the Sangha, commission holy object s, accumulate merit and purify 
my negative karma. Therefore, I am justified in being upset when someone prevents me from 
making money to do these things.”     The very first verse of this chap ter points out how a single moment  of anger can destroy the roots 
of virtue amassed over a thousand eons. Since that is so, generating anger in the course of amassing wealth would defeat the purpose of acquir ing such wealth in the first place, in addition 
to which, we still have to experience the ne gative results of our anger in future lives. 
 Five reasons have been given as to why we shou ld not be angry with those who hinder us from 
accumulating wealth. If we should find ourselves in these situations, we should reflect on these 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
62  
points to deflect our anger.   
 
In the Mahayana sutras, anger must be stoppe d at all costs and is never justified unlike 
attachment and desire, which may be used on th e path. There are no exceptions to this.  All 
possible justifications for generating anger are re futed in this chapter. As said in one text, 
“Compared to the arising of desire in our minds a hundred times, generating anger in our minds 
just once is much worse.”  
 
d” It is unreasonable to be furious because others lack faith in you 
(1) As speaking ill of oneself caus es one to lose faith in the speaker, it is reasonable to be 
furious with [the act of] speaking ill of others  
(2) If one can bear the lack of faith in others, it is reasonable to bear the lack of faith 
others may not have in oneself due to the afflictions   
(1) As speaking ill of oneself causes one to lose faith in the speaker, it is reasonable to be furious 
with [the act of] speaking ill of others  
(6, 62) 
-Surely I should be angry with those 
Who say unpleasant things that weaken  other beings’ (confidence in me)- 
In the same way, why am I not angry 
With people who say unplea sant things about others? 
 
We feel justified in our anger when someone critic ises us to others. We may think, “I should be 
angry with my critics. If they continue saying nasty things about me, they will generate negative 
karma and end up in the lower realms. Therefore I should display my anger and displeasure.”  
 
But when someone else is being criticised, we ar e not angry with the critic then. Our concern for 
the critic creating negative karma and going to th e lower realms should also be applicable when 
someone else is being criticised.  The way to  stop our anger is to reflect on this.   
(2) If one can bear the lack of faith in others, it is reasonable to bear the lack of faith others may 
not have in oneself due to the afflictions  
(6, 63) 
If I can patiently accept this lack of confidence 
Because it is related to someone else, 
Then why am I not patient with unpleasant words (about myself), 
Since they are related to the aris ing of disturbing conceptions? 
 When we see people criticising each other, due to their lack of faith in each other and their 
different perspectives and viewpoints, even when on e party is at fault, we think, “If I get angry 
and become involved, there will be no end to th is situation. These people just have different 
perceptions of each other.  It is better for me to keep quiet, be patient and not say anything.  
This is their problem.” In the same way, when th e enemy says nasty things to us, we should be 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
63  
patient and bear with it, remembering that he is acting under the control of his negative 
emotions.

     to abandon them

    objects that give rise to anger 
2’ Arresting fury towards that which brings unwanted things  
a’ Arresting fury towards those who wrong you 
1” Being patient with those who would create suffering for you 2” Being patient with those who would belittle you and so forth 
b’ Arresting fury towards those who wrong those dear to oneself 
1” Arresting it by means of the patience that is intent on

a” The reasons it is unreasonable to be furious with those

a” The reasons it is unreasonable to be furious with those who cause harm to the bodies [of 
those dear to oneself] etc.  
(6, 64) 
Should others talk badly of or even destroy 
Holy images, reliquaries and the sacred Dharma, 
It is improper for me to resent it, 
For the Buddhas can never be injured. 
 
Objection : It is fine when the harm is directed at  me, but my anger is justified when someone 
harms or criticises objects of my faith e.g.  statues of the Buddha or holy objects.  
Reply:  Even so, it is still inappropriate to be an gry with these people. The Buddha can never be 
d i r e c t l y  h a r m e d  b y  t h e s e  a c t i o n s .  H o l y  o b j e c t s  are simply matter. Instead, we should generate 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
64  
compassion for these people who are ignorant of the negative co nsequences of their actions or 
who proceed, even when they are aware of the consequences. In both instances, they are 
controlled by their three mental poisons and are not acting independently.  Such people are 
committing very heavy negative karma that will br ing about immediate retribution, i.e. rebirth in 
the hell realms, in the next life. When bodhisa ttvas see these people, they only feel strong 
compassion for them.  
b” Likewise, it is reasonable to be patient with those who cause harm to those dear to oneself 
(6, 65) 
I should prevent anger arising towards those 
Who injure my spiritual mast ers, relatives and friends. 
Instead I should see, as in  the manner shown before, 
That such things arise from conditions. 
 
It is inappropriate to be angry with those who ha rm our loved ones such as our teachers, parents, 
relatives and friends. They are experiencing harm  because they have created the cause of harming 
others in the past. We should stop our anger by reflecting in this way. In the case of our gurus, 
this reflection only applies to  those who are ordinary beings, not those who are already buddhas 
since a buddha is no longer subject to th e consequences of their actions.    
 
2” Arresting it by means of the patience that pays no heed to those who cause harm 
a” It is unreasonable to be furious only with  animate beings [lit. those who have minds] 
b” The reasons fury is unreasonable 
c” Reflecting on one’s own faults 
d” Reflecting on the benefits of patience 
a” It is unreasonable to be furious only with  animate beings [lit .those who have minds] 
(6, 66) 
Since embodied creatures are injured 
By both animate beings and inanimate objects, 
Why only bear malice to the animate? 
It follows that I should patiently accept all harm. 
 
When our loved ones are harmed by animate be ings (i.e. those with minds) and inanimate 
objects, such as the four elements (wind, wate r, fire, and earth), we are upset only with the 
former. We patiently accept the harm of the elem ents understanding that this is their nature.  
But the animate and inanimate objects are equal in  terms of harming our loved ones. There is no 
reason to be angry only with animate beings. Th erefore, when our loved ones are being harmed 
by animate beings, we should stop our anger from arising and cultivate patience.     
b” The reasons why anger is inappropriate  
(6, 67) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
65  
Should one person ignorantly do wrong, 
And another ignorantly become angry (with the wrongdoer), 
Who would be at fault? 
And who would be without fault? 
 
When we witness someone who, ou t of ignorance, commits negati ve actions such as stealing, 
abandoning the Buddha’s teachings, etc., we gene rate anger instead of compassion towards him. 
Who is blameless then?  Both parties are at faul t and are creating negative karma. Enemies who 
harm our loved ones probably think badly of us. We are no better if we respond to harm with anger.  Therefore, it is inappropriate to be  angry with those who harm our loved ones.  
c” Reflecting on one’s own faults 
(6, 68) 
Why did I previously commit those actions, 
Because of which, others now cause me harm? 
Since everything is related to my actions, 
Why should I bear malice towards these (enemies)? 
 
(6, 69) 
When I have seen this to be so, 
I should strive for what is meritorious 
(In order to) certainly bring about 
Loving thoughts between all. 
 Whether the harm is directed at us or our loved ones, it is the result of our actions committed in 
the past. There is, therefore, no justification in our bearing malice towards our enemies.  
Understanding this, we should strive to develop loving kindness and compassion towards all living beings and focus our minds on virtue.    
d” Reflecting on the benefits of patience 
(1) Making effort so that one’s virtue does not deteriorate 
(2) Accepting small sufferings will act to ov ercome the cause for the sufferings of the 
hells 
(3) It is reasonable to enjoy hardships that accomplish greater purposes 
(1) Making effort so that one’s virtue does not deteriorate  
(6, 70) 
For example, when a fire in one house 
Has moved into another house, 
It is right to get rid of straw and such things 
That will cause the fire to spread. 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
66  
(6, 71) 
Likewise, when the fire of hatred spreads 
To whatever my mind is attached to, 
I should immediately get rid of it 
For fear of my merit being burned. 
 
When a neighbouring house is on fire, there is th e danger of the fire spreading to our house and 
destroying all our valuables.  In order to stop  the fire from spreading, we would get all our 
flammable items, including our valuables, out of the house. We should no t be attached to them 
for the sake of saving our home. 
 
Likewise, when we see our loved ones, to whom we  have strong attachment, harmed, the fire of 
hatred blazes in our minds. We need to stop anger from arising towards those who are harming 
our loved ones. The way to do this is to temp orarily suspend our attachment towards our loved 
ones.   Just as the external fire that is spreading from house to house, damages our valuables, similarly, 
the fire of hatred burning in our minds, direct ed at those harming our loved ones, destroys our 
stores of merit. Just as we have to throw any fl ammable objects out of the house when the fire is 
coming, we should abandon our attachment towards our loved ones (which will ignite the fire of 
hatred in our minds) in order to prot ect our stores of virtue and merit. 
 
Question : But when we see someone hurting our loved ones, such as our parents, shouldn’t we 
protect them instead of just letting it happen?  
 
Answer:   We are discussing here all the different fa ults and disadvantages of anger. Any actions 
motivated by anger are bad. In whatever we do, we should make an aspirational prayer, “May all 
my actions not be influenced by anger, desire or attachment.”  
 
When our loved ones are harmed, it is our respon sibility to stop the harm. In fact, it is the 
responsibility of bodhisattvas to stop harm direct ed at other sentient beings. But the point is to 
do this without anger. In a situation where on e is extremely angry at the person harming our 
loved ones, at this time it is important to ab andon our attachment towards our loved ones and 
keep some distance from them.  When two parties are strongly atta ched to one another, a lot of 
unhappiness and anger can arise. In such a case , remaining together brings more harm than 
benefit and it is better to physically di stance oneself from the other party.  
 There is no contradiction in what we have discu ssed here. It is the practice of bodhisattvas to 
benefit others and to stop them from being harmed. We are aspiring to follow the bodhisattvas’ deeds. Bodhisattvas are able to see all sentient beings as their parents and loved ones. It goes 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
67  
without saying that we must take care of our parents of this life. 
 
In the case of our parents, when  they are old and become highly dependent, taking care of them 
c a n  b e    e x t r e m e l y  c h a l l e n g i n g .  B u t  w e  s h o u l d  d o  s o  w i t h  a  m i n d  o f  l o v i n g  k i n d n e s s  a n d  
compassion, with the patience that is able to be ar any hardships that might come along the way.  
This is the best approach.   
(2) Accepting small sufferings will act to overco me the cause for the su fferings of the hells 
(a) Example 
(b) Meaning 
(a) Example  
(6, 72) 
Why is a person condemned to death not fortunate 
If they are released afte r having a hand cut off? 
Why am I who am experiencing human misery not fortunate 
If by that I am spared from (the agonies of) hell? 
 
Objection : If I have to give up my attachment towards my loved ones, in order to stop the fire of 
hatred from spreading in my mind, I will experien ce unhappiness, suffering and dissatisfaction.    
Reply:  A man being led to the execution ground is re leased by merely having his hand cut off. 
Similarly, what is wrong with bearing the sma ll suffering of being separated from your loved 
ones, when that can replace the sufferin g of the hell realms in the next life? 17  
(b) Meaning 
(6, 73) 
If I am unable to endure 
Even the mere sufferings of the present, 
Then why do I not restrain myself from being angry, 
Which will be the source of hellish misery? 
 
If w e are u n able t o  bear t he s uffe rin gs  o f t his  life such as hunger, thir st, cold, and separation 
from our loved ones, how are we going to bear the unbearable sufferings in the lower realms? 
Therefore, it is reasonable to stop anger.   
                                             
 
17 Reflect well on the differences between short-term an d long-term suffering, thinking, “A man who is to be 
executed is overjoyed when he is freed from execution by having merely his finger cut off. How excellent it would 
be if similarly, by means of this slight suffering of human hardship, I could permanently dispel the suffering of 
limitless cyclic existence in general and in particular the su ffering of miserable rebirths such as the hells, etc.” If 
you do this well, you produce fearless courage with respect to hardship. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 175)  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
68  
In essence, these two verses are pointing out that it is inappropriate to be  angry, because when we 
are angry, we create the cause for even greater su ffering than what we are presently experiencing. 
Therefore, it is appropriate to voluntarily acce pt small sufferings for the sake of preventing 
greater suffering in the lower realms in the future.18 
Lesson 13   Chapter VI - verse 74-79 
It is important that we motivate ourselves to continue with our Dharma  studies and practice, 
especially when there is the opportunity to do  so. We should not wast e this chance. All the 
conditions are here to facilitate our Dharma st udies: we have liked-minded friends attending 
classes with us coupled with  our own amazing interest to study.  We should,  therefore, 
encourage ourselves to continue our studies and not to procrastinate. We are not yet old and 
useless. When we become old, even if we wanted to,  we might not be able to study and practise 
so well. Remembering this, we should focus our mind s, listen to the teachings and try our best to 
put them into practice.   
 Coming to class does not mean just being physic ally present and marking the attendance sheet. It 
is not about the body sitting in class. The mind has to be present as well. We are studying the 
practise of patience here. We should focus our minds on these teachings and check how they can 
be beneficial for us. It is our responsibility to ex tract from this chapter on patience material that 
will  really benefit our lives.  Even if we cannot  apply everything we learnt, at the very least, we 
should be able to think: “I reme mber that piece of advice from the text. That is really good.” 
When others ask us what we have learnt from this chapter, our replies should not, “I am not 
sure. I can’t remember anything. ” We should at le ast be able to say,  “This is something I know 
about patience.”   It is extremely important to put aside time for Dharma practice instead of spending our entire lives, being completely engrossed in our worldly ac tivities.   The Kadampa masters have said that 
we can never finish our worldly activities. The only  way to finish them is to put a stop to them. 
Therefore, no matter how busy we are, it is our responsibility to ensure we put aside some time 
for our Dharma practice.   
                                             
 
18 If you get angry at a harmdoer through an inability to be ar suffering, it is contradictory because, even as you are 
failing to bear slight suffering in the present, you are aggressively creating th e cause of measureless suffering in the 
miserable realms. Therefore induce a sense of embarrassmen t, thinking “I am very stupid,” and work to contain 
your anger. 
 The suffering generated by harm is the effect of prev ious bad karma; by experiencing it, you exhaust this 
karma. If you bear the suffering, you do not accumulate new sins and you greatly increase your merit. Therefore 
you must not consider how harmdoer ruin  their virtue, but view them as kind in that it is as though they are 
engaged in actions for the sake of clearing away your sins. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 164)  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
69  
We are studying the practice of patience in order to deal with the stubborn negative emotions in 
o u r  m i n d s ,  e s p e c i a l l y  h a t r e d .  I f  w e  h a v e  p r o b l e m s  w i t h  a n g e r ,  t h e n  w e  h a v e  t o  a p p l y   t h e  
antidote, patience. This should be our motivation for learning  the ways to  deal with our anger. 
We should familiarise ourselves with these methods through meditation.

1’ A general presentation of the divisions [of anger in terms of] the objects that 
give rise to anger 
2’ Arresting fury towards that which brings unwanted things

c’ Arresting fury towards those who do well by one’s enemies 
3’ Arresting fury towards that which hinders one’s desires 
(a) Feeling regret for how many bodies we have wasted in the past and still not accomplished 
either our own or others’ welfares 
(6, 74) 
For the sake of satisfying my desires, 
I have suffered numerous  burnings in hell; 
But by those actions, 1 neither fulfilled the purpose 
Of myself nor others. 
 
We should reflect on this verse when we find it hard to endure the problems and  harm caused 
by our enemies.   
While circling continuously  in cyclic existence in the past, we had engaged in all sorts of activities 
for the sake of fulfilling our desires. In the pr ocess, we accumulated the karma for rebirth in the 
hells countless times. We had been suffering in  the cold hells and the hot hells. Yet, despite 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
70  
suffering so much, we still have not fulfilled ou r own and others’ purposes. Reflecting like this, 
we would then voluntarily accept the harm directed at us by our enemies. 
(b) It is reasonable to enjoy accomplishing the welfare of all migrators by being patient with 
present hardships 
(6, 75) 
Yet now, since great meaning will accrue 
From harm which is not ev en (a fraction) of that, 
I should indeed be solely joyful 
Towards such suffering that dispels the harms of all. 
 
This verse means that we should voluntarily accept the suffering of harms directed at us and our 
loved ones. Such suffering is nothing compared to our past suffering in the hells. By voluntarily 
accepting this suffering now, we can achieve th e state of full enlightenment and accomplish the 
welfare of ourselves  and all other living beings . Reflecting like this, we see no reason for 
unhappiness when we are  experiencing harm. In fact, we should rejoice and be happy that we 
will be able to practise patience through this ex perience of   voluntarily accepting those harms. 
 
These, then, are two ways to deal with our inability to bear harm directed at ourselves or our  
loved ones:- Reflect on how, for the sake of accomplishing sm all purposes in the past, we had experienced the 
consequences of those actions by taking rebirths in hell for a very long time. In the end, we did not fulfil our own purposes or the purposes of  others. If we were to compare our present 
suffering to what we had experien ced in the past, it is insignif icant. Therefore,  we should 
voluntarily accept our enemies’  harm now. 
In addition, we should recall that,  by  voluntaril y accepting the present suffering, this  will act as 
a condition for us to achieve the state of full en lightenment whereby we can fulfil the purposes of 
all living beings. 
 
Bodhisattvas work only for the welfare and purposes of al l living beings. This is how the 
bodhisattvas practise when they  and their loved one are harmed. We should reflect in the same 
way and even if we cannot practise like that now,   we can make aspirational prayers to be able to 
do so in the future.    
c’ Arresting fury towards those who do well by one’s enemies 
1” Abandoning impatience with the praise and renown of one’s enemies 
a” As it is the cause of one’s own happiness [it is unreasonable to wish for such] 
b” As it is the cause of others’ happiness, it is unreasonable to not wish for it 
2” Abandoning impatience with the accomplishment of our enemies’ pleasure 
3” Abandoning impatience with the accomplishment of material gain 
 
“Arresting fury towards those who do well by one’s enemies,” means stopping anger when 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
71  
something good happens to our enemies.   
a” As it is the cause of one’s own happiness [it is unreasonable to wish for such] 
(6, 76) 
Should someone else find joyous happiness 
In praising (my enemy) as an excellent person, 
Why, mind, do you not praise them, too, 
And likewise make yourself happy? 
 
(6, 77) 
That joyous happiness of yours 
Would be a source of joy, not something prohibited, 
A precept given by the Excellent Ones 
And a supreme (means) for assembling others. 
 
We get angry when others praise our enemy. In th is situation, there are three parties involved:- 
ourselves our enemy or the  person we dislike  the person praising  our enemy.  The person praising our enemy does so happily. Since he is  not upset with our enemy or influenced by anger, he is able to see the good  qualities of our enemy. When we are angry with 
our enemy, we are unable to see any good qualitie s, perceiving the enemy only as unpleasant.   
 Why are we not following the example of that person who is praising our enemy and making 
himself happy at the same time? We should follow  his  example instead of allowing ourselves to 
be consumed by the fire of hatred or jealousy. We can also become happy through  praising the qualities of the enemy. When the enemy hears su ch praise, he also experiences happiness. In 
order to increase our enemy’s happiness, we  should praise his good  qualities.  
 
Our praising our enemy will also make the person  who was praising our enemy in the first place 
happy. This is virtue praised by the buddhas an d bodhisattvas that becomes the source of our 
own happiness. In the bodhisattvas’ practice of “T he Four Ways of Gathering Disciples,” one of 
them is the ability to speak pleasantly.  Being able  to see and praise the good qualities of others 
and abandoning our jealousy is the supreme method for doing so.  
 
b” As it is the cause of others’ happiness,  it is unreasonable  to not wish for it 
(1) If you do not want others to be happy, your own happiness will decline 
(2) Thus, it is reasonable to wish for others to be happy 
(1) If you do not want others to be happy, your own happiness will decline 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
72  
(6, 78) 
It is said that others are made happy through (bei ng praised) in 
this way. 
If in this way, you do not want (them to have) this happiness, 
Then, (since it makes them ha ppy), you should cease giving 
wages and the like (to your servants). 
However, you would be adversel y affected both in this and 
future lives. 
 
When our enemy is being praised, we might say,  “I have no problems with his good qualities but 
I am unhappy when I see my en emy happy at receiving such praise.” Our unhappiness comes 
from our jealousy.  
 
What is the condition for the enemy’ s happiness? It is the praises that had been  uttered. It is like 
saying that we do not like the praises that were uttered. In the same way, if we do not want our servan t to be happy, we should stop paying his wages 
since the wages are the condition for our servant’ s happiness. But by doing this, we will suffer in 
this life as our  servant will stop working for us. We will also suffer in the lower realms in future 
lives because we have created the negative karma of owing wages to our servant in this life.  
(2) Thus, it is reasonable to wish for others to be happy 
(6, 79) 
When people describe my own good qualities 
I want others to be happy to, 
But when they describe the good qualities of others 
I do not wish to be happy myself. 
 We like it when  our enemy praises our good qual ities. At the same time, we are also happy with 
the happiness he derives from praising us. But,  we are not happy with the happiness that our 
enemy experiences  when his own good qualities are praised by others. Likewise, we should be happy with this happiness felt by our enemy, when he is being praised.  
Lesson 14   Chapter VI - verse 79-86 
The difference between a Buddhist  who has studied and reflected on the teachings extensively 
(hereinafter referred to as ‘A’) and a Buddhist wh o has done neither (hereinafter referred to as 
‘B’) can be seen in the quality of their meditation. ‘A’ will be able to meditate in a very extensive 
way while ‘B’ will find difficulties in sustaining his meditation.   When reciting the refuge prayer, at the mere me ntion of “Sang gyä” (going for refuge in the 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
73  
Buddha) ‘A’ will be able to reflect in the followin g way: “Who is the Buddha? He is in the entity 
of the 4 holy bodies19 and is someone who has eliminated all faults and perfected all his good 
qualities.” Being familiar with such reflections, ‘A ’ is able to recall many different aspects of the 
refuge practice instantly and his faith in the Bu ddha  increases. When ‘B’ recites, “Sang gyä,” 
however, he may only recall the Buddha as the historical figure who achieved enlightenment 
under the bodhi tree over 2,500 years ago. It woul d be difficult for someone like ‘B’ to have any 
deep sense of faith. If this is so for a person like  ‘B,’ then obviously there would be even less faith 
in someone who have not even he ard of the historical Buddha. 
  
When ‘A’ recites “chö” ( ‘I go for refuge to th e Dharma’), he will immediately understand that 
there are conventional and ultimate Dharma, and the object of refuge here is not merely the 
physical scriptures themselves, but their  contents,  which clarify  the true path (the antidotes to 
our afflictions) and true cessation (what we achieve once all our afflictions are abandoned). Understanding that the Dharma is the actual refu ge and that, “This is what protects me,” will 
definitely increase ‘A’s’  faith. He will then ge nerate the faith of aspiration, which is the mind 
that aspires to actualise the Dharma.   When ‘B’ recites “chö,” he may only recall the phys ical texts and he is unlikely to have the same 
level of faith as ‘A.’  When ‘A’ recites, “Tsog kyi chog nam la” (I go for refuge to the Supreme Assembly), he 
understands immediately that the Sangha Jewel refe rs here to all the arya beings, including the 
hearer superior beings, the solitary realiser supe rior beings and the bodhisattva superior beings. 
He knows the inconceivable qualities of these holy beings, in particular, how the superior 
bodhisattvas practise at each level, actualisin g all the ten perfections and how they achieve the 
higher realisations on the path. Based on such  understanding and knowledge, irreversible faith 
will arise in ‘A’s’ mind towards these arya beings and he will aspire to emulate their example. ‘A’ 
will never give up his faith in the Three Jewe ls regardless of whatever conditions he may 
encounter.   When ‘B’ recites, “Tsog kyi chog nam la,” he will only remember that the Sangha refers to the 
ordained community and he is reminded to be respectful towards them.  With such limited 
understanding, it is very difficult to have real fa ith in the Sangha Jewel because it is difficult to 
see all ordained persons as pure and perfect and one may end up criticising them or questioning 
the benefit of ordination.   The difference between ‘A’ and ‘B’ is very clear fr om simply examining the quality of their refuge 
practice. The Buddhist who has studied will have stable and irreversible faith while the Buddhist 
                                             
 
19 The four holy bodies of the Budd ha are the Truth Body (made up of the Wisdom Truth Body & the Nature 
Body) and the Form Body (made up of th e Enjoyment Body & Emanation Body).  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
74  
who does not study will give up his faith easi ly when faced with the smallest obstacle.  
 
We should put into practice, familiarise oursel ves and reflect upon whatever we have learnt. 
Even if we engage in extensive listening  and re flection, it would not be beneficial without such 
mental familiarity.  
 Using the earlier example of refuge, we can a pply it to other topics and see the difference 
between engaging in practice with and with littl e or no understanding.   When we realise the 
benefits of studying and reflection, the aspiration  to do so will arise naturally from our own side. 
When this happens, no matter what difficulties we  encounter, we will try our best to come to  
class and listen to the teachings.    Lama Tsongkhapa said, “On the outset, seek ex tensive teachings. In the middle, reflect on 
whatever you have learnt. In the end, day and ni ght, put into practice the understanding of the 
teachings that you have ascertained. This is wh at I have done. You who want to follow after me 
should do likewise.”   He also said that, at the end, “You should alw ays dedicate your merit towards the preservation 
and flourishing of the Buddha’s teachings.” Even th ough this dedication verse seems short, it is 
very meaningful. Why do we need to dedicate in this way?  Understanding the perfect qualities of 
the Buddha’s teachings, if such teachings were to  flourish in the world, they will benefit many 
beings.    
c’ Arresting fury towards those who do well by one’s enemies 
1” Abandoning impatience with the praise and renown of one’s enemies 
a" As it is the cause of one’s own happiness, [it is unreasonable to wish for such 
b” As it is the cause of others’ happiness, it is unreasonable to not wish for it 
(1) If you do not want others to be happy, your own happiness will decline 
(2) Thus, it is reasonable to wish for others to be happy 
2” Abandoning impatience with the accomplishment of our enemies’ pleasure  
3” Abandoning impatience with the accomplishment of material gain 
(2) Thus, it is reasonable to wish for others to be happy 
(6, 79) 
When people describe my own good qualities 
I want others to be happy to, 
But when they describe the good qualities of others 
I do not wish to be happy myself. 
 
We are happy when people praise our good qual ities and we accept that our admirers feel 
happiness in praising us. Yet when others  praise our enemy, due to our jealousy,  we cannot bear 
our enemy experiencing such happiness.  Applying  this same reasoning, we should then reject 
the praise of our admirers, saying, “Forget it, I don’t want to be happy.” 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
75  
 
Geshela : “When your enemy sincerely praises you, you feel happy. You like that happiness. In the 
same way, when you praise your enemy, your enem y will experience the same kind of happiness. 
We should accept our enemy’s  happiness.” 
 In other words, we do not want our enemy to be happy at all, yet we do not mind the enemy’s happiness when he is praising us.  This is a contradiction. 
2” Abandoning impatience with the accomplishment of our enemies’ pleasure  
(6, 80) 
Having generated the Awakening Mind 
Through wishing all beings to be happy, 
Why should I become angry 
If they find some happiness themselves? 
 When we took the bodhisattva vows, we had pled ged to work for the benefit of all sentient 
beings and to place them in the state of buddhahood . So why should we be upset when they find  
happiness on their own,  without our help?  If we profess to work for the benefit of all sent ient beings, we should rejoice when they find 
happiness. Instead, due to our jealousy, we  are unhappy when they find happiness.  
 
3” Abandoning impatience with the accomplishment of material gain 
a” Reflecting on how it accomplishes one’s own desires 
(1) It is reasonable for oneself to enjoy sentient beings’ accomplishment of 
material gains 
(2) Example 
(3) If one does not want th at, bodhicitta will deteriorate 
b” Reflecting on how there is nothing to not want [in that] 
(1) It is reasonable for oneself to enjoy sentient beings’ accomplishment of material gains 
(6,  81) 
If I wish for all sentient beings to become 
Buddhas worshipped throughout the three realms, 
Then why am I tormented 
When I see them receiving merely mundane respect? 
 
When we develop bodhicitta, we generate the wi sh to bring all sentient beings to buddhahood. 
As buddhas, they would be  worshipped by all sent ient beings of the 3 realms. Yet we cannot bear 
to see our enemies receiving some material gain, re spect or service from others, which is partially 
fulfilling the goals of our altruistic intention. This is another contradiction. Our unhappiness, 
therefore, is once again not justified.20 
                                              
20 Contemplate as follows, “After I have  engaged the spirit of enlightenment fo r the sake of accomplishing all living

(6, 82)

This verse is self-explanatory. 21 
(3) If one does not want th at, bodhicitta will deteriorate 
(6, 83) 
If I do not wish for beings to have even this, 
How can I wish for them to awaken? 
And where is there an Awakening Mind 
In one who becomes angry when others receive things? 
 
How would can it be possible for us to bring all sentient beings to full enlightenment when  we 
cannot even bear our enemies enjoying simple mu ndane respect or material gain? We can never 
generate bodhicitta with such jealousy in our mi nds. Even if we had developed bodhicitta in our 
mental continua, our bodhicitta will deteriorat e if we continue to entertain such thoughts. 
Therefore, we should strive to abandon our jealousy.    
 
Question:  When we are practising patience, are we advi sed to abandon our jealousy as well as our 
anger? 
 
Answer : The practice of patience de als with anger as well as jealousy, because jealousy is a 
derivative of anger. Jealousy is defined in Asanga’s ‘Compendium of Knowledge’  :  “ D u e  t o  o n e ’ s  
strong attachment to material gains and respect, one’s mind is disturbed and upset and unable to 
bear others enjoying these things.” Since jealousy  is the derivative of anger,  the antidote of 
patience acts against both jealousy and anger.22 
                                                                                                                                                           
beings’ benefit and happiness, I get angry at harmdoers when they obtain happiness on their own. After I have said 
that I want all beings to become buddhas, I get unhappy when harmdoers get even minimal prosperity or honor. 
This is extremely contradictory.” You must eliminate your  jealousy regarding any sort of attainment by other 
persons and delight in it from the depths of your he art. Otherwise your spirit of enlightenment and the 
achievement of the welfare and happiness of beings are nothing but words. ( Lamrim Chenmo, Vol. 2, pg. 170)

b” Reflecting on how there is nothing to not want [in that] 
(1) It is unreasonable to feel jealousy for the material gain of others

(1) It is unreasonable to feel jealousy for the material gain of others 
(6, 84) 
What does it matter if (my enem y) is given something or not? 
Whether they obtain it 
Or whether it remains in the benefactor's house, 
In either case, I shall get nothing. 
 
When we are competing against our enemy for do nations and a benefactor makes a donation to 
him instead of us, we become angry with  both of them.   
 
But we receive nothing when the benefactor give s the money to someone else, and even if he 
does not give a donation, we still get nothing. Therefore, there is no reason to get angry and 
jealous when the benefactor makes a donation (or not) to someone other than ourselves since we 
will be receiving nothing anyway. 
(2) It is unreasonable to discard one’s own qualities  
(6, 85 ) 
So why, by becoming angry,  do I throw away my merits, 
The faith (others have in me ) and my good qualities? 
Tell me, why am I not angry (with myself) 
For not having the causes for gain? 
 
We need to create the merit to acquire the materi al gains enjoyed by others. Others also need to

When we get angry, we destroy our merits, which ar e the causes for receiving material gain. If we 
want to be angry, therefore, we should be angry with ourselves. 
(3) While it is right to feel sorrow for one’s own nega tivities, it is not right to be jealous of others’

attachment to gain and honor. It produces serious undesira ble (consequences) in both th is and future worlds (i.e., 
rebirths); in this life there will be mental unhappiness, and in future lives one will be impelled in to bad migrations.

Let alone not having any remorse 
About the wrongs that you committed, (O mind,) 
Why do you wish to co mpete with others

Sometimes, when we cannot get some thing, we also do not wish for others to be able to get that 
thing, thinking: “If I can’t get it, no one else will  either.”   
 
What we should be doing, however, is  generate regret for having accumulated the past negative 
karma that prevents us now from getting what we want, esp. the karma of stealing from or 
robbing others. To create the causes to get what we want, we need to accumulate a vast amount

have created the cause for such pr osperity in the past. Since this is so, how can we compete with 
those who have great merit and vi rtue? Therefore, it is inappropriate to be jealous of others’ 
virtue. Instead we should feel remorse  for our own negativities.

1” Disliking enemies is of no benefit to oneself

We are happy when something bad happens to ou r enemies and upset when they are saved from 
harm. This kind of wrong rejoicing is not beneficial for us in any way.  
2” Enemies are not harmed by your developing ill will 
(6, 87 cd) 
Your merely wishing (for them to be hurt) 
Did not cause them to be injured. 
 
Our hatred for our enemies can be so strong that we may even make perverse prayers for them to 
die soon. Whether our enemies suffer or not depe nds solely on whether they have created the 
causes for such suffering.  Therefore, we shou ld abandon our malicious thoughts and perverse 
prayers since they can neither harm our enemies nor benefit us in any way. 23

It is certain that I shall be cooked 
In cauldrons by the guardians of hell. 
 
We are happy when we see the suffering of our en emies since  this is our heart wish. What could 
be more despicable than that? We are only creating the causes for our own suffering and 
downfall in this and future lives. Our maliciou s thoughts cannot harm our enemies but, because 
of entertaining such thoughts, we will have to ex perience their fruitional effects, which can ripen 
even in this very life. If we have received th e bodhisattva vows, such malicious thoughts mean 
that we are abandoning sentient beings and our bodhicitta. This is a root downfall for which we 
will have to suffer in the lower realms in the next life.  
 The analogy in verse 89 describe s how fishes caught by the fish ermen’s hooks are pulled out from 
the sea and cooked in a pot. Similarly, the fish ermen are our afflictions and once we are hooked 
by the karma generated by our hatred we will defi nitely be cooked in the same way by the hell-
guardians.    The third and fourth lines of verse 88 and verse 89 are under the outline,
 If one holds onto that 
joy, it will become a cause of great suffering for oneself.  We will generate great suffering for 
ourselves if we take delight in our enemies’ suffering.25

25 Even your mere malicious thoughts that delight in your enemies’ troubles or that wish for their destruction do

(6, 91) 
If I were aware of what  held meaning for me, 
What value would I find in these things? 
 
Amongst the 8 worldly dharmas,  we desire praise and fame and we are upset when our 
reputation or our receiving praise is interfered with or hindered in any way.  The antidote to 
such unhappiness is given in these verses.  
 
Praise and fame, unlike virtue and merit, cannot be nefit us in our future lives.  A group of 15-20 
people may continuously praise us, like a grou p of monks doing puja together, but such praise 
and fame cannot extend our lifespan, strengthen our bodies or help us recover from illness.  They cannot even offer the  pleasure and satisfacti on that we experience from eating a mouthful 
of delicious food.  Praise and fame cannot help us reach  those mean ingful goals, which we hope to achieve.  We 
can stop our anger directed at those who hinder our acquisition of  praise and fame by reflecting in this way. 
26 
(2) It is unreasonable to seek merely mental joy  
(6, 91) 
If all I want is (a little) mental happiness, 
I should devote myself to gambling, drinking and so forth. 
 
Objection : Praise, fame, and honour may not bring any pl easure or benefit to my  body, but they 
do give me mental happiness. Therefore, I will be angry with those who ruin my praise and fame. 
 
Reply : If your goal in life is merely to seek shor t-lived mental happiness, you may as well indulge 
in gambling, drinking or sex, etc., the prohibit ed activities of those holding the individual 
liberation vows of ordained persons. It is ina ppropriate to pursue such temporary happiness as 
our ultimate  goal.   
 
(3) Grasping for merely that is erroneous 
(a) There is no need for just praise and renown 
(b) To dislike the loss of praise and renown is childish 
                                              
26 When others praise you and spread your fame, it serves neither of two purposes: 
-  for this life it does not bring you long life, health, and the like, and 
-  for futures lives it does not bring merit and so forth. 
Therefore, do not get attached to fame and praise, but reproach yourself by thinki ng, “My displeasure when my 
praise and fame are ruined is no different from when small children cry upon the collapse of their sand castles, 
which lack any of the requisites for a dwelling. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol.2, pg. 167)  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
82  
(a) There is no need for just praise and renown 
(6, 92) 
If, for the sake of fame, 
I give away my wealth or get myself killed, 
What can the mere words (of fame) do then? 
Once I have died, to whom  will they give pleasure? 
 
There are those who crave for fame as philanthropist s, even though their wealth is limited or as 
courageous warriors in battle. But, having attained  such fame for their generosity and courage, it 
brings them neither physical nor mental benefit at the time of death. Therefore, it is a mistake to 
pursue such goals.  
(b) To dislike the loss of praise and renown is childish 
(6, 93) 
When their sandcastles collapse, 
Children howl in despair; 
Likewise, when my praise and reputation decline, 
My mind becomes like a little child. 
 Children are very excited and happy when they have completed building their sand castles and they cry and experience great distress when such sa nd castles are destroyed, as if they had lost 
something extremely important. In the same way,  we are excited and happy when we are praised 
and we become upset and depressed when our pr aise and fame decline. This is childish 
behaviour.  
(4) The reasons it is erroneous 
(a) It is unreasonable to be attached to the joy one feels for praise of oneself 
(b) If, because of the joy [one associates with them, praise and renown] were something to strive for, then it would be reasonable for all beings [who have that] to enjoy them [but 
they don’t] 
(a) It is unreasonable to be attached to the joy one feels for praise of oneself 
(6, 94) 
Since short-lived sounds are inanimate, 
They cannot possibly think of praising me. 
But as it makes (the be stower of praise) happy, 
(My) reputation is a source of pleasure (for me)- 
 
(6, 95) 
Whether this praise is directed  at myself or someone else 
How shall I be benefited by the joy (of the one who bestows it)? 
Since that joy and happiness is theirs alone, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
83  
I shall not obtain even a part of it. 
 
When we are praised, what is it that is making us happy? Are we happy with the sound of the 
words that we hear? Or are we happy with the mind of the person who is praising us, our 
admirer?   If it is the former case, our happiness is inappr opriate since the words or  sounds themselves had 
no intention to praise us.   In the latter case, we are happy because the person  who is praising us is happy. Our happiness is 
still inappropriate because the happiness in the mind of our admirer belongs to him alone and 
cannot be shared or given to us.    
 
(b) If, because of the joy [one associates with them, praise and renown] were something to strive 
for, then it would be reasonable for all beings [who have that] to enjoy them [but they don’t] 
(i) The actual 
(ii) Dismissing the joy of praise for oneself as child’s play 
(i) The actual 
(6, 96) 
If I do find happiness in their happiness, 
Then surely I should feel the same way towards all? 
And if this were so, then why am I unhappy 
When others find pleasure in that which brings them joy? 
 
We are happy with praise and fame because our admi rer is happy. In that case, we should also be 
happy when our admirer praises our enemy, since it makes him happy to do so. But this is not 
the case because we are unhappy when our enemy is happy.  
 
Furthermore, our reasoning – the happiness of our admirer makes us happy as well – should be 
applied to all beings. When others (including ou r enemies) are happy being praised, we should 
be happy seeing their happiness. The happine ss of others becomes the cause of our own 
happiness. This is how bodhisattv as think. Therefore, we should  be happy when good things 
happen to our enemies.  
(ii) Dismissing the joy of praise for oneself as child’s play 
(6, 97) 
Therefore the happiness that arises 
From thinking, "I am bein g praised," is invalid. 
It is only the behaviour of a child. 
 
“Therefore”  refers to what have been mentioned earlier:  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
84  
(a) that the sound of pleasant words directed at  us have no intention to praise us; and   
(b) that the happiness in the mind of our ad mirer cannot be shared or given to us.  
 
Thinking otherwise has been established as invali d. Since praise and fame are made up of mere 
words, which have  no  essence, attachment to th em is akin to a child’s attachment to his sand 
castle.

3’ Arresting fury (anger) towards that which hinders one’s desires

a” It is unreasonable to be furious with that which hinders one’s own

b” Reflecting on the benefits 
(1) As that which acts to hinder praise and renown [also] arrests the

(a) Grasping after praise and renown(fame) leads to the 
development of all wrongdoing 
(b) That which acts to arrest that [also] arrests the lower realms 
(2) As it is something that acts to liberate one from conditioned existence (samsara), it is unreasonable to be furious with it 
2” It is unreasonable to be furious with that which hinders merit  
(a) Grasping after praise and renown (fame) leads to the development of all wrongdoing 
(6, 98) 
Praise and so forth distract me, 
And also undermine my disillusion (with cyclic existence) 
I start to envy those wh o have good qualities, 
And all the very best is destroyed. 
 
Verse 98 shows us how to stop our anger with those who damage our repu tation and fame. Such 
praise, fame and  respect are obstacles for Dharma  practitioners because they become the causes 
for us to generate pride, arrogance and attachme nt to material gain. We end up spending our 
time, effort and even our whole lives pursuing such worldly goals.  As a result, there will be no time for Dharma practice, attending cla sses or reflecting on the teachings.  
 The more distracted and attached we are to the ob jects of desire,  the more difficult it becomes 
for us to develop renunciation, the sense of disenc hantment with samsara and even if we were to 
generate some renunciation, it quickly disintegrated.   Without renunciation,  it is very difficult for us to appreciate or aspire to achieve qualities such 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
85  
as concentration, which will remain undeveloped.  We will not engage in Dharma practice with 
our minds due to the many mental distractions and even our attemp ts to engage in physical and 
verbal virtues will be feeble.   
 Moreover, since the praise and fame that we receiv e are the  results of whatever small qualities we 
may possess, such admiration from others become s the condition for us to be jealous with the 
good qualities of others, especially when they ha ve more qualities than we do. With such anger 
and jealousy, we destroy all the good things that we crave  - praise, fame, material comforts -  in this and all our future lives.   So the way to stop anger with those who preven t us from receiving praise and fame is to 
understand the faults of such things. Anger will  n o t  a r i s e  w h e n  w e  a p p r e c i a t e   h o w  t h e y  c a n  
become the source of wrongdoings and hinder th e attainment of our spiritual goals. Then, we 
may even feel happy when someone stops us from receiving such praise and admiration.
27 
(b) That which acts to arrest that [also] arrests the lower realms 
(6, 99) 
Therefore, are not those who are closely involved 
In destroying my praise and the like, 
Also involved in protecting me 
From falling into the unfortunate realms? 
 
‘Therefore, ’ refers to the previous verse on how prai se, fame, and honour actually  destroy us. 
Since this is the case, are those involved in dest roying our praise, fame and material gains, our 
pursuit of which will definitely send us to the lo wer realms, not protecting us from such a fate ?  
 Driven by our attachment to these worldly dharma s, we engage in non-virtuous actions and are 
too distracted to be interested in any virtuous activities.  Our critics and enemies are preventing 
our descent into the lower realms. We should, th erefore, reflect on the kindness of the enemy 
instead of fixating on him as an  enemy.   
(2) As it is something that acts to liberate one from conditioned existence, it is unreasonable 
inappropriate to be furious with it 
(a) As that which hinders praise and so forth acts to liberate one from conditioned 
existence (samsara), it is unreasonable to be furious with it 
(b) As it is something that cuts the avenues of suffering, it is unreasonable inappropriate 
to be furious with it 
                                              
27 Develop disgust for praise and so forth, thinking, “Praise, fame, and honor distract my mind with the 
meaningless, destroy my disenchantment with cyclic existence, make me jealous of those with good qualities, and 
spoil my virtuous activities. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 167)  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2

(a) As that which hinders praise and so forth acts to liberate one from conditioned existence, it is 
unreasonable to be furious with it 
(6, 100) 
I, who am striving for freedom, 
Do not need to be bound by material gain and honour. 
So why should I be angry 
With those who free me from this bondage? 
 
When we are striving for liberation and freedom from the bondage of our afflictions, material 
gain, praise, fame and honour will only hold us back. Those who are preventing us from 
acquiring these things are helping us achieve our goals. Therefore, we should not be angry with 
them. 
(b) As it is something that cuts the avenues of suffering, it is unreasonable to be furious with it  
(6, 101) 
Those who wish to cause me suffering 
Are like Buddhas bestowing waves of blessing. 
As they open the door for my no t going to an unfortunate realm, 
Why should I be angry with them? 
 Our attachment to praise, fame and  material gain s is a sign that we had opened the door to the 
lower realms. We enter that door, which we have opened ourselves, by engaging in seeking these 
worldly dharmas.   Those who obstruct us from acquiring these thin gs are, therefore, like the buddhas bestowing 
blessings on  us as they stop us from entering that door to the lower realms. It is inappropriate to be upset with them, because they are the ones   who close  the door to the lower realms.
