b” It is not a hindrance to merit 
c” Reflecting on it as an object one ought to have respect for 
(1) One should abide by the supreme austerity  
(6, 102) 
-But what if someone should obstruct my gaining merit?- 
With them, too, it is incorrect to be angry; 
For since there is no fortitude similar to patience, 
Surely I should put it into practice. 
 
Objection: It is, indeed, inappropriate to be an gry with someone who prevents me from seeking 
the 8 worldly dharmas. But my anger is justified when someone obstructs me from acquiring 
merit or engaging in the practice of generosity and ethics.   
 
Reply : It is still inappropriate for us to be angry with those who are obstructing our practice of 
virtue because there is,  “no fortitude like patience ,” which is the supreme virtue. 
(2) If that acts as a hindrance, you yourself will become an obstacle to merit  
(6, 103) 
If due to my own failings, 
I am not patient with this (enemy), 
Then it is only I who am preventing myself 
From practicing this cause for gaining merit. 
 
When  we are not patient with those who obstruct our acquisitio n of praise, fame and material 
gains, due to our jealousy and hatred, then we  are obstructing our acq uisition of the supreme 
form of merit,  the fortitude of patience  
b” It is not a hindrance to merit 
(1) A general presentation 
(2) Establishing that by an analogy 
(1) A general presentation 
(6, 104) 
If without it, something does not occur, 
And if with it, it does comes to be, 
Then since this (enemy) would be the cause of (patience) 
How can I say that (the  enemy) prevents it? 
 
An enemy is the indispensable cause for the deve lopment of patience. Patience cannot arise in 
the absence of an enemy.  How can we then say that enemies prevent us from generating patience? The enemy is not an obstruction to the generation of merit (which, in this context, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
88  
means patience). 
(2) Establishing that by an analogy 
(6, 105) 
A beggar is not an obstacle to generosity 
When I am giving something away, 
And I cannot say that those who give ordination 
Are an obstacle to becoming ordained. 
 
When we are seeking a recipient with whom we can practise generosity, the appearance of a 
beggar would aid, rather than hinder, our practice . In the same way, the presence of the abbot 
helps, rather than obstructs, th ose  seeking ordination. Similarly,  the enemy is the cause for our 
practice of patience.    
 
Question : Is the enemy not preventing me from getting the kind of merit I want by putting me in 
a situation whereby I have to practise patience? For example, the enemy hinders my practice of 
generosity by stealing the money I had set aside for offerings.  
 
Reply : What you want is actually merit. We ha ve already established that there are no 
circumstances under which anger can be justified.  Since the perfection of patience is more 
difficult to practise than the perfection of generosity, you can accumulate more merit from 
practising patience. 
 
c” Reflecting on it as an object one ought to have respect for 
(1) As it is something that acts to de velop qualities, one should respect it  
(a) Its benefits for oneself are great 
(i) The rarity of the special field  
(ii) It is reasonable to take joy in that  
(iii) It is reasonable to adopt the attitude that it is beneficial 
(b) It is not reliant upon having reflected upon the qualities (c) Seeing it to be like the Teacher 
(2) Due to faith in the Teacher, one should respect it 
(i) The rarity of the special field 
 
(6, 106) 
There are indeed many beggars in this world, 
But scarce are those who inflict harm; 
For if I have not injured others, 
Few beings will cause me harm. 
 
When comparing the two, we will find that there are more objects of generosity (such as beggars) 
than objects of patience (such as the enemy). The reason for this is shown in the last 2 lines, “ For 
if I have not injured others,/Fe w beings will cause me harm.”  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
89  
 
Bodhisattvas  work solely for the benefit of ot hers, seeing all beings as beneficial and never 
harming them.  Because of this, they are not ha rmed in return and, for them, the objects of 
patience are very rare.  
 
Geshela:   I guess it must be easier for bodhisattvas to  practise generosity than to practise patience 
due to the rarity of the objects of  patience. Therefore, the virtue acquired through the practice of 
patience is greater than the virtue acquired throug h the practice of generosity. This goes with the 
outline, The rarity of the special field,  where the field refers to the  object of patience. 
(ii) It is reasonable to take joy in that  
(6, 107) 
Therefore, just like treasu re appearing in my house 
Without any effort on my part to obtain it, 
I should be happy to have enemies 
For they assist me in my  conduct of Awakening. 
 
‘Therefore,’  refers to what we have established earlier: The rarity of the object of patience.  When 
an impoverished person, who lacks even the basi c necessities of life, suddenly and effortlessly 
finds a great treasure in his home, he will be very happy.  
 Similarly, when bodhisattvas find their objects of patience, they are as overjoyed as the poor man. 
So, when we meet with an enemy with whom we can practise patience, which is one of the six 
perfections, we should rejoice instea d of becoming angry with the enemy. 
(iii) It is reasonable to adopt the attitude that it is beneficial 
(6, 108) 
Because I am able to practice (patience) with them, 
They are worthy of being given 
The very first fruits of my patience, 
For in this way, they are the cause of it. 
 
There was once a famous, wealthy and well-respecte d spiritual master. One day, he was robbed of 
all his possessions. Because of this, he genera ted renunciation and retreated to a mountain 
hermitage, turning his back on all worldly activities. In his autobiography, he wrote, “When 
someone asked me who my teacher was, my answer was, ‘It is that very kind person who robbed 
me for he was the one  who sent me  up here into the mountains’.”’  
 
We should apply this perspective when we encoun ter problems. When we meet with difficulties, 
we tend to focus on the negative aspects, thinki ng, “Why me? Why am I sick? Why did he do this 
t o  m e ? ”  T h i s  o n l y  a g g r a v a t e s  o u r  s u f f e r i n g .  B y  s e e i n g  t h i n g s  d i f f e r e n t l y ,  w e  c a n  u s e  s u c h  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
90  
hardships  to complement, rather than see them as obstacles to  our practice.   
 
It may seem easier to practise when things are go ing well. But, at such times, nothing gets done 
because we are so absorbed in pursuing fame, honour and material possessions.  As Buddhist 
practitioners, when we meet with difficult situatio ns, that is the time to apply whatever we have 
learnt so that we can patiently accept the pr oblems and suffering. Otherwise, despite having 
studied and reflected on the teachings, in times of  crisis,  those of us who claim to be Dharma 
practitioners may end up worse off than ordina ry people. We need to reflect on how to 
transform adverse conditions into the path.  
 If we are determined to do so, we will be able to develop patience by depending on our enemies.  Since the enemy is the condition for us to develop patience, it is appropriate to dedicate to him 
the very first fruits of our practice (which come s from applying the various methods outlined in 
the text). Therefore, as stated in  the outline, it is appropriate to adopt a positive attitude and to 
benefit our  enemy. 
Lesson 17   Chapter VI - verse 109-114 
In the last lesson, we considered how the enem y, an essential condition for the practice of 
patience, occurs rarely. Therefore, like the bodh isattvas, we should rejoice when we meet the 
enemy and dedicate to him  the very first fruits of our practice of patience.   
 
2” It is unreasonable to be furious with that which hinders merit 
a” It is unreasonable  to be furious because merit has been hindered 
b” It is not a hindrance to merit 
c” Reflecting on it as an object one ought to have respect for 
(1) As it is something that acts to develop qualities, one should respect it 
(Reflecting on it as an object of respect) 
(a) Its benefits for oneself are great 
(b) It is not reliant upon having reflected upon the qualities 
(i) It does not cease to be an object worthy of offerings just 
because one does not have the attitude that it benefits oneself 
(ii) It does not cease to be an object worthy of offerings just 
because one has the attitude that it is harmful to oneself  
(iii) Thus, as it acts as a focal condi tion for patience, it  is worthy as 
[an object of] offerings 
(c) Seeing it to be like the Teacher 
(2) Due to faith in the Teacher, one should respect it 
(i) It does not cease to be an object worthy of offerings just because one does not have the 
attitude that it benefits oneself 
(6, 109) 
-But why should my enemy be venerated, 
who has no intention for me to practice patience?- 
Then why venerate the sacred Dharma? 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
91  
(It too has no intention) but is a fit cause for practice. 
 
Because the enemy causes us to develop  patience, we are advised to dedicate the very first fruits 
of our practice of patience to the enemy. 
Qualm : Why should I venerate the enemy who has no  intention of helping me to develop my 
patience? 
Reply:  It follows then that the holy  Dharma, which motivates us to  develop virtue,  is not a 
worthy object of worship, since it too has no intention to benefit us.  
The holy Dharma is made up of the ulti mate and conventional Dharma Jewels.  
The ultimate Dharma Jewel refers  to the true paths and true ce ssation while the conventional 
Dharma Jewel consists of the 12 branches of script ures.  The scriptures do not have any intention 
to benefit us because they are physical form an d not consciousness. True cessation also has no 
intention to benefit us since it is an uncompos ed and, therefore, permanent phenomenon that 
does not possess a mind. (Whatever is consciousn ess or intention is necessarily impermanent). 
 
A person can ‘possess’ an intention, but we have  to check if true path s possess any intention to 
benefit or not. We may argue that ‘possess the inte ntion to benefit’ is in true paths’ nature; but 
we need to be clear as to what this means.  
 
Question (from student) : Don’t the true paths in the mental continua of superior bodhisattvas 
possess the intention to benefit others?  
 
Reply:  Superior bodhisattvas have actualised true  paths. Bodhisattvas possess bodhicitta; since 
they possess bodhicitta, bodhisattvas possess the in tention to benefit others as well. Bodhicitta 
benefits others, but does bodhicitta po ssess the intention to benefit others?  
 
Upon further analysis, we can argue that since a true path is a main mind, it is necessarily 
accompanied by the 5 omnipresent mental factors,  of which intention is one. (The others are 
feeling, discernment, contact and attention).  In this context, the Dharma refe rs to the scriptures. Scriptures containing the Buddha’s teachings 
may not possess any intention to benefit us yet they  are worthy of veneration because they are the 
Dharma. Therefore, the intention to benefit is not a pre-requisite for an object to be worthy of 
worship.    
Geshe-la:  Whatever is a true cessation necessarily does not possess mind. Is there a buddha who 
does not possess compassion? Are the true cessations possessed by the Buddha a buddha? 
 (Students) reply : Yes, it is a buddha. 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
92  
Geshe-la : Do the true cessations possessed by the Buddh a have great compassion? It is helpful if 
you think about this in an extensive way. Then yo u can come to the firm conclusion that the true 
cessations possessed by the buddha superior ar e the final Dharma Jewel. According to the 
Prasangika-Madhyamika tenet school, this is the Nature Truth body. If we maintain that the true 
cessations possessed by the buddha superior are no t buddha, then we are also saying that the 
Nature Truth body is not the buddha, in wh ich case a buddha would only have 3 bodies. 29 
(ii) It does not cease to be an object worthy of of ferings just because one has the attitude that it 
is harmful to oneself  
(6, 110) 
-But surely my enemies are not to be venerated 
For they intend to  cause me harm- 
Then how could patience be practised  
If, like doctors, people al ways strove to do me good? 
 
Qualm : The Dharma may not have the intention to benefit me, but it also does not have the 
intention to harm me. Therefore, it is worthy of worship. My enemies, however, have only the 
intention to harm me.   
Reply:  How could we practise patience  if our enemies were always we ll-behaved and only strove to 
benefit us? As mentioned earlier, the existence o f   b e g g a r s  i s  a  n e c e s s a ry condition, not an 
obstruction, for our practice of generosity. In  the same way, the exis tence of the enemy who 
harms us is a necessary condition for our developm ent of patience. Therefore, we should rejoice 
in the existence of the enemy.  For those who do  not wish to practise patience, the enemy only 
causes the generation of irritation and anger.  
(iii) Thus, as it acts as a focal condition for pati ence, it is worthy as [an object of] offerings 
(6, 111) 
Thus, since patient acceptance is produced 
In dependence upon (one with) a very hateful mind, 
That person should be wo rthy of veneration just like the sacred Dharma, 
Because they are a cause of patience. 
 
‘Thus, ’ refers to the fact that we will never be ab le to develop patience with people who always 
treat us well. Since patience is developed in dependence on the enem y, he is worthy of 
veneration, even though he never had any  intention to benefit us, just as the Dharma is worthy 
of veneration because it causes  us to develop virtue. 
                                              
29 The four bodies of a buddha are th e emanation body, the complete enjo yment body, the wisdom truth body 
and the nature truth body 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
93  
 
(c) Seeing it to be like the Teacher 
(i) An extensive explanation 
(1’) The scriptures teach that sentient beings and Buddhas are comparable fields 
of merit 
(2’) Establishing that by reason  (3’) Abandoning objections 
(ii) Synopsis 
(1’) The scriptures teach that sentient beings and Buddhas are comparable fields of merit 
(6, 112) 
Therefore the Mighty One has said 
That the field of sentient beings  is (similar to) a Buddha-field30, 
 
The Buddha said in the Sutra of Pure (Perfect) Prayer31 that to attain the state of full enlightenment 
which perfectly fulfils the two purposes, we need to depend on the field of sentient beings and 
the buddha-field.  
 
The field of sentient beings is the cause for achi eving the state of full enlightenment. Since we 
need to depend on it in order to develop the four immeasurables and meditate on love and compassion, it is perfect for fulfilling the two pu rposes. The buddha-field is  also a necessary cause 
for us to achieve the state of full enlightenment. Since we need to go for refuge to the buddhas in 
order to achieve enlightenment, the buddha-field fulfils the purposes of ourselves and others. 
The buddhas and sentient beings are therefore comparable in terms of both of them being 
necessary causes for us to achieve enlightenment.  An alternative interpretation of what the Buddha sa id in this sutra is that the field of sentient 
beings is the Buddha-field. The Buddha-field is the ba sis upon which we achieve enlightenment. 
Since sentient beings are the causes for us to ac hieve enlightenment, the field of sentient beings 
itself is the Buddha-field.     
(2’) Establishing that by reason  
(a’) One attains the final object of desire by  having faith in Buddhas and sentient beings 
(b’) As having faith in both is comparable in terms of attaining Buddhahood, it is 
unreasonable to discard one and embrace the other 
(a’) One attains the final object of desire by  having faith in Buddhas and sentient beings 
(6, 112 cd)

‘The field of sentient beings is a Buddha field because it  is from that Buddha field that the qualities of a Buddha 
are attained. Hence, in that field, it is incorrect to practise in a mistaken way.’ 
 
31 The Dharma-samgiti-sutra  is translated in the Lamrim Chenmo  as the Compendium of the Teachings Sutra.

For many who have pleased them 
Have thereby reached perfection. 
 
“The final object of desire,” here refers to buddhahood. Many people have achieved 
enlightenment through generating love and compa ssion that pleased many sentient beings and 
through practicing Buddhadharma, which made the buddhas happy.  
(b’) As having faith in both is comparable in te rms of attaining Buddhahood, it is unreasonable to 
discard one and embrace the other 
(6,113) 
A Buddha's qualities are gained 
From the sentient beings an d the Conquerors alike, 
So why do I not respect them 
In the same way as I respect the Conquerors? 
 
When we are enlightened, we achieve many qualities such as the 10 powers and the 4 
fearlessnesses32. Such enlightenment comes from having faith in both the buddhas and sentient 
beings, so they  are similar (comparable) in terms of being causes for us to achieve enlightenment. 
It is, therefore, inappropriate to pay respect only  to the buddhas. We should also pay respect to 
all sentient beings33.  
                                              
32 The four fearlessnesses of a Buddha are:- 
1 fearlessness in revealing the Dharma of renunciation 
2 fearlessness in revealing the Dhar ma of overcoming obstructions 
3 fearlessness in revealing the Dhar ma of excellent abandonment and 
4 the fearlessness in revealing the Dharma of excellent realizations 
The ten powers of a tathagata:  
(1) knowing what is possible and what is not possible,  (2) knowing the results of actions,  (3) knowing the aspirations of men,  (4) knowing the elements, (5) knowing the higher and lower powers of men,  (6) knowing the path that leads everywhere, (7) knowing the origin of kleshas, which leads to meditation, liberation, samadhi, and equanimity,  
(8) knowing previous lives,  (9) the knowledge of tran sference and death,  
(10) knowing that the defilements are exhausted.

and final well-being through sowing the seeds of generosity and so forth in the field of living beings, you will

Question:  I thought there was no duality or diffe rence between the object and subject for 
buddhas. So how can we say that Buddha observes others and is pleased by them?  
 
Reply:  We can still talk about the obje ct and the subject in relation to the buddhas, e.g. that the 
buddhas possess certain qualities such as the 4 fearlessness and the 10 powers, or that the 
buddhas have hands, noses and eyes. The budd has also do not have  any afflictions.  
 The buddhas generate great compassion by focusi ng on sentient beings, who become the objects 
of their compassion. No conventionalities appe ar to the perspective of a non-buddha who is 
absorbed in meditative equipoise focusing on em ptiness. But for the buddhas, although they are 
always in meditative equipoise on emptiness, conventionalities do appear to them. They see 
everything. In the case of buddhas, only emptine ss appears to the perspective of the mind seeing 
emptiness.

2” It is unreasonable to be furious with that which hinders merit

(b) It is not reliant upon having reflected upon the qualities

from being killed, you go to a happy realm and have a long life there. In the same way, stealing or giving away your

(c’) It is reasonable to have faith because there is unlimited merit 
in making offerings to sentient beings who possess only a fraction of the qualities of a Buddha 
(ii) Synopsis 
(2) Due to faith in the Teacher, one should respect it 
(a’) Rejecting [the position that holds ] that it is invalid to cultivat e equal faith in objects that are 
not equal in terms of their qualities 
(6, 114) 
(Of course) they are not similar in  the quality of their intentions 
But only in the fruits (that they produce); 
So it is in this respect that  they have excellent qualities 
And are therefore (said to be) equal. 
 
We should respect sentient beings in th e same way that we respect the buddhas.  
 
Qualm : The buddhas and sentient beings may be equal in terms of being objects of my 
accumulation of merit but  they are not equal in te rms of their qualities. This being the case, I do 
not need to give sentient beings the sa me respect that I give to the buddhas.  
Reply:  By having faith in and respect towards th e buddhas, we create the cause to achieve 
enlightenment. In the same way, when we respec t sentient beings, we also create the cause to 
achieve enlightenment. Therefore, though the buddhas and sentient beings are not equal in 
terms of the qualities they possess, they are equal in terms of being causes for us to achieve 
enlightenment.       
(b’) The greatness of faith in both: It is reasonabl e to cultivate equal faith [on the basis that] they 
are comparable [in terms of their ability to act as] causes for Buddhahood  
(6, 115) 
Whatever (merit comes from) vene rating one with a loving mind 
Is due to the eminence of sentient beings. 
And in the same way, the meri t of having faith in Buddha 
Is due to the eminence of Buddha. 
 
(6, 116)   
Therefore, they are asserted to be equal 
In the share they have in e stablishing Buddha-qualities. 
 
If someone was to make offerings to a sentient being absorbed in meditative equipoise focusing 
on love, the merit generated is said to be so powerful that he would experience the positive 
fruitional karmic effects in this very lifetime.  This  can be attributed to the greatness of the object 
of the meditator’s concentration, i.e. all sentient beings.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
97  
When we make offerings as small as one flower or a stick of incense to a buddha, with strong 
faith and palms joined, the merit generated will continue to produce positive effects until we 
achieve enlightenment. This is due to the power of  the object, i.e. buddha.  This is the greatness 
of the buddhas.  
 Therefore, buddhas and sentient beings are eq ual in terms of being causes of achieving 
enlightenment.       
(c’) It is reasonable to have faith because there is unlimited merit in m aking offerings to sentient 
beings who possess only a fraction of the qualities of a Buddha 
(6, 116 cd)   
Yet none of them are eq ual (in good qualities) 
With the Buddhas who are boun dless Oceans of Excellence. 
 
(6, 117) 
Even if the three realms were offered, 
It would be insufficient in paying veneration 
To those few beings in whom a mere share of the good qualities. 
Of the Unique Assemblage  of Excellence appear. 
 Since buddhas possess infinite qualities such as  the 10 powers, the 4 fearlessness, the 18 
unshared characteristics, etc. beings cannot be comp ared with them in terms of qualities. Even if 
we were to offer the three realms to sentient be ings who possess only  a fraction of the Buddha’s 
qualities, this would be insufficient since they de serve far more than that. This shows that we can 
accumulate vast amounts of merit through making su ch offerings; this aligns with  the outline: 
because there is unlimited merit in making offerings to sentient beings who possess only a fraction of 
the qualities of a Buddha.  
(ii) Synopsis 
(6, 118) 
Thus since sentient beings have a share 
In giving rise to the supreme Buddha-qualities, 
Surely it is correct to venerate them 
As they are similar in merely this respect? 
 In summary, the buddhas and sentient beings may  not be equal in terms of qualities, but they 
are equal in being causes for us to achieve enlighte nment. Since this is so, it is appropriate for us 
to venerate sentient beings.    
(2) Due to faith in the Teacher, one should respect it 
(a) One should respect [the Teacher] because he acted as if all sentient beings were his 
only child 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
98  
(i) Accomplishing the principal method required of Buddhas 
(1’) [Showing respect and cherishing other beings is] identified as the 
principal method for repaying the kindness of the Buddhas in light of what 
they have done 
(2’) Establishing that  
(a’) One should be patient with harm done by sentient beings 
(b’) Abandoning conceit one feels in relation [to other] sentient 
beings 
(c’) Abandoning harm 
(1”) The reasons it is unreasonable to cause harm (2”) There is no way to please the Buddhas while one is 
harming others 
(ii) Confessing previous displeasing actions (iii) Refraining from [allowing nega tivities] to arise in the future 
(b) Thus, this shows in brief that we should respect sentient beings as well 
(1’) [Showing respect and cherishing other beings is] identified as the principal method for repaying the kindness of the Buddhas in light of what they have done
 
(6, 119) 
Furthermore, what way is there to repay (the Buddhas) 
Who grant immeasurable benefit 
And who befriend the world without pretension, 
Other than by pleasing sentient beings? 
 
‘Furthermore, ’ here means there are more reasons why it is important to venerate sentient beings 
in the same way we venerate the buddhas. The buddhas and bodhisattvas are honest, without 
deceit and pretension. They work only to benefit sentient beings, whom they treat as their loved 
ones, bringing them the happiness of this life an d all future lives. There is no better means to 
repay such kindness than by pleasing and benefiting sentient beings. 
 
(2’) Establishing that 
(a’) One should be patient with harm done by sentient beings 
(6, 120) 
Therefore, since benefiting these beings will repay 
Those who give their bodies and enter the deepest hell for their sake, 
I shall behave impeccably in all (that I do) 
Even if they cause me a great deal of harm. 
 
The buddhas happily sacrifice their bodies, which ar e formed from many virt ues, by entering the 
hell realms, in order to accomplish the welfare of sentient beings. To repay the great kindness of 
such buddhas, we should voluntarily accept whatever harms our enemies may inflict on our 
bodies or possessions.  
(b’) Abandoning conceit one feels in relation [to other] sentient beings  
(6, 121) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
99  
When for their sake, th ose who are my Lords 
Have no regard even for their own bodies, 
Then why am I the fool so full of self-importance? 
Why do I not act like a servant towards them? 
 
When working for others, the buddhas (“ my Lords”)  disregard their bodies and are prepared to 
endure any hardships in order to benefit sentient  beings. But we behave like fools, generating 
great pride and arrogance towards those very sent ient beings who are the objects of the buddhas’ 
actions. We should abandon such behaviour. Just  as we hold the buddhas in great esteem, the  
buddhas themselves see sentient beings as very important and hold them in great esteem. Since 
this is so, we should abandon our arrogance, co nceit and pride towards sentient beings and treat 
them in the same way a good servant serves her master.    
 
(c’) Abandoning harm 
(1”) The reasons it is unreasonable to cause harm 
 
(6, 122) 
Because of their happiness, the Conquerors are delighted. 
However, if they are harmed (the Conquerors) are displeased. 
Hence by pleasing them I sh all delight the Conquerors, 
And by harming them I sh all hurt the Conquerors. 
 
The best way of pleasing the buddhas is to bene fit sentient beings. Harming them, we displease 
the buddhas. Therefore, it is incorr ect to harm sentient beings.    
(2”) There is no way to please the Buddhas while one is harming others  
(6, 123) 
Just as desirable sense-objects wo uld give my mind no pleasure 
If my body was ablaze with fire, 
Likewise, when living creatures are in pain 
There is no way for the Compassionate Ones to be pleased. 
 
If our bodies were on fire, there is no way we could experience any mental happiness, even if we 
were given delicious food, wealth or possessions.  Such offerings would only add to our suffering. 
In the same way, when we continue to harm sent ient beings while making extensive offerings to 
the buddhas,  there is no way the buddhas will be pleased with our offerings and conduct.   
(ii) Confessing previous displeasing actions 
(6, 124) 
Therefore, as 1 have caused harm to living beings, 
Today I openly declare all my unwholesome acts 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
100  
That have brought displeasure  to the Compassionate Ones. 
Please bear with me, O Lords, for th is displeasure I have caused you. 
 
I n  t h e  p a s t ,  w e  h a r m e d  m a n y  l i v i n g  b e i n g s  a n d  t h u s  d i s p l e a s e d  t h e  b u d d h a s .  N o w ,  i n  t h e  
presence of the buddhas, we completely confess our past misdeeds and ask the buddhas for their 
forgiveness.  
(iii) Refraining from [allowing nega tivities] to arise in the future  
(6, 125) 
From now on, in order to delight the Tathagatas 
I shall serve the universe and definitely cease (to cause harm). 
Although many beings may kick  and stamp upon my head, 
Even at the risk of dying, may I delight the Protectors of the World (by not retaliating). 
 
After confessing our past negativities, we gene rate the strong determination to please  the 
buddhas and bodhisattvas by refraining from such  negative actions again in the future. We make 
a pledge, “I promise, henceforth, to refrain from such negativities and to abandon pride, conceit, 
and arrogance towards sentient beings. Even if, in the future, they stamp on my head or kick me 
with their feet, I will not retaliate. Through such re straint, may I be able to please the buddhas.”    
(b) Thus, this shows in brief that we should respect sentient beings as well 
(6, 126) 
There is no doubt that those wi th the nature of compassion 
Regard all these beings (the same) as themselves. 
Furthermore, those who see (this Bu ddha nature) as the nature of sentient beings also see the Buddhas 
themselves; 
Why then do I not respect (sentient beings)? 
 
When the buddhas and bodhisattvas were on the path, they trained thei r minds in the method 
of exchanging themselves for othe rs. Just as we cherish ourselves more than others, the buddhas 
and bodhisattvas cherish others with the same inte nsity.  Why then do we  not see those sentient 
beings as buddhas and pay respect to them? 
 Another way of interpreting this verse is that all sentient beings possess buddha-nature, which 
means they all  have the potential to become  buddhas. We should, therefore, respect sentient 
beings in the same way we respect the buddhas. There are many ways of interpreting the third 
line: ‘ Furthermore, those who see (this Buddha na ture) as the nature of sentient beings’  We can discuss 
the meaning of this  nature in the future. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2
