Lesson 19   Chapter VI - verse 127-134 
2) Engaging the mind in the methods for accomplishing patience 
a) Arresting the causes of anger 
b) The benefits of cultivating patience 
i) A brief presentation 
ii) Explaining the benefits by means of an analogy 
1’ The meaning of the analogy in terms of benefit 
a’ Analogy 
b’ Meaning 
2’ Explaining [that the reality of it] surpasses the analogy 
iii) A condensed presentation of the types of benefit 
1’ The principal effect 
2’ The visible effects 
3’ Explaining the fully ripened effect 
i) A brief presentation  
(6, 127) 
(Pleasing living beings) delights the Tathagatas 
And perfectly accomplishes my own purpose as well. 
In addition, it dispels the pain  and misery of the universe, 
Therefore I should always practise it. 
 
By practising the many different methods for ge nerating patience with our enemies offered in 
this text, we are able to respect all sentient be ings. Our constant practice of patience is the 
supreme method for pleasing the buddhas and comp leting the collection of merit in our mental 
continua. The patience of accept ing the harm inflicted by our en emies and responding only with 
affection and respect may bring them to th e Dharma, which is the supreme means for 
eliminating their suffering. Since pleasing an d respecting sentient beings are the supreme 
methods for delighting the buddhas, accompli shing our own purposes and eliminating the 
sufferings of other living beings, we should al ways strive to perfect such conduct.    
 
ii) Explaining the benefits by means of an analogy 
1’ The meaning of the analogy in terms of benefit 
a. Analogy 
b. Meaning 
a. Analogy  
(6, 128) 
For example, should some of the king's men 
Cause harm to many people, 
Farsighted ones would not return the harm 
Even if they were able (to do so). 
 
(6, 129 ) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
102  
For they see that (these men) are not alone 
But are supported by th e might of the king. 
 
When the king’s tax officials act on royal instru ctions, intelligent people, though unhappy with 
being taxed, do not harm such officials even if th ey can, because they know  these officials have 
the support and protection of the king.   
b. Meaning  
(6, 129 cd) 
Likewise, I should not underestimate 
Weak beings who cause me a little harm; 
 
(6, 130) 
For they are supported by  the guardians of hell 
And by all the Compassionate Ones. 
 
In the same way, sentient beings are supported  by the frightening hell guardians and all the 
buddhas and bodhisattvas. We are, therefore, advise d not to retaliate or harm sentient beings as 
this displeases the buddhas and bodhisattvas. Th en, their blessings do not enter our minds and 
we distance ourselves from them.  
2’ Explaining [that the reality of it] surpasses the analogy.   
(6, 130 cd)  
So, (behaving) like the su bjects of that fiery king, 
I should please all sentient beings. 
 
(6, 131) 
Even if such a king we re to become angry, 
Could he cause the pain of hell, 
Which is the fruit I would have to experience 
By displeasing sentient beings? 
 
(6,132) 
And even if such a ki ng were to be kind, 
He could not possibly grant me Buddhahood, 
Which is the fruit I would obtain 
By pleasing sentient beings. 
 The outline means that the meaning is more exalted than the analogy. Just as the king’s subjects 
have to bear with the edicts of the king, no ma tter how  absurd they may be, we are also advised 
to please the sentient beings who harm us. For, ev en if the subjects chose to rise up against the 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
103  
king, the most he could do was torture them, conf iscate their possessions and at the very worst, 
sentence them to death. Displeasing sentient bein gs, on the other hand,  results in our going to 
the hell realms - something that a king is incapable of doing to us.  
 If we were to please the king, at the very most , he would reward us with money and property. By 
pleasing sentient beings, however, we can attain the state of full enlightenment, which the king is 
unable to confer.   It is commonly acknowledged that pleasing or di spleasing our superiors may result in rewards of 
wealth and property or, at worst, a death senten ce. But pleasing or displeasing sentient beings 
leads us, respectively, to the attainment of the supreme bliss of full enlightenment or the 
suffering of  the hell realms. There is, therefor e, a significant difference between the analogy and 
its meaning.   
Question from Geshe-la : How can you achieve enlightenment by  pleasing sentient beings and by 
pleasing the buddhas? 
(Student’s answer not on recording.)  
 
iii) A condensed presentation of the types of benefit 
1’ The principal effect 
2’ The visible effects 
3’ Explaining the fully ripened effect 
1’ The principal effect 
(6, 133 ) 
Why do I not see 
That my future attainment of Buddhahood, 
 
Both the temporary benefit of happiness in this life and the  future attainment of buddhahood 
comes from pleasing sentient beings. Therefore, it  is appropriate to please and be patient with 
them.  
2’ The visible effects 
(6, 133 cd)  
As well as glory, renown and happiness in this very life, 
All come from pleasi ng sentient beings? 
 
When we practise patience by ac cepting the harm inflicted by ou r enemies and pleasing sentient 
beings, this results in our being well-liked, we enjoy great wealth and renown and bring others 
into our fold. In short, when we practise patien ce, we get along with people wherever we go and 
experience the happiness of this li fe. Therefore, we should always strive to make others happy.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VI - PATIENCE  
 
 
104  
3’ Explaining the fully ripened effect 
(6, 134) 
While in cyclic existence, patience causes 
Beauty, health and renown. 
Because of these, I shall live for a very long time 
And win the extensive pleasure of  the universal Chakra Kings. 
 
The practice of patience brings about a very pleasa nt appearance, complete fa culties, long life and 
good health, fame and renown, while we are in  cyclic existence. We can also enjoy the 
possessions and happiness of the wheel- turning kings.   In summary, the principal benefit of practising patience is our future attainment of 
enlightenment; but, even in this life, we will  always experience happiness wherever we are.  
b The chapter’s name 
The name of this chapter is, “ Teaching patience .” 
 
In essence, then, why is there a need to cult ivate patience? There are 3 types of patience: 
 
(1) The patience that pays no heed to those who cause harm : It is very difficult to engage in any virtue 
with very strong anger in our minds. Unable to  accomplish our own purposes, how are to work 
for the welfare of others? Therefore, there is a need to cultivate this patience. 
 
(2) The patience of voluntarily accepting suffering : When the problems we experience are so intense 
and cause so much mental suffering, it becomes impossible to engage in virtue. We neglect our 
Dharma practice. We need to develop this kind of  patience to cope with  such  circumstances. 
 
(3) The patience that is intent on dharma : This is the antidote for those whose interest in the 
Dharma and virtue is weak or th ose who give up their practice wh en they encounter the slightest 
difficulty, thinking, “I don’t want to study this text  because it’s so difficult,” or “I’m not going to 
follow karma because it’s so difficult.” 
 Whenever we experience problems or unhappiness and our minds are no longer able to focus on 
our Dharma practice, it is important to investigat e what is causing these situations and work on 
cultivating  the three types of patience.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
105  
The Seventh Chapter: Teaching Joyous Effort  
Lesson 20   Chapter VII - verse 1-2 
Whether we are prostrating or reciting “Om Mani Padme Hum,” it is our responsibility to make 
this beneficial for our minds by doing this happily  and willingly from our hearts. It is a mistake 
to think that studying or listening to the teac hings is purely to accumulate information and 
knowledge, leaving our hearts and minds untouched.  
 We have to do our own Dharma practice. We sh ould mind our own instead of other people’s 
business, focus on our practice and check our prog ress to see how far we have been able to apply 
what we have learnt. Dharma should be used to check up on ourselves, not others. It is not hard to find examples of good practitioners. When we look at the examples set by the holy beings, we 
should be inspired to strive and pray to be like them one day.  
 We have now reached the chapter on joyous effort  or joyous perseverance. As mentioned before, 
the first three chapters of this te xt relate to generating bodhici tta, the next three chapters show 
how to prevent the bodhicitta generated from degenerating and the following three chapters 
(joyous effort, concentration and wisdom) deal with  how to increase the bodhicitta that has not 
degenerated. 
3. Explaining the way to train in the remaining four perfections 
A. The way to train in patience 
B The way to train in joyous effort 
a Explaining the chapter’s material 
1) Imploring one in the need to undertake joyous effort 
a) The actual 
b) Identifying joyous effort 
2) How to undertake joyous effort 
b The chapter’s name  
C. The way to train in the absorptions included in the common and uncommon calm 
abidings D. The way to train in wisdom - the nature of insight 
a) The actual 
(7,1) 
Having patience, I should develop enthusiasm; 
For Awakening will dwell only in  those who exert themselves. 
Just as there is no movement without wind, 
So merit does not occur without enthusiasm. 
 
Having patience, I should develop enthusiasm  accords with the Ornament for the Mahayana Sutras by 
Maitreya which states that the latter perf ections depend on the former ones.  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
106  
The first perfection is the perfec tion of generosity, the antidote to attachment. As our generosity 
increases, our attachment will subside, making  it easier for us to practice ethics.  
 
There are 3 kinds of ethics, one of which is refraining from committing negative actions. 
Supported by such restraint, it is easier  for us to practice patience as well.  
 There are different kinds of patience: the patience of disregarding harm done to you the patience of voluntarily accepting suffering the patience that is intent on dharma Cultivating these kinds of patience will make it easier for us to develop joyous effort.  
 In order to fully develop our joyous effort, our practice of patience must be very stable. Such stability comes from the preceding pr actice of ethics. In order to ha ve strong ethics, we must have 
minimal or no attachment.  We overcome our attachment to our wealth and possessions by 
giving them away through our practice of generosity.   Those who want to achieve full enlightenment quickl y need to develop joyous effort on the basis 
of developing the patience of disregarding ha rm done to them and being able to endure 
difficulties. Enlightenment exists and can be achi eved. With joyous effort , enlightenment is not 
too far away and within our grasp.    The flame of the butter lamp will not flicker wh en there is no wind around. Without joyous 
effort, we will not accumulate the collections of merit and wisdom. Conversely, when there is 
wind, the flame will flicker. In the same w ay, with joyous effort, we can complete our 
accumulations of merit and wisdom and achieve enlightenment. This verse is, therefore, 
imploring us to develop joyous effort.  
b) Identifying joyous effort 
(7, 2) 
What is enthusiasm? It is find ing joy in what is wholesome. 
 
Joyous effort is the mind that delights in virtue. The effort we put into non-virtuous activities or 
activities that are neutral by nature is not joyous  effort. The virtuous activities engaged in by our 
bodies and speech are also not considered joyo us effort. We usually think that someone who 
does a lot of prayers, practices and prostrations  and works very hard at their commitments, as 
having a lot of joyous effort. But this is not joyous effort. It is like giving the name of the cause to 
the result.   In the Lamrim Chenmo, the division of joyous effort has three parts: 1. Armour-like joyous effort 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
107  
2. Joyous effort of gathering virtue  
3. Joyous effort of acting for the welfare of living beings  
The Ornament for the Mahayana Sutras classifi es four types of joyous effort while the 
Compendium of Knowledge refers to five types of joyous effort.  
 The armour-like joyous effort is ak in to soldiers putting on armour  for protection before going to 
war and refers to generating determination before we engage in any virtuous activity.   When engaged in virtuous activities, we then apply the joyous effort of gathering virtue, 
developing and maintaining delight, happiness in and the sense of enthusiasm for those 
activities.   Irreversible joyous effort is the mind that deli ghts in virtue and does not get disheartened or 
disturbed regardless of whatever conditions may aris e when engaged in such virtue.  This kind of 
joyous effort is essential for bringing the virtuous activity to completion.  
 Question from student:  What is the difference between joyous effort and the patience that is 
intent on dharma? Answer: They are different entities. Joyous effort is the mind that delights in virtue, while the 
patience that is intent on dharma remains un disturbed by any hardships that may arise.  
 For example, one is cultivating the patience that is intent on dharma. Reflecting on the law of 
cause and effect, one concludes that  that it is inappropriate to re taliate when one is harmed. The 
mind is thus undisturbed by whatever cond ition one faces, but that mind may not be 
enthusiastic about the situation.   
Then, there is the joyous effort of non-complacenc y, which is a mind that delights in virtue and 
is not satisfied with whatever has been achieved  but always aims for something higher, like the 
way a hungry yak eats grass. 
34  
 
In order to bring our practice to completion, we need to have these four kinds of joyous effort. 
 
2) How to undertake joyous effort 
a) Abandoning that which is incongruous with joyous effort 
i) Identifying that class of things that are incongruous [with joyous effort] 
ii) How to abandon those 
                                              
34 The supreme beings of the past said that one should prac tise like a hungry yak grazing. As a yak tugs at one 
clump of grass, its eye is already fixed on the next. In the same way, before you finish one Dharma practice, tell 
yourself that as soon as you have finished your present practice you will start this or that new practice. ( The Words 
of My Perfect Teacher  by Patrul Rinpoche, pg 246)  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
108  
b) Increasing the strength of joyous effort - the antidote 
i) Identifying that class of things that are incongruous [with joyous effort] 
(7, 2 cd) 
Its opposing factors are explained 
As laziness, attraction to what is harmful 
And despising oneself out of despondency. 
 
There are three forms of laziness: the laziness that relishes idleness, the laziness th at clings to unwholesome activities , and the 
laziness that is disheartened about virtue.   The first is the laziness that delights in inacti vity, deriving pleasure in  doing nothing, making 
one’s body and mind unserviceable for any virt uous activities. The laziness that clings to 
unwholesome activities refers to our attraction to non-virtuous activities such as singing and 
dancing, etc. The laziness that is disheartened ab out virtue refers to our disinterest in virtue, 
discouraging oneself by thinking, “I can’t do this. It is too difficult and beyond me.”  
 These are the three opposing factors to joyous effo rt and we must overcome them in order to be 
able to apply joyous effort in ou r pursuit of full enlightenment.  
Lesson 21   Chapter VII - verse 2-10 
And please remain stable, without separa tion from my body, speech, and mind, 
 until I attain enlightenment 
 
This is an important prayer from the Lama Tsongkhapa guru yoga  practice that we have just recited. 
It is important for us to pray an d think that Lama Tsongkhapa is at our hearts all the time. It 
makes a definite difference to our sense of his taki ng care of us in all our future lives by being 
able to meet his teachings again. 
 
Meditating on the great Lama Tsongkhapa at our hearts and remaining there inseparably is also 
one of the best ways of doing the protection wheel  meditation to protect ourselves  from spirit 
harms and the different kinds of obstacles.  We benefit from visualising, with faith, Lama Ts ongkhapa abiding at our hearts as he embodies 
the protectors of the three lineages: Chenrezig,  Manjushri and Vajrapani. This visualisation  
helps to develop a good heart since Lama Tsongk hapa is inseparable from Chenrezig. We also 
develop our wisdom because Lama Tsongkhapa is  the manifestation of Manjushri and since he 
also embodies Vajrapani, it helps us to overcome our problems and obstacles. So if we do this 
practice with faith, we enjoy all these benefits . Furthermore, it will help us to meet Lama 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
109  
Tsongkhapa’s teachings again in all our future lives. 
 
There is also a great difference when we meditate  on guru devotion conjoined with entrusting 
ourselves to  Lama Tsongkhapa abiding at ou r hearts. This is because Lama Tsongkhapa, 
embodying the protectors of the three lineages, is  the definitive spiritual master. Relying on him 
as our protector, with strong faith and with th e determination to accomplish all his wishes and 
advice, he becomes our ultimate object  of refuge.   
 There are many different kinds of prayers we can do on top of the many commitments we may 
have; it will be very beneficial if  we can do this visualisation with this short practice of Lama Tsongkhapa Guru Yoga.  We always emphasise the importance of the motiva tion in whatever we do, such as coming to  
listen to the teachings. The motivation must be correct and pure. Otherwise, we will only 
encounter problems and sufferings in the future. For example, some people think, “I need to 
come to class and take exams because I need to gi ve face to Geshe-la. I need to support him to 
make him look good.” This is a completely  wrong motivation.  I don’t need any support. When 
we listen to the teachings or practise the Dhar ma, we are practising the Dharma for ourselves, 
aren’t we? From my side, when there are students, I’ll teach. When there are no students, I won’t 
teach. It makes no difference to me at all. But if you have this kind of wrong motivation, then 
you are the ones who  end up with mental unhappiness.  
 
Then there are those who rely on a teacher with the thought, “If I rely on this teacher, he will 
like me, be close to me and show me affection an d care.” When one relies on a teacher with that 
kind of motivation and the care and affection do not materialise, again that leads to the mind 
feeling unhappy and dissatisfied. Then one gives up and criticises the teacher. Such reliance on a 
teacher is completely wrong and mistaken. Why do  these things happen? Because, from the very 
start, the motivation was wrong.  
 The whole point of relying on a teacher is not to seek his attention but to learn how to travel on 
the path from now until one achieves full enligh tenment, to build the proper foundation from 
the very beginning in order to be able to achieve happiness for one’s future lives. We rely on a teacher to learn the Buddha’s teachi ngs from him and to be able to  generate the realisations of 
the path. That should be our motivation. If the teacher is able to give us what we need, he helps 
us accomplish those goals. Since we benefit so mu ch from our relationship with him, it is most 
unlikely that we will lose faith in him. If the te acher is not able to offer us the Buddha’s teachings 
and help us on the path, then he is not really a teacher, right? 
 In essence, what I am saying is that whatever we do,  make sure our  motivation is correct and pure. Otherwise, even though, externally, our ac tions seem correct and positive, we will meet 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
110  
with difficulties and problems at the end. 
 
2) How to undertake joyous effort 
a) Abandoning that which is incongruous with joyous effort 
i) Identifying that class of things that are incongruous [with joyous effort] 
ii) How to abandon those 
1’ Abandoning the laziness that relishes the taste of idleness  
a’ Examining the causes of laziness and abandoning them  
b’ How to abandon them 
2’ Abandoning the laziness that clings to unwholesome (ignoble) activities  
3’ Abandoning the laziness that is disheartened about virtue 
b) Increasing the strength of joyous effort- the antidote 
a’ Examining the causes of laziness and abandoning them  
(7, 3) 
Because of attachment to the pleasurable taste of idleness, 
Because of craving for sleep 
And because of having no disillusion wi th the misery of cyclic existence, 
Laziness grows very strong. 
 
In order to abandon laziness, we first have to iden tify its causes, one of wh ich is the, “attachment 
to the pleasurable taste of idleness.” This is th e attachment to the pleasure of doing nothing at 
all, of simply ‘spacing out.’ Because of the atta chment to this form of laziness, we spend days, 
weeks, our entire lives, doing nothing. There is no  j o y o u s  e f f o r t  s i n c e  w e  h a v e  n o  i n t e r e s t  i n   
engaging in virtue.   Then there is the craving for sleep, especially the attachment to comfortable beds or blankets, 
which obstructs the mind that wishes to engage  in virtue, rather than the sleep itself.  
 Attachment to these two causes of  laziness, treating them as if they are our main goals in life, 
gives rise to a lack of disenchantment with cy clic existence, which, in turn, becomes another 
cause of laziness. Such a lack of disenchantme nt with cyclic existence, together with the 
attachment to the pleasurable taste of idleness an d craving for sleep, act as the causes for laziness 
to grow.
35  
 
a’ Examining the causes of laziness and abandoning them  
b’ How to abandon them 
1" Abandoning laziness by reflecting on the disadvantages of it in this life 
a" Explaining by means of an analogy that death will soon cause [this body] to perish 
(1) It is clear to see that death causes [this body and life] to perish 
                                              
35 Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds  says: “Laziness arises from craving based upon indolence, enjoyment of pleasure, 
and sleep; and from a lack of disenchantment with the suffer ing of cyclic existence.” Some assert that the first two 
lines indicate the form that lazine ss takes rather than its causes. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 187)  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
111  
(2) Explaining that by means of an analogy 
b" As even you yourself are under the dominion of death, it is inappropriate to 
carry on as if you had time 
c" You will be tormented by suffering if you do not make effort in virtue 
2" Abandoning laziness by reflecting on the disadvantages of it in future lives  
(1) It is clear to see that death causes [this body and life] to perish 
(7, 4) 
Enmeshed in the snare of disturbing conceptions, 
I have entered the snare of birth. 
Why am I still not aware 
That I live in the mouth of the Lord of Death? 
 
This is the antidote for the first kind of laziness, the laziness that  is attached to the pleasurable 
taste of idleness. Fishermen trap fish by luring th em into the net. In the same way, our afflictions 
have lured us into the snare of cy clic existence, which is made up of the 3 realms. Once the fish 
are in the net, it is definite th at they will die. Similarly, on ce we enter the snare of cyclic 
existence, death is certain. We are definitely go ing to enter the mouth of the Lord of Death. Why 
are we not afraid? Understanding this, we should engage in virtue.  
 
These verses are explained in terms of analog ies in the outlines of other commentaries but 
Gyaltsab Je explains them in relation to our di rect daily experience. In this life, death will 
definitely come. Many people died in the past. Ma ny more are dying in the present. Seeing death 
directly in this way, why are we not afraid? 
 What is it that prevents us from engaging in vi rtue? It is the laziness that relishes the taste of 
idleness; the laziness that is attached to the plea sure that comes from doing nothing. The way to 
stop this particular form of laziness is to meditate on death and impermanence. 
(2) Explaining that by means of an analogy 
(7, 5) 
Do I not see 
That he is systematically slaughtering my species? 
Whoever remains soundly asleep 
(Surely behaves) like a buffalo with a butcher 
 We have seen many deaths, yet we remain attrac ted to the pleasurable taste of idleness and sleep 
without engaging in virtue. Such  behaviour is like ned to a buffalo that lives near a butcher. Even 
as they see other buffalos being slaughtered by the butcher, they still roam around grazing happily 
and enjoying sleeping. We consider ourselves supe rior to animals but on closer examination, we 
are no better than buffalos in this respect.   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
112  
In fact, we are even more foolish than buffalos.   They are blameless because they do not know 
the Dharma and have no capacity to critically  analyse issues, whereas we have both Dharma 
knowledge and critical ability yet we still behave like them.  
 S u c h  a d v i c e  s h o u l d  b e  u s e d  t o  m o v e  o u r  m i n d s  t o w a r d s  e n g a g i n g  i n  v i r t u e .  W h e t h e r  t h i s  happens or not depends on whether we reflect on these points.  If we are happy remaining more 
foolish than buffalos, we can carry on as before. But for those of us who accept past and future 
lives and wish to do well in our future rebirths, it is our responsibility to motivate ourselves to engage in virtue by reflecting on the advice given in this text. The choice is ours.   
b" As even you yourself are under the dominion of death, it is inappropriate to carry on as if you 
had time 
(1) As you yourself are under the dominion of the lord of death, you should not be lazy 
(2) As death will come swiftly, make effort in virtue 
(3) The time of death is no time to abandon laziness 
(4) As death will come suddenly without your having been able to do all that you want, 
you should not be lazy 
(1) As you yourself are under the dominion of the lord of death, you should not be lazy 
(7, 6) 
When having blocked off every (escape) route, 
The Lord of Death is look ing (for someone to kill), 
How can I enjoy eating? 
And likewise, how can I enjoy sleep? 
 Imagine that we are travelling along a road. All other routes are blocked and we have no 
alternative other than this road. Somewhere along this road, our enemy is waiting in ambush to 
kill us.  In the same way, death closes all other roads and we have no choice but to walk towards death. The Lord of Death can take us at any time. This being so, it is completely inappropriate to 
spend our lives  in distractions such as eating, sl eep, etc. This outline is referring to us now and 
our present situation.    As long as we take rebirth in any of the three realms, under the power of karma and afflictions, 
death is certain.  Accumulating negative karma leads us to a lower realm. When we accumulate 
virtue, we go to a happy realm or can even achi eve liberation and enlightenment. Therefore, we 
must engage in virtue before death overcomes us.    
(2) As death will come swiftly, make effort in virtue 
(7, 7)   
For as long as death is actually approaching 
Then I shall accumulate merits; 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
113  
Understanding that death is certain, we may still th ink, however, “I still have some time. I can do 
it later.” This is not true, because death can su ddenly come.  Before that happens, we have to 
ensure that we have engaged in some virtue. Ther e are many causes of death – sicknesses, spirit 
harms and natural disasters, even the  danger of  nuclear weapons. It is uncertain when these 
conditions will occur but it shows th at death can happen very quickly.   
(3) The time of death is no time to abandon laziness 
(7, 7 cd) 
Even if I then put a stop to laziness, 
What will be the use? That is not the time! 
 
When we are about to die, we will experience gr eat discomfort. Even if someone gives us food 
and drink, we will be unable to swallow it.  If the thought of engaging in virtue arises in us then, 
we won’t be able to do anything about it as it will be too late. So we must practise virtue before 
death overcomes us.  
(4) As death will come suddenly without your having been able to do all that you want, you 
should not be lazy 
(7, 8) 
When this has not been done , when this is being done 
And when this is only half finished, 
Suddenly the Lord of Death will come. 
And the thought will occur, "Oh no, I am done for!" 
 
We spend our whole life thinking, “I’m going to  start on this project.” When the project is 
underway death suddenly comes, not waiting for us to finish our project.  Death possesses great 
independence and power as it can come at any time. When the Lord of Death suddenly appears, 
we have so much regret and remorse, thinking, “I  couldn’t do this. I couldn’t finish that.”  
 As mentioned in the Lamrim, death is certain and the time of death is uncertain. It can come 
suddenly, without fanfare or warning. Because of this, we have to start on virtue right now. 
When we wake up in the morning, it is good to remind ourselves of this. By reflecting on death 
and impermanence, we persuade our minds to put aside some time for  engaging in virtue.  
c" You will be tormented by suffering if you do not make effort in virtue 
(1) At the time of death, you will be tormented with sorrow 
(2) If you do not pursue virtue from this mome nt forward, you will not accomplish what is 
necessary 
(1) At the time of death, you will be tormented with sorrow 
(7, 9) 
Their faces flowing with tears 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
114  
And their eyes red and swollen with sorrow, 
My relatives will finally lose hope, 
And I shall behold the vision of the messengers of death. 
 
At the time of death, we experience great sorrow.  Surrounded by our loved ones and relatives, we 
realise that we cannot bring them with us. Thei r eyes are red and swollen with tears as they 
realise we are dying. We may also experience the fearful karmic appearance of the Lord of Death 
or sometimes of our relatives and  friends re gardless of whether we like it or not.  
(2) If you do not pursue virtue from this moment forward, you will not accomplish what is 
necessary 
(7, 10) 
Tormented by the memory of my wrongdoing, 
And hearing the sounds of hell, 
In terror, I shall clothe my body in excrement. 
What virtue can I do in  such a delirious state? 
 
At the time of death, we may also be tormented by  the memories of our past  negative actions. If 
w e  a r e  g o i n g  t o  b e  r e b o r n  i n  t h e  h e l l s ,  w e  m a y  s t a r t  t o  h e a r  t h e  c r i e s  o f  t h e  h e l l  b e i n g s  undergoing their sufferings there. We will be so terrified that we will lose control of our minds 
and bodily functions. Hence, I shall clothe my body in excrement.  At this point, it will be too late to 
do anything at all. Therefore, while we still have  control over our lives and the choice to engage 
in virtue, we should start straightaway.  
Lesson 22   Chapter VII - verse 11-18 
The foundation of all good qualitie s is the kind and venerable guru; 
Correct devotion to him is the root of the path, 
By clearly seeing this and applying great effort, 
Please bless me to rely upon him with great respect. 
 
The first verse of The Foundation of All Good Qualities  composed by Lama Tsongkhapa stated that 
we must put effort into our practice of guru devotion, which is, the root of the path . This  shows 
that correct devotion to the guru  is the root of the entire path  to enlightenment. Recognising 
this, we  rely properly on the spiritual master, in both thought and action, and generate faith and 
respect towards him.  
 The Lamrim stated that we should first investig ate and check if the prospective spiritual master 
possesses the requisite qualities to lead us alon g the path to enlightenment, whether they can 
teach and help us develop the realisations of the path in our mental continua. It is important to 
do this. Having decided to pursue a Dharma connection with that gu ru, from that point 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
115  
onwards, it is our responsibility to correctly de vote ourselves to him in both thought and action. 
Incorrect devotion to the guru brings many proble ms in this life. Needless to say, it will also 
bring  problems and sufferings in future lives. If we practise guru devotion correctly, we will find 
success in whatever we do in this life. Our activiti es to benefit others will also increase. Kyabje 
Lama Zopa Rinpoche always points out that wh ether lamas and geshes are successful in their 
Dharma careers to benefit sentient beings and the teachings depends on how well they devote 
themselves to their gurus.  
 I will give my own example. I believe I may have  told you this story before but I am not sure. 
When I was studying in Sera, I had many teachers  there.  I was around 25 or 26 years old at the 
time. I received daily teachings from this one specific teacher, except on Tuesdays because 
Tuesday was our day off. One day, I had to pass a message and some offerings to my teacher.  
When I arrived at his rooms, he was occupied with  someone else so I could not go in.  I waited 
outside for almost an hour. My teacher was quit e upset with me because he was conveying some 
secret instructions to this other student. I did not hear anything since I was outside his door. I 
knew he was with someone and I thought that I sh ould wait till the other person came out before 
I went in to pass the message and the offerings to him. After an hour, he shouted loudly at me 
from inside, “Hey!” So, I opened the door and we nt inside. He asked me, “Why are you listening 
to all my secrets?” He was very upset with me an d proceeded to scold me for the next five to ten 
minutes. At that time, I was quite upset and an gry because I thought I had not done anything 
wrong, especially since I had stood outside for almo st an hour. I placed the offerings on his table 
and left. I went back to my room. I was quite upse t and thinking of what to do. What is the best 
thing for me to do now? Should I give up this teacher or go back and apologise? I had only two choices. Fortunately, after deliberating for over two hours, I decided that I did not want to give 
up my teacher. I thought, “This teacher has taug ht me for seven years so far and I have many 
more years to go before I can complete my stud ies.” So, after some time, I went to buy some 
biscuits and food. Still, I did not want to apologise because it was not my fault and I was not eavesdropping at all. I had no intention to harm  him and yet he still shouted at me. I could not 
accept that kind of shouting. So I could not say so rry and I did not say sorry in the end. I went to 
my teacher’s room and pretended that I was not angry. He did not say anything. I offered the 
fruit and asked some Dharma questions.   This only happened to me once . The problem was I thought that  I had not made any mistake, 
but just because I was right, in this situation, it would still have been inappropriate for me to give 
up my teacher. I am fortunate to be able to thin k like this. This is my personal experience. That 
was the only time in my life that I was angry wi th my teacher, thinking that I was right and he 
was wrong.   Having such thoughts can lead to us abandoning  our teachers. We need to remember how much 
they benefit us, how much they teach us. This happens in Singapore also. Sometimes, students 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
116  
forget the benefits they have re ceived from their teachers. Just like my example, due to a small 
misunderstanding, they get angry with th eir teachers, whom they then give up. 
 
In the end, who loses out? When I think about my situation, that teacher would have been fine 
because he had many students; he would have no t lost anything by not teaching me. For me, 
though, it would have meant a big difference. It  is possible for this to happen to anybody. 
Generally, when you rely on a teacher, you have to think carefully whethe r you want to give him 
up or not. If we do, in the end, we lose out.  As mentioned in the Lamrim, there would be no 
success not only in this life bu t also in our future lives.  
 I  h a v e  v e r y  s t r o n g  d e v o t i o n  t o  t h a t  p a r t i c u l a r  t e a c h e r .  W h e n e v e r  h e  t a u g h t  a  p h i l o s o p h y  t e x t ,  there would be some passages that I’d try to figure  out but I wouldn’t be able to get the answer. 
Once I listened to him giving the correct answe r, the feeling would be different. There’d be a 
strong feeling – of “How come he understood this  logic and I could not?”  Then there is faith. 
Every time I learnt something new from him, the st ronger my devotion to that guru became. This 
is just my personal experience.  Jealousy can also cause problems in student-teac her relationships. I did not have such problems. 
My teacher had many students. Many of us helped and served him. From my side, I was never jealous with regard to the other students who were serving him. There were times when my 
teacher liked certain students better and others no t so much. I am the kind  of student that does 
not talk very much to my teacher,  but I have never felt jealous of  my fellow students. I was only 
angry once at my teacher in that incident I mentioned earlier. That was a big mistake.  
 We all have anger since we have not perfecte d our patience. We make  mistakes as do our 
teachers but it does not matter wh o made the mistake. We need to think very carefully. That is 
the most important point.   Therefore, it is important that once a Dharma connection is made, we must rely correctly on our guru. The greater our faith in our guru, the grea ter will be the benefit  derived from that 
relationship, especially when we receive teachings from him. If we have great faith in him, then 
his teachings will benefit us greatly even though th e words he uses are the same as that of another 
teacher giving the same teaching . Without such faith, his teachi ngs will not benefit us as much 
because we will start to think, “I have heard this teaching before.” 
 In the section in the Lamrim on ho w to listen to the teachings and the attitudes to be adopted, it 
mentions the importance of respect for the teachi ngs and the teacher. The listener is encouraged 
to cultivate the attitude that the teacher  giving the teaching is the Buddha himself. This does not 
mean, in reality, that the teacher is necessarily  a buddha nor is t here a need for him  to be a 
buddha.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
117  
 
It is only when we listen to the teachings with su ch a positive attitude that  we benefit from the 
teachings. Therefore, the motivati ons for both teaching and listenin g to the teachings have to be 
pure - that we are engaging in such activities in order to subdue our own minds. Otherwise, the 
mind degenerates and becomes more stubborn. That which is supposed to be medicine becomes 
poison. Joyful at hearing the teachings, faith arises in our minds when we see how logical, 
profound and extensive the teachings are. Apprecia ting these qualities of the Buddha’s teachings, 
faith will also arise towards the Buddha himsel f, who founded and taught the entire Dharma. 
We then think, “How amazing and inconceivable the Buddha is.” This also helps us to develop 
faith in our guru who explains and actualises the teachings in his practice.   
2) How to undertake joyous effort 
a) Abandoning that which is incongruous with joyous effort  
i) Identifying that class of things that are incongruous [with joyous effort] 
ii) How to abandon those 
1’ Abandoning the laziness that relishes the taste of idleness  
a’ Examining the causes of laziness and abandoning them  
b’ How to abandon them 
1" Abandoning laziness by reflecting on the disadvantages of it in this life 
2" Abandoning laziness by reflecting on the disadvantages of it in future 
lives 
a" Suffering is certain to arise 
b" It will be difficult to bear 
c" It is contradictory to want happiness yet not pursue virtue 
d" Imploring one to pursue the methods by which one may be 
liberated from suffering  
2’ Abandoning the laziness that clings to unwholesome (ignoble) activities  
3’ Abandoning the laziness that is disheartened about virtue 
b) Increasing the strength of joyous effort- the antidote 
a" Suffering is certain to arise 
(7, 11) 
If even in this life I shall be gripped with fear 
Like that of a live fish being rolled (in hot sand), 
Why even mention the unbe arable agonies of hell 
That will result from my unwholesome deeds? 
 
The suffering that we may have to experience at the time of death is likened here to a live fish left 
on the hot sand. This is only the suffering at the time of our death. We also accumulated so 
much negative karma in our past lives, which ca n only lead to rebirth in the hot hells. Once 
there, we will have to undergo the suffering of  being burnt, boiled, fried, etc. These are the 
definite consequences of our negative karma. Wh y are we not afraid now? This being so, while 
we still have the time, we should strive in  virtue instead of idling and being lazy.  
b" It will be difficult to bear 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
118  
(7, 12) 
How can I remain at ease like this, 
When I have committed the actions (that will bear fruit) 
In my delicate infant's body encountering boiling acids 
In the hell of tremendous heat? 
 
We accumulated so many negative actions in our past  lives. In this life, we continue to spend our 
time completely absorbed in the pleasures of th e many distractions around us, creating causes 
that will only lead us to the he ll realms where vats of molten iron  and acids await us. Once there, 
our suffering will be intensified because our skin wi ll be as delicate as a baby’s. Therefore, while 
we still have time, we should stop laziness and engage in virtue.  
c" It is contradictory to want happiness yet not pursue virtue 
(7, 13) 
Much harm befalls those with little forbearance 
And those who want results wi thout making any effort. 
While clasped by death, they  shall cry like the gods, 
"Oh no, I am overcome by misery!" 
 We want happiness but we do not create its caus e s .  W e  w a n t  t o  b e  l e a r n e d ,  g a i n  d i f f e r e n t  
attainments and be born in a pure land, yet we do not create the  causes for this to happen. 
Those who have a low threshold for pain and su ffering often find themselves in trouble and 
suffer even more.   We will definitely die once we are reborn in any of the 3 realms. Yet we behave like desire realm gods, completely distracted by pleasures. We do not remember death and impermanence, 
thinking we are immortal and spending our entire lives  accumulating only negative karma. Such 
behaviour can only lead to problems and sufferings in this life and future lives. Therefore, it is appropriate to remember death and impermanence  at all times and to strive in virtue.  
d" Imploring one to pursue the methods by which one may be liberated from suffering 
(7, 14) 
Relying upon the boat of a human (body), 
Free yourself from the great river of pain! 
As it is hard to fi nd this boat again, 
This is no time for sleep, you fool. 
 On the basis of the human life of leisure and o pportunity that we have obtained now, we can 
free ourselves from the river of suffering. If we let the boat leave without boarding it, it will take a 
long time for it to come back. Likewise, it is extremely difficult to find such a human life of 
leisure and opportunity again in th e next life, because the causes fo r attaining it are so rare. As 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
119  
we are not creating the causes for such  a rebirth, it will not come again: As it is hard to find this 
boat again, / This is no time for sleep, you fool. Therefore, while all the conditions for practising the 
Dharma are present, we must trai n in the paths of the person of small, medium or great capacity, 
(according to our abilities) and free ourselves from the river of suffering.  
 T h e  D h a r m a  s h o u l d  b e  u s e d  t o  m o v e  o u r  m i n d s  to strive for virtue at times when we are 
distracted by non-virtuous activities. We mu st use the Dharma to benefit our own minds, 
checking to see whether our mental states are impr oving or not, rather than  using it to check on 
the progress of others, which is none of our business!
36 
 
Verses 3-14 contain the advice for overcoming the laziness that relishes the taste of idleness. In 
summary, reflect on:   
• The great purpose that can be achieved with this human life of leisure and opportunity, 
which can bring us across the river of suffering;  
• How difficult it will be to achieve such a rebirth again;  
• How death is certain.   
Meditating on these points will stop this fo rm of laziness and move our minds to engage 
wholeheartedly in virtue.   
 
2) How to undertake joyous effort 
a) Abandoning that which is incongruous with joyous effort  
i) Identifying that class of things that are incongruous [with joyous effort] 
ii) How to abandon those 
1’ Abandoning the laziness that relishes the taste of idleness  
2’ Abandoning the laziness that clings to unwholesome activities  3’ Abandoning the laziness that is disheartened about virtue 
b) Increasing the strength of joyous effort- the antidote 
2’. Abandoning the laziness that clings to unwholesome activities  
(7, 15) 
Having rejected the supreme joy of the sacred Dharma, 
Which is a boundless source of delight, 
Why am I distracted by the causes for pain? 
Why do I enjoy frivolous amusements and the like? 
 
The Dharma is the source of endle ss joy in this life and future li ves, but we do not practise it. 
                                              
36 The achievement of the final goal, ce rtain goodness, is spoken of in the Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds:  
“Relying on the boat of the human body, free yourself from  the great river of suffering.” As Shantideva says, you 
must rely on a life in a happy realm – exemplified in  the verse by the human body – and cross the ocean of 
existence, reaching omniscience. Moreover, you must pass successively through many  lifetimes. Consequently, 
ethical discipline – the pre-eminent cause of achieving a body in a happy realm – is the root of the path. ( Lamrim 
Chenmo,  Vol. 2, pg. 262)  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
120  
Instead, we are distracted by those frivolous am usements, which are only the sources of suffering. 
Why do we enjoy such pleasures? It is incorrect to be attached to them because they are only the 
source of pain and suffering, while it is appropriat e to strive in the Dharma as it is the source of 
boundless happiness in this and all future lives. 
 
3’. Abandoning the laziness that is disheartened about virtue 
a’ Instructions on making effort in the antidote to being disheartened  
b’ How the scriptures teach the way to put the antidote into practice  
c’ If one makes effort, laziness will be arre sted and one will be capable of accomplishing 
enlightenment 
a’ Instructions on making effort in the antidote to being disheartened  
(7, 16) 
Without indulging in despondency,  I should gather the supports  
(for enthusiasm) 
And earnestly take control of myself. 
(Then by seeing) the equality  between self and others, 
I should practise exchanging self for others. 
 Being disheartened is a form of laziness. It is th e mind that thinks, “How can I ever practise the 
Dharma. I can’t do it.”  An analogy was used earlier for joyous effort.  Be fore going into battle, the generals put on their 
armour and ensure that all the necessary preparat ions have been completed. During the battle, 
they do not turn back regardless of whatever hardships they may encounter. Even when the 
enemy is defeated, they do not sit idly by but continue to be alert,  watching to see if the enemy is 
reassembling and returning again to challenge them.   Applying this to the training of the bodhisattvas, prior to engaging in the practice of virtue, they 
uplift their minds and generate very strong dete rmination. Then they strive to gather the two 
collections. When practising,  they utilise both mindfulness and vigilance.  T h e y  s t r i v e  t o  g a i n  
complete control over their bodies, speech, and mi nd to make them  serviceable in their pursuit 
of virtue. They visualise exchanging themselves for others. We can also apply these 4 types of 
joyous efforts in order to strive in virtue.   
b’ How the scriptures teach the way to put the antidote into practice  
(7,17) 
I should never indulge in despondency by entertaining such thoughts as, 
"How shall I ever awaken?" 
For the Tathagatas who speak what is true 
Have uttered this truth: 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
121  
(7, 18) 
"If they develop the strength of their exertion, 
Even those who are flies, mosquitoes, bees and insects 
Will win the unsurpassable Awakening, 
Which is so hard to find." 37 
 
This is the advice for stopping the discouragement that thinks, “I  can’t do this.” We may think, 
“The Buddha was an exceptionally capable pers on who had joyous effort and had accumulated 
the two collections over 3 great countless eons and achieved enlightenment. I’m incapable of 
such a feat.” We s hould never think like this . Such dis couragement is a form of laziness. The 
Buddha said that even vultures, dogs , insects, etc. can attain enligh tenment, so we should rely on 
joyous effort.  
 
I have linked this outline to verse 17 alone. It seems all right to include verse 18 though I am not 
100%  sure of this.

Lesson 23   Chapter VII - verse 19-27 
The Lamrim texts teach that the wh ole purpose of explaining and listening to the teachings is to 
subdue our own minds, in order to  benefit othe rs. This is the motivation we should set for 
attending this session, thinking, “For the sake of  subduing my own mind, I am listening to these 
teachings.” 
 We studied the antidotes to the first two kinds of laziness: (1) the laziness that relishes the taste 
of idleness and (2) the laziness that  clings to ignoble activities. 
 Even if we are able to overcome these two form s of laziness and the wish to practise Dharma 
arises in our minds, that wish may be impaired by  the feeling of despondenc y, “I wish to practise 
the Dharma but I don’t have the ability to do so.”  We have to stop this third form of laziness, 
which is disheartened about virtue, by uplifting our minds and generating the determination to encourage ourselves. Otherwise, we will  cont inue our lives as before, without any Dharma 
practice.   
2) How to undertake joyous effort 
a) Abandoning that which is incongruous with joyous effort  
i) Identifying that class of things that are incongruous [with joyous effort] 
ii) How to abandon those 
1’ Abandoning the laziness that relishes the taste of idleness  
2’ Abandoning the laziness that clings to unwholesome activities  
3’ Abandoning the laziness that is disheartened about virtue 
a’ Instructions on making effort in the antidote to being disheartened  
b’ How the scriptures teach the way to put the antidote into practice  
c’ If one makes effort, laziness will be arrested and one will be capable of 
accomplishing enlightenment 
1" Reflecting on the way in which one will be capable of attaining 
enlightenment if one develops the strength of effort  
2" It is reasonable to bear the hardships that accompany working to attain 
enlightenment, for they are not even a fraction of those of the lower 
realms' suffering 
3" As the king of doctors prescribed gentle rites for healing the great 
disease, it is reasonable to bear [the hardships involved] 
4" There is no suffering in that and happiness increases, therefore, it is 
reasonable to take joy in that 
b) Increasing the strength of joyous effort- the antidote 
1" Reflecting on the way in which one will be capable of attaining enlightenment if one develops 
the strength of effort  
(7, 19) 
(It will be) so, if I do not forsake the Bodhisattvas' way of life. 
Why should someone like myself, who has been born, in the human race, 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
123  
Not attain Awakening, since I am able to recognize 
What is beneficial and what is of harm? 
 
In verse 18, the Buddha said that animals and inse cts are able to complete the accumulation of 
the two collections and achieve enlightenment th rough the power of joyous effort. We have 
achieved a human rebirth,  considered the best of the 6 kinds of rebirths, we are able to articulate 
and to understand what needs to be abandoned an d cultivated. If we do not get discouraged, if 
we develop the altruistic intention and follow the bo dhisattva way of life, then we will definitely 
be able to achieve the state of full enlightenment.   When we start to think: “How will I be able to develop this bodhicitta? How it is possible for me to engage in such great deeds to benefit countle ss sentient beings? How is it possible for someone 
like me to achieve enlightenment?” - that is the ti me to recall what is ment ioned in verse 19: “If I 
put in the effort,  I will definitely be able to achieve enlightenment.”   The text says that there is nothing that cannot be  accomplished when one has  joyous effort. If 
animals can achieve it, then we human beings ca n definitely achieve enlightenment. Therefore, 
there is no reason to be discouraged.  
2" It is reasonable to bear the hardships that accompany working to attain enlightenment, for 
they are not even a fraction of those of the lower realms' suffering 
a" It is unreasonable to fear the hardsh ips of giving up one's arms and legs, etc. 
b" One does not need to experience even a fraction of the lower realms' suffering 
c" An analogy illustrating the reasonableness of bearing mino r suffering in order to destroy 
the great disease 
a" It is unreasonable to fear the hardsh ips of giving up one's arms and legs, etc. 
(7, 20) 
-Nevertheless, it frightens me to think 
That I may have to give away my arms and legs- 
Without discriminating between what  is heavy and what is light, 
I am reduced to fear through confusion. 
 
(7,21) 
For over countless myriads of aeons 
I have been cut, stabbed, burned, 
And flayed alive innumerable times, 
But I have not awakened. 
 We established earlier that when we possess joyous effort, we will be able to achieve 
enlightenment.    
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
124  
Qualm : That is true but in order in achieve enlighte nment, I must practise the charity of giving 
my arms and legs etc. I cannot do this as it is too difficult.  
 
Reply:  This fear arises from the failure to discriminate  between what is heavy and what is light,  as 
mentioned in verse 20. We should not be afraid  of the wrong things.  Why be  afraid of 
something  we need not be afraid of?  
 Verse 21 points out that we have been in cycl ic existence since beginningless time, experiencing 
all sorts of sufferings such as having our heads ch opped off, being stabbed, and taking rebirth in 
the hell realms where we were burnt countless time s. Yet such sufferings did not benefit us at all 
nor help us in our quest for  enlightenment. Sa crificing our limbs is nothing compared to the 
sufferings we  endured in the past. Instead of bein g afraid of having to experience these terrible 
sufferings again, we are afraid of the much smaller suffering of offerings our limbs to others.  We  are afraid for the wrong reasons: we are not conc erned about that which is really serious but are 
worried about the trivial.   Should fear arise in our minds with regard to having to sacrifice our arms and legs for the 
purpose of achieving enlightenment, then we have to reflect that we have   experienced far worse 
suffering in samsara  since beginningless time. 
b" One does not need to experience even a fraction of the lower realms' suffering  
(7, 22) 
Yet the suffering 
Involved in my awakening will have a limit; 
It is like the suffering of  having an incision made 
In order to remove and destroy greater pain. 
 The suffering of sacrificing our limbs for the purp ose of achieving enlightenment is far less when 
compared with the suffering we experienced in th e lower realms in the past. The duration of the 
suffering of such sacrifice is also much less comp ared to the length of time we suffered in the 
past.    At the very most, it takes three countless great eons for us to complete the two accumulations 
with the application of patience and joyous effort  to achieve enlightenment.  There is, therefore, 
a time limit to our exertions and whatever sufferin gs that may have to be endured to achieve our 
goal. On the other hand, the duration and intensit y of suffering in the lower realms is endless 
and far greater.     If we were shot and the bullet was lodged in our stomach, the do ctor would have to operate on 
us to remove the bullet. We would probably feel some pain. But we would be prepared to put up 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
125  
with the pain in order to achieve the greater goal  of removing the bullet. In the same way, we 
should put up with the small suffering invo lved in order to achieve the big goal of 
enlightenment. 
c" An analogy illustrating the reasonableness of bearing minor suffering in order to destroy the 
great disease 
(7, 23) 
Even doctors eliminate illness 
With unpleasant medical treatments, 
So in order to overcome manifold sufferings 
I should be able to put up with some discomfort 
 
Enduring the hardships that come  with Dharma practice in order to achieve greater happiness 
and eliminate samsaric suffering is analogous to the patient who has to put up with some 
discomfort during his treatment in order to reco ver from his illness. We develop determination 
and courage by reflecting that by putting up with  smaller problems and hardships, we can achieve 
something really worthwhile. This prev ents us from getting disheartened. 
 
3" As the king of doctors prescribed gentle rites for healing the great disease, it is reasonable to 
bear [the hardships involved] 
a" The Teacher taught a method by which we may be cured of the great disease without 
even needing to experience the mere suffering associated with curative treatments 
b" Giving away one's body when one still finds  it difficult to do so is prohibited 
c" It is taught that one gives away one's body [only] when one is familiarised with the idea to 
a certain point, and thus there is no difficulty [in giving it away] 
a" The Teacher taught a method by which we may be cured of the great disease without even 
needing to experience the mere suffering associated with curative treatments 
(7, 24) 
Yet the Supreme Physician does not employ 
Common medical treatments such as these, 
With an extremely gentle technique, 
He remedies all the greatest ills. 
 
The ordinary doctor may use unpleasant meth ods to treat their patients. But the methods 
prescribed by the Buddha for us to achieve en lightenment are neither harsh nor difficult.  His 
methods are deemed gentle, peaceful and blissful  as he taught that by depending on the blissful 
path, we can attain the blissful state. His me thods for achieving enlightenment are primarily 
accomplished with our minds, not our speech or bodies. On the basis of abiding in meditative 
equipoise with perfect concentrat ion, we can definitely achieve enlightenment. With wisdom, we 
can eliminate the obstructions to liberation, the afflictions as well as their predispositions and 
free ourselves from the sufferings of cyclic existence.   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
126  
Qualm : In order to achieve enlightenment, I need  to give up my limbs. How can that be 
considered gentle or blissful? 
Reply : This appears in verse 25.  
b" Giving away one's body when one still finds  it difficult to do so is prohibited 
(7,25) 
At the beginning, the Guide of the World encourages 
The giving of such things as food. 
Later, when accustomed to this, 
One may progressively start to give away even one's flesh. 
 
The Buddha did not say that we need to give ou r legs and arms right from the very beginning. 
Instead, we start by giving away something small th a t  i s  e a s y  f o r  u s  t o  p a r t  w i t h ,  s u c h  a s  a n  
offering of food. Accustomed to this, we progress to giving away things of greater value such as 
our wealth. Finally, when we are familiar with th e practice of giving without any sense of loss or 
attachment, only then is it permissible to give away our bodies.38 
 
It has been said that since practitioners of the perfection vehicle must give away their bodies and 
lives and undergo such torment, their paths are very  difficult to follow. This text clearly refutes 
this view, clarifying that we do not give away our bodies as long as we find it difficult to do so but 
only do so once it becomes very easy, like giving away food.  
c" It is taught that one gives away one's body [only] when one is familiarised with the idea to a 
certain point, and thus there is no difficulty [in giving it away] 
(7, 26) 
At such a time when my mind is developed 
To the point of regarding my body like food, 
Then what hardship would there be 
When it came to giving away my flesh? 
 
When our minds are so developed that we can give away our bodies as easily as giving away food, we will not experience any difficulty or  hardships in giving away our bodies. 
 
Qualm : In order to work for the benefit of sentient  beings, I have to take rebirth in cyclic 
existence for a long time and endure the suffering  that comes with such a rebirth. That is too 
                                             
 
38 With respect to giving away your body, you do not give  it in the beginning when you are afraid. But through 
graduated training in generosity, you end your attachment to your body. Once you have increased the strength of 
your great compassion, you ha ve no difficulty when you give it away , provided it is for a great purpose.( Lamrim 
Chenmo , Vol.2, pg. 191)

difficult and it takes too long. I am afraid this is beyond me. 
  
Reply:  The answer to this qualm can be found in the next outline.  
 
4" There is no suffering in that and happiness increases, therefore, it is reasonable to take joy in 
that 
a" Although there is physical and mental discomfort for a person unknowledgeable in the 
stages of accomplishing that path, there is no suffering from such hardships for one who is 
knowledgeable 
b" Therefore, for a great being, there is no cause for despairing in the prospect of remaining 
in cyclic existence 
c" Due to such reasons, in terms of progressing along the path, they are explained to be 
more knowledgeable than a Hinayanist 
d" Therefore, it is unreasonable to be disheartened in the bodhisattvas' conduct 
a" Although there is physical and mental discomfort for a person unknowledgeable in the stages 
of accomplishing that path, there is no suffering from such hardships for one who is 
knowledgeable  
(7, 27) 
Having forsaken all transgression, there would be no suffering, 
And due to wisdom, there would be no lack of joy; 
But now my mind is afflic ted by mistaken conceptions 
And my body is caused ha rm by unwholesome deeds 
 
Since bodhisattvas take rebirth in cyclic existenc e due to the power of their  prayers and wishes, 
unlike sentient beings who take such rebirths under the power of their karma and afflictions, 
they are not stained by the fault of cyclic existence just as the lotus remains unstained by the mud 
from which it grows.  Having abandoned all form s of negativities, bodhisattvas are no longer 
subject to suffering or experiencing any physical pain. Also, since they have directly perceived and 
are skilled in the meaning of selflessness, due to  such wisdom, they are always joyful.  Another 
commentary notes that the bodhisattvas are expert wi th regard to when it is appropriate to give 
away their bodies. Due to such knowledge,  they also have no “lack of joy”. 
 “And my body is caused harm by unwholesome deeds,” refers to our perceiving ourselves as having a ‘self,’ grasping at ‘I,’ and ‘mine.’ Due to  such mistaken conceptions, we experience great 
mental unhappiness. Due to engaging in all sorts of  negative actions such as killing and stealing, 
we experience the consequences in the form of physical harm.
39 
                                              
39 Qualm:  R e a c h i n g  b u d d h a h o o d  r e q u i r e s  t a k i n g  l i m i t l e s s  r e b i r t h s  i n  c y c l i c  e x i s t e n c e ,  s o  I  w i l l  b e  h a r m e d  b y  t h e  
suffering therein. I am not capable of such a thing. 
Reply : Reflect as follows. Bodhisattvas have eliminated all sin; therefore, sin's affect—the feeling of suffering—will 
not arise because they have stopped the cause. Through firm knowledge that cyclic  existence lacks an intrinsic nature, 
like a magician's illusion, they also have no mental suffer ing. Given that their physical and mental bliss increases, 
they have no reason to become disheartened even though they are still in cyclic existence.( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol.2, 
pg. 191)

Through the collection of merit, we eliminate the suffering of the body. 
Through the collection of wisdom, we eliminate the suffering of the mind. 
Lesson 24   Chapter VII - verse 28-32 
We will achieve the blissful result by depending on the true path taught by the Buddha. There 
are qualms as to how the Buddha’s teachings can be blissful when we have to give up our bodies 
in order to gain enlightenment.  But  there ar e no contradictions in the teachings. Buddha 
taught us to familiarise ourselves by starting wi th giving something small and gradually increasing 
the giving until we are ready to sacrifice our bodies . Only when giving our bodies is like offering 
food to someone is it permissible fo r us to make such a sacrifice.  
 
Superior bodhisattvas at the first ground (or first bhumi ), having abandoned all negativities, attain 
the extremely exalted mind generation, which enab les them to experience no physical pain or 
suffering even when sacrificing their bodies. They also know exactly the appropriate time for such 
giving and, having directly realised emptiness, they  do not experience any mental suffering. 
Chandrakirti said in his Supplement to the Middle Way  that, for bodhisattvas on the first bhumi, 
merely hearing from afar someone asking for thei r flesh, brings them far greater happiness than 
the bliss experienced by Hearer and Solitary Realis er arhats. This being so, what need is there to 
mention the happiness they derive from  actually giving away their flesh?  
 
Ordinary beings, on the other hand, experi ence great mental suffering due to their 
misconceptions adhering to a self and apprehen ding things to be mine. Since they have not 
abandoned their negativities, they also experience  physical pain and suffering. If we wish to 
analyse this further, we can consider the followin g: Since hearer superiors and solitary realiser 
superiors have direct realisations of emptiness, do they still experience mental unhappiness? Do 
they have physical suffering?

b" Therefore, for a great being, there is no cause for despairing in 
the prospect of remaining in cyclic existence 
c" Due to such reasons, in terms of progressing along the path, they 
are explained to be more knowledgeable than a Hinayanist  
d" Therefore, it is unreasonable to be disheartened in the 
bodhisattvas' conduct 
b) Increasing the strength of joyous effort- the antidote 
b" Therefore, for a great being, there is no cause for despairing in the prospect of remaining in 
cyclic existence 
(7, 28) 
As their bodies are happy due to their merits, 
And their minds are happy due to their wisdom, 
Even if they remained in cyclic existence for the sake of others, 
Why would the Compassionate Ones ever be upset? 
 
Wherever bodhisattvas abide, because they are kn owledgeable in the meanin g of selflessness, they 
do not experience any mental unhappiness and due to their store of virtue, they also do not 
experience any physical suffering.  They are never stained by the faults of the three realms while 
they remain in cyclic existenc e for the sake of accomplishing the welfare of sentient beings. 
Through their familiarity  with the two collectio ns, they are always happy, finding no cause for 
despair.   Question from student: It says ‘Their bodies are happy.’ I thought happiness is associated with 
the mind rather than the body. Answer: Physical happiness or bliss refers to the f eelings of pleasure or bliss associated with any 
of the 5 sense consciousnesses. 
40 
c" Due to such reasons, in terms of progressing along the path, they are explained to be more 
knowledgeable than a Hinayanist  
                                              
40 What is the difference between bodily and mental feelings ? Feelings that arise in the retinue of the five sense 
consciousness are called “bodily feelings”; feelings that arise in the retinue of the mental consciousness are called 
“mental feelings.” ( A Necklace for Those of Clear Awareness Clearly Revealing the Modes of Minds and Mental Factors  by 
Yeshe Gyaltsen, pg. 18)   
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
130  
(7, 29) 
Due to the strength of  the Awakening Mind, 
The Bodhisattvas consume their previous transgressions 
And harvest oceans of merit: 
Hence they are said to excel the Shravakas.    
 
The Mahayana vehicle is said to be superior to  the Hinayana (or lesser vehicle) because of the 
manner in  which one travels on the path. Due to the power of their bodhicitta, all past 
negativities of bodhisattvas are completely dest royed as taught in the earlier chapter on the 
benefits of bodhicitta. Nothing is more powe rful than bodhicitta, which is likened to, the fire at 
the end of an age , (2, 14), in terms of purifying negativiti es.  Whether we are asleep or awake, as 
long as bodhicitta is in our mental continua, our merit will constantly increase. This is how 
bodhisattvas accumulate and complete the collection  of merit, which also ac ts as the antidote to 
past negativities.  
 
The generation of bodhicitta is the primary reason why bodhisattvas are superior to Hearers. It is 
bodhicitta that transforms their direct realisat ion of emptiness into a full-fledged realisation. 
Unlike the direct realisation of emptiness achi eved by the bodhisattvas, the realisation of 
emptiness attained by the Hearers and Solitary Re aliser arhats cannot act as the antidote to the 
obstructions to omniscience. Bo dhisattvas are, therefore, supe rior to the Hinayana arhats in 
terms of both their collection of merit and wisdom.        
d" Therefore, it is unreasonable to be disheartened in the bodhisattvas' conduct 
(7, 30) 
So, having mounted the horse of an Awakening Mind 
That dispels all discouragement and weariness, 
Who, when they know of this mind that proceeds from joy to joy, 
Would ever lapse into despondency? 
 
So, refers to the reasons stated in the preceding verse. Discouragement , relates to mind while 
weariness   relates to the body. Bodhisattvas have ne ither because they are both eliminated by 
bodhicitta, the blissful path, in dependence on which they  achieve the state of full 
enlightenment.  
 
Who, when they know of this mind:  Knowing this, how could anyone with discernment become 
discouraged? Some commentaries  take this discer nment to refer to the ability to differentiate 
between what is right and wrong. Another commentar y points out that this quote refers to those 
who possess realisations. Yet another commentary interprets this to mean those who really 
understand the benefits of bodhicitta. If we take mind  here to mean bodhicitta and know of this 
mind,  as the knowledge of the benefits  of bodhicitta, then it can be  interpreted as, ‘‘a person who 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
131  
knows the benefits of bodhicitta  will never become dispirited.” 
 
Having mounted the horse of an Awakening Mind: We can easily reach our destination by riding a 
subdued horse on a paved road. The horse, here refers to the blissful  path, bodhicitta.  Mounting 
the  horse of bodhicitta, which has been gene rated in our minds, we ultimately reach our 
destination -   buddhahood. Those who can disc ern between right and wrong and wish to  
achieve enlightenment never become disheartened. Th erefore, strive in joyous effort and do not 
lapse into despondency.  
 In summary, it is inappropriate to become dishea rtened with accumulating the two collections of 
merit and wisdom – the causes of enlightenment – for they will quickly eliminate all suffering of 
body and mind. It is completely appropriate, howe ver, to be disheartened with accumulating the 
causes of cyclic existence, because such causes only bring about limitless suffering. The former 
puts an end to suffering, while the latter only le ads to more suffering. Knowing the qualities of 
one and the faults of the other, why should we then become disheartened about achieving 
enlightenment?
41     
 
Questions from Geshe-la:  
How does bodhicitta stop you from becoming discouraged?  
How does bodhicitta stop physical suffering and pain?  Some may think, “When I reflect on the suffering of others, it makes me disheartened.” The text 
says that when we have bodhicitta, we do not become disheartened. Since compassion is a mind 
wishing sentient beings to be free from suffering, we must reflect on the suffering of others and 
develop love and compassion. On that basis, we  generate bodhicitta and stop ourselves from 
becoming dispirited. But how does this accord wi th the feeling people usually have when they 
think of the suffering of others?  Another mistake people make is  thinking, “I may develop bodh icitta and enter the Mahayana 
path. But to accumulate the two collections, I must train in the limitless deeds of bodhisattvas for 
three countless great eons. This is too difficult fo r me.” Discouraged, they look for a quick path 
and engage in tantra.    Such dispiritedness causes the degeneration of their bodhicitta. With su ch a degeneration, how 
would they be able to achieve anything even if  they found a quick path ? The bodhicitta in the 
mind of a practitioner seeking a quick path must be so strong that he cannot bear to spend such 
                                             
 
41 Likewise, do not become discouraged even by being delaye d in cyclic existence for an immeasurable length of 
time, because a long length of time is not in itself a reas on to be disheartened. If suffering is extremely intense, 
even a brief period of it is disheartening. But if there is no suffering and you are happy, even a long time is not 
disheartening. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 193)  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
132  
a long time to achieve enlightenment before he ca n work for the benefit of sentient beings. Such 
bodhicitta is further supported by great courage and determination. It is therefore much more 
powerful than the bodhicitta that is required for a practitioner  travelling on the sutric path to 
enlightenment.42
