1' A brief presentation by means of identifying the four powers   
(7, 31) 
The supports when working for the sake of living beings 
Are aspiration,25 steadfastness, joy and rest.26 
Aspiration is developed through fear of misery 
And by contemplating the bene fits of (aspiration) itself. 
 
(7, 32) 
Thus in order to increase my enthusiasm 
I should strive to abandon its opposing forces, 
To (amass the supports of) aspiration, self-confidence,27 joy and rest, 
To practice in earnest and to become strong in self-control.43 
                                              
42 It is most wonderful to think, “If only I could attain bu ddhahood in a brief time for the sake of living beings,” 
because you are moved by the very inte nse power of your love, compassion, and spirit of enlightenment. However, 
when you are not within the sphere of these motivations,  and you see the necessity of a very long training in 
endless deeds and that much hard work is required, you might think, “If this is the case, who could possibly do it?” 
If you should then claim that you are seeking a quic k path, you directly damage the engaged spirit of 
enlightenment and indirectly damage the aspirational sp rit of enlightenment. Your capacity for the Mahayana 
lineage steadily weakens, and your enlightenment fades in to remote distance, for you have utterly contradicted 
what Nagarjuna and Asanga determined to be the Conquero r’s own thought on how to increasingly strengthen the 
spirit of enlightenment. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg.193-194)  
 
43 25 In the root text the term ‘appreciation’ (mos-pa; Skt: adhimukti) appears, but Thog-me Zang-po, in agreement 
with the Indian commentary ‘Grel-chen and the Tibetan tr anslator Lama Pang, substitutes ‘aspiration’ (dun-pa; 
Skt: chanda). See Thog. P139 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
133  
We may agree with all the reasons given so far, but we may still  think, “I cannot work for the 
benefit of sentient beings.” The bodhisattvas counter such thoughts by relying on the four 
powers:  
 (1) The power of aspiration (or the power of esteem): The term “belief” is used in the 
Tibetan root text, which some commentaries take to mean just that while ot hers interpret this to 
mean “aspiration”. In the division of the 5 object -ascertaining mental factors, there is a difference 
between “belief” ( mos pa ) and “aspiration” ( dun pa ).44 
 Since aspiration is one of the bases for the de velopment of joyous effort, I think it may be 
more appropriate to interpret ‘mos pa’ as  the mental factor of aspiration. Regardless of the 
different interpretations, what is referred to here is the need for us to re flect on karma – the law 
of cause and effect. Based on such reflection, we  aspire to abandon the objects of abandonment 
and to cultivate the objects of cultivation. Unde rstanding how positive and negative actions lead 
to happiness and misery respectively, we seek to cultivate positive actions and to abandon 
negative actions. Such a mind is referred to as “a spiration” in this context. Not only do we need 
to have aspiration, but our aspiration must also be irreversible.  
 (2) The power of steadfastness (or the powe r of stability): We analyse the objects of 
abandonment and cultivation by checking which activities are appropriate and which are not. 
On that basis,  we finally engage in the practice  of abandonment  and cultivation, and bring that 
practice to completion without turning back. Th is power causes our joyous effort to become 
irreversible. 
 (3) The power of joy: This mean s that when we first engage in  an activity, we do it with 
delight and enthusiasm, and once so engaged, we  are insatiable in that we do not want to 
discontinue the activity. The Lamrim Chenmo gives an analogy of children at play who cannot be 
satiated.  
 (4) The power of relinquishment (or the po wer of suspension): When our minds and 
bodies are tired from our joyous exertions, we sh ould rest for a while. Immediately after we have 
rested, we should continue with the practice of virtue again.

stanzas 63,66, and rest in stanza 67.

44 Belief is a knower which holds the object that has been as certained by its valid cognizer, thinking, “It is like this 
and not otherwise.” Here its function is specified as “non -captivation” because when one has gained firm belief in 
the object that one has ascertained, an opponent cannot steer one away from it; whereas aspiration is a knower 
that, upon observing some intended thing, seeks it. It is the very wish to be endowed with this or that (attribute) of 
a desired thing. It has the function of acting as a support for making effort. ( A Necklace for Those of Clear Awareness 
Clearly Revealing the Modes of Minds and Mental Factors  by Yeshe Gyaltsen, pg. 26, 28)   
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2

134  
Understanding its benefits and the fear of cyclic  existence leads to the development of the power 
of aspiration. In order to develop joyous effort, we need to: 
(a) eliminate its opposing factors whic h are the three forms of laziness,  
(b) cultivate its supporting condit ions which are aspiration, pride, joy, and relinquishment,  
(c) practise earnestly,  applying mindfulness and vigilance, and  
(d) gain control over our bodies  and minds.   Some commentaries classify six powers, the additi onal ones being the power of mindfulness and 
vigilance and the power of mastery of or control over one’s body and mind. We increase our joyous effort through mastery of our bodies and minds, which are cultivated through the 
application of the four powers together with th e power of mindfulness an d vigilance. The third 
line of verse 32 refers to the four powers; To practice in earnest , refers to the power of mindfulness 
and vigilance and To become strong in self-control , refers to the power of mastery of one’s body and 
mind. 
Lesson 25   Chapter VII - verse 33-43 
2) How to undertake joyous effort 
a) Abandoning that which is incongruous with joyous effort  
b) Increasing the strength of joyous effort- the antidote 
i) Increasing the strength of the conditions conducive to joyous effort  
1' A brief presentation by means of identifying the four powers  
2' An extensive explanation 
ii) Devoting oneself to the practice of the conduct by means of mindfulness and 
alertness 
iii) Accomplishing the conduct is within your power 
 
In the last class, we looked at the four powers  conducive for the generation of joyous effort, 
which are: 
1. the power of  aspiration (or esteem) 
2. the power of steadfastness (or stability) 3. the power of joy  4. the power of relinquishment (or suspension) 
Some commentaries add the following two powers making a total of six:  
5.  the power of mindfulness  6.  the power of mastery of one’s body and mind  To cultivate joyous effort, we have to strive to abandon the discordant (or opposing) factors, 
which are the three types of laziness: 1. the laziness that relishes the taste of idleness 
2. the laziness that clings to unwholesome activities  
3. the laziness that is disheartened about virtue  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT

2' An extensive explanation 
a’ The power of esteem  
1" The esteemed object  
a" Discarding wrongdoing 
b" Adopting qualities 
c" Examining the list of things to do and not to do 
2" The effect 
3" The cause for esteem  
4" Conclusion 
b’ The power of stability 
c’ The power of joy 
d’ The power of suspension 
a" Discarding wrongdoing  
(7, 33) 
I shall have to overcome 
The boundless faults of  myself and others, 
And (in order to destroy) each of these faults (alone) 
(I may have to strive until) an  ocean of aeons is exhausted. 
 
(7, 34) 
Yet if within myself I do not perceive 
Even a fraction of the perseverance (required) to exhaust these faults, 
Then why do I not have a heart attack? 
For now I have become an abode for infinite misery. 
 
When we generate bodhicitta, we  pledge to destroy the 84,000 negative emotions of sentient 
beings and ourselves, even though abandoning one of them would take many eons and great 
effort. When we examine our minds, we see that we do not have even a fraction of the joyous 
effort that is required to abandon just one of those faults. Due to these faults, in the future, we 
will take rebirth in the hell realms, where imme asurable suffering awaits us. Therefore, why are 
we not panicking? These verses help us develop the aspiration to overcome, the boundless faults of 
myself and others.  
b" Adopting qualities 
(7, 35) 
Likewise, I shall have to realize 
Many excellent qualities for myself and others, 
And (in order to attain) each  of these qualities (alone) 
I may have to acquaint myself with  its cause until an ocean of  
aeons is exhausted. 
 
(7, 36) 
However, I have never developed acquaintance 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
136  
With even a fraction of these excellences. 
How strange it is to squander 
This birth I have found by some coincidence. 
 
When developing bodhicitta, we also pledge to endow other sentient beings and ourselves with 
liberation and enlightenment, for which we need to acquire the infinite qualities of a buddha. 
To create the causes to acquire just one of these qualities requires many eons and a lot of effort. 
Presently, we are neither familiar nor acquainted with even a fraction of these qualities. 
 It is very meaningful for us to apply this huma n life we have attained, endowed with leisure and 
opportunity and extremely difficult to achieve, to acquiring buddha qu alities. It is very strange to 
squander this precious opportunity to be nefit our present and future lives.   
 This advice is for us to cultivat ing joyous effort perseverance. This verse is directed at our minds, 
asking us why  we doing this to ourselves when  we should be working to acquire the infinite 
qualities of the Buddha.  Verses 33 and 34 are encouraging us to abandon our faults. Verses 35 and 36 are urging us to 
acquire the infinite qualities of  the Buddha. We do both by depending on joyous effort.  
c" Examining the list of things to do and not to do  
(7, 37) 
I have not made offerings to the Lord Buddhas, 
I have not given the pleasure of great festivals, 
I have not performed actions for the teachings, 
I have not fulfilled th e wishes of the poor, 
 
(7, 38) 
I have not granted fearlessness to the frightened 
And I have not given happiness to the weak. 
All I have given rise to is 
The agonies in the mother's  womb, and to suffering. 
 
I have not made offering s to the Lord Buddhas,  refers to the fact that we  have not made offerings to 
the Buddha when he was alive and now that the Buddha is no longer here, we do not even 
visualise making mental offerings to him.    
I have not given the pleasure of great festivals,  points to our failure to make any offerings of food or 
robes to Sangha communities and not making offe rings to superior bodhisattvas. Furthermore, 
we have not studied the three collections of script ural teachings (the three pitakas) nor put into 
practice the realisational teaching s of the three higher trainings. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
137  
 
Verse 37 refers to the giving of material gifts wh ile verse 38 deals with the giving of fearlessness. I 
have not granted fearle ssness to the frightened : We have not saved sentient beings when they were in 
danger, e.g. we have not engaged in liberating  animals. We have not given teachings of the 
Buddha even though we have been capable of doin g so nor have we  shar ed the teaching with 
others, guiding them as to what is to be cultivated and what is to be abandoned.  
 
We have benefited neither the objects of our wo rship and offering (the Three Jewels) nor the 
objects of our compassion (sentient beings). We ha ve either failed to make any offerings to the 
Three Jewels or we have acquired our offering s to them through wrong livelihood, accompanied 
by negative motivations. With re gard to the Buddha, we have no t practised his teachings. With 
regard to sentient beings, we have not practise d the three types of giving and not given them 
what they have needed - we have not benefited them in any way. It seems that our purpose in 
coming into this world is only to give our mothers great suffering!  
 
Seeing that we have not done any justice to ou rselves nor done anything beneficial for other 
sentient beings, we must develop joyous effort, courage and determination and keep 
d i s c o u r a g e m e n t  a t  b a y .  T h i s  a d v i c e  i s  f o r  the bodhisattvas. For us, such advice seems 
disheartening because we have neither bodhicitta  nor determination but it should not be like 
that.   It is important to know when we should reflec t on these points. For example, when things are 
not going well and we are overwhelmed with proble ms and feeling hopeless, that is not be the 
time for applying this advice.   
2" The effect 
a" It is unreasonable to abandon esteem for the dharma 
b" The reasons for that  
a" It is unreasonable to abandon esteem for the dharma 
(7, 39) 
Both now and in previous lives, 
Such deprivation has arisen 
Because of my lack of aspiring for the Dharma. 
Who would ever reject this aspiring for Dharma? 
 In this and our previous lives, due to our lack of aspiration for the Dharma, we have not engaged 
in virtue and we experience suffering as a resu lt. Those seeking happiness will never give up; they 
only work at enhancing their aspiration for the Dharma.   
 Just as a dog is completely disinterested when we  eat vegetables in front of it, we have neither 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
138  
interest in nor aspiration for th e teachings even though the Dharma  is right in front of us. We 
have access to the wonderful and profound teachi ngs of the people of small, medium and great 
capacity and the tantric teachings, but we do  not have the aspiration to cultivate them. 
b" The reasons for that  
(7, 40 ) 
The Mighty One (Subduer) himself has said 
That aspiration is the root of every facet of virtue; 
 
The Buddha says in the sutras The Ocean of Intelligence  and The Moon Lamp , that the roots of 
virtue of working for liberation and enlightenmen t all come from the aspiration for the Dharma.  
Therefore, it is inappropriate to abandon aspirati on.  It is obvious that those who have strong 
aspiration for the Dharma become good practiti oners. Those with middling aspiration become 
middling practitioners and those who have no as piration for the Dharma remain as ordinary 
beings. Aspiration is important in whatever we do, be it studying or practising; without it, 
nothing gets done. When studying the Buddha’s teac hings, we need aspiration to learn as much 
as we can. Without it, we only end up receiving imprints in our minds and they remain forever at 
that level.   
  It is important to aim high. When  we see a person doing prostrat ions, we should not think, “I 
can’t do that,” but instead take that person as a role model and aspire to become like him. But 
when we see an angry person, we should not foll ow his example. No one is perfect; nobody has 
all the qualities and is free from all faults and obscurations from the start. If such a perfect 
person existed, there would be no need for the Dharma. Everyone has different karma and levels 
of intelligence. Some enjoy good conditions to practise Dharma while others do not.  Due to 
such differences, there will also  be differences in people.  
 By aiming high and striving to develop joyous e ffort, all this helps us develop the aspiration for 
the Dharma. Without it, our Dharma practice will degenerate and we will only experience 
unhappiness.  What we want is happiness. Therefor e, we have to create the cause for virtue and 
practise the Dharma. Aspiration for the Dharma is  essential and extremely important. In order to 
experience perfect happiness, we  must create perfect causes in  terms of perfect virtue and 
practice. This requires the perf ect aspiration for the Dharma.

i) Increasing the strength of the conditions conducive to joyous effort  
1' A brief presentation by means of identifying the four powers  
2' An extensive explanation

(1)  Reflecting on mixed karma and its effects

(3)  Reflecting on black karma and it effects.

b’ The power of stability 
c’ The power of joy

ii) Devoting oneself to the practice of the conduct by means of mindfulness and alertness 
iii) Accomplishing the conduct is within your power 
a" Presentation 
(7, 40 cd) 
Its root is constant acquaintance 
With the ripening-effects (of actions). 
 
The root of aspiration is the constant acquaintance  with the fruitional effects of karma – 
understanding how happiness is the fruit of virtuous  actions and suffering the fruitional effect of 
negative actions. Meditating on the law of cause and effect and generating the faith of conviction 
in karma becomes the root for the generation of aspiration.45 T h i s  i s  t h e  s a m e  a d v i c e  a s  t h e  
major outline in the lamrim literature which states  that, “meditating on ka rma is the root of all 
happiness and goodness.”  
 
With our faith of conviction in karma, we wi ll naturally have the aspiration to abandon non-
virtuous actions, which cause suffering and, na turally, we have the aspiration to engage in 
virtuous actions, which causes happiness. At best  our aspiration is weak because we do not have 
the heart-felt faith of conviction in karma.   
 
 
                                              
45 It then states how to develop aspiration 
Its root, in turn, is constant meditation 
On karma’s fruitional effects.   
This means that the way to develop aspiration is to medi tate on how pleasant and unpleasant effects arise from 
virtuous and nonvirtuous karma respectively . This is because it is taught that faith acts as the basis for yearning, so 
that the faith of conviction in the two types of karma and their effects will generate two kinds of desire: to elimi-
nate non virtuous karma and to adopt virtuous karma.  
Furthermore, you consider karma and its effects in general,  and in particular, the causality underlying the benefits 
of the bodhisattva deeds and the faults of violating them.  
Once you aspire to the Mahayana, you enter its door  through a commitment to clear away all faults and 
achieve all good qualities for yourself and others. You must exhort yourself, thinking, "I will have to meditate for 
many eons to purify myself of every single fault along wi th its latent propensity and to develop every single good 
quality to the fullest extent. Yet since I have not even a fr action of the joyous perseverance needed to clear away 
faults or accomplish good qualities, I have pointlessly wasted my leisure.” ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 195)  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
140  
When we have achieved the faith of conviction in karma, then whenever we engage in virtuous 
actions, we will be confident that these actions wi ll definitely lead to good results and happiness. 
We will avoid negative actions because we will know  that they only result in suffering. With such 
strong conviction, the aspiration for Dharma will arise naturally.  
 
Gyaltsab Je says in his commentary that without fa ith of conviction in karm a, it is difficult for 
our Dharma practice to be pleasing to the bu ddha and bodhisattvas. Therefore, from the very 
start, we should meditate on karma to deve lop conviction. Gyaltsab Je also mentions 
practitioners who proclaim they have realised em ptiness yet have total disregard for the workings 
of karma in the way they lead their lives. Such people have no realisation of emptiness to speak 
of and their understanding of emptiness is  also mistaken. The stronger our correct 
understanding of emptiness, the stronger will be our faith of conviction in  karma. To understand 
emptiness, we must understand dependent arisin g. The more we understand what constitutes 
dependent arising, the more our faith of conviction in karma will increase.   
b" Explanation 
(1) Reflecting on mixed karma and its effects 
 
(7 41) 
(Physical) pain, mental, unhappiness, 
All the various kinds of fear, 
As well as separation from what is desired 
All arise from an unwholesome way of life. 
 
(7, 42) 
(However) by committing wholesome actions, 
Which are (motivated by aspiration) in the mind, 
Wherever I go I shall be presented with 
Tokens of the fruit of that merit. 
 
(7, 43) 
Yet by committing negative (actions), 
Although I may wish for happiness, 
Wherever I go I shall be completely overcome 
By weapons of pain (cause d) by my wrongful life. 
 If the faith of conviction in karma gives rise to aspiration, how do we produce this faith? All the 
unpleasant and suffering experiences of this life - physical suffering, unhappy minds, or danger of 
losing our lives - are the effects of negative ac tions. We do not get (or we are separated from) 
what we desire. On the other hand, our aspiration  f o r  v i r t u e  c a u s e s  u s  t o  e n g a g e  i n  p o s i t i v e  
actions. Then, due to the accumulation of those virtues, we will experience happiness regardless 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
141  
of where we may be born. Suffering or happiness cannot arise by merely wishing for it. Suffering 
and happiness arise due to causes, which are non-vi rtue and virtue respectively. When we engage 
in non-virtuous actions, even though we wish to be happy, no matter where we are born, we will 
have to experience the effects of those negativi ties in the form of unpleasant experiences and 
suffering. As verse 43 points out, one will be completely overcome by weapons of pain. 
 
Karma can be divided into propelling karma, which determines our rebirth, and completing 
karma, which determines the conditions experience d in that rebirth. For example, when a person 
is born as a human being and experiences good health, long life and wealth in that lifetime, that 
is the result of virtuous propelling karma and completing karma.   When a sentient being is born in the hell real ms, they will experience suffering continuously 
until that lifespan is finished. That rebirth is the result of non-virtuous propelling karma and 
completing karma. For beings born as humans or gods who nevertheless experience ill health, 
sickness, poverty or lack of enjoyments, the rebi rth is the result of virtuous propelling karma 
while the unpleasant experiences are the resu lts of non-virtuous completing karma.   
 Some beings born as animals owned by wealthy peop le do not lack food or  comfort. This animal 
rebirth is the result of non-virtuous propelling  karma, but their pleasant experiences are the 
result of virtuous completing karma. There can al so be situations where the rebirth is the result 
of virtuous propelling karma and the experiences of  that life are a mixture of the results of both 
virtuous and non-virtuous completing karma.  There is an historical account of a butcher who vowed to only slaughter animals during daytime 
and would not do so in the evening. He was reborn in the surrounding hells, where he experienced much suffering during the day, but, when night fell, he experienced great 
enjoyments. There is also an account of a person  who engaged in sexual misconduct during the 
night but vowed to refrain from doing so during  the day. Similarly, this person experienced 
enjoyments during the day but great suffering at night.  A human rebirth is the result of virtuous prop elling karma. But as human beings, we have 
different experiences throughout our lives. Some  may enjoy good health and wealth when they 
are younger but as they grow older, they ex perience a lot of problems and suffering. The 
happiness or suffering experienced is the result  of the negative or positive actions.  
 As mentioned in the Lam Rim, one of the four  characteristics of karma is its certainty. 
46 If we 
have not created the action, we will not experience its effects. But once the action has been done, 
the effect will not disappear. In this particular ou tline, it does not mean that the karma itself is 
                                              
46 The four characteristics of karma are:- 1. The certainty of karma, 2. The magnification of karma, 3. Not 
experiencing the effects of actions that you did not do, 4. That the actions you have done do not perish.

mixed up. It is referring to the fact that, as a resu lt of negative and positions actions, in this life, 
one can experience both happiness and suffering.

iii) Accomplishing the conduct is within your power 
(2) Reflecting on solely white karma and its effects 
(7, 44) 
As a result of virtue, I shall dwell in the spacious, fragrant and  
cool heart of a lotus flower, 
My radiance will be nourished by  the food of the Conqueror's  
sweet speech, 
My glorious form will spring fr om a lotus unfolded by the  
Mighty One's light, 
And, as a Bodhisattva, I shall abide in the presence of the Conquerors. 
 
This outline refers to how the bodhisattvas ar e born in the pure lands, which are spacious, 
fragrant, pleasant and cool, unlike the mother’s womb which is vile and smelly. As a result of 
having familiarised themselves with positive ac tions, bodhisattvas spring forth from beautiful 
lotuses. If a bodhisattva is reborn in the pure la nd of Amitabha Buddha, he is sustained by that 
buddha’s holy speech, he gains realisations and exudes radiance as a result. A baby in the 
mother’s womb, on the other hand, only gets to feed on coarse nutrients.   At the time of birth of a bodhisattva, the lotus opens due to being struck by light emitted from 
the holy body of Amitabha. This special birth is unlike birth from a womb where both the infant 
and mother suffer. The bodies of bodhisattvas in  the pure lands are also endowed with special 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
143  
marks and signs. The ways in whic h bodhisattvas are cared for or sustained in the pure lands are 
also different. Once bodhisattvas are born from a lotus, they remain in the presence of Amitabha 
Buddha and receive the nectar of his holy speech.  
 The special birth place, method of  birth, type of body, sustenan ce and care received are the 5 
special features that are the results of having en gaged solely in white actions. This being so, we 
should persevere joyfully at accumulating positive karma.   Reflecting on this verse in such a way helps us to  generate the power of aspiration in our minds. 
(3) Reflecting on solely black karma and its effects 
(7, 45) 
However, as a result of non- virtue, my skin will be ripped off by the henchmen of Yama. 
In this feeble state, liquid copper melted by tremendous heat will be poured into my body. 
Pierced by flaming swords and daggers, my  flesh will be cut into a hundred pieces, 
And I shall tumble upon the fiercely blazing iron ground. 
 The fruitional effect of having engaged only in non- virtue, such as killing, is rebirth in the hell 
realms. Once there, we will be attacked by the frightful hell guardians, the henchmen of Yama , with 
their flaming swords and daggers. As our skin is ri pped off, we will suffer tremendously since our 
skin will be as delicate as that of a baby. We wi ll experience unbearable pain as molten iron and 
copper are poured onto our bodies. We will be pierced by blazing weapons, cut into a hundred 
pieces and spread out on the, fiercely blazing iron ground . All these unbearable sufferings will have 
to be borne day and night for a long time. Unde rstanding that these experiences are the results 
of non-virtuous actions, we generate the aspira tion wanting to abandon the negative actions we 
currently engage in and refraining from such actions again in the future. 
4" Conclusion 
(7, 46) 
Therefore, I should aspire for virtue, 
And with great respect acquaint myself with it. 
 
Therefore  r e f e r s  t o  w h a t  w e  d i s c u s s e d  e a r l i e r ,  i . e .  e n g a g i n g  i n  p o s i t i v e  o r  n e g a t i v e  a c t i o n s  w i l l  
produce positive or negative results respectively. Therefore, we must aspire to engage only in 
virtue, develop the faith of conviction in karm a and its effects and abandon what needs to be 
abandoned and cultivate what needs to be cultiv ated. This completes the extensive explanation 
of the first power, the  power of aspiration.

a" Examining it well and undertaking it 
(7, 46) 47 
Having undertaken the wholesome  in the  manner of Vajradhvaja,28 
I should then proceed to acquaint myself with self-confidence. 
 
(7, 47) 
First of all, I should examin e well what is to be done, 
To see whether I can pursue it or cannot undertake it, 
(If I am unable,) it is best to leave it, 
But once I have started  I must not withdraw. 
 
The sun and moon gods are included in the 8 classes of wrathful ones. The Buddha said in 
chapter 6 of the Avatamsaka Sutra , “When the sun shines, its rays are not obstructed by the 
height of the mountains or the evenness of the gr ound. It simply illuminates everything in its 
path. Likewise, bodhisattvas, who pledge to work  for the benefit of all, do not turn back 
regardless of the kind of situations or people they  encounter. Instead, they continue to work for others in accordance with their inte rests, dispositions, and abilities.” 
 Remembering this, we should generate the though t, “Like the bodhisattvas, once I start on any 
activity to benefit others, I shall ensure that I complete that activity.” We familiarise ourselves 
with the pride of developing great determinatio n, always thinking, “I can do this.” Before 
engaging in any virtuous activity, we have to ex amine whether we have the ability to undertake it. 
We should proceed only when we see that we can do it. Otherwise, it is best not to undertake it, to avoid giving up mid-way before the activity  can be completed. We must complete such 
virtuous activities without giving up. Most of us start a virtuous activity without first examining it 
                                             
 
47 Root text footnote pg174 - Avatamsaka  Sutra, Thog.p.144: ‘For example, De vaputra, when the sun shines forth 
it illuminates any suitable place without being turned back  by such obstacles as blindness in people or uneven 
mountain formations. Likewise when a Bodhisattva shines fo rth for the sake of others he ripens and liberates any 
suitable disciple and is not turned back by th e various obstacles present in sentient beings.’ 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2

145  
carefully. We give up in the middle and then move on to another commitment. In this way, 
nothing is well done or  accomplish ed in the end.            
 
Question from student : When we think we cannot do a project,  how do we know whether it is the 
laziness of despondency or our in ability to accomplish the task? 
 
Reply:  The laziness of despondency is a mind that thinks, from the very beginning, “I cannot do 
this,” without even checking. As mentioned earlier, the Buddha said  that even insects can attain 
enlightenment. So we humans can definitely attain enlightenment as long as we put effort into it. 
It is this laziness of despondency that prevents  the generation of determination. Since this 
laziness is the mind that thinks, “I cannot do th is,” from the onset, wi thout even examining the 
activity, there is no determinat ion in the very first place.  
 
Here we are examining the pride that thinks, “I can do it.” The determin ation is there from the 
very beginning. Producing such a thought alone ho wever, does not necessarily mean that we will 
accomplish our goal. We may not have checked, pr ior to embarking on the project, whether it is 
achievable. For instance, we may have the determ ined thought to give away $100,000. But if we 
do not have the money in the first place, nothin g is achieved by such determination, in which 
case, it is better not to make such a pledge to others.  
b" The disadvantages of discarding it after one has undertaken it 
(7, 48) 
(If I do,) then this habit will continue in other lives 
And wrongdoing and misery will increase; 
Also, other actions done at the time of its fruition 
Will be weak and will not be accomplished. 
 There will be negative consequences when we do not honour pledges we make, e.g. abandoning a 
retreat half-way through or promising to do a certain commitment but giving up before we complete it. The negative karma accumulated by su ch actions increases daily and we will have to 
experience great suffering in the future.   This also applies practicing a particular meditation al deity. Wishing to accomplish a specific deity 
practice, we take the initiation and engage in th e practice. After some time, we hear of another 
deity who appears to be more powerful or well- known. Without having accomplished anything 
with the first deity, we take on another initia tion and set of commitments. In this way, we 
complete neither the first nor second deity practice   and we take a long time to see any results, 
which are only of inferior quality if they do appear.   The following example may also appl y to us. Having decided to read a text, we start reading a few 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
146  
pages; but after a while we lose interest and st art on another text thinking, “This one might be 
better.” In the end, we complete nothing. In the beginning, we should select a text that we wish 
to study.  Having established that we can study it, we should follow through and complete what 
we have started. If our physical or mental abilit ies deteriorate to the point that we can no longer 
carry out our project, then nothing can be done. But, as long as we are able, we should strive to 
complete what we have committed ourselves to doing. 48   
(1) A brief presentation 
(7, 49) 
Self-confidence should be appl ied to (wholesome) actions, 
The (overcoming) of disturbing conce ptions and my ability (to do this). 
 
This outline addresses three types of pride: pride about karma, being proud of one’s capacity and 
being proud of the affliction. We should not ge t discouraged but instead generate determination 
by developing pride with regard to karma (or action), our afflictions and ability.  
 
The pride about karma (or action) means enthusiasm for and delight in engaging in virtue for the 
benefit of others. 
Being proud of the afflictions is a mind that wishes to destroy the afflictions. 
Being proud of one’s capacity is a mind that th inks, “I can engage enthusiastically and with 
delight in actions to benefit others, and I can overcome my afflictions.”   Some commentaries clarify  that these 3 types of pride are not to be cultivated prior to engaging 
in an action, but are to be generated when we be come disheartened in the course of engaging in 
the virtuous activity we had chosen. 
                                             
 
48 Developing the power of steadfastness means that you br ing to conclusion, without turning back, anything at 
which you joyously persevere. First, do not try to do ever ything; examine the situation ca refully. If you see that you 
can do it, you engage in it; whereas if you cannot do it, yo u do not engage in it. You should not even start in the 
first place things you will do for a while and then discard. Th e reason is that if you beco me habituated to giving up 
in the middle what you have committed yourself to do, then  through this conditioning you will in other lives again 
abandon your commitment to the training, etc. Consequently, your si n will increase in that life, and in subsequent 
lives the suffering that is the effect of this sin will in crease. Furthermore, you will not accomplish other virtues 
because you will be thinking of carrying out your earlier commitment; there will be an inferior result because you 
turned away from your earlier commitment; and your earlier commitment itself will not be fulfilled because you 
did not follow through. In sum, committing yourself to do something but leaving it unfinished is a hindrance to 
your accomplishment of other projects, and the conditioni ng also destabilizes your commitment to the vows you 
have taken. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 196)

(i) Identifying the pride of karma  
(7, 49) 
Thinking, "I alone shall do it," 
Is the self-confidence of action. 
 
In the previous lesson, the sun, the residence of the sun god, was described as analogous to 
nectar. We depend on the sun for ripening our cr ops and its rays illuminate the entire universe. 
In a similar way, the bodhisattvas, who possess gr eat compassion and the wisdom that realises 
emptiness, work to liberate all sentient beings . Regardless of the nature of the people they 
encounter – be they ignorant, hot-tempered or fu ll of afflictions - they never give up. With 
wholehearted determination and courage, bodhis attvas think, “I alon e will accomplish the 
welfare and happiness of these sentient beings.” This kind of motivation and determination in 
the minds of bodhisattvas is said to  be the pride of karma (or action).49 
(ii) The reasons one acts in that way 
(7, 50) 
                                              
49 Pride about action means that no matter who else may be  your companion as you practice the path, you do not 
count on them but accomplish it yourself alone. The th ought, “I alone shall achieve this without having any 
expectation of others,” is simi lar to pride, so it is given the name “Pride about action.” 
(Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 197) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
148  
Powerless, their minds disturbed, 
People in this world are un able to benefit themselves. 
Therefore, I shall do it (for them), 
Since unlike me these beings are incapable. 
 
As sentient beings are controlled by their negati ve emotions, whatever they do becomes mistaken 
actions. As sentient beings cannot even accomplish  their own purposes and welfare, it is unlikely 
that sentient beings will engage in any activity  that will benefit others. When bodhisattvas 
observe that sentient beings cannot benefit others  as they cannot even benefit themselves and see 
no one else working to accomplish the happi ness of sentient beings, they generate the 
extraordinary courage and determination that, “I alone shall do this for sentient beings.”  
(iii) Being proud of assu ming the burden of others of inferior karma 
(7, 51) 
(Even) if others are doing inferior tasks, 
Why should I sit here (doing nothing)? 
I do not do those tasks because of self-importance; 
It would be best for me to  have no such pride. 
 Different commentaries offer different explanatio ns to the first two li nes of this verse: 
(Even) if others are doing inferior tasks,  Why should I sit here (doing nothing)? 
 One explanation is: Sentient beings who are controlled by  the 8 worldly dharmas overcome their enemies due to 
their anger. Because of their attachment, they enga ge in all sorts of activities to support their 
loved ones and those on their side. “I,” in line 2, refers to the bodhisattva who observing this 
thinks, “I cannot just sit here and behave like thes e people. I need to train in the six perfections, 
instead of engaging in such inferior activities.”   We should not engage in such virtuous behaviou r with a proud and arrogant attitude thinking, 
“While these inferior worldly beings are engaging  in such worldly activities, I, the superior 
practitioner, will do something far better than them.” Such conceit an d afflicted pride are 
negative emotions we must avoid if we wa nt to perfect the bodhisattva conduct.  
 Another explanation is: When bodhisattvas see helpless, destitute sentie nt beings who are old, sick and poor, it is 
impossible for them to do nothing and remain ha ppy. In this context, “old, destitute people” 
refers to those who are menial labourers and have to carry heavy loads. The bodhisattvas think, “I 
must do something to help them,”  and they proceed to do so. We should learn from this advice; 
when we see helpless people we should try to  help them instead of doing nothing.   
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
149  
Determination and courage seem to be the same as ordinary pride so we must be able to 
distinguish between the two. Ordinary or afflicte d pride is a negative emotion, which has very 
limited qualities. When one is conceited about one’s  own qualities, such pride is an obstacle to 
the development of real courage and wholehearted  d e t e r m i n a t i o n .  I t  l e a d s  t o  d i s r e s p e c t  f o r  
others and only becomes the basis for experiencing  suffering. Such suffering is then followed by 
discouragement.  
 The pride of karma of the bodhisattvas, on the ot her hand, helps them to develop strong courage 
and determination to benefit othe rs. In fact, when bodhisattvas generate such pride, it only 
makes them more respectful of others and be comes the basis for experiencing happiness.  
 The Lam Rim texts mention pride as a great obstac le for the development of good qualities and 
realisations. The karmic result of having pride is rebirth in a lowly state as a servant or  slave. The 
Precious Garland states that pride causes one to be born in  a lower caste, jealously causes one to be 
born without  charisma, and anger causes one to have an unpleasant appearance. Having studied 
different teachings, we should subdue our pride in stead of increasing it as there is great danger 
that we may feel proud of our Dharma knowledg e and feel superior to others who may be less 
knowledgeable.   Studying the Buddha’s teachings is different fr om studying ordinary secular subjects. The 
purpose of studying the Dharma is to weaken an d not reinforce our  nega tive emotions such as 
pride and jealousy. It is important that we  overcome our conceit, arrogance and pride.
50 
 
The Buddha never said that the teachings were rese rved exclusively for certain castes or types of 
people, even though during his time a caste system existed. Anyone with the ability to practise his 
teachings was welcome to do so.  
 However, there were certain restrictions related to  the time and place. For example, in the Vinaya 
(the code of conduct for ordained people), bu tchers and blacksmiths are not allowed to take 
ordination. (In certain countries, being born in a butcher’s family is not considered to be a good 
thing.) This restriction was set by the Buddha in accordance with the social situation during his 
time. If butchers and blacksmiths had become or dained, it would have caused people to look 
down upon and criticise the teachings and genera te negative karma. For the same reason, the 
handicapped were also prohibit ed from taking ordination. 
                                             
 
50 Furthermore, practice while thinking, “If these beings stri ve at lowly activities without interruption, why should 
I not perform the actions that will achieve the perfect effe ct?” However, when you achieve these two prides [about 
action and ability], you should not do so with self-conceit , out of contempt for others. Rather, regard others with 
compassion, and do not mix in any pride. Thinking, “Others are not capable; I am able,” again resembles pride, so 
it is labelled “pride”. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 197)

(b) Being proud of [one's] capacity 
(i) The shortcomings of taking no pride in one's capacity 
(ii) The benefits of taking pride [in one's capacity] 
(iii) Abiding in pride as an antidote  
(iv) It is unreasonable to be proud of the afflictions 
(v) The benefits of pride as an antidote 
(i) The shortcomings of taking no pride in one's capacity 
(7, 52) 
When crows encoun ter a dying snake, 
They will act as though  they were eagles. 
(Likewise,) if (my self -confidence) is weak, 
I shall be injured by the slightest downfall. 
 
( 7, 53) 
How can those who, out of faint-hear tedness, have given up trying to 
Find liberation because of this deficiency? 
 
These verses help us to overcome our negative emotions. Without pride in our own ability, we 
will be, injured by the slightest downfall . The downfall , here refers to the root downfalls and faults of 
the bodhisattva vows. When we commit such tran sgressions, we are discouraged and unable to 
apply any joyous effort to accomplishing the purposes  of others and ourselves. It is difficult then 
for us  to achieve liberation, How can those who, out of faint-heartedn ess, have given up trying to find 
liberation because of this deficiency . Without such joyous effort, we wi ll only experience all types of 
problems in this and our future lives. How can we ever be free from suffering then?  
 The crow behaving  like an eagle before a dying snake is an analogy for th ose of us who are weak 
in cultivating the antidotes to our negative em otions. When such antidotes in our minds are 
weak, we are easily harmed by even small downfa lls or faults; we become discouraged and give 
u p .  I f  t h i s  h a p p e n s ,  w e  w i l l  c o n t i n u o u s l y  e x p e r i e n c e  p r o b l e m s  a n d  s u f f e r i n g .  S o   w e  n e e d  t o  cultivate powerful antidotes to the negative emotions in our minds.  
(ii) The benefits of taking pride [in one's capacity]51 
(7, 53)  
Yet even the greatest (obstacle) will find it hard to overcome (one with a firm mind). 
 
(7, 54) 
                                              
51 Pride about ability means that you accomplish your own an d others’ welfare, thinking: “Since living beings are 
under the power of afflictions, they ar e unable to achieve even their own welf are, much less the welfares of others. 
I am able to accomplish the welfare of both myself and others.” ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 197)  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
151  
Therefore, with a steady mind, 
I shall overcome all falls, 
For if I am defeated by a fall, 
My wish to vanquish the thr ee realms will become a joke. 
 
One with a firm mind,  refers to someone who has cultivated  very strong antidotes to his own 
negative emotions. Such people are able to  puri fy whatever negativities they have accumulated 
and they are not harmed by downfalls; they don’t take rebirth in the lower realms. We need to 
cultivate this pride in our own ability to over come all downfalls. When the reverse happens, 
instead of us overcoming the downfalls, the downfalls overwhelm us. Then our wish to achieve 
the state of full enlightenment and complete vict ory over all the objects of abandonment of the 
three realms becomes a mere joke. 
Lesson 28   Chapter VII - verse 55-60 
Despite our busy working lives and feeling tired at the end of the day, we are still able to put 
aside time to attend Dharma classes. Since we ar e here, we should spend this time meaningfully 
by listening attentively to the teachings. Even if we cannot absorb everything taught in class, we 
will be able to find something that is beneficial  and helpful for us. We can then try to put that 
into practice.  
 
We listen to the teachings and practise the Dhar ma not for achieving just the happiness of this 
life. What is more important is the happiness of our future lives, which can be accomplished by 
practising the Dharma and engaging in virtue. Th ere is no other way of benefiting our future 
lives.   We need to attend class with this  understandin g that we are working to secure the happiness of 
our future lives. This is the motivation we should  generate for our attendance. When we listen to 
the teachings, we plant imprints in our mental continua. We should familiarise ourselves with 
what we have understood so that we can improve from life to life until the completion of our 
practices. Securing successive good human rebirt hs in our future lives, our minds are naturally 
more virtuous and we are more good-hearted. Havi ng placed the imprints in this life, whatever 
practice we do in our future lives will also beco me much easier. Obviously, the more attentively 
we listen to the teachings, th e more stable and powerful our mental imprints will be.   
 In order to cultivate joyous effort, we have to ov ercome the three forms of laziness. With laziness 
no Dharma gets done. The reflection on deat h and impermanence, base d on the 9-point death 
meditation mentioned in the Lam Rim texts,  is the best antidote. When this reflection is not 
done well, we can forget about Dharma practice as  it is almost impossible to develop any form of 
joyous effort.    
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
152  
 
The way to develop the aspiration for the Dharma is to reflect on karma (the law of cause and 
effect). The Lam Rim mentions that  the faith of conviction in karm a is the root of all excellent 
things. When we generate this faith in our minds,  naturally, from our hearts, we will aspire for 
virtue. We also need to generate courage an d determination by thinking, “I can and I will 
succeed in my practice,” rather than giving in to  thoughts of self-defeatism: “This is too difficult 
for and is beyond me.” We need to give up th e laziness of despondency by thinking, “If others 
can do it, so can I.” 
As mentioned in this chapter on developing jo yous effort, we need to  abandon the  factors 
incongruous with joyous effort, i.e. we need to abandon the three forms of laziness. We need to 
cultivate the favourable conditions for developing joyous effort and that is achieved through the 
generation of the four  powers. We have covere d the first power, the power of aspiration. Now 
we are looking at the second power, the power of stability.  When we do not apply the relevant 
antidotes, we will be stained by even the  smalle st fault, which may cause us to fall into the lower 
realms.

(iii) Abiding in pride as an antidote  
(7, 55) 
I will conquer everything 
And nothing at all shall conquer me! 
I, a child of the Lion-like Conqueror, 
Should remain self-confident in this way. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
153  
 
I will conquer everything and no thing at all shall conquer me!  means that we will conquer all the 
objects of abandonment of the three realms. The “I” here refers to a child of the Conquerors. 
The text mentioned earlier that pride is something to be abandoned, but now it says that pride is 
something to be cultivated. We will find the answe r to this apparent contradiction in the next 
verse. 
(iv) It is unreasonable to be proud of the afflictions 
(a') Decrying pride in the afflictions  
(b') The disadvantages of that  
(c') The reasonableness of abandoning it  
(a') Decrying pride in the afflictions   
(7, 56) 
Whoever has self-importance is destroyed by it, 
Is disturbed and has no self-confidence. 
For those with self-confidence do not succumb to the power of the enemy, 
Whereas the former are under the sway  of the enemy of self-importance. 
 
We need to differentiate between the pride of one’s capacity (or the pride of ability) and afflictive 
pride. When bodhisattvas generate the pride of abilit y, thinking, “I can do this,” it only serves to 
increase their respect for others, their determ ination and happiness.  We have to abandon 
afflictive pride because it causes us to disrespect others and only gives rise to suffering. When we rely on afflictive pride, we are controlled by afflictions.    
(b') The disadvantages of that  
(7, 57) 
Inflated by the disturbing conception of my self-importance, 
I shall be led by it to the lower realms. 
It destroys the joyous festival of being human. 
I shall become a slave, eating the food of others, 
 
(7, 58)  
Stupid, ugly, feeble and everywhere disrespected. 
 Pride causes rebirth in the lower realms. Even when reborn as humans, we will experience 
unhappiness  as we will be in lowly positions, such as being  servants and the like, not knowing 
what to abandon and cultivate ( stupid ), with unpleasant physical appearances, being weak ( feeble ) 
and disrespected everywhere. Despite not having done  anything to hurt or offend others, we will 
be bullied or looked down upon by others.  
 
Question by student : We see different words like “self-impo rtance,” “self-confidence” and “divine 
pride” in the English translation. Is it the same in Tibetan? Is the “pride about ability” the same 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
154  
as “divine pride”? 
Reply:  In Tibetan, the root word for “pride” is the same, which is “nga rgyal.” For “divine pride,” 
we just add one more word “ha”, making it "ha ng a rgyal.” “Pride of ability” is not the same as 
“divine pride.” After bodhisattvas  start a particular  task, they cultivate the three kinds of pride by 
generating the determination to complete the ta sk. But when we engage in deity yoga, on the 
basis of visualising ourselves as  deities, we labe l, “This is me.” We do not identify ourselves as 
deities with the ordinary “I”. “Divine pride” is generated to overcome ordinary appearance. This 
is the way of meditating on oneself as a deity.  
(c') The reasonableness of abandoning it  
(7, 58)  
Tough people bloated by conceit 
Are also counted amon g the self-important; 
Tell me, what is more pathetic than this? 
 
Pride, which is a puffing up of the mind, prev ents us from practising the Dharma. This is 
something to be abandoned and is not included in  the pride of ability. Otherwise, there would 
be no one that we could point to  as a ‘lowly’ person who could no t cultivate the pride of ability. 
In other words, if we posit that afflictive pride is included in the pride of ab ility, then all sentient 
beings would have the same ability as the bodh isattvas to cultivate the pride of ability.  
(v) The benefits of pride as an antidote 
(7, 59) 
Whoever seizes self-confidence in order to  conquer the enemy of self-importance, 
They are the self-confident on es, the victorious heroes, 
And in addition, whoever definitely conquers the spread of this enemy, self-importance, 
Completely (wins) the fruit of a Conquero r, fulfilling the wishes of the world. 
 It is this pride of ability that enables bodhisattvas  to be victorious over the enemy and  afflictive 
pride. Also, it earns them the title, “heroes” due to their destruction of this affliction from the 
root, thereby attaining the state of full enlightenmen t, through which they ca n fulfil the wishes of 
limitless sentient beings.  
(c) Being proud of the afflictions 
(i) One should develop the strength of the antidotes to the afflictions 
(ii) One should act so as not to fall under their power even in the slightest 
(iii) One should develop a special intention to stabilize the antidotes 
(i) One should develop the strength of the antidotes to the afflictions 
(7, 60) 
If I find myself amidst a crow d of disturbing conceptions, 
I shall endure them in a thousand ways; 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
155  
Like a lion among foxes, 
I will not be affected by this disturbing host. 
 
When we find ourselves amid the objects that are the causes for the three mental poisons (anger, 
attachment, and ignorance) to arise, we need to cultivate the antidotes in a stronger way than we 
normally would: in a thousand ways.  Just as lions cannot be harmed  by foxes, we should ensure 
that we are not affected by the hosts of afflic tions through cultivating very strong antidotes 
against them.  
 We can apply different types of antidotes when we are surrounded by the objects of anger or 
desire. The best practitioners can transform ad verse conditions into the path. The middling 
practitioners should apply the relevant antidote s while remaining where they are and, by so 
doing, they can remain unaffected by these obje cts. The novice practitioners should distance 
themselves from the objects. This advice pertains  to how we should deal with different kinds of 
objects to ensure we will not be affected by them.

b' The power of stability 
1" Stable effort

(ii) One should act so as not to fall under their power even in the slightest  
(7, 61) 
Just as men will guard their eyes 
When great danger and turmoil occur, 
Likewise, I shall never be swayed by  the disturbances within my mind, 
Even at times of great strife. 
 When we are in great danger, we protect our eyes. Similarly, when afflictions such as attachment 
a n d  a n g e r  a r i s e  i n  o u r  m i n d s ,  w e  s h o u l d  e n s u r e  t h a t  w e  a r e  n o t  c o n t r o l l e d  b y  t h e m  i n  t h e  slightest way.

Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
156  
(7, 62)52 
It would be better fo r me to be burned, 
To have my head cut off and to be killed, 
Rather then ever bowing down 
To those ever-present disturbing conceptions. 
(So likewise, in all situations, I should  do nothing other than what is fit.)53 
 
This verse is saying that regardless of how bad or  difficult the situation we may find ourselves in 
is, it is better for us to endure the physical hard ships than to give in to our negative emotions. 
The worst that can happen is that we will be killed but our killer will not be able to send us to 
the lower realms. However, if we give in to ou r negative emotions, we will definitely find 
ourselves there. The suffering there is far worse and more unbearable than the worst suffering we 
will ever encounter in cyclic existence. This was already explained in earlier lessons when we 
looked at the karma of engaging in negative acti ons and the resultant rebirth in the hell realms.  
 The essence of verses 60 - 62 is  t he importance of ensuring that we do not allow ourselves to 
succumb to our negative emotions, by cultivatin g very strong antidotes against them and not 
engaging in any inappropriate or improper conduct.

1" Making effort in virtue without considering the fully ripened effect  
(7, 63) 
                                              
52 Footnote of root text, pg. 174 
 
53 These two lines occur in some editions of the text, but it is not certain whether they are the actual words of 
Shantideva. See Thog. p. 148 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
157  
Just like those who yearn for the fruits of play, 
(Bodhisattvas) are attracted 
To whatever task they may do; 
They never have enough, it  only brings them joy. 
 
When children are engrossed in playing, they ar e only concerned with th e happiness that comes 
from playing. They don’t think about future happine ss. They don’t get tired; they just wish for 
the playtime to never end.   In the same way, when the bodhisattvas are enga ged in bodhisattva conduc t - the practice of the 
six perfections - they do not expect anything in  return nor are they concerned about the future 
fruitional effects of their actions.  Th ey simply focus on their practices.  
 When bodhisattvas are working to benefit others , they do so happily with great delight and 
enthusiasm, just like children at  play. Just as children do not tire of playing, so bodhisattvas 
never tire of working for others. They engage in their practices continuously. 
 In the same way, when we are working to benefi t others, we should have  no expectations of 
receiving anything  in return nor should we look forward to the positive fruitional results that await us in the future.  Like children playing, we should work to benefit others with great delight 
and enthusiasm, without distraction or tiredness.
54  
2" Accomplishing virtue by mentally engaging the effect  
(7, 64) 
Although people work in order to be happy, 
It is uncertain whether or not they will find it; 
But how can those whose work itself is joy 
Find happiness unless they do it? 
 
(7, 65) 
If I feel that I never have  enough sensual objects, 
Which are like honey smeared upon a razor's edge, 
Then why should I ever feel that I have enough 
Merit which ripens in happiness and peace? 
 There is no certainty as to whether the worldly activities we engage in to achieve happiness – 
                                             
 
54 So strive with an attitude like that of children who enga ge in play without being satiated. That is, you must be 
just as insatiable about what causes pleasurable results as you are about the results themselves. For, if ordinary 
persons strive even when they are unce rtain whether they will obtain a pleasant  result, what need is there to speak 
about activities which are certain to bear pleasurable result? (Lamrim Chenmo ., Vol. 2, pg. 200) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
158  
going to work, doing business, etc. -  will brin g us the happiness we seek. On the other hand, the 
Bodhisattvas practice of the six perfections is gu aranteed to yield positive results and happiness. 
We want that kind of happiness yet we are not prepared to engage in its cause. How on earth 
then can we expect to find happiness in this and our future lives?  We are very attached to the five sense objects of  desire. When these objects contact the respective 
consciousnesses, we experience and become atta ched to these minute moments of pleasure. We 
begin to crave for them and yet we are never satiated  by these pleasures. It is like drinking salt 
water, which can never quench our thirst.   Licking honey that is smeared upon a razor’s edge is very dangerous as we could cut our tongues but the resultant pleasure is minimal. In the same  way, the activity of enjoying sense objects is 
fraught with danger, yet it brings limited conten tment.  The more attached we are to them, the 
greater control our attachment has over our minds.  Once we are controlled by our afflictions, we 
will definitely go to the lower realms. Therefore, is there greater danger or profit from indulging 
in the five objects of desire?  The collection of merit and wisdom, on the other hand, is the cause for us  to achieve the higher 
rebirth states of humans and gods and the goals of  liberation and full enlightenment. Working to 
accumulate merit and wisdom only benefits us, so we  should engage in these activities as much as 
we can.    In summary, we should develop contentment toward s those activities that are highly dangerous 
and bring only limited benefit. But, we should never be satisfied by how much we engage in 
virtuous activities that are highly beneficial and safe for us.   
Reflecting on these different points helps us to  keep our minds enthusiastic and delighted when 
we are engaging in virtue. This is the power of joy.

3" The way to apply oneself with the power of joy 
(7, 66) 
Thus in order to complete this task, 
I shall venture into it 
Just as an elephant torm ented by the mid-day sun 
Plunges into a (cool, refreshing) lake. 
                                              
55 Develop an attitude of being insatiable, thinking, “Indul ging in sensual pleasure is like licking honey off the 
sharp blade of a razor; it is the source of a little sweetness,  but it slices up the tongue. If I cannot get enough of this 
experience, which gives me great suffering for the sake of just a slight, temporary plea sure, what sense could there 
be in feeling that I have had enough of the collections of merit and sublime wisdom, which give flawless, infinite 
happiness, both immediate and long-term?” (Lamrim Chenmo , Vol.2,pg.200)

When bodhisattvas are working to complete thei r virtuous activities to benefit others they 
generate enthusiasm and take delight in the activi ties, like the elephant who is tormented by heat 
plunging happily into a pool of water. Just as the elephant suffers when it cannot find a lake to 
cool off in, so bodhisattvas suffer when they are unable to find any opportunity to benefit others. 
W h e n  w e  e n g a g e  i n  v i r t u o u s  a c t i v i t i e s  t o  b e n e f i t others, we should do so enthusiastically and 
happily like the bodhisattvas.  
d' The power of suspension   
1" Periodic suspension  
2" Total suspension  
1" Periodic suspension  
(7, 67) 
When my strength declines, I should leave whatever I am doing 
In order to be able to continue with it later. 
 
We should always complete any virtuous activity th at we have started. However, if along the way, 
we fall ill or become extremely worn out and ou r physical condition no longer allows us to 
continue, we should rest awhile thinking, “I am  only resting to regain my physical strength. 
When I have recovered, I will co mplete the task at hand.”   
The determination and thought to continue the task once we have recovered is extremely 
important. Therefore, this is called periodic  suspension or temporary relinquishment.  
2" Total suspension  
(7, 67)  
Having done something well, I should put it aside 
With the wish (to accomplish) what will follow. 
 
In order to achieve the state of full enlightenmen t, we need to engage in and accomplish many 
virtuous activities and practices. Having comple ted a specific practice well, we should aim to 
engage in more advanced practices and not be complacent or be content with what we have 
accomplished. In order to start the new advanced practices, we have to relinquish the preceding 
activities that we completed. 56 
                                              
56 Developing the power of relinquishment:  If you be c o m e  p h y s i c a l l y  o r  m e n t a l l y  f a t i g u e d  f r o m  y o u r  
perseverance, you must rest for a while. Otherwise, yo u will become exhausted and very disheartened, thereby 
later preventing your joyous persev erance. Immediately after you have re sted, persevere again, and when you 
have completely finished your earlier activity, do not let this satisfy you. You must jo yously persevere at other, 
higher activities. The next task is important, because if  you consider each earlier g ood quality sufficient, this 
will be a big obstacle to the attainment of many higher attributes. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 201)

ii) Devoting oneself to the practice of the conduct by means of mindfulness and alertness 
1' Devoting oneself to conscientiousness 
2' Devoting oneself to mindfulness and alertness 
3' Not distinguishing a time of wrongdoing for mindfulness and alertness

1' Devoting oneself to conscientiousness 
(7, 68) 
Just as an old warrior approaches 
The swords of an enemy upon the battlefront, 
So shall I avoid the weapons of  the disturbing conceptions 
And skilfully bind this enemy. 
 
When an experienced warrior goes to battle and sees the enemy approaching, he looks out for 
the enemies’ weapons and plans how to avoid be ing hit by the enemy. At the same time, he 
thinks of ways to destroy the en emy. In the same way,  we should check if  the weapons of our 
afflictions are harming our minds an d think of how to uproot them.57 
2' Devoting oneself to mindfulness and alertness 
(7, 69) 
If someone dropped a sword during a battle, 
They would immediately pick it up out of fear. 
Likewise, if I lose the weapon of mindfulness, 
I should quickly retrieve it, being afraid of hell. 
 
When someone engaged in battle dropped his sword, he would immediately pick it up out of fear. Our sword is related to our mindfulness, the mindfulness of not forgetting the chosen 
object of virtue. In the same way when we lose the mindfulness of the virtuous object, we come 
under the control of the negative emotions and we experience all kinds of suffering and go to the 
lower realms. By remembering this and generati ng fear we need to conscientiously apply 
                                             
 
57 For example, when seasoned warriors – the adepts who ar e accustomed to the activity of fighting battles – enter 
into a sword fight, etc., with their en emy, they do not put value only on de stroying their opponent. Rather, they 
must accomplish two things – skilfull y avoiding the blows of weapons directed at them and destroying their 
opponent. Likewise, when practitioners battle their afflicti ons, they must persevere as they become proficient in 
two things – taking defensive precautions and thus avoi ding a wound to their mind, and, on the offensive, 
destroying the afflictions by applying their remedies. For,  otherwise, while they may use the remedy to stop the 
activity of one portion of the afflictions,  they are either robbed of some aspect of virtue by other afflictions, or else 
they develop a great fault in their mind so that the harm of  the afflictions and the creation of virtue are equal, in 
w h i c h  c a s e  i t  w i l l  b e  h a r d  t o  m a k e  f u r t h e r  p r o g r e s s  i n  t h e  v i r t u o u s  p r a c t i c e  o f  a p p l y i n g  t h e  r e m e d y .  ( Lamrim 
Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 202)

mindfulness.58 
3' Not distinguishing a time of wrongdoing for mindfulness and alertness  
(7, 70) 
Just as poison spreads throughout the body 
In dependence upon the (circulation of) blood. 
Likewise, if (a disturbing conc eption) finds an opportunity, 
Unwholesomeness will permeate my mind. 
(7,71) 
Those who practice should be as attentive 
As a frightened man carrying a jar full of mustard oil, 
Who is being threatened by someone with a sword, 
(And told) that he will be kille d if he spills just one drop. 
 
We usually think in this way: “When a small delusi on arises in my mind, it is not serious as it 
cannot harm me so there is no need for me to put in the effort to overcome  it.”  This is not a 
correct way of thinking; the reason is in verse 70 : When we are injected with poison, it spreads 
throughout our bodies via the bloodstr eam and puts us in grave danger.   
 Even the slightest distraction can fill our minds with different kinds of problems. When our minds are clouded by these mental poisons, our pa th to a good rebirth is completely blocked and 
we go to the lower realms. Therefore, as mentione d in verse 70, we must stop the arising of even 
the smallest affliction in our minds at once.  
                                             
 
 
58 Furthermore, regarding the object to which mindfulne ss attends, mindfulness apprehends an object that 
wisdom has fully discerned; mi ndfulness does not distinguish its object on its own.  
Question : What does wisdom discern? 
Reply:  I n  g e n e r a l  w i s d o m  d i s c e r n s  e v e r y t h i n g  t o  b e  a d o p t e d and everything to be cast aside as explained in 
scripture; in particular, it discerns what is to be adopted and what is to be cast aside according to the vows that you 
have taken. Therefore, once you apply mindfulness and vigilance to these, you will complete your practice; you will 
not be successful by just applying mindfulness and vigilance within the narrow confines of attention to an object of 
meditation. 
What is more, when warriors are in a battle, they strive from the beginning not to lose their sword; when by 
chance they do drop it, they pick it up immediately. Th ese two actions rest on a fear of being killed that is not 
mere words. Likewise, those who cultivat e the path are afraid to lose the mind fulness that does not forget what is 
to be adopted and what eliminated; even if they do lo se it, they immediately reapply it. These two actions are 
based on the development in their minds of a real terror  of falling into miserable realms as a result of the 
pollution of infractions and faults that occur when mindfulness lapses. This, in turn, depends on having made 
karma and its effects central to their practice and then sustaining that a pproach. Those who fa il to develop the 
awareness that these points are profou nd instructions sever the root of the good qualities ensuing from the 
practice that delights the learned, the sacred foundati on of the path. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 203) 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
162  
 
We can use this analogy to motivate ourselves: im agine we are forced to carry a jar full of mustard 
oil on our backs down an uneven and slippery path. We are told that we will be killed if we spill 
a drop of this oil. In this situation, our minds will be very focussed and we will be very careful 
indeed.   
 Bodhisattvas protect their vows by relying on mindfulness, aler tness and conscientiousness and 
remembering the suffering of the lower realms and cyclic existence. We should do likewise to 
protect the vows we have taken.  
4' One should overcome wrongdoing as soon as it arises 
 
(7, 72) 
Just as I would swiftly stand up 
If a snake came into my lap, 
Likewise, if any sleep or laziness occurs, 
I shall quickly turn it back. 
 
(7,73) 
Each time something unwholesome occurs 
I should criticize myself, 
And then contemplate for a long time 
That I shall never let this happen again. 
 
Just as I would swiftly stand up, if a snake came into my lap : When laziness suddenly strikes, by 
reflecting on death and impermanence and reme mbering the suffering of the hell realms, we 
should immediately engage in virtue.  
 The point is to stop any kind of wrongdoing i mmediately by generating the thought, “I am not 
going to do this again.” How do we do this? We’v e encountered all sorts of undesirable situations 
due to  our past negativities. By reflecting on this and generating regret for our past negative 
actions, when we find ourselves repeating th ese actions we should reprimand ourselves and 
abstain from them by applying the four opponent powers, as they will harm us in this and our future lives. 
59 
                                              
59 Furthermore, do not merely discontinue faults, but actively feel displeasure at their having occurred. 
Contemplate as follows, "Because I proc eeded in this way in the past as we ll, I have been wandering in cyclic 
existence up to now. In particular, it is especially blameworthy that I have taken the bodhisattva vows and yet con-
tinue with things that are incompatible with the vows' prec epts." Become inspired to henceforth restrain yourself, 
thinking, "From now on I shall make sure that this fault never occurs." Frequently employ both these attitudes. 
(Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2,pg.204) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                   CHAPTER VII - JOYOUS EFFORT  
 
 
163  
5' One should make effort in reasonable activities  
(7, 74) 
"Likewise in all these situations 
I shall acquaint myself with mindfulness." 
With this (motivation) as a cause, I shall aspire 
To meet (with teachers) or accomp lish the tasks (they assign me). 
 
By depending on conscientiousness, mindfulness and alertness, we prevent the arising of afflictions  in our minds and we aspire to meet  with teachers who can show us the unmistaken 
path. Even if we are unable to meet such spir itual masters, we should continue to practise 
whatever instructions or precepts we have rece ived by depending on mindfulness and alertness. 
We should engage in whatever is appropriate and abandon inappropriate actions.

iii) Accomplishing the conduct is within your power 
1' Through pliancy of body and mind, one should even arise in virtue  
2' Applying the analogy to the meaning  
1' Through pliancy of body and mind, one should even arise in virtue  
(7, 75) 
In order to have strength for everything, 
I should recall before undertaking any action 
The advice in (the chapter on) conscientiousness, 
And then joyfully rise (to the task). 
 
B e f o r e  e n g a g i n g  i n  a n d  i n  o r d e r  t o  c o m p l e t e  a n y virtuous activities, we need to generate the 
power of joyous effort. We need conscienti ousness to do this. In order to develop 
conscientiousness, we need to listen to and remember well the instructions for developing it. 
When we engage in virtue, we should be able to do so easily, at any time. Pliancy , refers to both 
physical and mental pliancy.61 
                                              
60 Strive at any deep causes that give rise to the continuous, powerful mindfulness that is the root of this practice. 
Rely on such activities as keeping the company of excellent teachers and excellent companions, and broad 
learning, which are the causes of this powerful mindfulness. In this vein,  Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds states: 
"In any of these situations 
I will practice mindfulness." 
With this motive aspire to meet teachers And engage in appropriate activities.  
In summary, you must study and discern well what bodhisattva training requires you to adopt and to cast aside, and then joyously persevere at continuo usly being mindful in all your conduct of what you have understood about 
what to adopt and what to cast aside. Hence, it is extr emely important not to err about that at which you are to 
persevere. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 205)  
 
61 The method for using joyous perseverance to make the mind and body serviceable is the power of mastery.
