2' Applying the analogy to the meaning  
(7, 76) 
Just as the wind blowing back and forth 
Controls (the movement of ) a piece of cotton, 
So shall I be controlled by joy, 
And in this way accomplish everything 
 
Just as the wind blowing back and forth, controls (the movement of) a piece of cotton :  
Due to the power of enthusiasm for virtue, we will be able to employ our bodies, speech and 
minds to accomplish any virtue we like, just like  the piece of cotton drif ting wherever the wind 
blows.  
b. The chapter's name 
The name of the chapter is Teaching Joyous Effort 
                                                                                                                                                           
Shantideva sets forth in the chapter on conscientiousness [in his Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds  the necessity of 
learning the bodhisattva trainings; th e extremely grave consequences if you do not train in these once you have 
pledged to do so; how to regard the afflictions as the enem y; and the ways to generate the courage that looks upon 
the hardship of battling the afflictions as an ornament rath er than as a burden. Meditate  on these before taking up 
the task of training in the bodhisattva deeds. Thus sto pping all the inhibitions that prevent you from using your 
body and mind for virtuous activity, rise gladly to that task. ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 206)

165  
The Eighth Chapter: Teachi ng Absorbtion (Concentration) 
Lesson 30   Chapter VIII - verse 1-3 
In The Placements of Mindfulness , Buddha said that laziness is one of the bases for the generation 
of all our mental afflictions. Anyone who has la ziness in their  mental continuum will find it 
d i f f i c u l t  t o  e n g a g e  i n  v i r t u e .  I n  o r d e r  t o  o v e r c o m e  l a z i n e s s ,  w e  h a v e  t o  r e l y  o n  j o y o u s  e f f o r t .   
Therefore, we should strive to develop it.   To develop joyous effort in our minds, we have to eliminate its obstructin g factors by relying on 
the favourable conditions for generating this qual ity. The three forms of laziness are identified to 
be incongruous with the generation of joyous effort. We also need to generate the four powers that are conducive conditions for its development.     What are some of the obstacles that prevent us from developing joyous effort? We understand 
the need to practise Dharma and we know that we  can practise but nothing gets done at the end 
of the day. Why does this happen? We procrastin ate and postpone the practice to some time in 
the future, thinking, “I still have time and I will  do it later.” We are completely overwhelmed by 
our attachment to ignoble activities. Due to our low self-esteem, we think, “I can’t do this” and 
become discouraged.   In order to overcome the laziness of procrastination,  thinking, “I still have time and I will do it 
later”, we reflect on how our bodies are disinteg rating quickly as we mo ve towards death. When 
death occurs, due to our failure to engage in any be neficial or positive actions, we will fall into 
the lower realms. We should also remember how difficu lt it is to obtain this human life of leisure 
and opportunities.   To overcome the laziness of attachment to igno ble activities, we should reflect on how the 
practice of the holy Dharma is the source of  happiness in this and our future lives. The 
meaningless pursuit of reputation and worldly go als will only cause our virtue to degenerate. 
Relying on worldly activities will only generate more suffering for us.   The other obstacle that prevents us from developing  joyous effort is the th ought, “I can’t do this. 
It is beyond me.” To overcome this, there are three different antidotes.  Some people become discouraged, thinking, “The Buddha was an exceptionally capable person. How can I ever hope to achieve his limitless qualit ies?” In this instance, we should reflect on how 
the Buddha attained buddhahood. In the beginning, the Buddha was like us and he did not have 
a n y  s p e c i a l  a d v a n t a g e .  B u t  h e  w o r k e d  v e r y  h a r d  a n d  i m p r o v e d  h i m s e l f  f r o m  l i f e  t o  l i f e  t i l l  h e  finally attained enlightenment. All the buddhas of  the past were once ordinary beings like us. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
166  
Buddha points out that if inferior beings such as animals and insects ca n achieve enlightenment, 
then obviously we can achieve enlightenment if we are willing to work hard for it. 
 
Others are discouraged at the thou ght of the extensive practices of  the bodhisattvas such as the 
sacrifice of one’s limbs, in order to achieve en lightenment.  But the Buddha never expects us to 
make such sacrifices. In fact, he  stipulates we should not do so until we have perfected our 
practice of giving - when giving away our bodies  would mean no more to us than giving away a 
plate of food.  We will not experien ce any difficulties then. By reflecting like this, we will be able 
to overcome this form of discouragement.  
 Yet, there are still others who are discouraged by the thought of how the bodhisattvas have to 
take rebirth repeatedly in cyclic existence and su ffer there in order to benefit sentient beings. But 
when the superior bodhisattvas (who have achiev ed the direct realisation of emptiness and have 
abandoned all their afflictions) take rebirth in cyclic existence, they do not experience any 
physical suffering. Because of their direct percepti on of emptiness, all samsaric sufferings appear 
illusory to them and they do not experien ce any mental unhappiness. These superior 
bodhisattvas are, therefore, both  physically and mentally happy wh en they are abiding in cyclic 
existence. Again, there ar e no grounds for this form of discouragement.  
 By depending on the various antidotes, we  can overcome all the different forms of 
discouragement.   We also have to cultivate the fo ur powers conducive to the develo pment of joyous effort. We rely 
on the power of aspiration to generate joyous effo rt for the first time. Then, we rely on the power 
of stability (or steadfastness) to prevent this joyous effort from degenerating, rendering it 
irreversible. When engaging in virtuous work, we should do so with grea t delight and enthusiasm 
by cultivating the power of joy, which is like th e joy of a child completely engrossed in play.   
 Having developed joyous effort, we also have to be skilful in its application. In the process of 
cultivating virtue, we may overtax ourselves and our health may deteriorate. We then need to 
cultivate the power of relinquishment and su spend our activities, either temporarily or 
completely.   Some texts mention two additional powers that are also included in the root text though they are 
not explicitly named. These are the power of ea rnest application and the power of mastery. We 
need to generate very powerful antidotes to over come our negative emotions. In order to do this, 
we have to generate the power of  earnest application where we a pply ourselves to the cultivation 
of mindfulness and vigilance. Through such applic ation, we gain mastery (or control) over our 
bodies and minds, which can then be employed  for virtue as and when we wish. Negative 
emotions are easily subdued. Th is is the power of mastery. 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
167  
Developing joyous effort makes it easier to ac complish calm abiding. On the basis of calm 
abiding, we can then cultivate special insight fo cusing on emptiness. This becomes the direct 
antidote to our negative emotions, which can be removed from the root. This will be explained 
in the chapter on wisdom. We need joyous effo rt to develop calm abiding and we need calm 
abiding to generate special insight. Hence, we  have this chapter on teaching absorption.  
 
3. Explaining the way to train in the remaining four perfections 
A. The way to train in patience 
B. The way to train in joyous effort 
C. The way to train in the concentrations included in the common and uncommon calm 
abidings  
a Explaining the chapter's material 
1)  Instructions on cultivating concentration 
a) The reasons it is necessary to accomplish calm abiding 
b) Instructions on abandoning the class of things incongruous with 
calm abiding 
2)  Abandoning the class of (discordant) things incongruous with calm abiding 
a) Abandoning the bustle of the world 
i)  Identifying the causes of attachment to the world  ii) The way to abandon these 
b) Abandoning erroneous conceptions  
3)  How to cultivate calm abiding 
b The chapter's name 
D. The way to train in wisdom - the nature of insight 
 
Common calm abiding can also be achieved by non-Buddhists.  Uncommon calm abiding is cultivated by Buddhists and refers specifically to the type of calm abiding that leads to the 
generation of special insight. 
a) The reasons it is necessary to accomplish calm abiding 
(8,1) 
Having developed enthusiasm in this way, 
I should place my mind in concentration; 
For the person whose mind is distracted 
Dwells between the fangs of  disturbing conceptions. 
 
(8,2)  
However, through solitude of body and mind 
No distractions will occur; 
 
O n  t h e  b a s i s  o f  h a v i n g  d e v e l o p e d  j o y o u s  e f f o r t  by following the instruct ions in the previous 
chapter, we should now focus on developing the concentration that is free from the faults of 
excitement and laxity and is able to remain focu ssed on the chosen object of observation without 
distraction.  What are the disadvantages of not having this kind  of concentration? When we distracted by the 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
168  
different sense objects and our minds pursue them, this will give rise to afflictions such as 
attachment or anger, which destroy our virtue an d lead us to the lower realms and immeasurable 
suffering. A distracted mind is like living amid dangerous wild animals ready to devour us. This 
verse points out the need for physic al isolation and mental solitude. 
 
How do we overcome distractions while living in th e hustle and bustle of life? We have to engage 
in physical and mental isolation. Physical isol ation means abandoning the hustle and bustle of 
life and abandoning living with others. Mental  isolation means keeping the mind free from the 
disturbance of afflictions such as anger and attachment. 
b) Instructions on abandoning the class of things incongruous with calm abiding 
(8, 2) 
Therefore, I should forsake the worldly life, 
And completely discard distorted conceptions. 
 Physical solitude is achieved by abandoning th ose who are close to us, such as our friends, 
parents, loved ones and relatives and our attachme n t  t o  t h e  h u s t l e  a n d  b u s t l e  o f  l i f e .  M e n t a l  
isolation is achieved by distancing ourselves fr om attachment and anger (some texts also mention 
sleep and lethargy) and abandoning the objects of desire.  There are different levels of concentration: mund ane (worldly) and supramundane. It is very rare 
for one to achieve mundane concentration. To achieve enlightenment and supramundane 
concentration is even rarer. If we are dist racted, how can we hope to develop mundane 
concentration? And if it is so difficult to de velop mundane concentration, how can we achieve 
enlightenment?  Therefore, the Buddha mentions  in the sutras the importance of not being 
distracted.   
2)  Abandoning the class of things incongruous with calm abiding 
a) Abandoning the bustle of the world 
i)  Identifying the causes of attachment to the world  
ii) The way to abandon these 
b) Abandoning erroneous conceptions  
i)  Identifying the causes of attachment to the world  
(8,3) 
Worldly life is not forsaken because of attachment (to people), 
And due to craving for mate rial gain and the like; 
 
Worldly life is not forsaken because of our atta chment to the self and others, material gain, 
respect and honour. We have to abandon the atta chment that comes from this sense of “I” and 
“mine”, which leads on to attachment for othe rs and material gain, respect and honour and 
keeps us completely bound to the hustle and bustle of life.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
169  
Lesson 31   Chapter VIII - verse 3-11 
In the last lesson, we considered why we need to  cultivate calm abiding, where we generate the 
concentration that is able to place the mind single-pointedly on a chosen virtuous object. 
Without stable concentration, our minds will be  under the influence of laxity and excitement, 
which will weaken the virtue in our mental co ntinua. We become like someone surrounded by 
dangerous wild animals ready to pounce on and devour us.   
 If we are overcome by laxity, excitement and dist ractions, we will create negative karma, which 
will lead us to the lower realms. The way to stop this from happening is to strive for physical and 
mental solitude. The distractions come from the hu stle and bustle of  worldly life and the mind 
that is attached to worldly activities, due to  our attachment to the “self” and “mine”.  
 In this lesson, we identify the causes of attachment to different objects, such as sentient beings, wealth, honour and gain. These ar ise primarily from this attachme nt to the “self” and “mine”, 
from which follows the attachment to  worldly life. 
 
In order to eradicate all these forms of attach ment, we need special insight to help us to 
eliminate our self-grasping. The development of  special insight must be preceded by the 
development of calm abiding. In order to develo p calm abiding, we must give up all forms of 
distraction and in order to do this, we  must give up our worldly activities.

a' Instructions on abandoning attachment  
(8, 3 cd)  
Therefore I should entirely forsake these things, 
For this is the way in which the wise behave. 
 
The instructions on abandoning attachment are to abandon attachment to both inner (sentient 
beings) and outer phenomena. We are encouraged to reflect on the instructions of the great 
scholars who were expert in what is  to be abandoned and cultivated.  
b' Identifying the antidotes that act to abandon attachment  
(8, 4) 
Having understood that disturbing co nceptions are completely overcome 
By superior insight endowed with calm abiding, 
First of all, I should se arch for calm abiding. 
This is achieved through the genuine jo y of those unattached to worldly life. 
 Through habituation to the concentration that is able to place the mind single-pointedly on a 
c h o s e n  v i r t u o u s  o b j e c t  o f  o b s e r v a t i o n ,  w e  w i l l  d e f i n i t e l y  b e  a b l e  t o  o v e r c o m e  l a x i t y  a n d  excitement. This is done through the nine mental  states. We achieve calm abiding through these 
(nine mental states) as a preliminary. When we achieve the bliss of phys ical and mental pliancy 
we have reached fully qualified calm abiding.   When our analysis is engaged within such a state o f  c a l m  a b i d i n g ,  w e  w i l l  b e  a b l e  t o  i n d u c e  
pliancy; that is when we can achieve special insi ght - the tool that enables us to destroy all the 
afflictions of the 3 realms together with their seeds.  Without the special insight focusing on emptine ss, we cannot abandon self-grasping, attachment, 
etc. Prior to achieving this special insight we mu st achieve the realization of calm abiding.  To 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2

171  
achieve calm abiding, we must give up attachme nt to the external and internal worlds (i.e. 
sentient beings, our bodies and enjoyments). Then within this state of mind, we generate a mind 
aspiring to achieve this calm abiding. Attachment has to be abandoned; if not, our mind will fall 
under the influence of  laxity or excitement. 
 
2' The methods for developing the antidote 
a' Inner: Abandoning attachment to sentient beings  
1" The disadvantages of attachment 
a" Not encountering what one wants 
b" Lack of discrimination in regards to sensory objects 
c" Not being content despite the fact that one has obtained [what one sought] 
d" It acts to hinder liberation e" It causes one to waste the leisures and endowments  
a" Not encountering what one wants 
(8, 5) 
Because of the obsession one transient being 
Has for other transient beings, 
They will not see their beloved ones again 
For many thousands of lives. 
 
We ourselves and the objects of our attachment i. e. our loved ones are, by nature, impermanent 
and, therefore, not stable and everlasting. Therefor e, it is inappropriate for us to be attached to 
them. The problem with bein g attached to others is that we su ffer in this life and in the future 
for countless lifetimes. We will never meet with wh at we desire; we will be reborn in unpleasant 
places and will encounter unpleasant situations. Th ese are the fruitional effects of attachment.    
b" Lack of discrimination in regards to sensory objects   
(8, 6)  
Not seeing them , I am unhappy 
And my mind cannot be settled in equipoise; 
 This outline could refer to the host ility that is induced by the obje cts of desire. Wh en we see only 
unpleasant objects or encounter unpleasant situat ions, it gives rise to mental unhappiness, which 
causes our minds to be distracted and makes it  difficult to achieve the meditative equipoise 
required to develop calm abiding. When this outl ine becomes clearer, I will explain it again as to 
how these 2 lines go with the outline.  
c" Not being content despite the fact that one has obtained [what one sought]   
(8, 6 cd) 
Even if I see them, there is no satisfaction 
And, as before, I am tormented by craving. 
 Craving remains even when we meet people we like and suffering comes from this lack of 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
172  
satisfaction. The suffering we experience having  met such people is ex actly the same as the 
suffering we experience before meeting them. This is also presented in the Lamrim Chenmo  in the 
section on the eight types of suffering.62 
d" It acts to hinder liberation 
(8, 7) 
Through being attached to living beings, 
I am completely obscured from the perfect reality, 
My disillusionment (with cy clic existence) perishes 
And, in the end, I am  tortured by sorrow. 
 
“Being attached to living beings, I am comp letely obscured from the perfect reality ”: Attachment to people 
is a hindrance to our direct realisation of the na ture of reality (emptiness) and prevents us from 
being disheartened with cyclic existence. We destroy whatever  disillusionment with cyclic 
existence we may have developed with atta chment and we will not achieve liberation. 
Furthermore, it is only a matter of time before we will be separated fr om them and when that 
happens, we will be overcome by sorrow.   
e" It causes one to waste the leisures and endowments 
(8, 8) 
By thinking only of them, 
This life will pass without any meaning. 
 
Due to our attachment to our loved ones, we thin k of them all the time and end up wasting our 
                                              
62 (v) The suffering of encountering what is unpleasant 
1) Merely encountering your enemies, fo r example, causes pain and anguish.  
2) You dwell in fear of being punished by them.  
3) You fear they will speak mali ciously, without kind words.  
4) You dread death. 
5) You worry that you will go to a miserable realm after de ath for having acted in ways contrary to the teaching.  
 (vi) The suffering of separati on from what is pleasant  
Again, there are five points to cont emplate. When you are separated from a very dear relative, for example:  
1) In your mind, sorrow arises.  2) In your speech, you lament.  3) You bring harm to your own body. 4) You are saddened, recalling and missing the good qualities of what you have 5) Recourse is no longer avai lable for what you have lost.  
 
(vii) The suffering of not getting what you want: Again, th ere are five points, similar to separation from what is 
pleasant. Seeking but not getting what you want means, for example, farming a field but reaping no harvest, or 
engaging in business but receiving no profits. The pa in of disappointment comes from working hard to get 
something that you long for, and then not getting it. (Lamrim Chenmo, Vol. 1, pg. 278) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
173  
entire  lives without engaging in any virtue that wi ll benefit us in our future lives. We accumulate 
many negativities instead. 
  
We can look at attachment from the perspective of either: 
(1) the object of attachment (the objective aspect), or  (2) the person who is attached (the subjective aspect).   
W e  h a v e  b e e n  l o o k i n g  s p e c i f i c a l l y  a t  t h e  f a u l t s  o f  a t t a c h m e n t  t o  s e n t i e n t  b e i n g s   f r o m  t h e  
subjective aspect. We will now discuss the faul ts of attachment from the objective aspect.  
 
2" Abandoning them by knowing the disadvantages  
a" Reflecting on the disadvantages  
(1) An extensive explanation 
(a) It destroys what could otherwise be very meaningful and leads one to the 
lower realms 
(b) Do not rely on the company of ch ild[-like people] and the difficulty of 
doing so 
(c) One does not obtain any benefit from it but rather receives harm 
(a) It destroys what could otherwise be very meaningful and leads one to the lower realms 
(8, 8 cd) 
(Furthermore,) impermanent friends and relatives 
Will even destroy the Dharma (which leads to) permanent (liberation) 
 
(8, 9) 
If I behave in the same way as the childish, 
I shall certainly proceed to lower realms, 
And if 1 am led there by those unequal (to the Noble Ones), 
What is the use of entrusti ng myself to the childish? 
 
When we are attached to sentient beings who ar e by nature as unstable as rainbows, even the 
holy Dharma, which enables us to achieve the stat e of liberation, will be destroyed. The last two 
lines of verse 8 can be seen as the subjecti ve aspect of the faults of attachment.   
 
There are many different ways of interpreting the word, childish , depending on the context. It can 
refer to:  
(1) ordinary beings as opposed to superior beings,  (2) the foolish ones who are ignorant of what is  to be abandoned and cultivated as opposed to 
the wise ones who know this, or  (3) youngsters.  
Here, childish  refers to the second meaning. Those unequal (to the Noble Ones),  means those who 
are unwise. When our behaviour is childish and we engage in childish activities, we will go to the 
lower realms. An intelligent person does not a llow himself to follow after the childish. We are 
now discussing the faults of attachment from its objective aspect. What are the faults of 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
174  
associating with the foolish or childish? We will not accomplish our desires or goals and 
undesirable things will increase. Therefore, we must  not entrust ourselves to the childish.       
(b) Do not rely on the company of child[-like people] and the difficulty of doing so  
(8,10) 
One moment they are friends, 
And in the next instant they become enemies. 
Since they become angry ev en in joyful situations, 
It is difficult to please ordinary people. 
 
(8, 11) 
They are angry when something of benefit is said, 
And they also turn me away  from what is beneficial. 
If I do not listen to what they say, 
They become angry and henc e proceed to lower realms. 
 
The childish ones are those people  who are ignorant of what is to be abandoned and cultivated.  
In an instant, they can become our friends but at  the slightest change of behaviour or manner of 
speaking, they can instantly become our enemies.  When we advise these people on what is to be 
abandoned and cultivated, they do not li sten and become angry at us instead. 
 We may have a friend who possesses many good qualities and great wealth and possessions, for 
which we should rejoice. However, we are jealou s and unhappy about this instead. Therefore, it 
is difficult to please the childish. When we tr y to help them practise the Dharma, which will 
benefit them in this and their future lives, not on ly do they not listen, saying, “I have no time,” 
but they also become  angry at us. If we seriousl y want to practise the Dharma well and correctly, 
such childish people will create obstacles for us  with their remarks, “Why are you so serious? 
Why can’t you be like other people and be more  normal?” When we tell them not to say such 
things, they get angry and create the causes  for their rebirth in the lower realms.

3)  How to cultivate calm abiding 
(c) One does not obtain any benefit from it but rather receives harm   
(8,12) 
They are envious of superiors, competitive with equals, 
Arrogant towards inferiors, conceited when praised, 
And if anything unpleasant is said, they become angry. 
Never is any benefit derived from the childish. 
 
(8,13) 
Through associating with the childish, 
There will certainly ensue unwholesomeness, 
Such as praising myself, belittling others 
And discussing the joys of cyclic existence. 
 
The childish , refers to those who are not knowledgeable of what is to be abandoned and 
cultivated. The harms of associating with the childish are as follows: 
They are jealous of associates who are of hi gher status or have superior qualities  
They are competitive when amongst their peers an d often end up quarrelling and fighting with 
them  
They feel superior to those who have lower or inferior status to  themselves and develop pride   
Arrogance and conceit arise easily wh en they are slightly praised   
They become angry when their faults are pointed out.  What is the benefit of keeping comp any with such childish people?  
 As this verse points out, when the childish a ssociate with other childish people, they praise 
themselves, belittle others and only indulge in  meaningless chatter and gossip. These are non-
virtuous actions. The advice is that association wi th the childish is not beneficial and only results 
in the creation of negative actions. It is an entirely different matter when we associate with 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
176  
superior beings. 
(2) Synopsis 
(8, 14) 
Devoting myself to others in this way 
Will bring about nothing but misfortune, 
Because they will not benefit me, 
And I shall not benefit them. 
 
When we associate with the chil dish non-virtuous actions will increase and our virtue will 
degenerate. So, there is neither benefit to the ot her party nor ourselves; it will only lead to 
suffering in this and future lives for everyone concerned. 
b" The way to abandon them  
(8, 15) 
I should flee far away from childish people. 
When they are encountered, though, I should please them by being happy. 
I should behave well merely out of courtesy, 
But not become greatly familiar. 
 
(8,16) 
In the same way as a bee takes honey from a flower, 
I should take merely (what is necessary) for the practice of Dharma, 
But remain unfamiliar, 
As though I had neve r seen them before. 
 Our goals and the goals of others will not be fulf illed through association with the childish. Since 
association is not beneficial, we should di stance ourselves and go into isolation.  
 
This is a quotation from the great yogi Milarepa’s Collection of Songs :  
 
By staying in this mountain hermitage, even if I, called Milarepa, fall ill, 
No one will ask after my welfare, “How are you?” If I die in this mountain hermitage, ther e will be no one to cry and wail for me. 
If I, Milarepa, die in this mountain hermitag e, the wishes of the yogi will be fulfilled. 
 We cannot talk about helping to subdue the minds of others when our own minds are uncontrolled. Trying to help others when we are in this condition will not benefit them; likewise, 
the other party also cannot benefit us.   The teachings therefore encourage us to go into so litary retreat. In retreat, we can stabilise our 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
177  
practice through the attainment of realisations an d with these stable realisations we will then be 
able to really benefit others in a meaningful way. Otherwise, without the stable practice and 
realisations we acquire through isolated retrea t, we cannot benefit - we only end up harming 
others. Likewise, others will be unable to benefit us – they only harm us in return.  
 There is no greater benefit than staying alone in  retreat, working to subdue our own minds. In 
isolation, we do not harm others and we have the opportunity to subdue the mind. Going into 
isolation to do retreat is the best advice. Those who can do so are rare an d those who want to do 
so are also rare.  When we stay in isolation in the mountains, we m ay need to go into town occasionally to get our 
provisions; (Sangha go alms seeking.) We may meet  our friends or loved ones in town. If so, we 
should behave respectfully towards them, speak po litely and adopt a pleasant disposition.  We 
should not spend too much time wi th them and get caught up in their chatter. After getting our 
necessities, we should return to our retreat pl ace without feeling any attachment for what we 
have left behind, like bees extracting the essenc e from flowers. They take whatever is necessary 
and leave without remembering the shape or colour of the flower. We may even have a meal with our loved ones but we should leave that happy feel ing behind and return to the retreat place. It 
should be as if we had not seen them at all.   
Question from student : The earlier chapter on patience emphas ised treating others well and seeing 
them as being very precious. But here we are aske d not to associate with th e childish and go into 
solitary retreat. This seems to be contradictory. Geshe-la:  This is a question to which you have to find an answer. 
 
What we have been discussing so  far has been dealing with the fa ults of attachment to sentient 
beings from the perspective of the object of one’s  attachment. The faults are also presented from 
the perspective of the subject, the person  who is experiencing that attachment. 
 
2' The methods for developing the antidote 
a' Inner: Abandoning attachment to sentient beings  
b' Outer: Abandoning attachment to gain and honor 
1" The subject's faults 
a" The disadvantages of pride  
b" The disadvantages of  attachment 
2" The object's faults 
a" The disadvantages of pride  
(8, 17) 
"I have much material we alth as well as honor, 
And many people like me." 
Nurturing self-importance in this way 
I shall be made terrified after death. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
178  
 
I shall be made terrified after death,  refers to the fear of going to the lower realms after death. 
 
When we are proud of our material wealth an d honour, we become attached to them, which 
then becomes a condition for us to become conceite d and arrogant. This attitude will lead us to 
commit non-virtuous actions which, in turn, wi ll lead us to the lower realms after death. 
b" The disadvantages of  attachment 
(8,18) 
So, you thoroughly confused mind, 
By the piling up of whatever objects 
You are attached to, 
Misery a thousand fold will ensue. 
 
(8, 19) 
Hence the wise should not be attached, 
(Because) fear is born from attachment. 
 
Due to our thoroughly confused minds attaching to  and grasping at this “I” and “mine,” we are 
distracted by different kinds of objects of desi re and attachment. That attachment becomes very 
strong and the result can only be suffering. Even if the attachment is weak, it can still lead to 
powerful suffering results since it is one of the general characteristics of  karma to magnify. The 
wise, i.e. those who are knowledgeable about what  is to be abandoned and cultivated, are not 
attached to material gain or honour because they know they will suffer in this and future lives if 
they fall under the influence of attachment. 
Lesson 33   Chapter VIII - verse 19-28 
The main cause for rebirths in cyclic existence is the afflictions in our ment al continua making us 
create the karma that brings us back to samsara.  The only way to stop this from happening is to 
stop its cause i.e. to destroy the afflictions, which is achieved through special (or superior) 
insight, focused on emptiness, the true nature of reality.  
 We must achieve calm abiding prior to attain ing special insight. Although calm abiding 
suppresses manifest afflictions, this realisation alone cannot cut the root of samsara. Rebirth in 
the form and formless realms can be achieved thro ugh the attainment of ca lm abiding. But when 
the karma of these beings is exhausted, they have  to take rebirth again in one of the six realms. 
This is evidence that the realisation of calm abid ing alone is insufficient to cut the root of cyclic 
existence. Only calm abiding conjoined with spec ial insight can completely destroy the negative 
emotions in our minds.    
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
179  
We are now looking at ways to attain calm abiding.  The main obstacle is di stractions arising from 
anger or attachment. Different methods of dealin g with anger were explained in the chapter on 
patience.  
 
In the case of attachment, we are attached to  either sentient beings or gain and honour. 
Attachment to other sentient beings can be coun tered by contemplating the faults of attachment 
from the perspective of the object of our attachme nt and from the perspective of the subject, the 
person experiencing the attachment. This method can also be applied to attachment to material 
gain and honour.

a" Objects of desire are not reliable 
(8, 19 cd) 
With a firm mind understand well 
That it is the nature of th ese things to be discarded! 
 
(8, 20) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
180  
Although I may have much material wealth, 
Be famous and well-spoken of, 
Whatever fame and renown I have amassed 
Has no power to accomp any me (after death). 
 
Even if we get the things we desire, these object s of attachment are, by nature, impermanent and 
will have to be discarded in time. No matter ho w much wealth or fame we may have, they are 
unstable and cannot last for long before they disi ntegrate. We cannot take any of the good things 
of this life with us into our next life. Therefore, ther e is no reason to be attach ed to these things.     
b" There is neither benefit nor harm in praise and belittling  
(8,21) 
If there is someone who despises me, 
What pleasure can I have in being praised? 
And if there is another who praises me, 
What displeasure can I have in being despised? 
 We become attached to words of praise and beco me happy; we are angry when we are criticised. 
In either case, our minds are distracted by attachment or anger. This verse is advising us against these types of responses.   When we are praised, there is no justification fo r conceit and attachment because there is always 
someone who dislikes and criticises us. In the sa me way, there is no reason to be unhappy and 
angry when we are criticised because there is al ways someone who likes us. Whatever qualities we 
possess do not increase or decrease in dependence  on praise and criticism. For example, getting 
angry at  a rich man and calling him a beggar wi ll not make him poor nor will telling a beggar, 
“You are so rich,” make him so.  We need to understand that there is neither bene fit nor harm in receiving praise or criticism. 
Understanding this, we can weaken our attachment or anger when we receive praise or criticism.    
 
3' The disadvantages of bustle 
a' As it is difficult to keep company with child [-like people] do not be attached to their 
preoccupations and diversions [lit. bustle]  
b' Stating the proof for that 
a' As it is difficult to keep company with ch ild [-like people] do not be attached to their 
preoccupations and diversions [lit. bustle]  
(8, 22) 
If even the Conqueror was unable to please 
The various inclinations of different beings, 
Then what need to mention a malicious person such as I? 
Therefore, I should give up the inte ntion (to associate with) the worldly. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
181  
 
(8, 23) 
They scorn those who have no material gain 
And say bad things about those who do; 
How can they, who are by nature  so hard to get along with, 
Ever derive any pleasure (from me)? 
 
Qualm:  If I please everyone, no one will harm or  criticise me. I will then experience only 
happiness and when I am always happy, I can work to benefit others.  
 
Reply:  It is impossible to find two living beings with exactly the same kind of karma. Everyone’s 
karma is different. Due to this diversity of karma,  each sentient being obtains a different kind of 
rebirth and body with different dispositions an d interests, etc. If even the Buddha – the 
Omniscient One who knows exactly the diverse disp ositions, interests and mental abilities of all 
living beings – is unable to please everyone, what need is there to talk about ourselves who do 
not even know the dispositions or abilities of ou r long-time friends? Therefore, we should forsake 
any intention to associate with worldly or childi sh people and go into isolation to work on 
developing concentration.   ‘ T h e  c h i l d i s h ’  r e f e r s  t o  p e o p l e  w h o  d o  n o t  k n ow what to abandon and cultivate. When the 
childish see the destitute, they criticise them, sayi ng, “This is the result of  their bad karma. They 
did not do virtue. That is why they are in this st ate.” When they see rich people, they say, “They 
are so rich because of their illega l activities.” We should not associ ate with these difficult childish 
people. 
b' Stating the proof for that63 
(8, 24) 
It has been said by the Tathagatas 
That one should not be friend the childish 
Because, unless they get their own way, 
These children are never happy.30 
 
The Buddha advises us in various sutras not to befriend the childish as these people are never 
happy unless they get their own way. Gyal tsab Je’s commentary quotes the source64 as the Moon 
Lamp Sutra . 
 
When we say something beneficial to ordinary be ings, such as the Dharma, they are disinterested 
                                              
63 Footnote Text pg 174, 30 De-bzhin gsbegs-pa thams-cad kyi yul-la 'jug-pa, Thog. p. 157, 
'Just as wild animals are never happy in a crowd, there is no satisfaction in involvement with the childish.' 
 
64 There are also other sources such as  the King of Concentration Sutras.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
182  
and may even become angry with us. No matter how long our association with them has been, 
they are never happy with us and may even harm us. It is the nature of the childish to behave in 
this way. Therefore, the wise ne ver associate with the childish. 
The sutra says that, just as we are never happy when we are surrounded by many dangerous wild 
animals, so we are never happy as long as we a ssociate with the childish. It continues, “Those 
who are intelligent should never associate with th e childish who are ignora nt of what is to be 
abandoned and what is to be cultivated. Instead, th e wise rely on physical isolation to attain calm 
abiding and special insight.”  
 
Question from student : On the one hand, the text teaches us  how precious others are and how we 
must cherish them. But, on the other hand, it says  here that we have to abandon these childish 
people and go into isolation. Is this not a contradiction?  
 Geshe-la’s reply : When we are living with someone, can this person benefit our Dharma practice 
by contributing to the development of our minds?  Since we both have afflicted minds, it is 
unrealistic to expect any benefit from one another. Fo r most of us, it is very difficult to be of real 
benefit to others. We are like someone without ha nds, trying to pull out someone, who also has 
no hands, from quicksand. There is a danger of ourselves being pulled in and then both of us 
perishing. In the same way, when  we try to do help others, we may be drawn into their problems 
and, as a result, we suffer.   Until our qualities and attainments are stable, we will not be able to truly benefit others. 
Therefore, we must first develop ourselves. When  we are overwhelmed by the hustle and bustle 
of this life and all its distractions, we spend our whole lives engaging in meaningless activities. In 
the end, neither we nor others benefit.   The advice given in earlier chapters was to please  and benefit all sentient beings by remembering 
their kindness and developing and stabilising our minds to such an extent that we will never 
forsake sentient beings, regardless of whatever co nditions we meet. While doing so, we must keep 
in the depths of our heart, the motivation, “I mu st do something beneficial for others.” This is 
done on the mental rather than the physical level.  
 We recognise we will not accompli sh anything by being attached to the hustle and bustle of life. 
This being the case, we should rely on isolat ion, motivated by the thought never to forsake 
sentient beings and determined to accomplish the welfare of others.  
 There is no contradiction because we are encourag ed to go into isolation to develop our minds 
in order to benefit others, since we are Mahayana practitioners.  It would be wrong to go into 
isolation for our own benefit. When we are able to subdue our minds and develop the 
realisations of bodhicitta and emptiness by  depending on solitude, we then become a 
bodhisattva. When we reach this level, we are ad vised to come out of isolation and work for the 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
183  
benefit of others.  
 
4' The benefits of relying on isolation  
a' Companions 
b' Place 
c' A point on livelihood  
d' A point on discernment 
e' A point on non-distraction 
 
We need to be prepared to go into isolation. Be fore going into retreat, we must have heard many 
teachings on our meditation topic and, only af ter having gained correct understanding of the 
subject matter, do we then forsake association with  worldly people and go into solitary retreat. 
a' Companions 
(8, 25) 
When shall I come to dwell in forests 
Amongst the deer, the birds and the trees, 
That say nothing unpleasant, 
And are delightful to associate with? 
 
If we dwelt in the forest, our companions would be  the birds, deer, trees, etc.  These companions 
would never make us unhappy or say unpleasant thin gs to us. We would be delighted by this very 
pleasant environment.   It is very difficult to find such happiness in worl dly life with worldly people. Even if we do find 
some happiness from our friendships, these experi ences transform into suffering very quickly. 
How is it possible to experience any mental pe ace when we are caught up with praise and 
criticism? The mind can only remain constantly ha ppy if we dwell in physical isolation away from 
the bustle of life.  
b' Place 
 
(8, 26) 
When dwelling in caves, 
In empty shrines and at  the feet of trees, 
Never look back- 
Cultivate detachment. 
 
(8, 27) 
When shall I come to dwell 
In places not clung to as "mine", 
Which are by nature wide and open, 
And where I may behave as I wish, without attachment? 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
184  
 
The place for our retreat may be a cave, empty ho use or the foot of a tree. Having chosen our 
retreat place, we should be determined to turn our backs on the attachments we have 
abandoned. We must be detached from those things and think, fr om the very beginning, that we 
will remain in that frame of mind  for the duration of our retreat. 
 
The place for our solitary retreat should be: very  pleasant; unowned (so that there will be no 
danger of being evicted during our retreat); open  and spacious (as small and narrow spaces will 
disturb the mind) and; somewhere that we can do whatever we wish without worrying about 
what others might think or needing to ask perm ission. When we have found such a place, we 
generate the aspiration, “How wonderful it would be if I could remain here fo r as long as I wish,” 
without developing, at the same time, any attachment to the chosen spot.  
c' A point on livelihood  
(8, 28) 
When shall I come to  live without fear, 
Having just a begging bowl and a few odd things, 
Wearing clothes not wanted by anyone, 
And not even having to hide this body? 
 We should live with little food, have few posse ssions and wear clothes not wanted by thieves and 
robbers. Otherwise, if we are not careful, we may develop attachme nt to the necessities of life 
e v e n  t h o u g h  w e  a r e  i n  r e t r e a t .  If  w e  h a v e  m a n y  p o s s es s i o n s  o r  go o d  t h i n g s , w e  l i v e  i n  f e a r  o f  thieves and robbers and with our minds so distur bed, there will be no benefit to staying in 
retreat. We must also give up attachment to ou r bodies. We should develop the aspiration, “How 
wonderful it would be if I could live in isolatio n with just simple food and clothes.” If we are 
serious about going into retreat in the future to  w o r k  o n  d e v e l o p i n g  c a l m  a b i d i n g ,  w e  m u s t  
prepare ourselves now by reducing our desires and learning to live in contentment, instead of 
leaving it at the level of aspiration.      
 
Question from student : Am I right in saying that, in order to develop other realisations, we first 
need concentration acquired through solitude, beca use we will not be able to perfect the other 
bodhisattva trainings when our minds are not stable?  
 
Geshe-la’s reply : Here, we are talking about concentration in general, not the perfection of 
concentration. This also applies to the earlier prac tices we have discussed: joyous effort, patience, 
ethics, and generosity. 
 To develop concentration, we must be stable in our practice of joyous effort for, without it, we cannot go into isolation. In order to have stable joyous effort, patience is needed. In order to develop patience, we must have ethics. In order to  have good ethics, we must have practised the 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
185  
three kinds of giving. There is a specific order in terms of the practices and this is the way to train 
our minds.  
 
But when we look at the six perf ections in relation to the bodhis attva grounds, then there is the 
perfection of generosity on the 1st ground, the perfection of ethics on the 2nd ground, the 
perfection of patience on the 3rd ground and so forth. That is the fixed order with regard to the 
perfections.  
 
In the case of someone engaging in solitary re treat to develop calm abiding, he must have 
practised generosity, ethics, patience , and joyous effort and so forth.

b' Reflecting on the disadvantages of desire  
2' Reflecting on the unclean nature of the body and so forth 
3' Reflecting on how [desire] produces all sorts of unwanted [consequences]  
ii) Cultivating a joy for isolation 
1" Discerning the antidotes to being attached to body  
(8,29) 
Having departed to the cemeteries, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
186  
When shall I come  to understand 
That this body of mine and the skeletons of others 
Are equal in being subject to decay? 
 
(8, 30) 
Then, because of its odor, 
Not even the foxes 
Will come close to this body of mine; 
For this is what will become of it. 
 
The antidote to overcoming our attachment to ou r bodies is to reflect on their impermanence 
and to recall that they are, by nature, unpleasant  and unclean. When we are very attached to our 
bodies, we should visit the cemetery, where we ca n see both fresh and decaying corpses.  Seeing 
them, we understand that our bodies, which we gr asp to as permanent and stable, are actually no 
different in nature from these corpses. When we die, our bodies will be the same as the corpses 
before us. Remembering this, we reflect on impermanence.   Verse 29 points out how the impermanent nature of  our bodies is like the corpses found in the 
cemetery. Verse 30 deals with the attachment to our bodies through the reflection on their dirty 
and unpleasant nature.    We should understand that when we die, our bodies  will be exactly the same as the corpses in the 
cemetery. When a corpse is not a ttended to, it begins to decay giving off a foul smell. If the 
corpse is placed in the forest, even meat-eating animals like foxes and wolves are repelled by the 
stench of the rotting body.   
By reflecting on these points, we can overcome our attachment to our bodies. 
 
2" Discerning the antidotes to being attached to those dear to you  
a" Attachment to those dear to you is unreasonable  
b" The reasons for that 
c" Thinking of oneself as a guest who spends one night in each place  
a" Attachment to those dear to you is unreasonable  
(8, 31) 
Although this body arose as one thing, 
The bones and flesh with  which it was created 
Will break up and separate. 
How much more so wi ll friends and others? 
 This verse contains the instructio ns to deal with our attachment to our loved ones. We came into 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
187  
this world from our mother’s womb, unaccompanied by our friends and relatives. We were only 
accompanied by our bodies, made of flesh and bone s. When it is time for us to die, the bones 
and flesh that make up our bodies will break up an d go their separate ways. In a similar way, we 
will have to be separated from our loved ones. The Lamrim talks about how birth always ends in 
death and gathering always ends in dispersal. We have to reflect on these points to dispel our 
attachment to our loved ones. 
b" The reasons for that 
(8, 32) 
At birth I was born alone, 
And at death, too, I shall die alone; 
As this pain cannot be shared by others, 
What use are obstacle-making friends? 
 
We often think that it is good to live with our parents and relatives and be close to our friends so 
that we can support each other and go throug h good and bad times together. We may then 
think, “I do not want to be separated from my parents/spouse/close friend so I am not going to 
go into isolation.”   Verse 32 is the response to such wrong thinki ng. When we live together, attachment to one 
another is inappropriate.  When we were born, we were not born with our loved ones. When we 
die, our loved ones will not follow us. Due to the attachment to our loved ones in our previous lives, we accumulated all sorts of negativities on  their behalf for which we are experiencing the 
fruitional effects in the form of suffering, proble ms and sicknesses in this life. We are the ones 
experiencing these difficulties, not our loved ones. When we are sick, our loved ones cannot 
experience the sickness on our behalf. If we create  the cause to be possessed by spirits, we have to 
experience the spirit harms on our own.  When it comes to engaging in virtuous actions such as studying the Buddhadharma or going into retreat,  our loved ones often create obstacles that 
interfere with our practice. What is the point then of being attached to them?  
c" Thinking of oneself as a guest who spends one night in each place 
(8, 33) 
In the same way as travellers on a highway 
(Leave one place) and reach (another), 
Likewise, those travelling on the path of conditioned existence 
(Leave) one birth and reach (another). 
 There are different ways to interpret this verse.  Travellers on a highway often stop at a rest statio n, where other travellers gather, on their way to 
their destination.  These travellers are not interested or attached to one another.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
188  
 
We take rebirth in cyclic existence under the powe r of our karma and delusions. In this life, we 
are born as human beings in this world togeth er with many other human beings, who share a 
similar karma with us. In the same way, we meet wi th our parents and loved ones of this life.  We 
are like the traveller meeting other travellers at the rest station.  
  
Just as travellers go their separate ways after havi ng rested, it is only a matter of time before we 
have to move on to our next life, leaving ev erything behind and going our separate ways. 
Remembering this, there is no need for us to be so possessive of or attached to our loved ones 
and friends. When it is time for us to die and go on to the next life, even if we do not wish to go, we have no choice as death will definitely take place. Therefore, there is no point in us being so attached to our loved ones.  We can reduce our attachment to our loved ones  b y  r e f l e c t i n g  o n  t h e  three points under the 
outline, Discerning the antidotes to being attached to thos e dear to you. 
  
4' The benefits of relying on isolation  
a' Companions 
b' Place 
c' A point on livelihood  
d' A point on discernment 
e' A point on non-distraction 
1" Wise ones' reliance upon isolation is reasonable 
2" The benefits of relying upon isolation 
1" Wise ones' reliance upon isolation is reasonable 
(8, 34) 
Until the time comes for this body 
To be supported by four pall-bearers 
While the worldly (stand arou nd) stricken with grief, 
Until then, I shall retire to the forest. 
 Once we are born, we will definitely have to die. At the time of death, we will be surrounded by our loved ones who will wail and cry with great sorrow and we will also suffer and feel sad. Our bodies will become corpses and will be taken out of the house. Before this happens, we should 
retire to the forest and go into isolation. The wi se ones go into isolation and retreat before being 
stricken by grief, where they can then strive in virtue.   
2" The benefits of relying upon isolation 
a" There is none of the torment of mourning and so forth  
b" The virtuous side of one's life does not deteriorate but rather flourishes 
c" Therefore, [think] "It is reasonable for me to rely on isolation" 
a" There is none of the torment of mourning and so forth  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
189  
(8,35) 
Befriending no one and begrudging no one, 
My body will dwell alone in solitude. 
If I am already counted as a dead man, 
When I die there will be no mourners. 
 
When we are in solitary retreat in our mountain  hermitage, attachment and hatred will not arise 
as we will be separated from our objects of atta chment and anger.  After some time, due to not 
hearing from us, our parents or loved ones will lose hope, thinking we have died and give up 
their attachment to us. If we continue our solitary life, at the actual time of death, there will be 
no one to mourn us. If we are surrounded by wail ing relatives at the time of death, they will 
disturb our minds and make us sorrowful.  But wi th no one to mourn us, we will not encounter 
such problems. Since we have few necessities and belongings, we are less attached to these things, 
which also help to make our death more comfortable.   
 
This was mentioned in an earlier lesso n with a quotation from Milarepa’s Collection of Songs :  
By staying in this mountain hermitage, even if I, called Milarepa, fall ill,  
no one will ask after my welfare, “How are you?” If I die in this mountain hermitage, th ere is no one to cry and wail for me.  
If I, Milarepa, die in this mountain hermitag e, the wishes of the yogi will be fulfilled. 
b" The virtuous side of one's life does not deteriorate but rather flourishes 
(8, 36) 
And as there will be no one around 
To disturb me with their mourning, 
Thus there will be no one to distract me 
From my recollection of the Buddha. 
 Living in solitude, we will not be harmed by ou r enemies or disturbed by the wailing of our loved 
ones at the time of death. Due to the absence of  our loved ones, our minds will be at peace and 
not distracted. We will be able to remember the Three Jewels and die in a virtuous state of mind. 
It is mentioned in many texts and sutras that  the best way to die is while remembering the 
Buddha, which we can do easily due to the lack of distraction at the time of death. If we die with that mind of faith in the Buddha, there is hope of securing a good rebirth in the next life.   The wise remember the different points mentione d here and abandon the bustle of life and go 
into isolation. As mentioned in the outline, when  one lives in isolation, one’s virtuous activity 
does not deteriorate but flourishes instead.  
c" Therefore, [think] "It is reasonable for me to rely on isolation" 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
190  
(8,37) 
Therefore, I shall dwell alone, 
Happy and contented with few difficulties, 
In very joyful and beautiful forests, 
Pacifying all distractions.  
 
The advice then is to live in the forest, which acts as a condition for one’s mental and physical 
happiness to increase. The qualities of the retr eat place are mentioned in the Lamrim Chenmo. 
Sustenance should be easily obtainable and as mentioned in the Or nament of the Mahayana 
Sutras, the place should not induce sickness and one’s friends should be virtuous. One should 
live in a place where there are no thieves, robbers  or wild animals. If we stay in such a special

b) Abandoning erroneous conceptions  
i) Cultivating weariness for desire 
1' Reflecting on how the effect of honing one's desires involves fear  
a' Instructions on exerting effort in virtue 
b' Reflecting on the disadvantages of desire  
2' Reflecting on the unclean nature of the body and so forth 
3' Reflecting on how [desire] produces all sorts of unwanted [consequences]  
ii) Cultivating a joy for isolation  
a' Instructions on exerting effort in virtue  
(8, 38) 
Having given up all other intentions, 
Being motivated by only one thought, 
I shall strive to settle my mind in equipoise (by means of calm abiding), 
And to subdue it (with superior insight). 
 
Having given up all other intentions,  refers to going into isolation after giving up all thoughts of 
living in the hustle and bustle of worldly life and its distractions. Gyaltsab Rinpoche mentions 
when we go into isolation, we work on cultivating our minds and meditating on a specific 
subject, hence, Being motivated by only one thought.  We generate the intention, “I am going to 
meditate to develop the two types of bodhici ttas; conventional bodhicitta and ultimate 
bodhicitta.” With this motivation , we strive to develop the concentration that is able to focus 
single-pointedly on any chosen virtuous object. Ba sed on that, we go on to  develop the realisation 
of special insight, which is the an tidote to our negative emotions.  
 Another commentary says, Look at your own mind and then aim to subdue  it. With the thought of 
subduing our minds, we proceed to develop calm abiding followed by special insight and then we 
go on to develop the union of calm abiding and special insight.  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
191  
One sutra suggests that the ordained bodhisattvas  should ask themselves, “What is the purpose 
of staying in the monastery? Why live in isolatio n and what is the purpose of doing retreat?” The 
ordained bodhisattvas question themselves in this  way because there is no guarantee that they are 
necessarily engaging in virtue simply by physically  living in the monastery or retreat hermitage.  
The Buddha points out that physical isolation does not necessarily equate with engaging in 
virtue, since thieves and robbers also live secretly in isolation as do the wild animals that roam in 
the forest.  The purpose of staying in isolation is to remain in virtue and develop and conjoin 
calm abiding and special insight and meditate on developing the two types of bodhicitta.  
 
These are questions we should ask ourselves when we  go into retreat, “What is this for? Why am 
I doing this?”  We should go into isolation out of fear of the anger, attachment, ignorance and 
the distractions that come with our normal worldly lives.  We should go into isolation to engage in virtue. 
b' Reflecting on the disadvantages of desire  
(8,39) 
Both in this world and the next, 
Desires give rise to great misfortune: 
In this life killing, bondage and flaying, 
And in the next, the existence of the hells. 
 All sorts of discursive thoughts arise in our minds due to the three mental poisons: anger, 
attachment and ignorance. The chapter on patience  contains the antidotes to discursive thoughts 
arising from anger; the antidotes to discursive th oughts arising from ignorance will be studied in 
the ninth chapter on wisdom.   Chapter 8 gives the instructions for dealing wi th our desire and attachment for people and 
material things. Now, we are talking about attach ment to sentient beings. The next few verses 
deal with the attachment and desire for sex. Sh antideva had a different audience and he spoke 
from the perspective of a man, i.e. a man’s desire  for a woman but it applies equally to a woman’s 
desire for a man.  All the problems of this life arise from attach ment. We experience many problems and hardships 
and engage in all sorts of activities to fulfil our desire for wealth and possessions, respect and 
honour. We work enthusiastically and happily to achieve these goals. But as we engage in these 
actions out of attachment, we have to take rebirt h in the lower realms. When it becomes time to 
experience the results of our action s, then all we can do is cry.

ii) Cultivating a joy for isolation  
1" There are no effects of acting to experience a corpse 
(8,40) 
For the sake of (women), many requests 
Are first of all made through go-betweens, 
All forms of transgressio n and even notoriety 
Are not avoided for their sake. 
 
(8, 41) 
I engage in fearful deeds for them, 
And will even consume my wealth. 
But these (very bodies of theirs), 
Which I greatly enjoy in the sexual embrace, 
 
(8, 42) 
Are nothing other than skeletons, 
They are not autonomous  and are identityless. 
Rather than being so desiro us and completely obsessed, 
Why do I not go to the state beyond sorrow (instead)? 
 
(This outline may also be translated as, There are no results/effects that come from experiencing the 
taste of desire.  In Tibetan, the word for ‘corpse’ is the same as the word for ‘taste.’ ) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
193  
 
According to ancient custom, a family with a so n of marriageable age send a matchmaker to the 
family of a prospective bride, to request th e hand of their daughter in marriage. These 
matchmaking activities are for the purpose of fulf illing the young man’s desire for the opposite 
sex.  
 When our desire and attachment for the other party is  very strong, we may   engage in all sorts of 
negative actions to achieve our goal, including killi ng the parents of our prospective partner, etc. 
We discard the vows and commitments we have take n, not caring that our reputation will be 
ruined in the process. We are able to bear any kind of difficulties, be it hunger or thirst or even giving up our lives. Our negativities will propel us to the lower realms in our next life. This is the result of our strong desire and attachment.   We have no qualms about wasting the wealth an d resources that we have amassed over a long 
period of time, for the sake of this meaningless goal instead of using them to create virtue by 
making offerings to the Three Jewe ls.  We do this because we wa nt to experience the pleasure 
that comes from sexual intercourse. Yet, if we anal yse the nature of the body  of our partner, it is 
no different from embracing a skeleton . What is pleasurable about that?   
 
They are not autonomous and are identityless : They are not autonomous, means that they are not 
established from their own side but are merely labe lled on a collection. Since these bodies do not 
exist from their own side, they are without self or, identityless . Why then do we grasp at these 
bodies as possessing a self, as if they existed from their own side? Why do we not aspire to 
achieve the state beyond sorrow instead? 
 
Autonomous,  can also refer to the fact that if our obje ct of desire belongs to someone else, then 
he/ she is not ours. Again, why do we cling to someone who is not available to us?  
 Sexual desire is an obstacle to achieving liberation. To achieve liberation, we must engage in the three higher trainings of which the training in ethi cs is the basis for the other two trainings. It is 
not possible to engage in the training in ethics  when we have strong attachment and desire; 
without this preliminary,   it is impossible to co mplete the three higher tr ainings. Therefore, we 
must abandon our desire for both inner and outer phenomena, i.e. sentient beings and objects. 
Without abandoning, at the very least, manifest desire for these phenomena, it is difficult to 
achieve concentration. Without achieving concentr ation, there is no way we can achieve special 
insight. Without special insight, we will continue to  be bound to cyclic existence, because we will 
not be able to overcome the afflicti ons of the Peak of Cyclic Existence
65 – the highest rebirth that 
                                              
65 Cyclic existence includes three realms: the desire, fo rm, and formless realms. The form realm includes four 
progressively higher levels of meditative stabilization, and the formless realm includes four meditative absorptions 
(Lamrim Chenmo, Vol. 3 pg 397 endnote 65). The Peak of Cy clic Existence is the highest of the four meditative 
absorptions in the formless realm, also called, “neither  discrimination nor non-discrimination” (Lamrim Chenmo, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
194  
we can attain in samsara even though below this level, afflictions can be temporarily suppressed if 
we have calm abiding. In essence, the advice give n here is that to achieve the state of liberation, 
we must give up attachment and desire.  
 
I said earlier that this outline can be translated as, There are no results from experiencing the taste of 
desire. Taste  here is not as gross as the taste included in the five objects of desire: form, sound, 
smell, taste and touch. When we ar e attached to the ‘taste’ of thes e forms of desire, it blocks our 
achievement of liberation and brings no results be cause it does not benefit us either in this or 
our future lives.  
 This is my own point of view, because it is much  easier to explain the Tibetan word as ‘taste’ 
rather than ‘corpse.’ But we can also explain the verse according to the original translation; there 
are no results that come from embracing the sexual  object that is no different from a skeleton. 
2" In the end, [our bodies] do not pass beyond the cemetery  
(8, 43) 
In the first place, I made efforts to lift (her veil), 
And when it was raised she bashfully looked down. 
Previously, whether anyone looked or not, 
Her face was covered with a cloth. 
 
(8, 44) 
Yet now, why do I run away 
Upon directly beholding 
This face, which disturbs the mind 
As it is being revealed to me by the vultures? 
 When a man, out of desire and attachment, wants to look at the face of the girl he is attracted to, 
he does not see it because the girl, being shy, looks down.   
In verse 43, Previously,  means, before going to a cemetery . According to ancient custom, at the time 
of marriage, the bride’s face is covered up. Unab le to see her face, the eager groom suffers. Due 
to some misfortune, the bride passes away and the bo dy is brought to a cemetery. Once there, the 
body is fully exposed, especially when the vultures arrive to tear it apart.  At that time, the groom 
sees everything but why is there so much fear then?  
 
                                                                                                                                                          
 
Vol. 3, pg 402 endnote 214). The four meditative stabiliz ations of the form realm are the first concentration (3 
levels), the second concentration (3 levels), the third co ncentration (3 levels), and the fourth concentration (3 
levels + 5 pure places). The four meditative absorpti ons of the formless realm are Limitless Space, Limitless 
Consciousness, Nothingness, and Peak of Cyclic Existenc e (Ref: “Cyclic Existence / Samsara: The Three Realms” – 
Handout from Basic Program, Module 1).

195  
Some commentaries mention that Shantideva taught this text to (his audience of mainly fully-
ordained) monks to remind them of  the faults of attachment.   The face of the woman one longs 
to see when she is alive is the same face she ha s when she is lying in the cemetery. What is the 
reason then for being so desirous of one and fear ful of the other? And why be attached to that 
face when it is alive and not so when it lies at the cemetery? 
3" It is unreasonable to stingily guard one's own and others' bodies  
(8, 45) 
(Previously) I completely protected (her body) 
When others cast their eyes upon it. 
Why, miser, do you not protect it now, 
While it is being devoured by these birds? 
 
When we have strong attachment to our partners, we get jealous when others look at them. We 
do everything to guard and protect them. But why do we not protect them in the same way when 
the vultures are devouring their corpses in the cemetery? And if we do not protect them then, 
why do we protect our obje cts of attachment now? 
 
Question from student : Where is the mention of guarding one’s  own body as stated in the outline? 
Geshe-la’s reply:  Although the outline says, It is unreasonable to stingil y guard one’s own and others’ 
bodies , the verse mentions explicitly the bodies of others. But we can explain the outline in this 
way: Just as we are very attached to and protective of the bodies of others, so we are attached to 
and protective of our own bodies.   
4" The silliness of wearing ornaments  
(8, 46) 
Since vultures and others are eating 
This pile of meat that I behold, 
Why did I offer flower garlands, sandalwood and ornaments 
To that which is now the food of others? 
 Although the bodies of our objects of attachment are, by nature, unclean, we see them as being 
clean and pleasant and adorn them with perfumes, flower garlands, ornaments, etc. These bodies 
are, in fact, piles of meat  that will become the food of jackals , vultures, maggots and the like. Since 
we do not adorn corpses with ornaments, there is al so no need to do so wh en they are alive.   
5" The reasonableness of feeling extreme trepidation    
(8, 47) 
If I am frightened by the skeletons I see, 
Even though they do not move, 
Why am I not frightened by walking corpses 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
196  
Which are moved around by a few (impulses)? 
 
When bodies are brought to a cemetery for crematio n, what remains at the end is a pile of bones 
and ashes. We are scared at the sight of thes e remains even though they do not move.   
 
Inside a living body are the wind energies, the mind and the afflictions. If we are terrified of a pile of bones and ashes, we should be even more  afraid of the walking corpses who are controlled 
by their three mental poisons and more likely to harm us. There is, therefore, no reason to be 
attached to these people.  
6" The silliness of being attached to the wearing of clothes  
(8, 48) 
Although I am attached to it wh en it is covered (with skin), 
Why do I not desire it when it is uncovered? 
Since I have no need for it then, 
Why copulate with it when it is covered? 
 When desire is very strong, the mere sight of a woman’s body, even when fully clothed, can be 
exciting. But why are we not attached to that very  same body lying naked in the cemetery? This is 
because we see the corpse as being very unpleasant , dirty and unclean, even though this was also 
its nature when it was alive.   
Question from student : The root text says, when it is covered (with skin ). Does this refer to skin or 
clothing? Geshe-la’s reply : It refers to clothing. 
 
b' Reflecting on aspects of those who are still alive 
1" Attachment [is seen to be] unreaso nable once the lack of cleanliness becomes 
apparent
