b" Arresting the conception [of things] as clean  
c" Individually, the body and mind are nothing to be attached to  
d" Reflecting on those faults in relation to oneself 
e" Physical appearance [lit. shape] is nothing to be attached to 
a" The unreasonableness of being attached to physical sensations  
(8, 49) 
Since both excrement and saliva 
Arise solely from food, 
Why do I dislike excrement, 
And find joy in saliva? 
 
(8, 50) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
197  
Cotton, too, is soft to the touch, 
But while I find no (sexual) delight in a pillow, 
I think that (a woman's body) does not emit a putrid odor. 
Lustful One, you are confused  as to what is unclean! 
 
(8,51) 
Thinking that they cannot  sleep with this cotton, 
Although it is soft to the touch, 
Confused, negative and lustful people 
Become angry towards it (instead). 
 
If we enjoy the saliva of a woman/man, why do we not have the same desire for their excrement 
and urine which is also produced from the food they eat? Why are we a ttached to one and not 
the other?  
 
We say that we like to touch a woman because he r skin is soft and smooth. In that case, we 
should be even more delighted to touch cotton wh ich is far softer than a woman who is no more 
than a filth-making machine. Due to our mistaken  conceptions, we grasp at that which is by 
nature unclean as clean. Those with these mistak en conceptions would say, “How can I have sex 
with cotton? Where is the sexual pleasure in touching cotton?” and become angry towards it. This shows that they are not at tached to the pleasant sensat ion of touching cotton but are 
actually attached to the unclean body.   
b" Arresting the conception [of things] as clean  
(8, 52) 
If I am not attached to what is unclean, 
Then why do I copulate with th e lower parts of others' bodies, 
Which are merely cages of bones tied together with muscles, 
Plastered over with the mud of flesh? 
 
(8, 53) 
I myself contain many unclean things 
Which I constantly have to experience; 
So why, because of an obsession for uncleanliness, 
Do I desire other bags of filth? 
 We claim we are not attached to dirty and unplea sant things. But the bodi es we are attached to 
are cages of bones tied together with joints an d muscles and plastered over with flesh. These 
bodies that we embrace are, by nature, unclean. The body is full of filth: phlegm comes from the throat, snot comes from the nose, and urine and excrement from the lower orifices. Everything 
that emerges from our nine orifices is dirty and unclean. This is the kind of body we have. Not 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
198  
satisfied with living with one dirty body, we look  for the bodies of others, which are equally dirty 
and unclean. Why are we so attached to the bodi es of others when we have a body of our own?   
Lesson 36   Chapter VIII - verse 54-64 
We discussed the outline, Arresting the conception  [of things] as clean,  in the last lesson. This 
conception that perceives phenomena to be clean needs to be corrected.  
In the Four Noble Truths, the first truth, the Truth of Suffering, has four characteristics; 
impermanence, suffering (or dhukka ), empty, and selfless. 
 
The opposite of these four are the wrong conc eptions (or erroneous views) of grasping at 
impermanent phenomena to be permanent, grasping  a t  t h a t  w h i c h  i s   u n c l e a n  t o  b e  c l e a n ,  
grasping at things which are in the nature of  suffering to be pleasurable and grasping at 
phenomena that are selfless to have a self.  All phenomena are, by nature, selfless but we gras p at them as existing in the opposite way. 
Because of that, we engage in all sorts of negati ve actions and  accumulate karma, the results of 
which we have to experience.    
We also grasp at things that are, by nature, uncl ean to be clean and pleasant and once again, we 
get into trouble and suffer. An example of ou r attachment to unclean phenomena is sexual 
desire. Our strong desire for the bodies of others  is due to our erroneous conception grasping at 
them as clean when, in reality, they are unclean.  Once we understand th e filthy nature of the 
body, there is no basis for the arising of attachment.   
 
Verses 52 and 53 deal with stopping the erroneou s conception of phenomena that are unclean as 
clean. The Buddha mentions in th e sutras that our bodies are fi lth-making machines. We know 
t h i s  i s  t r u e  w h e n  w e  a n a l y s e  t h i s  f u r t h e r .  O u r  w h o l e  b o d y  i s  f i l l e d  w i t h  s o  m a n y  u n c l e a n ,  unpleasant substances that it se ems to be no more than a filth- producing factory. Yet, we have 
strong  attachment to our own bodies. We need to  meditate on this and when done well, it can 
definitely reduce our attachment to our bodies.   The sutras mention that the childi sh, grasping at unclean things to  be clean, will even eat snot, 
pus, etc., just like maggots who consume pus produced by the body  due to their attachment,. The 
sutras also mention that, if we left our bodies alone – if we didn’t wash our bodies - they would stink. Yet we remain attached to our bodies as bees to honey.  One of the commentaries says that it is one of the greatest signs of our confusion and ignorance 
that we are attached to the bodies  of others. This attachment is li ke someone taking refuge at the 
foot of a tree at night. In the darkness, he cannot  see that the tree is surrounded by piles of dirty 
things and he may even sleep on top of this filth. At sunrise, when he can see clearly what he had 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
199  
slept on, he will be disgusted at the sight.  In the same way, when we reflect and meditate on and 
realise the unclean nature of our bodies and the bodies of others, we will also be repelled and 
disgusted. Our attachment will be then be reduced.   
 
If we want to develop concentration, we must give up attachment and desire. Otherwise, our 
minds will remain distracted and there will be no  way to develop concentration. In order to stop 
attachment, we have to know its faults and we mu st stop it from arising in the first place. How 
does attachment arise? It is due to grasping at unclean phenomena as clean. The way to stop 
attachment is to first understand how our  object  of attachment is, by nature, unclean. That is 
why we study these verses.  
1) Instructions on cultivating concentration 
2) Abandoning the class of things incongruous with calm abiding  
a) Abandoning the bustle of the world 
b) Abandoning erroneous conceptions  
i) Cultivating weariness for desire 
1' Reflecting on how the effect of honing one's desires involves fear  
2' Reflecting on the unclean nature of the body and so forth 
a' Reflecting on the disadvantages by considering what one finds in 
   cemeteries  
b' Reflecting on aspects of those who are still alive 
1" Attachment [is seen to be] unreasonable once the lack of cleanliness 
becomes apparent 
a" The unreasonableness of being attached to physical sensations 
b" Arresting the conception [of things] as clean  
c" Individually, the body and mind are nothing to be attached to  
d" Reflecting on those faults in relation to oneself 
e" Physical appearance [lit. shape] is nothing to be attached to

c" Individually, the body and mind are nothing to be attached to  
(8,54) 
-But it is the flesh that I enjoy- 
If this is what I wish to touch and behold, 
Why do I not desire it in its natural state, 
Devoid of any mind? 
 
(8,55) 
Furthermore, any mind that I may desire 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
200  
Is unable to be touched or beheld, 
And whatever I am able to  touch will not be mental; 
So why indulge in this meaningless copulation? 
 
Verse 50 talks about the desire for the pleasure th at comes from touching the soft, smooth body 
of a woman and questions why we do not have the same desire to touch cotton wool.  The 
response may be, “I am not attached to cotton w ool! What I enjoy is the pleasant sensation of 
touching a woman.” Because of ou r attachment to that pleasurable experience, the desire and 
wish to touch a woman’s body ar i s e .  I t  s h o u l d  f o l l o w  t h e n  t h a t  w e  s h o u l d  a l s o  e n j o y  t h e  
sensation of touching a female corpse.  
 
Student : Geshe-la a corpse and a woman are not the same. A woman is warm and moves.  
Geshe-la :  If the corpse moved, you would be scared. Why? 
 
What is it that  we actually desire?  Is it the body or the mind? A person exists by depending on the collection of the body and mind. We should iden tify precisely the object of our desire – is it 
the body or mind?  If our attachment is to the body , touching a woman’s corpse would also be  pleasurable. But as 
the student said, it is not so beca use the body no longer moves.  
So it seems we are attached to the mind of th e person, thinking, “This woman is great. She is 
kind, good-hearted and she showers me with affect ion and love.” If we are attached to the mind 
of the person, that consciousness is formless and cannot be seen or touched. What is the point then of engaging in sex with that person?  As mentioned earlier, we can reduce our attachme nt by reflecting on th e unclean nature of the 
body. This is another way to counter our attach m e n t  b y  r e f l e c t i n g  o n  w h a t  w e  a r e  a c t u a l l y  
attached to - the body or the mind. 
d" Reflecting on those faults in relation to oneself 
(8, 56) 
It is not so strange that I do not understand 
The bodies of others to be of an unclean nature, 
But it is indeed strange that I do not understand 
My very own body to be by nature unclean. 
 
We may not understand that the bodies of others are unclean. But what is really amazing is that 
we do not realise the unclean nature of our own bodies even though daily we see the products 
that emerge from our nine orifices. When we re alise the unclean nature of our own bodies, we 
will be able to understand how the bodies of others are also unclean, thereby stopping our 
attachment to them. 
e" Physical appearance [lit. shape] is nothing to be attached to 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
201  
(8,57) 
Having forsaken the young lotus flower 
Unfolded by beam s of sunlight free from cloud, 
Why, with a mind craving for what is unclean, 
Do I revel in a cage of filth? 
 
We may argue that it is not the body or mind that  we are attached to but rather we are attracted 
by the body’s shape and colour. If that is so, why are we not attach ed to the beautiful lotus flower 
that blooms in the sunlight? Why are we desirous  of the shape and colour of others’ bodies, 
which are receptacles for filth, instead of adorin g the shape of a beautiful flower? Reflecting in 
this way, we can stop attachment to the shape and colour of others’ bodies. 
 
2" By ascertaining the [characteristics of the] sexual organs, attachment [is seen to be] 
unreasonable 
a" Reflecting on their lack of cleanliness in light of the fact that they are the point at which 
unclean things exit the body 
b" Reflecting on their lack of cleanliness in light of the fact that the are the effects of those 
c" Illustrating the body's lack of cleanliness by means of an example 
d" Reflecting on the lack of cleanliness of one's own body 
 
The Tibetan word, dag means sign and in certain contexts, it also refers to the sexual organs of 
men and women. But this does not seem to a pply here. This outline can be translated as, Through 
the ascertainment that is derived from reas on, attachment is seen to be inappropriate.  Although I am not 
completely certain about this, this whole section seems to talk about how our attachment will be 
reduced once we come to the ascertainment of th e unclean nature of the body by depending on 
the correct reasoning. 
a" Reflecting on their lack of cleanliness in light of the fact that they are the point at which 
unclean things exit the body  
(8,58) 
Since I do not wish to touch 
A place that is smeared with excrement, 
Then why do I wish to touch the body 
From which that (excrement) arose? 
 
We will not sit on a cushion that is smeared wi th excrement, urine or vomit. Why are we then 
attached to the bodies of othe rs, which are the sources of such  filthy substances? How can we 
bear to touch the very source of these substances  when we would not even go near anything that 
is stained by them? This is looking at the unclea n nature of the body from the perspective of it 
being a cause for filthy substances.  
b" Reflecting on their lack of cleanliness in light of the fact that they are the effects of those   
(8, 59) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
202  
If I am not attached to what is unclean, 
Why do I copulate with the lower parts of others' bodies, 
Which arise from the unclean field (of a womb) 
And are produced by the seeds within it? 
 
(Geshe-la : It is possible that there is a different ed ition of the root text, but the one I am using 
does not have the words, lower parts ; it only mentions others’ bodies .) 
 
We claim we dislike unclean things. But when we examine our bodies, they came from the unclean 
field, referring to our mothers’ wombs and are prod ucts of the union of the sperm and eggs of 
our parents. The previous verse examined the uncl ean nature of the body from the perspective of 
it being a cause of unclean substances. This vers e examines the unclean na ture of the body from 
the perspective of it being an effect or the result of unclean substances.    
c" Illustrating the body's lack of cl eanliness by means of an example 
(8,60) 
I have no wish for a small, dirty maggot 
Which has come from a pile of filth, 
So why do I desire this body, whic h by nature is grossly unclean, 
For it too was produced by filth? 
 
(8,61) 
Not only do I not disparage 
The uncleanliness of my own body, 
But, because of an obsession for what is unclean, 
I desire other bags of filth as well. 
 
Gyaltsab Je’s commentary talks about Verse 60 from  the perspective of it being an analogy to 
illustrate how unclean the body is. Some other commentaries consider this verse under the 
outline, Reflecting on the unclean nature of the body fr om the perspective of it being a cause and effect . 
 
We are disgusted at the sight of a single maggo t or worm in our food, because we know they 
come from dirty things. Why then do we desire the body which is  the product of and completely 
full of filthy  substances?  
 
When we have very strong desire for the bodies of others, we need to reflect on how our objects 
o f  d e s i r e  i s  b y  n a t u r e  u n c l e a n  f r o m  t h e  p e r s pective of it being the causes, results and the 
combination of both causes and effects of dirty substances as shown in the above analogy.  Our 
own body is completely filled with unclean and unpleasant substances and, realising this, we 
should be disenchanted with it. Instead, we ge nerate desire for other dirty, unclean bodies. 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
203  
d" Reflecting on the lack of cleanliness of one's own body 
(1) Reflecting on the body's lack of cleanliness 
(2) If one is attached to that, then one ought to be attached to the bodies in a cemetery 
(1)Reflecting on the body's lack of cleanliness 
(8, 62) 
Even attractive things such as savoury foods, 
Cooked rice and vegetables, 
Make the ground dirty and unclean, 
Should they be spat out af ter being in the mouth. 
 
The food we eat may look very nice before it goes into our mouths. But once it goes into our mouths and is spat out, it change s into something disgusting. This is  a sign that the body is dirty. 
If we vomit on our clean clothes, we quickly chan ge our outfit. Why then do we not consider the 
source of the vomit – our body – to be dirty as well?    
(2) If one is attached to that, then one ought to be attached to the bodies in a cemetery 
(8, 63) 
Although such uncleanliness is obvious, 
If I still have doubts I should go to the cemeteries 
And look at the unclean bodies (of others) 
That have been thrown away there. 
 
(8,64) 
Having realized that, when their skin is rent open, 
They give rise to a great deal of fear, 
How will such things as these 
Ever again give rise to joy? 
 
If, in spite of having reflected on all the above poin ts we still have doubts, we are advised to go to 
the cemeteries and observe how the decaying bodies are rotting and being eaten by maggots. When the body is cut open, we see blood, mucus,  pus, excrement, urine, etc. and we will be 
terrified at such a sight. The insides of rotting corpses are no different from our own insides. We 
can  reduce attachment for our own bodies by re lating what we see in the corpses to our own 
bodies.  
Question from student : How do we relate these teachings to th e practice of deity yoga where we are 
supposed to see ourselves as the deity?  Reply from Geshe-la: The purpose of meditating on the unclean nature of the body is to stop 
attachment and desire. When we practise deity yo ga, we visualise ourselves as the deity and our 
environment as the celestial mansion. This is al so done to stop attachment. So there is no 
contradiction.

ii) Cultivating a joy for isolation  
(1) Perfumes such as sandalwood do not have the capacity to render an unclean body clean 
(8, 65) 
The scents with which some one's body is anointed 
Are sandalwood and the like, but not that of the other's body. 
So why am I attached to others' (bodies) 
Because of scents that are other (than theirs)? 
 
(8, 66)  
Since the body has a naturally foul odour, 
Isn't it good to be unattached to it? 
 
In the earlier lessons, we proved that our bodies ar e, by nature, dirty. Some people think that the 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
205  
body can be made pleasant by anointing it with  p e r f u m e .  W e  c a n  d e v e l o p attachment to this 
fragrant, perfumed body.  
 
Whatever perfume we use will not be the original  natural smell of the body. The perfume is not 
related in any way to the body to which it is applied. Why do we still have desire for that body? 
We confuse the smell of the perf ume on the body with the natura l scent of the body, forgetting 
that our natural body odour is foul and smelly. If  we claim to be attached to the smell of the 
body, then we are attached to some thing that actually stinks. In th is case, will it not be better not 
to be attached to the body so that we will not de velop attachment for its foul odour? So, when we 
are attached to someone’s body because of its nice smell, we have to check whether we are 
attached to the natural smell of that person’s body  or the perfume or fragrance that person uses.  
 
If we are attached to the smell of the perfume or  fragrance used, that has no relationship to the 
person’s body and there is no reason then for ou r attachment. If we are attached to the natural 
smell of the person, that is actually something fo ul and disgusting. Again, there is no reason to 
be attached to the body.  
 Gyaltsab Je says that the attachme nt that we have for our bodies is the condition for us to suffer 
and to experience unhappiness. When we are attached to our bodies, we will be worried all the 
time about our appearance and experience all th e problems and unhappiness that arise from this 
attachment.  Therefore, it is inappr opriate to be attached to the body. 
(2) Being attached to other scents that are [actually] unrelated to the [object of attachment] is not 
a suitable reason [for being attached to another's body and so forth] 
(8, 66 cd)  
Why do those who crave for the meaningless things of the world 
Anoint this body with pleasant scents? 
(8, 67) 
 
And furthermore, if it is the pleasant scent of sandalwood, 
How can it come from the body? 
So why am I attached to others' (bodies) 
Because of scents that are other (than theirs)? 
 
We perfume our bodies to make it smell nice du e to our attachment to our bodies. That nice 
smell is not a quality of or related in any way to the body itself.  Why are we then still attached to the body? 
b" The body does not pass beyond its perishable nature  
(8, 68) 
Since the naked body, (left) in its natural state, 
Is very frightening due to its long hair and nails, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
206  
Its yellowish foul-smelling teeth 
And it’s being coated with the odour of dirt, 
 
(8, 69)  
Why do I make such an effort to polish it, 
Like (cleaning) a weapon that will cause me harm? 
 
Our bodies and the bodies of others are the objects of our attachment. If we leave our bodies alone, our nails and hair will grow long and filt hy, our teeth will decay and our bodies will stink. 
If someone like that stood naked before us, we wo uld be repulsed by the sight. So, the body, our 
object of attachment, becomes the source of repulsion and perhaps even fear when left 
unattended.  It is like a weapon that will eventually harm us.   When we put in all that effort into grooming our bo dies, applying perfume and so forth, it is like 
someone polishing a weapon that will ultimately harm oneself. We need to meditate on these points. When we are attached to someone’s body, by imagining it 
in its unwashed, unattended state and thinking how unattractive it becomes will help us to 
reduce our attachment.   
c" Reflecting on it as a thing of revulsion 
(8, 69 cd) 
Hence this entire world is disturbed with insanity, 
Due to the exertions of those wh o are confused about themselves. 
 
(8, 70) 
When my mind rises (above worldly concerns), 
Through having beheld nothing but skeletons in the cemetery, 
Will there be any joy in graveyard cities 
Which are filled with moving skeletons? 
 
Due to the misconceptions of grasping at that wh ich is selfless by nature as having a self, and 
grasping at that which is unclean by nature as clean, we become attached to our own and others’ 
bodies. Due to this attachment, we perfume our bo dies to keep them attractive and pleasant. We 
are like someone who has drunk the wa ter that makes us lose our minds. 
 
Geshe-la : There is a kind of drink that driv es one insane when one drinks it. 
 
We are like crazy people, mad with desire and attachment. Everyone is like that. When the 
bodhisattvas look at us sentient beings, they ge nerate compassion and see us as the objects of 
compassion.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
207  
We are either mad with attachment or anger. Du e to our attachment, we cling to our loved ones 
and friends, those whom we consider to be on ou r side. Due to our anger, we generate hostility 
towards those who are against us and not on ou r side.  On top of that, we also generate 
competitiveness, jealousy and so forth. This is how everyone conducts their entire lives. 
 
When we examine our own actions and the actions of others, we see that everything we do is 
motivated by self-interest. If we look deeply into our hearts, even those supposedly altruistic 
activities carried out for the benefit of others ar e tainted with this self-interest and concern. We 
will not do anything if we do not stand to gain  something for ourselves. Gyaltsab Je says that 
when the bodhisattvas see us in such a st ate, they only feel compassion for us. 
 Relationships between spouses and partners are plagued with problems due to attachment and 
desire. When something happens to one’s partner, there is so much sorrow, anguish and fear of 
losing him/her. There is suffering again when on e has to be separated from one’s partner or one 
loses one’s partner to someone else.   When one’s partner dies, there is so much grie f and sadness at this permanent separation. One 
even adorns the dead body of the loved one with  flowers and beautiful clothes to make it look 
nice. These are the actions of crazy people.   We are usually afraid of corpses but when it co mes to the dead bodies of our loved ones, we 
cannot bear to leave them and cling on to them. Due to our strong attachment, our minds become disturbed and, due to our grief and pain, we behave like walking zombies. Yet, there is 
no difference between the corpses of others and the corpses of our loved ones.  When we walk into a cemetery, we are repulsed by the sight of the rotting corpses. The earlier 
verses mention how the world is filled with walking corpses i.e. people whose minds are 
completely full of the three mental poisons and whose bodies are merely sustained by the subtle 
wind energies in them. In the same way, we shou ld also reflect on how we and others are like 
walking skeletons, beneath our layers of skin and fl esh. These reflections will help us to generate 
disenchantment and reduce our attachment.  
3' Reflecting on how [desire] produces all sorts of unwanted [consequences] 
a' Presentation 
b' Explanation 
a' Presentation 
(8, 71) 
Furthermore, these unclean (female bodies) 
Are not found without paying a price. 
In order to obtain them, I exhaust myself 
And (in future) will be injured in the hells. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
208  
 
This verse points out that we need to pay a pric e to get our object of desire. We need money to 
sustain and beautify our bodies. We have to work hard and put up with all sorts of difficulties in 
order to earn this money and no matter how much we earn, it will never be enough.  
 
The main point is that in order to acquire the happiness and worldly goals of this life, we need to 
work for money. In the process, we engage in ne gative actions such as lying, divisive, harsh or 
offensive speech, competing with ot hers in a negative way, jealousy and so on, for which we have 
to experience the karmic resu lts in our future lives.  
 In summary, desire and attachment for our bodies  and the bodies of others cause problems for 
us in this and our future lives.  
b' Explanation 
1" Not obtaining what one desires 
a" There is no opportunity for enjoying one's desires  
b" Due to the suffering of exhaustion, one is not capable of enjoying one's desires 
c" Due to the object's intermittence, it is difficult to come upon the object of one's desire 
d" Due to being controlled by others, one receives many unwanted [consequences] 
a" There is no opportunity for enjoying one's desires  
(8, 72) 
As a child, I am unable to increase my wealth, 
And as a youth, what can I do (bei ng unable to afford a wife)? 
At the end of my life, when I have the wealth, 
Being an old man, what good will sex be then? 
 
To get what we want, we need resources and mo ney. When we are young, we have no money. 
When we grow up, we experience the unhappiness of  not being able to find our objects of desire, 
our life partners.  Even when we are able to find the right person, we need money to protect and 
k e e p  t h e m  s o  t h a t  t h e y  w i l l  n o t  l e a v e  u s .  T h e n  w e  h a v e  t o  w o r k  v e r y  h a r d  i n  t h i s  l i f e  t o  
accumulate wealth. It is not uncommon nowadays  to see couples separating due to financial 
problems. By the time we have accumulated enough  wealth, we will be quite old and no longer 
able to accomplish what we set out to do. 
b" Due to the suffering of exhaustion, one is not capable of enjoying one's desires 
(8,73) 
Some malicious and lustful people 
Wear themselves out by working all day 
And, when they return home (in the evening), 
Their exhausted bodies lie prostrate like corpses. 
 This verse is referring to the desire for sex. We  spend the whole day working hard at our jobs or 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
209  
businesses, returning home exhausted in the evenin gs.  Due to our tiredness, we can barely hold 
a proper conversation with our partners. When we  do talk, we may end up  arguing. We are too 
tired to even have sex. When we wake up th e next day, the whole cycle starts again.  
 
In Singapore, many people come to see me ab out their relationship problems. Due to their 
attachment and desire for one another, they are willing to put up with the problems and 
difficulties to earn a living in order to keep thin gs going and remain togeth er. They hope to earn 
money so that they can go home to enjoy a good relationship with their lo ved ones. This is what 
they want and what they hope will happen. But th ings do not work out that way in spite of all 
their efforts. This is the source of all the pr oblems, quarrels, unhappiness and disharmony in the 
family. 
c" Due to the object's intermittence, it is difficult to come upon the object of one's desire 
(8, 74) 
Some have the suffering of being disturbed with travel 
And having to go a long way from home. 
Although they long for their spouses, 
They do not see them for years at a time. 
 One of the reasons we get into relationships is fo r the physical intimacy it offers. Due to work 
commitments and in order to advance our businesses or careers, we may need to travel and leave our partners behind. Sometimes, these separations can last for years. Then our original motive 
for staying together is not fulfilled. Couple s who are separated because of work commitments 
suffer because of their attachment for one anot her and missing seeing each other. No such 
suffering will arise if there is no attach ment for one another in the first place. 
d" Due to being controlled by others, one receives many unwanted [consequences] 
(8,75) 
And some who wish for benefit 
Due to confusion, even sell themselves for the sake of (women and the like): 
But not attaining what they wish, 
They are aimlessly driven by the winds of others' actions. 
 Due to our self-interest and the wish to accumula te wealth, we have to do many things. We may 
become confused, even selling ourselves into slaver y to be fully controlled by others, losing our 
independence and freedom in the process. We may be cheated of the salary that we were 
supposed to receive. As a result of our negative actions, we experience problems both in this and our future lives.   This section deals with the problems of desire. Du e to the attachment that we have for ourselves 
and others, we never get what we want at the end of the day. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION

3' Reflecting on how [desire] produces all sorts of unwanted [consequences]  
a’ Presentation 
b' Explanation 
1" Not obtaining what one desires 
2" It is connected to the fault of numerous unwanted [consequences]  
a" Lacking independence and quickly throwing away one's life  
b" Being under the control of others and constantly besieged by various sufferings 
c" It acts to hinder liberation and puts to waste the leisures and endowments 
d" Developing the mind that strives for liberation by reflecting on its disadvantages 
e" Reflecting on how attachment to desires is the source for all disadvantages 
a" Lacking independence and quickly throwing away one's life  
(8,76) 
Some sell their own bodies 
And, without any power, are employed by others. 
Even when their wives give birth, 
Their children fall at the feet of trees and in lonely places. 
 
(8, 77) 
Some fools who are deceived by desire and, 
Wishing for a livelihood, think, “I shall earn my living (as a soldier)”; 
Then, although afraid of losing their lives, they go to war. 
Others become slaves for the sake of profit. 
 
Some people become slaves or servants for the sake of money, losing their freedom and 
independence and having to do as they are told . Sometimes they end up in relationships with 
their masters and become their mistresses. This happens in Singapore when the employer has an 
affair with the family maid. As a mistress, she cannot be kept at home but has to be housed 
elsewhere or even sent home. The employer has to continue supporting her and this creates 
additional problems for him. From the perspect ive of the maid, she had come to Singapore to 
work for money. As a mistress, her original goal  is not fulfilled and she also gets additional 
problems.   Some people become robbers and thieves because of  their desire for wealth and their attachment 
to themselves. When thieves or robbers are doing thei r work, they live in fear of losing their lives 
and worry about getting into trouble with the law. But they have to continue their work in order 
to earn a living.   
We may have encountered situatio ns where we had no choice but to part with our money, e.g. 
when we had to bribe an official or we were blackmailed, or asked for protection money. For 
example, people in Nepal are harassed by the Maoists and are forced to give them money.  
 
All these points highlight the fact that we get in to so much trouble because we have to do so 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
211  
many things to survive, due to our strong  attachment for ourselves and our bodies.  
b" Being under the control of others and constantly besieged by various sufferings 
(8, 78) 
Some lustful people even cut their bodies, 
Others impale themselves on the points of sticks, 
Some stab themselves with daggers, 
And others burn themselves—such things as these are quite apparent. 
 
(8, 79)   
Due to the torment involved in collecting  it, protecting and finally losing it, 
I should realize wealth to be fraught with infinite problems. 
 
Out of attachment and desire for the good things in life, people end up stealing and robbing. In the process, they are harmed or injured. They al so accumulate negative karma that will ripen in 
the form of suffering and problems in their future lives.  The main point is that out of our attachment for the objects of desire, we have to suffer all sorts 
of negative consequences in this life. We are li ke a butterfly attracted to the light of the butter 
lamp. We fly towards the light and get burnt and killed in the process.   
 There is also a certain kind of deer that is atta ched to sound. When they hear beautiful sounds, 
they are mesmerized and loose awareness of th eir surroundings. The hunter then takes this 
opportunity to kill them. 
 
When people go fishing, they usually use worms as  bait on the hook. The fi sh is attached to the 
smell or taste of the worm. Due to its ignorance and not knowing what will happen when it takes 
the bait, the fish is hooked and loses its life.  From the above examples, we see how, out of igno rance due to our attachment and desire, we get 
into problems and suffer. At the beginning, when  we do not have any possessions or wealth, 
because of our desire for these things, we work  to accumulate them. We suffer when we are 
working.  Having accumulated wealth and possessions, we ha ve to suffer to protect them and to maintain 
the status quo. At the end, we suffer again wh en we have to be parted from these things. 
Therefore, in relation to the acquisition, retention, and se paration from our wealth and 
possessions, there will be suffering in the beginning, middle and end.    All the hard work and effort we put into our da ily lives is to acquire something that is only 
relevant for this life alone. All our wealth and possessions cannot benefit us in our future lives. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
212  
We need to think seriously about this.   
 We have no contentment. Even when we have en ough, we are not satisfied, only wanting more. 
When we have sufficient resources to live on, that is the time for us to sit back and do something 
that will be beneficial and meaningful for our futu re lives, such as studyi ng the Dharma or going 
for retreat. It is a different case if we do not be lieve in or accept the existence of future lives. 
 
I n  o r d e r  t o  a c c o m p l i s h  s o m e t h i n g  b e n e f i c i a l  f o r  o u r  f u t u r e  l i v e s ,  w e  h a v e  t o  g i v e  u p  o u r  
attachment to this life. If our focus is on th e happiness of this life, there is no way we can 
accomplish anything beneficial fo r our future lives. We have to switch our emphasis from the happiness of this life to the happ iness of our future lives. We need to develop renunciation of 
samsara. We can do this by reflecting on the ad vice given above that deals with attachment and 
desire to the good things in this life.   
If we are attached to this life, then we are not practitioners at all. Our goal is to destroy the 
negative emotions through the realisation of specia l insight focusing on emptiness. Before we can 
achieve special insight, we first must develop calm  abiding. In order to develop calm abiding, we 
need to stop all the manifestations of attachment , without which, there is no way to develop calm 
abiding. So if we are intent on develop calm abid ing, we have to start doing all these things. But 
if we are not interested in developing calm ab iding, then we do not need all this advice.  
Lesson 38   Chapter VIII - verse 79-83 
Gyaltsab Je says that, for those of us interested in seeking liberation, we need to develop joyous 
effort in order to enter into the path and bring it to completion. Entering the path alone is not 
enough as, once we embark on the path, we need  to apply joyous effort to bring the path to 
completion. In order to develop that joyous e ffort, we need to rely on the four powers: 
• the power of aspiration 
• the power of stability (or steadfastness) 
• the power of joy and  
• the power of relinquishment 
 
The reason that joyous effort does not come easy  for us and we are unable to develop the four 
powers is due to our lack of clarity with regard  to what we really want. We are not clear about 
our own goals. We are stuck in a confusing situatio n.  Therefore, joyous e ffort does not arise in 
us.  In order to develop joyous effort, first we need to have stable faith of conviction in karma. This is what the first power, the power of aspiration, means. This power is developed on the basis of 
having this stable faith of convic tion in karma. We have to reflec t on karma: its nature, its causes 
and its effects and generate a stable faith in it s workings. Only then will we have the basis for 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
213  
developing joyous effort.  
 Deep inside our hearts, we should always be as king ourselves, “What am I looking for? Am I 
looking for the happiness of this life alone or am  I concerned with the happiness of my future 
lives?” This is a very important question that we  must ask ourselves. If we find that we have 
placed more emphasis on the ha ppiness of this life, then whatev er Dharma practices we have 
engaged in have not been pure, since the mind  was under the control of the three mental 
poisons (anger, attachment and ignorance).  
 When we wake up everyday we need to ask ours elves, “What matters to me most now? Am I 
more concerned with the happiness of this life or am I more co ncerned with the happiness of my 
future lives?”   If the emphasis is on our future happiness, then we have to investigate what we can do now that 
will benefit us in our future lives. We have  to examine this carefully and in detail.   
  
If we are really honest with ourselves and we look deeply into our hearts, we find that we 
instinctively put more emphasis on the happiness of this life than the happiness of our future 
lives. When we really check our minds, we see we instinctively look for the happiness of this life 
alone. This is our motivation regardless of what  we do. Due to our grasping to the happiness of 
this life alone, everything we do is influe nced by anger, attachment or ignorance.  
 Whatever we do - whether we ar e reciting our daily prayers, listening to teachings, taking 
initiations, consulting our guru or meeting the la mas - we are instinctively doing all this for the 
happiness of this life alone. Wh y - because this is our motiva tion deep inside our hearts. 
Everything we do is the manifestation of our desi re to achieve the happiness of this life, due to 
our goal of wanting to be happy in this life.   Because of this attitude, the Dharma practices th at we engage in may look like Dharma but in 
reality they do not become Dharma. These practices will not benefit us in our future lives. What 
we need to do now is shift our emphasis / goal to placing greater importance on the happiness of 
our future lives rather than just focusing on this life alone.   There is a reason for changing our minds in this way. As we consider ourselves to be Buddhists, we should accept the law of karm a.  We need to see how many more years we have left in this 
life.  Maybe, we can live till we are 60 years old. Compared to the lifespan of our future lives to come, we have to take rebirths for many eons to come. Based on the length of that time, the 
happiness of our future lives becomes far more important.    As we will be taking innumerable rebirths in the future, whether we end up with good or bad 
rebirths will depend on what we are doing in this present life. If we end up with bad rebirths in 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
214  
our future lives, we have to suffer for eons. When  we consider the amount of suffering we will 
have to endure in our future lives compared to the suffering we experience in this life, this life’s 
suffering is nothing and is  so important any more.  
 
We can definitely experience happiness in our fu ture lives if we create the cause now by doing 
something beneficial. The amount of happiness we can enjoy in our future lives is much more 
important than our lifespan in this life.  
 
I f  o u r  g o a l  i s  t h e  h a p p i n e s s  o f  o u r  f u t u r e  l i v e s ,  t h e n  w h a t e v e r  w e  d o  –  b e  i t  d o i n g  o u r  d a i l y  prayers, listening to teachings, ta king initiations, consulting the gurus or meeting the lamas - will 
all become Dharma. Once it becomes Dharma, then these actions will definitely benefit us in our 
future lives.    Therefore it is extremely important that we sit do wn, reflect and ask ourselves in whatever we do: 
“Am I doing this for this life or for my future lives?” Whatever our answer may be, we then have 
to ask, “Why am I doing this for this life? Why am I doing this for my future lives? Is this life 
more important or are my future lives more import ant?” We should consider this very carefully.  
 If our emphasis shifts to placing more importance  on the happiness of our future lives, then we 
have to think; “What can I do that will definitely  benefit me in my future lives?” As Buddhists, 
this is the minimum kind of attitude we need to have, i.e. placing gr eater emphasis on the 
happiness of our future lives than this life.   We need to be confident to have the type of a ttitude that thinks: “What I am looking for is the 
happiness of my future lives.” What do we stand to  gain by having this attitude? By placing more 
importance on the happiness of our future lives,  the three mental poisons will naturally be 
weakened and, as a result, we will have more mental peace and happiness.   If our life goals are focussed on the happiness of this life, the three mental poisons will become 
stronger in our minds. Then, there will only be more unhappiness, problems and suffering. All 
these difficulties are the result of our motivation focussed on this life alone.  From my side, it is my responsibility to tell you this. But whether this will benefit you or not depends on you. Just listening to this will not benefit you.  You need to think about this. Not 
just once but you need to think about this every day until you have some feeling or experience in 
your heart.   
Gyaltsab Je’s message is: “If you are someone wh o seeks liberation or en lightenment, you need 
joyous effort especially when you have this huma n life of leisure and en dowments; your faculties 
are complete; you are free of obstacles to your  Dharma practice; and you have the necessary 
conditions for your spiritual development. Having  found this opportunity, you should not waste 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
215  
it but use it to engage in something beneficial fo r your future lives. You need to develop joyous 
effort by generating the four powers.” 
 
Our problem is that we do not integrate the Dhar ma into our minds. We have listened to many 
teachings on, e.g., the topic of  the precious human rebirth. Although we have heard these 
teachings countless times, our minds remain unch anged. We do not reflect on the subject but, 
instead, we feel bored, thinking “I have heard this so many times.” We have no feeling for this 
subject and have little interest in it. It is impo rtant that we do not end up with this type of 
attitude. It is up to us whether we end up in this state or not.  
 It is important to avoid externally looking like a practitioner doing the commitments, reciting the 
prayers, engaging in the practices - but empty insi de. If our minds do not change, we will meet 
with many problems and much suffering when it  comes to the time of death. It would be 
ridiculous if we finally ended up in the lower realms. Therefore, whatever Dharma we may be 
engaged in, make sure it becomes Dharma. Whatever  virtuous actions we do, make sure they are 
virtue. This will only happen if we constantly recall; we need to be careful and check our minds all the time. If we do this, there will be nothin g to worry about; whatever we do will become 
Dharma.  Looking at the faults of desire and attachme nt, we can ask why attachment arises. Why do 
problems arise due to attachment? All this is due to the attitude of grasping to the happiness of 
this life. When attachment arises, it leads us to pr oblems in this life; it also leads us to suffering 
in our future lives. That is why we need to stop attachment from arising. We need to make a firm 
decision in our minds that our future lives are more important than the happiness of this life. 
Then, naturally, we will have fewer problems and difficulties in this life. When our goal is the happiness of our future lives, our disturbing emot ions will become weaker and we will experience 
more happiness and peace.   We all want to be happy and we do not want to suffer. Although we want to be happy, we do not create the causes that lead to happiness. Although we do not wa nt suffering, we keep on running 
after the causes of suffering. When we think abou t this seriously, we can see we are like crazy 
people. We want to be happy, yet we do not create the causes for happiness. We do not want to suffer, yet we keep on creating the causes for suffering. We are really quite mad.  
3' Reflecting on how [desire] produces all sorts of unwanted [consequences]  
a’ Presentation 
b' Explanation 
1" Not obtaining what one desires 
2" It is connected to the fault of numerous unwanted [consequences]  
a" Lacking independence and quickly throwing away one's life  
b" Being under the control of others and constantly besieged by various sufferings 
c" It acts to hinder liberation and puts to waste the leisures and endowments 
d" Developing the mind that strives for liberation by reflecting on its disadvantages 
e" Reflecting on how attachment to desires is the source for all disadvantages 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
216  
c" It acts to hinder liberation and puts to waste the leisures and endowments 
(8, 79 cd)  
Those who are distracted by their attachment to it 
Have no opportunity to gain freedom from  the misery of conditioned existence. 
 
(8,80) 
In the same way as animals drawing carriages 
Are only able to eat a few mouthfuls of grass, 
Likewise, desirous people 
Have many disadvantages such as  these, and little (profit), 
(8,81) 
And since even animals can obtain this (little profit), 
Those who are pained by their (previous) actions 
Waste these leisures and endowme nts, so difficult to find, 
For the sake of something triv ial that is not so scarce. 
 
When we are attached to wealth and possessions, we live our whole lives distracted by them. Due 
to this distraction, it will be impossible for us to  put the three higher trainings into practice. We 
will continually take rebirth in cyclic existence and there will be no opportunity for us to achieve 
liberation.    We can consider this from another perspective mentioned in the beginning of this chapter. 
When we are attached to our wealth and possessi ons, our minds are distracted and unable to 
achieve calm abiding. Without achieving calm abidin g, we will never be able to generate special 
insight and without special insight, we wi ll not be able to achieve liberation.  
 When we are only concerned with the happiness of this life, we will work very hard to 
accumulate wealth, property and possessions. Alon g the way, we will encounter difficulties and 
problems and we will not be happy. The gain then is very small despite getting what we wanted.   
 When we acquire these objects, the pleasure we experience is fleeting in nature but many 
problems arise from this acquisition. This is li kened to the carriage horse managing to eat only a 
few mouthfuls of grass along the road. For an instant, the horse may experience the pleasure of eating the grass but it is nothing compared to th e suffering that it has to endure the whole day. 
When we pursue wealth and possessions, driven by  our attachment to the happiness of this life, 
we are no different from the carriage horse.    As human beings, we always look down upon an imals, thinking that they are ignorant and 
stupid. But when it comes to looking for food and looking after themselves, animals are very capable.     
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
217  
If we human beings with our brains, intelligenc e and our ability to discriminate between right 
and wrong concentrate only on the happiness of this life by looking for wealth, food and 
possessions, then we are more foolish than these animals. This is something important for us to 
think about.  
 We work very hard in our careers or businesses for the happiness of this life thinking we have 
achieved something fantastic. However, what we are doing is no better than animals that also 
have the ability to look after themselves , finding food when they are hungry.  
 Sometimes, human beings are worse than animals.  T i g e r s  o r  l e o p a r d s  h u n t  o t h e r  a n i m a l s  f o r  
food but when they are full, they leave other animal s alone. Even if animals such as deer walk in 
front of them, they do not hunt for enjoyment.  We human beings do not have this sense of 
contentment. We always want to have more and we harm others in the process. In this sense, we are worse than animals. 
 
Animals have the mental poisons of jealousy and competitiveness but it is by no means certain 
that they are worse than us in th ese respects. Sometimes, it seems that we have more anger, more 
attachment, more ignorance and mo re jealousy than animals. When we look at sexual desire, 
animals have sex only during their mating season s. However, human beings are not like that and 
can experience sexual craving at any time and place.   The main advice is that we should be better than animals. As we are superior to them, we should start to work for the happiness of our future live s. Since we have now obtained a human life of 
leisure and opportunities, we should extract the essence of this life. If we just focus on the 
happiness of this life alone, then we are no bette r than animals. We have to switch our emphasis 
to looking for the happiness of our future lives.   On the positive side, human beings are definitely superior to animals in many ways. There are many things we can do but animals cannot. It is  up to us whether we want to use our human 
intelligence well. We can meditate on   developi ng bodhicitta and calm abiding, meditate on 
exchanging self for others etc. Only human beings  can do this. There is no way that animals can 
meditate on these topics.  
d" Developing the mind that strives for liberation by reflecting on its disadvantages 
(8, 82) 
The objects of desire will certainly perish, 
And then I shall fall into hellish states. 
However, Buddhahood itself is attained 
With just one millionth of the difficulty 
 
(8, 83) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
218  
Involved in continually exhausting myself 
For the sake of what is not very great. 
(Hence) the desirous experience greater misery th an (those following) th e Awakening way of life— 
But (for them) there is no Awakening. 
 
We have to reflect on the nature of the thing th at we are attached to. Our objects of desire are 
unstable, will disintegrate over time and are no t trustworthy. Attachment to these objects will 
only bring us problems in this and our future li ves. We will take rebirth in the lower realms and 
may even end up in hell. If this is the result of having attachment and running after wealth and 
possessions, then this goal does no t become worthwhile after all.  
  
Since time without beginning, we worked so hard  to achieve the happiness of our current life 
alone. If we exerted a fraction of that effort towards achieving enlightenment, we would probably 
be enlightened by now!   We could have achieved the ultimate happiness of  enlightenment in much less time and with far 
less difficulty but this is something that we did no t do. What did we do instead? We spent all our 
past lives working to achieve the happiness of thos e lives alone. Now we are left with nothing, 
still stuck in samsara. We did not get any happine ss or pleasure, only problems and suffering. It 
is really meaningless.  
 
Rather, we should work to achieve enlightenm ent - the ultimate happiness when all kinds of 
suffering are exhausted. This is what we should really want. The result of enlightenment will be 
far more meaningful.   For countless eons, since beginningless time, in ou r innumerable samsaric existences in the past, 
we only worked to achieve the happiness of those lives alone. However, there is a time limit to 
achieving enlightenment - only three countless grea t eons. It may seem a long time but at least 
there is a definite duration compared to the limitless eons we have already wasted.  If we reflect on what we do in our daily lives - working for 8, 10, 12 ho urs a day to achieve the 
happiness of this life – if we devoted just an ho ur every day to creating the causes to achieve 
enlightenment, then there would be  great profit in the future.  
 What we have discussed so far boils down to this : “What are we looking for in this life? What is 
our goal? Is this life more important or are our fu ture lives more important?”  If we are not clear 
about this, then everything else becomes difficult. 
Lesson 39   Chapter VIII - verse 84-90 
In the previous lesson, we studied the outline, “Developing the mind that strives for liberation 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
219  
by reflecting on its disadvantages,” which had 3 main points:  
• Understanding that all the objects of our desire  are by nature impermanent and unstable.   
• When we are motivated by attachment to acquire  our objects of desire, we engage in actions 
that create negative karma that pr opels us to the lower realms.   
• The results of our desire do not bring us any benefit. 
 
We have been stuck since beginningless time pursuing our objects of desire and attachment, 
incurring many faults and receiving very little bene fit in the process. The state of enlightenment, 
on the other hand, is stable and unchanging; it do es not entail any faults whatsoever and offers 
us great benefit. Furthermore, the amount of effort that we need to put into achieving 
enlightenment is only a fraction of what we have spent running af ter our objects of desire. Yet, 
it seems that we do not want to or are not inte rested in enlightenment.  We do not create the 
causes for it.   
Considering the problems and unhappiness that we have to endure in our pursuit of our objects 
of desire, what we get is of minimal benefit. If we put in less effort and yet were able to achieve 
something very beneficial, we would be considered  intelligent and wise. We  discover the faults 
of attachment by reflecting on the above points. Disillusionment with cyclic existence will then 
arise and we will develop the aspiration  for liberation and enlightenment. 
 
1) Instructions on cultivating concentration 
2) Abandoning the class of things incongruous with calm abiding  
a) Abandoning the bustle of the world 
b) Abandoning erroneous conceptions  
i) Cultivating weariness for desire

ii) Cultivating a joy for isolation  
(1) Reflecting on the disadvantages of desire 
(8,84) 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
220  
When one has contemplated the miseries of hell, 
(It will be clear that) there is nothing comparable 
To the harm caused to desirous beings, 
Even by weapons, poison, fire, ravines and foes. 
 
Motivated by our attachment to various objects of  desire, we look for material gain, respect, 
fame, etc. To achieve these goals, we engage in many negativities in our daily lives. As a result, we 
have to take rebirth in the lowe r realms. Once there, we are unable to cultivate virtue but we 
continually commit more negative actions. Therefor e, getting out of the lower realms is extremely 
difficult. The sufferings there are also far greate r than the worst suffering we can encounter in 
this life.    Thinking about the dangers we can experience in th is life frightens us. But, when we think about 
the consequences of our desire and attachment in our next life, we are not afraid at all! If we 
know how to achieve long-term happiness, we can discern between the suffering of this life and 
the suffering of the lower realms in our next li fe. Being killed by weapons or being poisoned 
cannot send us to the lower realms, whereas nega tive karma accumulated through our desire and 
attachment can do so.  
 When we reflect on this, we see that we are terrifi ed by things we need not be afraid of and we 
are not scared of the things we should be worried about.  Having obtained a human life of leisure 
and opportunity, as practising Buddhists we must not allow ourselves to fall into the lower realms. Otherwise, our human rebirth will have been completely meaningless.  
(2) Cultivating joy in isolation 
(a) Presentation 
(b) Explanation 
(a) Presentation 
(8, 85) 
Having in this way develope d disillusionment with desire, 
I should generate joy for solitude. 
 By seeing how attachment to sentient beings and/ or external possessions is the source of all our 
problems, we develop disillusionment with cyclic existence and these objects of desire. We begin 
to reflect on the benefits of living in isolat ion and we develop joy in living in solitude. 
 
(b) Explanation 
(i) A point on the excellences such as that of place 
(ii) The qualities of having obtained independence  
(iii) The qualities of contentment 
(i) A point on the excellences such as that of place 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
221  
(8, 85 cd)  
The fortunate ones stro ll in peaceful forests, 
Devoid of disputes and disturbing conceptions. 
(8, 86) 
(They live) in joyful houses of vast flat stones, 
Cooled by the sandal-scented moonlight, 
Fanned by the peaceful, silent forest breeze, 
Thinking of what is of benefit for others. 
 
Once we are living in solitude, there is no o pportunity for disputes to arise with regard to 
relationships or ownership of property. There are also no internal disputes, i.e. no arising of 
afflictions because our minds are at peace.  
 
The fortunate ones live in a peaceful place in the forest that is cool and bathed in moonlight and 
where there are no unpleasant sounds. They work on developing bodhicitta in their meditation 
sessions and between sessions they retain the wish to benefit others. When we are in such blessed 
places, our three mental poisons naturally beco me weaker and we become happier. This also 
benefits us in our future lives.     
(ii) The qualities of having obtained independence  
(8, 87) 
They dwell for as lo ng as they wish 
In empty houses, at the feet of trees and in caves. 
Having abandoned the pain of clingi ng to and guarding (possessions), 
They abide independent, free of care, 
 
A meditator can remain in physical isolation for as long as he wishes. He does not possess any 
property, or objects. He does not rely on his relationships with others and he is not worried 
about being disturbed or harmed by hi s enemies; so he is at peace.  
 O n  t h e  o t h e r  h a n d ,  l i v i n g  i n  s o c i e t y  m e a n s  w e  h a v e  n o  c h o i c e  b u t  t o  d e p e n d  o n  o t h e r s .  T o  achieve our goals, we have to deal with the rich and powerful and do as they say. We look down on our subordinates and are harmed by them in return. This dependence on others means we 
are not free, unlike someone who is living in solitude.  
 The benefit of going into retreat is freedom from  our daily problems. The main purpose of going 
into retreat is to develop our minds to benefit others. All the points discussed so far in this 
chapter relate to the development of calm abiding.  If we want to develop calm abiding, we must 
go into isolation.  
 
A rich businessman, who is married with three kids, told me his own happiness is not good 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
222  
enough. He needs to make his wife  and children happy. He ends up with so much suffering. His 
wife constantly nags him about his business trips to  China. He resorts to drinking to forget his 
problems. Now he has become an alcoholic. This is the kind of unhappiness he has. 
(iii) The qualities of contentment 
(8, 88) 
Living as they choose, desireless, 
Having no ties with anyone— 
Even the powerful have difficulty finding 
A life as happy and content as this. 
 
When we live in isolation, our contentment comes from being able to live on minimal 
necessities. It is important that we  are not attached to our retreat place. Living in solitude, there 
is no contact with the outside world and there are no ties to people. It is said that those who 
adopt this kind of lifestyle experience a special ki nd of happiness that is out of reach for even the 
most powerful worldly gods like Indra.   
 Once we have found the necessary conditions such as an appropriate place and accessibility of 
provisions, we go into isolation and engage in the yoga of the four se ssions. Gradually, as we 
adjust to the rhythm of our new life, it is said that we will start to experience this special kind of 
happiness. Without going into isolation, there is no way that we can experience this happiness 
and we have no idea how special this kind of feeling can be.   By reflecting on and understanding the faults of desire and attachment and the benefits of 
relying on isolation, we make a firm decision, “This is what I am going to do. Even if I die soon 
after going into retreat, I will have no regrets, ” because all our time would have been spent on 
virtue. On the other hand, it is pointless to live  to even a hundred amidst the hustle and bustle 
of worldly life, as those years would have been  spent in accumulating nonvirtuous karma.  
 
These three verses discuss the benefits of living  in isolation which we covered earlier in this 
chapter. Earlier in this chapter, there was a discu ssion on the benefits of living in solitude, which 
is meant to generate enthusiasm and delight in our minds for physical and mental isolation. Why 
is there a need then to mention these benefits again? Suppose we went into isolation on the basis of having reflected on these benefits and the fa ults of desire and attachment. After being in 
retreat for six months or a year, boredom may set in and we start to think, “Maybe I should not 
be doing this.” In order to maintain our enthusia sm for remaining in retreat, we have to reflect 
again on these points. This reason is explained in some commentaries.

a) The way to meditate on the equality of self and other

(8, 89) 
Having in such ways as these 
Thought about the excellences of solitude, 
I should completely pacify distorted conceptions 
And meditate on the Awakening Mind. 
 
(8,90) 
First of all, I should make an effort 
To meditate upon the equali ty between self and others. 
I should protect all be ings as I do myself 
Because we are all equal in (wanting ) pleasure and (not wanting) pain. 
 
Having in such ways as these thou ght about the excellences of solitude : What are the benefits of isolating 
ourselves from the external bustle of life? What are the benefits of isolating our minds from all 
discursive thoughts once we are in isolation? Wh at are the benefits of isolating our minds from 
the self-cherishing thought (the meaning of isolatio n when it is explained on the secret level)?  
 
When we are living in isolation, our bodies, speech and minds stay in isolation. By isolating 
them, we create the causes of happiness for both this life and our future lives. Externally, we 
isolate ourselves from the bustle of worldly life. Internally, we turn our minds inwards, isolating 
them from all our discursive thoughts - our emot ional roller coasters. We also isolate the mind 
on the secret level by distancing the mind  from our usual self-cherishing thoughts.  
 When we are able to live in isolation in thes e ways, the discursive thoughts and afflictions 
gradually become weaker and are then pacified. Af terwards, we are able to develop bodhicitta. In 
isolation, we repeatedly recall the faults of follow ing after desire and living in the midst of people 
while constantly remembering the benefits of remain ing in isolation.  Reflec ting in this way, we 
abandon any thoughts of giving up and keep ou r enthusiasm for staying in isolation alive.  
 B y  l i v i n g  i n  i s o l a t i o n  i n  t h e s e  w a y s ,  w e  s t r i v e  t o  d e v e l o p  c a l m  a b i d i n g .  W h e n  w e  a r e  a b l e  t o  achieve calm abiding, our bodies and minds beco me serviceable so that  w e  c a n  d i r e c t  t h e m  
towards any virtue at will. Developing bodhicitta is  easy with this serviceability of body and mind. 
Lama Tsongkhapa mentions that when calm abiding is achieved, we can place and fix our minds 
on any chosen object of virtue at any time an d place. Once our minds are fixed on the chosen 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
224  
object of virtue, they remain as immovable as the king of mountains66. 
 
Our main meditation is to develo p bodhicitta. The way to develop bodhicitta is to meditate on 
exchanging self and others. However, in order to produce the attitude that cherishes others more 
than ourselves, we first must meditate on and realis e the equality of self and others. It is equal in 
the sense that all beings equally want happiness and do not want suffering. 
Lesson 40 (new term for ABC)   Chapter VIII - verse 89-90 
The prayer we have just recited states: 
 Understanding that the precious freedom of this rebirth is found only  once,  
Is greatly meaningful, and is  difficult to find again, 
Please bless me to generate the mind that unceasingly, 
Day and night, takes its essence.  
We should reflect on this regularly and, day an d night, strive to take  the essence of this 
meaningful opportunity we have. Not only have we obtained a human body but our faculties and 
senses are all complete. We have the opportunity to listen to and study Dharma teachings. When 
we reflect deeply on this, we will be amazed and rejoice in our good fortune.   While all the conditions for us to study and practise exist, it remains up to us whether we want to 
study or practise the teachings. But th e opportunity to do so is there.  
 It is questionable whether we will obtain such a rebirth again in our next lives. As the prayer says, 
The precious freedom of this rebirth is foun d only once and is difficult to find again . Since we have 
obtained such a rebirth, it is important that we do something meaningful with it. We already 
find it difficult to practise with all the conduciv e conditions we have. There is no guarantee that 
our faculties or senses will be complete or pe rfect even if we were to obtain another human 
rebirth. Practice becomes even more difficult then . So, we need to study and practise right now.  
 
I know many of you recite this prayer, The Foundation of All Good Qualities,  on a daily basis but 
you should not leave it simply at that level. You should reflect on the meaning of the verses, 
especially the one dealing with the precious huma n rebirth. By doing so, it encourages you to 
practise the Dharma.  
 
Every day, we can see for ourselves the instabil ity of this human life. We are not immune to 
                                             
 
66 Lama Tsongkhapa says in his Song of Experience  (stanza 19a-d): Concentration is a king ruling the mind; When placed

induces the great bliss of serviceable body and mind .

sickness. Many people are well one day and sick the next and within a short time, they die and 
move on to the next life. While we are still heal thy, we should not postpone our Dharma studies 
and practice, as it will be too late to do so when we suddenly fall ill. We may wish to practise the 
Dharma then but it will be too late as our bodies will not allow us to do so.  The same applies to 
our studies. They should not be postponed. When we have the time, it is good to continue to come to class to listen to the teachings and to apply and put into practice, as much as we can, 
whatever we have learnt.  
  
All of you already know this so I am just remi nding you of these points. The classes stopped for 
three months. Whatever you did during that peri od, good or bad, that time is gone and can 
never return. We need to reflect and focus on how we are going to make this opportunity we now have meaningful and what we plan to do in the future.  
C. The way to train in the absorptions included in the common and uncommon calm abidings 
a. Explaining the chapter’s material 
1) Instructions on cultivating absorption 
2) Abandoning the class of things incongruous with calm abiding 
3) How to cultivate calm abiding 
2) Abandoning the class of things incongruous with calm abiding 
We will not be able to achieve our goals when ou r minds are distracted.  To  stop distraction, we 
have to stop the class of things that is discor dant with the concentrated mind and hinders the 
development of concentration.  
 
Attachment to inner and outer phenomena is th e major cause of mental distraction.  Outer 
phenomena refer to things like wealth, reputa tion and possessions. Inner phenomena refer to 
our desire for other people.  We are never satisfied with our objects of desire and we constantly 
crave to be with them. Blinded in this way, we  are unable to understand the true nature of 
phenomena (emptiness) and the workings of cause and effect. There is no disenchantment with 
cyclic existence when there is strong attachment in our minds. Even if there is some sense of 
renunciation, when desire arises strongly, that  slight disenchantment quickly disappears.  
 
Separation from the object of attachment brings suffering, unhappiness and sorrow. Due to our 
attachment, our minds are completely distracted. Th is is how we lead our lives, wasting them and 
making them meaningless.   If we want to achieve calm abiding, we have to rely on contentment and isolation. In isolation, there are fewer obstacles to the development of the concentrated mind. As long as we remain 
living in the hustle and bustle of worldly life we will have obstacles. 
3) How to cultivate calm abiding 
a) The way to meditate on the equality of self and other 
i) A brief presentation 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION

b) The way to exchange self and other 
i) A brief presentation 
(8, 89) 
Having in such ways as these

And meditate on the Awakening Mind. 
 
(8, 90) 
First of all, I should make an effort 
To meditate upon the equali ty between self and others. 
I should protect all be ings as I do myself 
Because we are all equal in (wanting ) pleasure and (not wanting) pain. 
 
Having in such ways as these, Thou ght about the excellences of solitude,  refers to our having 
contemplated the benefits of isolation and genera ting the determination henceforth to leave our 
worldly lives and rely on isolation.  In isolation,  we strive to develop calm abiding and meditate 
on bodhicitta. 
 How do we do this? First, we reflect on the equa lity of self and others and on this basis, we 
meditate on exchanging self and others.  We do many things to achieve happiness and to el iminate suffering for ourselves. We should do 
likewise for others and help them to achieve happiness and remove their suffering. Why do we 
need to do this? Because we are all equal in wanting to be happy and in wishing to avoid 
suffering. 
Lesson 41   Chapter VIII - verse 90-96 
This is advice from the Kadampa masters: Wh en our minds are virtuous and our motivation 
positive, then our physical and verbal actions will  naturally be virtuous and positive. We will not 
harm but instead benefit others. Conversely, when our minds are in negative, non-virtuous 
states, it is very difficult to ge nerate positive behaviour. We are most likely to give problems to 
others and be harmed by them in return. The Kadampa masters are advising us to generate a 
good heart and develop a positive mind and motivation.  
 
We are now studying the practice of exchanging ourselves for others and developing bodhicitta, 
the main point of which is to develop the virtuous, positive mind. A positive state of mind leads 
to positive and beneficial behaviour that helps us  to become good-hearted, virtuous people. It is 
v e r y  d i f f i c u l t  t o  c h a n g e  o u r  m i n ds  o v e r n i g h t .  W e  h a v e  t o  s t a r t  r e du c i n g  n e g a t i v e  p h y s i c a l  a n d 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
227  
verbal actions by reducing our negative states of  mind. While we may not be able to completely 
remove such negativities, we can work towards reducing them.  
 
What are the benefits to being good-hearted peop le? We will be protected by the worldly gods 
who delight in virtue and receive blessings from  the buddhas and bodhisattvas. Temporal goals 
are easily achieved. When death comes, we will move on easily to the next life and achieve 
enlightenment very quickly.
