1’ Explaining the purpose of meditating on the equality of self and other 
(8, 91)  
Although there are many different part s and aspects such as the hand, 
As a body that is to be protected, they are one. 
Likewise, all the different sentient beings , in their pleasure and their pain, 
Have a wish to be happy th at is the same as mine. 
 
In the brief presentation on this particular poin t covered in the last lesson, we were advised to 
meditate on bodhicitta on the basis of equalisi ng ourselves with others. We recognise that we 
want to be happy and we strive to achieve ha ppiness for ourselves. We should likewise seek 
happiness for others. Just as we work to eliminate our own suffering,  we are advised to generate 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
228  
the same wish in relation to others and to help them remove their sufferings. This is illustrated 
in the last 2 lines of Verse 90:  
 
(8,90) 
I should protect all be ings as I do myself 
Because we are all equal in (wanting ) pleasure and (not wanting) pain. 
 
Qualm : There are too many sentient beings in the 6 realms – in the hells, as hungry ghosts, 
animals, human beings, demi-gods and gods and each  realm has different types of sufferings. If I 
focus on this limitless number of  sentient be ings, there is no way I can equalise myself and 
others as I cannot see others as equal to my self since their sufferings are so varied.   
 Answer : Let us apply this analogy: Our bodies have various parts – arms, legs, heads, etc. Each 
bodily part is different but when it comes to prot ecting ourselves, we see them all as one entity, 
‘our body.’   
 In the same way, there are many different type s of sentient beings, with their own kinds of 
suffering and problems. Like us, however, they wa nt to be happy and do not want to suffer. In 
this sense, we are the same. By focusing on the suffering of these beings, we generate the thought 
to eliminate their  suffering and to help th em to achieve the happiness they are seeking. 
 
2’  The way to meditate on the equality of self and other 
a’ Abandoning the [position that holds tha t] it is unreasonable to equate discarding 
pleasure and taking on suffering 
b’ Explaining the reasons why it is reasonable to meditate on such an equation 
a’ Abandoning the [position that holds that] it is unreasonable to equate discarding pleasure and 
taking on suffering 
(8, 92) 
The suffering that I experience 
Does not cause any harm to others. 
But that suffering (is mine) because of my conceiving of (myself as) "I"; 
Thereby it becomes unbearable. 
 
(8,93) 
Likewise, the misery of others 
Does not befall me. 
Nevertheless, by conceiving of (others as) "I," their suffering becomes mine; 
Therefore, it too should be hard to bear. 
 
Qualm : Others people's problems and suffering cannot hurt me and my  suffering does not affect 
them. How can eliminating our suffering and the   suffering of others be the same?  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
229  
 
Answer : When we suffer from physical pain, it is true  that that pain does not affect other people 
and remains our problem. Why do we experience th at suffering? This is because of our grasping 
at the “I” and labelling it, “This is my suffering.” We conceive of our bodies as being “mine” and 
belonging to the “I”. When the body is hurt , the mind cannot bear the pain and suffering 
ensues. We need to remove that suffering, to be free of it.  
 When others suffer, although their problems ca nnot directly affect us and their pain cannot 
become our pain, we still need to remove their su ffering if we are  bodhisattvas. Why?  Because a 
bodhisattva cherishes all sentient beings just as he cherishes himself. It is unbearable for the 
bodhisattva to see others suffer.   Therefore, once we see that we and others ar e equal in not wanting suffering and equal in 
wanting happiness, then it is appropriate to stri ve to eliminate the sufferings of others and to 
help them to find happiness.  When our children fall sick, why do we find it so unbearable? Because we see  our children as 
being “mine.” We are not so agitated when other pe ople’s children fall ill because we do not have 
this notion of seeing their children as our own.   The same attitude applies to our possessions such as  our cars, homes or  watches. We get so upset 
when our watches get scratched. Why does this disturbance occur? Because we hold on to the 
concept of ownership, that this watch is “min e.” So, when someone accidentally scratches our 
watch, we become so unhappy.  Once we offer our watch as a gift to someone else , what happens to that watch no longer bothers 
us. Our feelings towards our watch before and afte r we give it away are completely different as 
our attitude towards it has changed. But it is th e same object. The ownership of the object does 
not come from the object's side. The object doesn't say, “I belong to you,” or, “I belong to him.” 
The whole idea of the “I” comes from oneself an d grasping at that “I” makes one think, “It 
belongs to me.”   
The Lamrim Chenmo  refers to the example of the two moun tains: the one on this side and the 
one on the other side. How do we posit the mountain  that is nearer to us as being “near”? It is 
dependent on the relationship between the two mountains with us. Whether this mountain is 
nearer to or further away from us depends on  us. It does not depend on the mountain.  
 
The “I” does not exist independently, is not self -sufficient and cannot stand on its own. Others 
exist in the same way. When we refer  to  the co ncept of “others,” this is  not something that is 
autonomous or independent. This shows that ph enomena exist through the force of conception 
or mental labelling and do not exist independently.  They do not exist by themselves but rather by 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
230  
dependence on others.  
 Just as we cherish ourselves through the force of familiarity, by cultivating the sense of others 
being equal to ourselves, we can become familiar with cherishing others.  
 For example, the very first time  we met the person that is to  become our friend, we did not 
consider him as a friend. But after more encoun ters and through familiarity, he becomes our 
“friend” and we find it unbearable when something bad happens to him.  That friend did not exist independently from his own side. In the same way as our friends’ problems become 
unbearable for us, it is possible to equalise ou rselves with others and to become motivated to 
help them overcome their difficulties.   
b’ Explaining the reasons why it is reasonable to meditate on such an equation  
1” An extensive explanation 
a” Stating the reason  
b” Establishing the pervasion 
c” Dispelling the hindrance of thinking that it is unsuitable to meditate on the equality of self and other 
2” Synopsis 
3” Abandoning objections 
a” Stating the reason 
(8, 94) 
Hence I should dispel the misery of others 
Because it is suffering , just like my own, 
And I should benefit others 
Because they are sentient be ings, just like myself. 
 
Why is it appropriate to eliminate the suffering of  other sentient beings? Because it is suffering, 
and that suffering is meaningless. Just as we el iminate thirst and hunger for ourselves, it is 
appropriate to do likewise for others.  It is also appropriate to accomplish the welf are and happiness of others. Why? Simply because 
they are sentient beings like ourselves.  We and others are equal in the sense in wanting happiness.  We and others are equal in  not wanting to suffer.  
We and others are equal in being selfless (i.e . lacking an inherently existent “self”). 
 Having said that, we can check whether it is po ssible to apprehend others as ourselves and vice 
versa. Is it possible to conceive of another sentie nt being as being oneself?  Is there any fallacy in 
holding such a conception?   
b” Establishing the pervasion 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
231  
(1) The actual established pervasion 
(2) Dispelling hindrances to that 
 
(8, 95) 
When both myself and others 
Are similar in that we wish to be happy, 
What is so special about me? 
Why do I strive for my happiness alone? 
 
(8, 96) 
And when both myself and others 
Are similar in that we do not wish to suffer, 
What is so special about me? 
Why do I protect myself and not others? 
 
There is no reason whatsoever to justify simply accomplishing happiness for ourselves and 
eliminating our own suffering and not helping others to do the same. So when we work for our 
own happiness and strive to remove our suffering, we should do likewise for other living beings. 
Lesson 42   Chapter VIII - verse 97-102 
Whether the outcome of a course of action is po sitive or negative depends on the originating 
intention or motivation. A virtuous intention produces positive results and a negative intention 
produces bad results. Therefore, we should always  rely on mindfulness and vigilance to keep our 
minds in a virtuous state. We assert that attend ing teachings is a virtuous act. However, if the 
motivation for listening to the teachings is not virtuous, then being present and listening to the 
teachings is not necessarily virtuous.  
 
A beneficial motivation would be thinking, “Wha tever knowledge I get in class, I am going to 
blend it with my mind and try to  practise it as much as possibl e.” When we make the effort to 
practise, we can have positive experiences that wi ll give us the understanding and confidence that 
the Dharma we are studying and practising really  works. What is the result of such positive 
experiences? Faith in the Dharma will naturally arise and faith in our virtuous friend and guru 
will be generated from the depths of our hearts. 
 
The problem is that people attend but do not a pply the teachings in thei r daily lives. When the 
teachings we hear remain simply at an intellectual level for us, without practising them, it is very 
difficult for us to generate faith in the Dhar ma. We do not taste the Dharma. Without such 
faith, it becomes very difficult to talk about generating faith in our guru who gives us the 
teachings. But when we blend the teachings with our minds and try to practise them, then over 
time, the quality of our minds will improve; we become more good-hearted and so forth. Our 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
232  
faith in the Dharma and our guru also increase.  
 Therefore, it is important that before engaging in any action, we should ask ourselves, “Am I 
motivated by a positive or negative state of mind?”   
 Kyabje Zopa Rinpoche has mentioned several time s that his desire to start Amitabha Buddhist 
Centre was so powerful, it was akin to the urge  to  vomit. We should  understand that, from 
Rinpoche’s side, such a special and powerful urge to  start a centre does not apply to all centres.  
 In the case of ABC, however, Rinpoche had this very strong wish to start the Centre. He must 
have seen the great benefit of doing so. Since the existence of ABC is so beneficial, as its 
members, it is our responsibility to help improve and sustain the Centre.  We should keep this in 
mind. You should feel that this is your Centre. If you are not from this Centre, this advice may 
not apply to you. But, for the members, it is  our job to at least not allow the Centre to 
degenerate, to remember Rinpoche’s origin al purpose in starting the centre.   
 We are all human and disagreements will arise. This is to be expected but ultimately, we should not do anything that is not beneficial for the Centre. That is not good. I have mentioned this 
before and we should always keep this in mind.   The responsibility for sustaining and improving the Centre does not rest only with the five or six 
people in the Executive Committee,  but with all of us. We should always think about how we 
can  improve the Centre.  I am not saying that we  are not doing this but we need to constantly 
improve our Centre, which benefits so many people. Our programme is so full of activities almost every day, except Mondays.    For myself, I was personally selected by Rinpoc he to come here, although he had originally 
planned to send me to Japan. Maybe the vegetarian food here is nicer (laughter ).  
 Since Rinpoche sent me here, whether I am capable or not, I am here to serve the Centre. That’s 
how I see my role here. When anyone needs help , from my side, if I can help, I do so. But 
anyone in need of help must let me know. If they  do not tell me, I won’t know they need help. It 
is not my nature to initiate this  type of assistance, like “I can do this and that for you.” Some 
people may have the mistaken notion that I am he lping some people and not others. It is not like 
that but if people need help, they have to tell  me. To date, I don’t recall having turned down a 
request for help if I have been able to be of assistance.    Whether we are the teacher or students of this Centre, we should think of ways and means to help improve the centre. We can help bring inte rested friends here and introduce them to the 
Dharma provided they are interested. Every time  we bring somebody to the Dharma, it is an 
improvement. If that person is not interested, there is not much we can do but, at least, we tried.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
233  
 
W e  s h o u l d  a l l  s t r i v e  t o  b e  k i n d  a n d  g o o d - h e a r t e d ,  t o  h a v e  v i r t u o u s  m i n d s  a n d  v i r t u o u s  intentions.  When our actions are motivated by the good heart, the results will be virtuous. 
When our motivation is negative, then we may be doing many prayers, sadhanas, daily 
commitments, prostrations and circumambulations, but it is very difficult  for these actions to 
yield positive results.  
 
The collection of precepts and the advice give n by the great Kadampa ma sters of the past is 
always the same, “Be a good-hearted person.” All their instructions can be condensed into this 
single piece of advice.   
 When we are good-hearted, it does not matter if we do not do a lot of extensive practices.  It is 
alright because the benefit of being good-hearted is that we will  enjoy temporal happiness and, 
ultimately, gain  enlightenment. We will not have  any difficulties at the ti me of death and we will 
move on to a good rebirth in our next life. That is why the Kadampa masters advise us to practise 
with a good heart at all times.

b) The way to exchange self and other 
(2) Dispelling hindrances to that 
The text mentions that we need to  eliminate the suffering of others  by meditating on the equality 
of self and other.  
 
Qualm : Since the suffering of others does not harm me  directly now, it is not necessary for me to 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
234  
protect others from their suffering.   
 
Answer : Based on this argument, then it is also ina ppropriate for us to make preparations in this 
life to avoid suffering in our future lives since th at future suffering does not affect us now. There 
is no need for us to make any preparations now for our future happiness. 
 
Similarly, we need not take any preventive meas ures now to forestall problems we may encounter 
in our old age since we are young and those difficult ies do not affect us now.  We need not make 
any preparations to counter problems  we ma y experience tomorrow since tomorrow’s problems 
cannot harm us today. 
 
(8, 97) 
Why should I protect them, 
If their suffering does not cause me any harm?- 
Then why protect myself against future suffering, 
If it causes me no harm now? 
 
Why protect myself against future suffe ring/If it causes me no harm now?:  We apply this reasoning in 
order to stop thinking that the suffering of othe rs is not our concern since we are not personally 
affected by it.   
 
Qualm : I understand that the suffering of others and my own future suffering in my old age and 
future lives are similar in that they do not harm  me now. But they are not the same because my 
own future suffering will definitely happen to me.  
  
Answer : The response to this qualm appears in Verse 98.  
 
(8,98) 
It is a mistaken conception to think 
That I shall experience (the suffering of my next life). 
For it is another person who dies, 
And another that will be reborn. 
 
We plan for our retirement and old age because we  do not want to suffer when we are old. Such 
preparations are made because we conceive of our present self and our future self to be one and 
the same, just as we feel that the person we were  this morning is the same as the person we are 
this evening.  But such apprehension of the “I” is , in fact, an erroneous or mistaken  conception.  
 
The reason that this conception is mistaken appears in the last line of the same verse: For it is 
another person who dies,/And another that will be reborn.  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
235  
For example, someone who is presently a human being but destined for a rebirth in the hells in 
his next life has the consciousness of a human bein g now and when he is reborn as a hell being, 
he will have the consciousness of a hell being.  These two selves of the person are the same. 
 
We discussed in an earlier module whether the inhe rently existent “I” is one with the aggregates 
or different from the aggregate. All phen omena are necessarily  one or different.  
 
We instinctively feel that the present “I” and the “I” that will exist tomorrow to be one entity.  
Because of that, the “I” that exis ts in the morning works very hard to avoid the suffering that will 
be experienced at night. We cannot see that the present “I”  and the future “I”  are, in fact, 
separate and different.   
 
(8,99) 
-Surely whenever there is suffering 
The (sufferers) must protec t themselves from it- 
Yet the suffering of the foot  is not that of the hand, 
Why then does one protect the other? 
 
Qualm : My suffering is mine and mine alone and can only be experienced by myself. No one else 
can experience my suffering for me so I have to  protect myself from this  suffering. Likewise, 
other people’s problems are their own business an d they should be responsible for solving their 
own problems. Their problems are not my problems. Surely whenever there is suffering, the (sufferers) 
must protect themselves from it, refers to this line of reasoning.   
 Answer : Yet the suffering of the foot is  not that of the hand,/Why th en does one protect the other  ? 
 
Since the one experiencing the suffering is differen t (i.e. I and other), the suffering of others is 
not my concern. But if we assert this, then when our foot steps on a thorn, there is no reason for our hand to pull the thorn out since it is the foot  that is experiencing the pain and not the hand. 
The pain of the foot need not be the concern of the hand.   
 We will come up with many reasons and justificatio ns to argue against  equa lising self and other, 
“I don’t need to do that because……..” We have  to eliminate them one by one by applying the 
various counter-arguments in this text.  
(8, 100) 
-Although this may not be justified, 
It is done because of grasping at a self- 
Yet surely whatever is not justified for myself or others 
Should at all costs be rejected  
 Although it is the foot that is  experiencing the pain, the hand instinctively pulls the thorn out. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
236  
This is due to our familiarity with grasping at the self as “I” since beginningless time. We 
apprehend our bodies as belongin g to the self, as being “mine”. 
 
Here, we are using self-grasping as justific ation for our actions. This is rebutted: Yet surely whatever 
is not justified for myself or othe rs,/Should at all costs be rejected. The apprehension of ourselves and 
others as being independent entities is a mistaken  conception that leads only to unpleasant and 
undesirable things. 
c” Dispelling the hindrance of thinking that it is unsuitable to meditate on the equality of self and 
other 
(8,101) 
Such things as a continuum and an aggregation 
Are false in the same way as  prayer beads and an army. 
There is no (real) owner of suffering, 
Therefore who has control over it? 
 
(8, 102)

There can be no distinction at all be tween (that of myself and others). 
Thus I shall dispel it because it hurts. 
 
Qualm : While the present person and the person of  the future are not the same, nevertheless 
they are of the same continuum. I accept my foot and hand are different but they belong to the 
same collection as they are all  parts of the same body. 
Lesson 43   Chapter VIII - verse 101-106 
The Kadampa masters point out, “The purpose of  all the Buddha’s teachings and all the great 
treatises and commentaries that clarify the meanin g of those teachings is to help us transform 
our minds for the better. When the mind does not improve, then even if we strive to accumulate 
virtue with our bodies and speech for eons, it is very difficult for those practices to become the 
cause for liberation.”  
 This advice reminds us of the purpose for coming to class and listening to the teachings, i.e., to 
improve the quality of our minds. Regardless of th e nature of our virtuous activities, we should 
always ask ourselves, “How can doing this help me to improve my mind?”  
W e  s h o u l d  r e l y  o n  m i n d f u l n e s s  a n d  v i g i l a n c e  w h e n  w e  e n g a g e  i n  o u r  D h a r m a  p r a c t i c e .  W e  
should check to see if the practice acts as a co ndition for us to change and improve our minds, 
whether it is beneficial for our minds or not.  When the mind does no t change, it is like 
immersing a stone in water. No matter how long  it remains in the water, the stone doesn't 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION

change.  
 It is important to generate a pure and the corre ct motivation for attending these classes. We 
should always remind ourselves why we are here , that we are here to learn how to better our 
minds. The purpose of studying the Dharma is no t to use it to check the minds and actions of 
others. The Dharma is for checking our own mind s and trying to improve ourselves. Using the 
Dharma against other people is a mistake.  That is not why we study the Dharma.  
 
Qualm : Since others’ suffering does not affect us di r e c t l y ,  w e  d o  n o t  n e e d  t o  d o  a n y t h i n g  t o  
eliminate their suffering.  
Answer : If that is the case, then there is also no  need for us to prep are for and prevent the 
suffering of our old age since that suffering does not affect us now.  
 Qualm : But we will be experiencing the suffering of  old age ourselves. Since we will have to 
endure that future suffering, it is valid for us to try to prevent that suffering as much as we can.  
Answer : We feel that way because we think that ou r current “I” and our future “I” when we are 
old are one and the same person. Because of ou r  mistaken conception of holding these two 
persons to be one, we strive to eliminate the suffe ring we anticipate will befall us in our old age.  
 
Qualm : Our suffering is ours alone and has to be experienced by ourselves. We cannot 
experience other people’s suffering. Since the ‘experie ncer’ of the suffering is separate in this way, 
the suffering of others need not be my concern  
Answer : Then, why is it that our hand instinctivel y move to remove the thorn in our foot? The 
‘experiencer’ of the suffering is the foot and not the hand.

FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
238  
b) The way to exchange self and other 
c” Dispelling the hindrance of thinking that it is unsuitable to meditate on the equality of self and 
other 
Qualm : The person now and the person of the future  may not be one, but they belong to the 
same continuum. Our feet and hands are also diffe rent but they belong to the same collection, 
our body. Therefore, our action is justified.  
 
(8, 101) 
Such things as a continuum and an aggregation 
Are false in the same way as  prayer beads and an army. 
There is no (real) owner of suffering, 
Therefore who has control over it? 
 
(8, 102) 
There being no (inherent) owner of suffering, 
There can be no distinction at all be tween (that of myself and others). 
Thus I shall dispel it because it hurts. 
 
We think of the “I,” who experiences the passage of time, as the same, un changing person as the 
present “I”. When we consider our collection of a ggregates, we also think of them as existing very 
solidly and independently.  Such conceptions ar e refuted by the first two lines of Verse 101: Such 
things as a continuum and an aggregation/Are false in the same way as pray er beads and an army.  
 
We conceive of the “I” as existing inherently an d independently. In the same way, we think of 
our bodies and minds as being solid and real, exis ting independently and inherently. But that is 
not how they truly exist. E.g. A rosary (or mala) is made up of many prayer beads. When all the 
prayer beads are strung together, the result is a rosary. We impute  the collection of prayer beads to 
be a rosary. 
 How does an army exist? It exists in dependence on a group of soldiers. When a group of soldiers come together, we impute and label it as an army. This is how an army exists.  
The rosary does not exist inherently and independently other than being merely imputed  upon the 
collection of prayer beads. Why is this so? When  we search for the inherently existent rosary 
amongst the individual prayer beads, we will  not be ab le to find it there. If there is an inherently 
existent rosary,  when we look amongst the bead s, we should find the rosary there. But after 
checking and setting aside each and every single prayer bead, we do not end up with a rosary. We 
apply this reasoning to the army . When we look for the army am ongst the group of soldiers, we 
will not be able to find the army. 
 We can relate this to our physical and mental aggregates, which form the bases for the label “I” 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
239  
the person. Some tenet systems assert that the “I ” is the continuum of th e physical aggregates 
while others assert that the “I” is found within the mental consciousness. Yet others claim that 
the person is the mere collection of the aggregates. 
 
The analogy of the rosary and army helps us  to eliminate the wrong consciousness which 
apprehends any continuum or collection as being inherently existent. Using the same logic and 
applying it to the person, we eliminate the possibi lities of the person being (1) the continuum of 
the physical aggregates (2) the mental consciousne ss or (3) the mere collecti on of the aggregates.  
 Since the person does not inherently exist, the pain, happiness or suffering experienced by that 
person cannot possibly exist inherently as well. As  there is no inherently existent continuum and 
collection, the “I” that we imputed cannot exist inhe rently either. Since this is the case, then the 
suffering that this “I”  experien ces also cannot exist inherently. Conversely, when there is no 
inherently existent suffering, there cannot be an  inherently existent “I” experiencing  it.  
 Understanding the above argument helps us understand selflessness. Proponents of the first tenet system, the Great  Exposition  school, assert that the person  is the mere collection of the 
aggregates.  If such a person exists, then we should  be able to find this person amongst either the 
physical or mental  aggregates. But such a pers on cannot be found. Nor can such a person be 
found within the continuum of the aggregates. This  shows that the person is not a collection or 
continuum of the aggregates.  This means that the inherently existent “I” does  not exist. How about the suffering experienced 
by such an “I”? We grasp at our suffering as real and solid. But in the absence of an inherently or 
independently existent “I,” how can the suffering  e x p e r i e n c e d  b y  s u c h  a n  “ I ”  b e  i n h e r e n t l y  
existent'?  Since there is no independently existent “I,” ther e is no independent owner of one's suffering. As 
there is no independent owner of the suffering,  in the same way, there is no independent 
“other.” When there is no in dependent “other,”  they cannot experience an independent 
suffering.   Neither the “'I” nor “other” exists inherently. Ne ither does the suffering of oneself nor others 
exists inherently. So whether we are talking abou t the “I” or “other,” one's suffering or other's 
suffering, they are all equal in the sense of be ing not existent independently or inherently. 
 The essence of this argument is to show that there is no independent owner of suffering. It is 
precisely this wrong conception that a fflicts us, the thinking that, “It is my problem. I  have to 
endure this . I  have no choice.” When we look at the suffering of others, we are afflicted by the 
conception of the independent “other” owning  that independently existent suffering.  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
240  
The conclusion must be that there cannot be an y suffering that is owned by the independently 
existent “I” and there cannot be a suffering that  is independently owned by others. That being 
the case, we should work to eliminate the suffering of others as well. 
2” Synopsis 
(8, 102 cd) 
Why am I so certain (that I shouldn't eliminate the suffering of others)? 
 
(8, 103) 
-But (since neither the suffering no r the sufferer truly exist), why should  I turn away the misery of all?- 
This is no ground for argument, 
For if I prevent my own (sufferings), surely I should prevent the (s ufferings) of all. 
If not, since I am just like (other) sentient beings , (I should not prevent my  own suffering, either). 
 
The main obstacle that prevents us from generati ng any interest in the problems and suffering of 
others is our conception  of the inherently existe nt “I” and the inherently existent “other,” seeing 
them as totally unconnected, separate  enti ties. This mistaken conception (or wrong 
consciousness) prevents us from having any wish to help to eliminate the suffering of others.  
 
Qualm : Since there is no inherently existent person , therefore suffering cannot affect that person. 
If the suffering is not being experienced by an yone, then there is no need to eliminate the 
suffering.  
 
Answer : Ultimately, there is no person experiencing suffering as there is no inherently existent 
suffering. But, conventionally, there is a person and there is suffering, so the suffering is being 
experienced by someone. At a conventional level, others suffer as we do. Therefore, just as we 
work to eliminate our own suffering, we should do likewise for others.  
3” Abandoning objections 
a” It is not valid [to say that] a bodhisattva hi mself falls prey to suffering by reflecting on 
the suffering of sentient beings 
b” The need to reflect upon suffering 
a” It is not valid [to say that] a bodhisattva hims elf falls prey to suffering by reflecting on the 
suffering of sentient beings 
Some people believe that  reflecting on the suffering of others only serves to increase their own 
problems and unhappiness.  There seems to be an  internal contradiction. On the one hand, we 
want to eliminate suffering. On the other hand, we  have to generate compassion, but doing so by 
reflecting on the sufferings of others only serv es to increase our own suffering. How then can 
suffering be eliminated? 
 
(8,104) 
-Since this compassion wi ll bring me much misery, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
241  
Why should I exert myself to develop it?- 
Should I contemplate the suffe ring of living creatures, 
How could the misery of compassion be more? 
 
Qualm :  In order to generate compassion, we have to  reflect on the suffering  of others. When we 
do this,  we also suffer. We are not abandoning suffering then but creating more suffering. This 
advice appears to be teaching us to generate, not eliminate, suffering.  
 There are different answers to this qualm.  
Answer #1: 
When we are reflecting on the suffering of the lowe r realms and, specifically,   on the suffering of 
the hell beings, anguish and unhappiness may ar ise. But that suffering cannot compare in 
intensity to the actual suffering of beings in the hell realms.   
 
Answer #2 
When the bodhisattvas reflect on  the suffering of others, their own suffering does not increase. 
Through the power of their reflection, the bodh isattvas destroy their own suffering from the 
root.   
 It is mentioned in the first chapter on the benefits  of generating bodhicitta, that we accumulate a 
vast amount of merit by doing so. The collection  of merit accumulated over time serves as the 
condition for us to develop the wisdom realisin g selflessness which is the sole antidote for 
eliminating the root of the self- grasping conception.   In this way, our compassion actually serves to pa cify our own suffering from the root, rather than 
increasing it. By developing compassion for othe rs, we increase our collection of merit. When 
this happens, we are brought  closer to buddhahood, which reduces our own suffering.  
b" The need to reflect upon sufferings 
 
(8,105) 
If, by one person's suffering, 
The suffering of many would be destroyed, 
Surely kind-hearted people would accept it 
For the sake of themselves and others? 
 
(8,106) 
Thus the Bodhisattva Supushpa Chandra, 
Although aware of the harm the king would cause him, 
 
Accepted his own suffering 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
242  
In order to eradicate the miseries of many.67 
 
When we meditate on compassion, we develop the thought to remove the suffering of all 
sentient beings. By meditating on compassion, it only serves to eliminate our own suffering 
rather than increase it. Even if our meditation on compassion were to bring us great anguish, 
that suffering is meaningful. The  suffering we experience through developing compassion will 
enable us to work to eliminate the suffering of others. 
 
For example, in order to recover from a serious il lness, we are willing to bear the pain of going 
through an operation.  In the same way, it is me aningful for us to experience small suffering in 
order to be able to eliminate greater suffering.  There was once a bodhisattva by the name of Supushpa Chandra who had  clairvoyant powers.  
He could see that if he were to go to the king's  palace alone to teach the Dharma, his life would 
be in danger. He also saw, however, that his teachings would benefit countless sentient beings, 
helping them to achieve a good rebirt h, liberation and enlightenment.  
 Disregarding the danger to his life, therefore, he  went to the palace an d stayed there for 7 days. 
During the day, he would teach the Dharma and at  night, he would circumambulate the relics of 
the Buddha.  The king was angry and, instigated by his ministers, he ordered Supushpa Chandra 
to be killed in a particularly brutal way. He wa s tortured and his limbs were cut off one by one. 
But when his limbs were cut, rainbows emitted from them and instead of blood, milk flowed out from his body.   The king generated great regret when he realised  what he had done so he enshrined Supushpa 
Chandra’s remains in a stupa and made many offerings to it.  
You can find this account in the Samadhiraja Sutra  (meaning  The Flower Adorned by the Moon ) in 
the King of Meditative Stabilisation Sutra  taught by the Buddha. The mora l of the story then is that 
it is appropriate to endure a smaller suffering in  order to be able to eliminate greater suffering. 
                                             
 
67 Pg 174 Root text footnote; Samadhiraja Sutra, Thog. p. 176: 'At the time of the decline of the doctrine of the 
Tathagata Rinpoche Pad-ma'i Da-wa Ngon-par phag-pa'i gyal-po there lived a monk called Supushpachandra. At 
one time, while dwelling in the Sama ntabhadra Grove together with seven thousand Bodhisattvas, he perceived 
with his heightened awareness that if he were to go to the jewelled palace of Ki ng Viradatta and reveal the 
Dharma, many living creatures would obtain  higher states of existence and liberation, but if he were not to reveal it 
they would not obtain these states. In addition, he knew th at if he went, the king woul d kill him, but nevertheless 
(he decided) to go there. Upon his arrival he spent seven days without partaking of any food; during the night he 
would circumambulate a reliquary containing the fingernails of the Tathagata, and during the daytime he would go to the palace and reveal the Dharma. After many million living beings had been led to Enlightenment through his 
teachings, the King then killed him. After a while, though, the King regretted what he had done and subsequently enshrined his bones in a reliquary and worshipped them.' 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
243  
 
In summary,  1. When we meditate on compassion, it will not in crease our suffering but rather it will reduce 
our suffering and ultimately remove them completely from the root.  
2. Even if some suffering is felt when meditating on compassion, this  suffering is meaningful.  
These are the two reasons why it is appropriate to  meditate on compassion. In order to meditate 
on compassion, we need to exchange ourselves fo r others. Before we can do that, we need to 
equalise self and others.
 68 
                                              
68 3' The stages of meditating on how to exchange self and other 
The phrases "exchanging self and other" and "making oneself others and others oneself" do not indicate a training 
in an attitude which thinks "I am others" or "Others' eyes , etc., are mine." They indicate  a change in the orientation 
of the two states of mind of cherishing yourself and neglecting others, wherein you develop the attitude of 
cherishing others as you presently do yourself an d neglecting yourself as you presently do others. 
 Therefore Shantideva’s st atement above that you must "exchange yo ur own happiness for others' suffering" 
means to view cherishing yourself as the enemy and then  to stop emphasizing your own happiness. In addition, 
y o u  m u s t  v i e w  c h e r i s h i n g  o t h e r s  a s  a  g o o d  q u a l i t y ,  s t o p  neglecting others' sufferin g, and instead emphasize the 
removal of their suffering. In brief, this statement means that you remove others' suffering while disregarding your 
own happiness. 
 There are two obstacles to training in this  attitude of exchanging self and other: 
 1. You make a categorical differentiation between people  who are happy or suffering—yourself and others—making 
them as different as blue and yellow. Then, you accomplish your happiness and remove your suffering because you 
think of them as "mine," and you negl ect others' happiness and suffering beca use you think of them as "belonging 
to others."  Therefore, the remedy for this is not to make a ca tegorical distinction between self and other as being 
essentially different. Rather, understand that self and ot her are mutually dependent such that when you are aware 
of self, you are aware of other; and when you are aware of ot her, you are aware of self. It is like being aware of near 
mountains and distant mountains, for ex ample. Relative to your being here, you think of a mountain then- in the 
distance as being a distant mountain, yet when you go to this distant mountain, you think of it as a near 
mountain. So the awareness of self and other is not like the awareness of a co lour, for, regardless of what colour 
blue is related to, you are aware of just blue and are not aware of some other colour. Moreover, Shantideva’s 
Compendium of Trainings  states: 
By becoming accustomed to the equality of self and other, 
The spirit of enlightenment becomes firm. 
Self and other are interdependent. Like this side and the other side of a river, they are false. The other bank is not in itself "other"; In relation to someone else it is "this bank."  Similarly, "self" does not exist in its own right;  In relation to someone else it is "other." 
Thus Shantideva indicates that self and other are merely po sited in relation to a particular reference point and do 
not essentially exist.  2. You must remove the obstacle of thinking, "I will not make an effort to dispel others' suffering because others' suffering does not harm me." It would be like not accumulating wealth in your youth out of fear of suffering in 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
244  
Question  from a student : What is the significance of milk, instead of blood, flowing out from the 
body of the bodhisattva Supushpa Chandra? 
Answer : We can infer that he was probably a su perior bodhisattva. This is because for 
bodhisattvas on the first ground, they no longer have contaminated aggregates but, instead, their 
aggregates are in the nature of the mind – a ‘mental’ body.   
Lesson 44   Chapter VIII - verse 107-114 
It is mentioned in the collection of advice by  the great Kadampa masters that we should check 
the state of our minds three times a day. “Thr ee times” means in the morning, afternoon and 
night. It also refers to  the past, present and future.  
 
We are advised to continuously check the stat e of our minds. Without doing so, it will be 
difficult for us to determine whether our physical an d verbal actions are positi ve or not. As I have 
mentioned before, when our physical and verbal actions originate from a good intention and a 
good heart, then these actions will be positive. If  we are motivated by a negative intention, then 
our  actions will be negative. When we check our minds and find that our motivation is negative, 
we should try to replace it with a positive intentio n. The advice is to live with a good heart at all 
times.   The Kadampa masters also advise that we should always look at our own mistakes and never seek 
out the faults of others. We should treat our fa ults as our enemies. Before we can positively 
influence and change others, we first have to ch ange ourselves. Without changing our minds for 
the better, it is very difficult for us to change other people in a positive way.  
 We need to correct ourselves first, i.e. identi fying our faults and removing them. “The faults” 
refer to our three mental pois ons and our physical and verbal  negativities. When we find 
                                                                                                                                                          
 
your old age because you think that your suffering in old ag e does no harm to you in your youth. Or, as stated in 
Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds ,67 it would be like not alleviating the pain in your foot with your hand, because 
your foot is "other." Old age and youth (or similarly, former and future lives) are just illustrations; this principle 
applies also to morning and evening, former and later days, and so forth.  
Qualm : But old age and youth are one continuum and the foot and hand are one collection ; therefore they are not 
the same as self and other. 
 
Response : "Continuum" and "collection" are designated to many  moments and to many parts; they do not have a 
self-sufficient essence. The "self" of yourself and the "self" of others must be posited to a collection and a 
continuum, so self and other are not established by wa y of an essence that can be posited independently. 
However, since beginningless time you have found your  own suffering unbearable because you have been 
conditioned to self-cherishing. Therefore, if you become conditioned to cherishing others, you will give rise to an attitude of finding others’ suffering unbearable. ( Lamrim Chenmo,  Vol. 2, pg. 53-54)  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
245  
ourselves doing things that are inappropriate and not beneficial, we should correct ourselves by 
remembering, “This is not good and no t productive. I should not do this.” 
  
We should not look for the faults of others beca use there is no benefit to  doing so and it does 
not fulfil our own purpose. It only causes our an ger and negative mind to increase.  We may have 
been in a positive state of mind but once we start fault-finding, our minds becoming unhappy 
and more negative.   We are harming ourselves an d, in the process, hurting others as well. We 
cannot change other people by looking at their fa ults and we cannot influence them in a positive 
way until we have improved ourselves.   When we point out their faults, people may get i rritated and angry. They may respond, “Who are 
you to correct me? I can do whatever I want.” We  hurt people by instigating their anger and we 
also harm ourselves in the process. It is a no-win situation and there is no benefit.   
 
The masters also advised that we should hide wh atever good qualities we have and we should 
praise the good qualities of others. Hiding ou r good qualities means that we should not be 
boastful about our qualities, e.g.  telling others,  “I have studied for so many years; I am good-
hearted; I have clairvoyance, you know.” Why sh ould we hide our qualities? Because boasting 
about them will only increase our arrogance, co nceit, pride and attachment. When the mind is 
conceited, it is under the influence of a negative emotion so we are the ones that are hurt. Again, 
there is no benefit. 
 We should praise and concentrate on the good qu alities of others instead of looking at their 
faults.  If that person has a good heart, we shou ld say so, “That is a good-hearted person.”  We 
should also proclaim those qualities for, by doing so, we will be able to see and recognise those good  qualities more clearly ourselves. This bene fits us in our developmen t of bodhicitta and the 
good heart. There is a connection; the more we ar e able to see the good qualities of others, the 
easier it will be for us respect them and deve lop the mind that cherishes them. These are the 
benefits.  When we consider the advice of the great Kadamp a masters, we find it is really wonderful and 
beneficial. We are supposed to check our minds co ntinuously day and night, but we do the exact 
opposite. Day and night, we appear to be practising virtue and physically doing recitations but we do not check our minds. This is a mistake that must be changed. 
 Regarding seeing our faults as our enemies, agai n we do the exact opposite; we always think we 
are in the right and we pick at the faults of others. In the sa me way, instead of hiding our 
qualities and praising others, we are boastful an d we forget to promote the qualities of others, 
only seeing their faults instead.   We should remember this collection of advice and remind ourselves of them  from time to time.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION

3’ The benefits of such meditations 
a’ Singly applying oneself to the welfare of others is not [in itself] great suffering b’ As the bliss of that is supreme, one engages in [working for] others’ welfare 
c’ One’s own arrogance comes to be pacified 
d’ [The pleasure and benefits of such meditations] do not depend on reciprocation 
and fully-ripened [effects] e’ It is due to such reasons that meditating on the equality of self and others is 
[said to be] reasonable 
4’ One can develop such [an attitude] is one familiarizes oneself with the equality of 
self and other
 
b) The way to exchange self and other 
i) A brief presentation 
1’ The way to exchange self and other 2’ The reasons it is appropriate to fa miliarize oneself [with such attitudes]  
ii) An extensive explanation 
a’ Singly applying oneself to the welfare of others is not [in itself] great suffering 
(8, 107) 
Thus, because they love to pacify the pains of others, 
Those whose minds are attuned in this way 
Would enter even the deepest hell 
Just as a wild goose plunges into a lotus pool. 
 
When we think of benefiting others, we often thin k that it is something t oo difficult to do. This 
is due to obstacles in our practice of equalising  ourselves and other. When we are accustomed to 
the reflection of equalising ourselves with other,  we can actually derive great joy from doing so. 
When our minds are attuned to seeing ourselves an d  o t h e r s  a s  e q u a l ,  w e  w i l l  b e  d e l i g h t e d  t o  
work for others and constantly look for ways to help them. We will do this  joyfully and happily, 
Just as a wild goose plunges into a lotus pool .  E v e n  w h e n  w e  h a v e  t o  g o  t o  t h e  A v i c i  h e l l  t o  h e l p  
others, we will do so happily because we will have developed the courage and determination to 
do so with great delight. Needless to say, we will be  able to do less difficult tasks as well. It is said 
that the bliss we experience from working for th e benefit of others is special and exalted.  
b’ As the bliss of that is supreme, one engages in [working for] others’ welfare 
(8, 108) 
Will not the ocean of joy 
That shall exist when all beings are free 
Be sufficient for me? 
What am I doing wishing fo r my liberation alone? 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
247  
Another hindrance to our meditation on the equa lity of self and other is the thought, “The 
happiness and bliss that come from working for othe rs is not the highest bliss. The highest bliss 
is overcoming suffering and achieving liberation . That is the real happiness, not the happiness 
that comes from helping others.”  
 The supreme happiness is to liberate all living beings from their problems and suffering; this 
happiness is said to be immeasurable. For a bodhis attva, achieving the liberation of all sentient 
beings is the most perfect and exalted form of bliss and the bodhisattva continually strives to 
achieve this goal. When all beings are liberated, his wishes are accomplished and he will be very happy to have  achieved his goal. This is the mo st exalted goal as opposed to thinking about our 
own happiness alone and wishing to achieve only self-liberation.  
c’ One’s own arrogance comes to be pacified  
(8, 109) 
Therefore, although working for the benefit of others, 
I should not be conceited or (consider myself) wonderful. 
 We may also think, “If I equalise myself with  others, there will be nothing in it for me. 
Benefiting others in this way will not benefit me.”  This is not true becaus e by benefiting others 
on the basis of equalising self and others, ou r  pride and arrogance will be pacified and the 
thought, “I did this for her,” will be banished.  
d. [The pleasure and benefits of such meditations] do not depend on reciprocation and fully-
ripened [effects] 
(8, 109 cd)  
And because of the joy there is in solely doing this, 
I should have no hope for any ripening-effect. 
 
When we do something sincerely and unconditionall y for the benefit of others, we do not expect 
any favour or good karma in return: I should have no hope for any ripening effect.   
 We encounter difficulties in helping others becaus e of our expectations of  the other party. When 
those expectations are not met, problems arise and we experience unhappiness and suffering. We 
also tend to focus on the difficulties of helpin g others, anticipating problems and worrying that 
there will be nothing in it for oneself. But, in fact, the opposite is true . When we help others 
sincerely, there will only be happiness and none of the difficulties.  
e. It is due to such reasons that meditating on the equality of self and others is [said to be] 
reasonable 
(8,110) 
Therefore, just as I protect myself 
From unpleasant things however small, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
248  
In the same way I should act towards others 
With a compassionate and caring mind. 
 
Therefore,  refers to the appropriateness of meditati ng on the equality of self and other and 
working for the benefit of others, because, Just as I protect myself/From unpleasant things however 
small,/In the same way I should act towards others/With a compassionate and caring mind. 
 
When someone criticises us, we are quick to defend  ourselves. In the same way, when others are 
criticised and we are in a position to do so, we should defend them too. At the very least, we can generate compassion towards them.  
4’ One can develop such [an attitude] if one familiarizes oneself with the equality of self and 
other  
(8, 111) 
Although the basis is quite impersonal, 
Through (constant) familiarity 
I have come to regard 
The drops of sperm and blood of others as “I”. 
 
(8,112) 
So in the same way, why should I be unable 
To regard the bodies of others as "I"? 
Hence it is not difficult to see 
That my body is al so that of others. 
 
Qualm : How is it possible for me to consider others  as myself, to see them  as equal and protect 
them in the same way I would myself ? I can’t do this. It is difficult  for me to see myself as equal 
with others, because I find it difficult to  see others to be the same as myself.  
 Answer : This verse reminds us that our bodies are th e products of our parents’ eggs and sperm. 
Our consciousness entered the union of egg and sperm during the process of rebirth and we were 
born with these bodies. Through  familiar ity, we see our bodies as, “my body”. 
In the same way therefore, through familiarity,  w e  s h o u l d  b e  a b l e  t o  a p p r e h e n d  o t h e r s  a s  
ourselves and cherish them. We should practise a pprehending other sentient beings as “I”. Just 
a s  i t  i s  p o s s i b l e  t o  c o n s i d e r  o u r  b o d i e s  w h i c h  b e l o n g e d  t o  o t h e r s  a s  “ I , ”  i t  i s  a l s o  p o s s i b l e  t o  conceive of others as “I”.  To meditate on bodhicitta, we begi n with the  meditation  on the equality of self and other before 
we proceed with exchanging self and other.  
b) The way to exchange self and other 
i) A brief presentation 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
249  
1’ The way to exchange self and other 
2’ The reasons it is appropriate to fa miliarize oneself [with such attitudes]  
ii) An extensive explanation 
1’ The way to exchange self and other 
(8,113) 
Having seen the mistakes in (cherishing) myself 
And the ocean of good in (cherishing) others, 
I shall completely reject all selfishness 
And accustom myself to accepting others. 
 
The self-cherishing attitude is th e source of all our problems and we should work at removing it. 
Cherishing others is the source of all good and desirable things. Therefore, we should eliminate 
our self-cherishing attitude and adopt the attitude of cherishing others.  Exchanging self and other does not mean that we  become other and other become us. Rather, we 
shift our focus from ourselves to others. Presentl y, because we see ourselves as more important, 
we are accustomed to cherishing ourselves and neglecting others. Exchanging self and other 
entails reversing this attitude. Instead of cher ishing ourselves and being concerned only about 
our own welfare, we cherish others instead.   
2’ The reasons it is appropriate to fa miliarize oneself [with such attitudes] 
(8,114) 
In the same way as the hands and so forth 
Are regarded as limbs of the body, 
Likewise, why are embodied creatures 
Not regarded as limbs of life? 
 
Qualm : I cannot exchange myself with others because there are too many of them. This is similar 
to the earlier qualm we had during the di scussion on equalising self and other. 
 
Answer : Just as we apprehend different  parts of our body – the hands,  limbs and so on – as parts 
o f  a  s i n g l e  e n t i t y ,  i n  t h e  s a m e  w a y ,  w e  s h o u l d  see all sentient beings as parts of the group of 
sentient beings. On that basis, we develop th e thought to benefit them and free them from 
suffering.   
 
Answer to student’s question : It is said that when it comes to making aspirational prayers, we should 
make them as extensive as possible, regardless of whether our wishes can be accomplished or not. 
The most extensive dedication is to achieve enli ghtenment to liberate all sentient beings from 
suffering. Similarly, there is no motivation more positive than the thought,  “I am doing this to 
benefit others.”  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
250  
The best of all motivations is bodhicitta, the al truistic intention. The best dedication is the 
dedication of all our virtues towards achieving enlightenment for the benefit of all sentient 
beings. These are the supreme motivation and dedication. 
 
There are different levels of motivation and de dication depending on the level of our minds – 
small, medium or big. Based on our aspiration and our inclination, we can make the relevant 
level of dedication prayer.  
 
Those who are able to practise the best motivati on and best dedication are very rare. It is 
important for each individual within the same ce ntre to help one another as much as possible. 
This depends on the individual level of ability. 
Lesson 45   Chapter VIII - verse 115-118 
It is important that we transform our actions of listening to and studying the Dharma into 
Dharma by generating the motivation, “I am partic ipating in this session so that I can  learn to 
develop a good heart.”   
3)  How to cultivate calm abiding 
a) The way to meditate on the equality of self and other 
b) The way to exchange self and other 
i) A brief presentation 
ii) An extensive explanation 
1’ Explaining the way to exchange self and other 
a’ An extensive explanation 
1” Familiarizing oneself with cherishing others 
a” The capacity to exchange self and other and the pacification 
of conceit 
b” Instructions on making effort in this meditation without looking to the fully-ripened [effect] 
c”  Doing more for the sake of others 
d” One will be able to develop [such an attitude] if one 
familiarizes oneself with it 
e” One should meditate on exchanging self and others with the 
desire to free both self and others from suffering 
2” Making effort to abandon self-cherishing 
3” Reflecting on the faults and benefits of primarily cherishing self 
and other [respectively] 
b’ Synopsis 
2’ The way to practice after having reflected on this exchange 3’ The way to practice application [of this attitude] 
 
Qualm : There are limitless sentient be ings, each with different wishes . It is, therefore, impossible 
for me to protect them all in the same way I protect myself. 
 
Answer : Our bodies are made up of many separate parts yet we conceive them all as parts of our 
bodies and protect them. In the same way, although  there are so many sentient beings, it should 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
251  
be possible for us to see them all as one and protect them.  
a” The capacity to exchange self and other and the pacification of conceit  
(8,115) 
Through acquaintance has th e thought of “I” arisen 
Towards this impersonal body; 
So in a similar way, why should it not arise 
Towards other living beings? 
 
(8,116) 
When I work in this way for the sake of others, 
I should not let conceit or (the f eeling that I am) wonderful arise. 
 
Qualm : We may think, “It is impossible for me to exchange myself with others because I cannot 
conceive of others as myself.” 
 
Answer :  We cannot find the “I” on the basis of ou r bodies, which are the products of the union 
of eggs and sperm of our parents. Our bodies do no t really belong to us yet. Through familiarity, 
our minds conceive of our bodies as “mine.”  
 
When the body is healthy and feeling good, we say, “I feel good.” But when the body is in bad 
shape and suffering, we say, “I am in pain and suffering.” Someti mes, we have the feeling that the 
“I” is the body and the body is the “I”. But when we analyse this carefully, we find that this is not 
possible.  
 Just as we come to conceive that the body belong s to “me” through familiar ity,  in the same way, 
through familiarity, we can conceive of others as  being similar to ourselve s. On that basis, it 
becomes possible to generate the thought of cherishi ng others. With familiarity, it is possible to 
conceive of the bodies of others as belonging to myself.  
Answer to student’s question :  When we refer to exchanging self and other, it does not mean that I 
become someone else and someone else becomes me. We normally cherish only ourselves and 
not others, neglecting their welfare. Exchanging  self and other means switching our focus from 
cherishing ourselves to cherishing others. 
 Is it possible to conceive of myself as others  and for others to conceive of me  as them?  
 
The Lam Rim Chenmo points out that “I” and ot her are not like blue and yellow but rather like 
the mountain on this side and the mountain on the other side. 
When we posit that “I” can be other and vice versa, it is same as saying that there is no difference 
between the two colours, blue and yellow.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
252  
 
The “I” and other both have different bases of designation. They are not independent entities 
but are relative to one another and posited in  dependence on one another. There is no 
autonomous “I” who inherently exists. The “I” is  not “I” from the perspective of others. To 
others, we become the “other”. In  the same way, the mountain on this side is not inherently so. 
When we stand on the other moun tain, the mountain on this side  will become the mountain on 
the other side. 
Through familiarity, we have come to conceive  o f  o u r  b o d i e s ,  w h i c h  a c t u a l l y  c o m e  f r o m  o u r  
parents, as belonging to us. If that is the case , there is no reason wh y we cannot conceive of 
others as belonging to this “I” as well. 
 After all, whether something belongs to the self  or not simply depends on our mental outlook. 
For example, before we buy an object, there is no apprehension of that object as belonging to us. 
However, after it is purchased, we see that object as ours. That sense of ownership does not come 
from the object’s side, but our mental attitude towards it. When we give this object away to 
someone else, we also give up the thought, “This belongs to me.”  
 It seems extremely difficult to conceive of others’ bodies as belonging to us but it is achievable 
through the power of familiarity.   
Question:  Is it possible to conceive of others’ bodies in the same way as we conceive of our own 
body? 
 
Reply : Using the above arguments, the answer is “yes ”. It becomes possible for us to cherish and 
to work for the benefit of others and to do so wi thout pride. When the affl iction of pride arises, 
it immediately disturbs our mental peace. When we  have the ability to exchange self and other, 
our arrogance and pride will be pacified. 
b”  Instructions on making effort in this medita tion without looking to the fully-ripened [effect] 
(8,116 cd) 
It is just like feeding myself- 
I hope for nothing in return. 
 
(8, 117) 
Therefore, just as I protect myself 
From unpleasant things, however slight, 
In the same way, I should habituate myself 
To have a compassionate and caring mind towards others. 
 
These verses are self-explanatory. 
c”  Doing more for the sake of others 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
253  
(8,118) 
It is out of his great compassion 
That the Lord Avalokiteshvara has (even) blessed his name 
To dispel the nervousness 
Of being among other people.69 
 
Just as we protect ourselves from the slightest ha rm from others, we should protect others in the 
same way by generating the mind of compassion towards them.  We should strive to eliminate 
even the smallest harm experienced by others. Mo tivated in this way, Avalokiteshvara blessed his 
name so that its mere mention would dispel mise ry and suffering. Even the nervousness of being 
a m o n g s t  o t h e r  p e o p l e  i s  d i s p e l l e d .  W h e n  h e  first developed bodhicitta, he dedicated his 
achievement to the happiness of sentient beings, so that by just remembering his name, the eight 
fears would be removed. This is why Avalokiteshv ara can benefit sentient beings so extensively. 
Following Avalokiteshvara’s example, when we si ncerely dedicate everything we do with our 
bodies, speech and minds for the benefit of others, we  should also be able to benefit them in the 
same way. 
Lesson 46   Chapter VIII - verse 119-130 
Guntang Rinpoche advises, “If we want to make  our days and nights meaningful, we should 
always check the state of our minds.” No beneficial  actions can result from a mind that is under 
the control of the three mental poisons (ignorance , anger and attachment). Therefore, we should 
always strive to keep our minds in a positive state, thinking constantly of how to benefit others. 
When our actions are motivated by a negative mind , it is questionable whether those actions can 
be beneficial.   
It is important to set a proper motivation before  we begin any virtuous ac tivities, such as doing 
our daily commitments. We are advised in the teac hings to always begin with the meditation on 
the breath to bring the mind to a state of equilibrium, especially when we find that our minds 
are agitated by anger or attachment.  Otherwise, it is difficult to generate a positive state of mind 
while doing the practices.  
 When the mind is in a state of equilibrium, it is easier to prevent negative thoughts from arising, 
even though we may not yet be able to elimin ate our attachment or anger from the root. It 
becomes possible for us to consider those we normal ly think of as enemies or objects of aversion 
as pleasant and as friends.   
                                             
 
69 Text note 32 
 Gandavyuha Sutra, Thog.p.180 
“By recollecting my name three times, may the fear  of being embarrassed among people be dispelled .’ 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
254  
 
When engaged in virtuous activities, we should pay heed to the objects of desire/attachment and 
the objects of aversion/anger. We should sincer ely dedicate the merit we accumulate from our 
practices to their welfare from the depths of ou r hearts. It is easy to habituate ourselves to 
dedicating our merit in this way compared to gi ving away material things such as our bodies.  
 
When we dedicate all the roots of our virtue to our enemies, does that mean there is nothing left 
for us, that we are not going to experience the bene ficial effects of those virtues? I don’t think so. 
So, don’t worry.  When we dedicate our roots of virtue sincerely in this way, it is difficult to say how much benefit will actually be received by the objects of our de dication but, without a doubt, we will benefit and 
see the improvement in our minds. We will defini tely benefit because we can see that all our 
problems and sufferings arise from attachment and/or anger in our lives.  
 
When we neglect checking the state of our mind s, then no matter how profound or extensive 
our prayers may be, it is difficult for those practices to be beneficial even for ourselves. When we do not benefit from our practices, then it  is difficult for us to benefit others. 
 If you wish, however, to make your life mean ingless and empty, then by all means, please 
continue to spend your whole life being conceite d and arrogant  and spend your time partying, 
gossiping and shopping.  
So to reiterate the advice  from Guntang Rinpoche – If you want to make your life meaningful day and 
night, always check the state of your  mind. If you wish to make your li fe meaningless and empty, then you 
can spend your time in  meaningless activi ties and abide in arrogance and so forth. We should always 
remind ourselves of this advice.
