d” One will be able to develop [such an a ttitude] if one familiarizes oneself with it  
(8, 119) 
I should not turn away from what is difficult; 
For by the power of familiarity, 
I may be made unhappy even when someone 
Whose name once frightened me is not around. 
 
Qualm : Exchanging self and other, cherishing othe rs and so forth – these practices are very 
beneficial but they are too difficult.  
 
Answer : Even if we think like this, we should not stop ourselves from wanting to train in these 
practices because all we need is familiarity. Once we are familiar with the practice, it definitely 
becomes possible. 
 Think of someone we dislike or someone we are af raid of. It is possible that, over time, that 
person may become a friend, even a good friend . It can happen. When that person becomes our 
c l o s e  f r i e n d ,  w e  w i l l  f e e l  l o n e l y  a n d  u n h a p p y  w h e n  h e  i s  n o t  a r o u n d .  O u r  a t t i t u d e  m a y  completely change.   Shantideva says that just as it seems impossib le to us now that an enemy can become a close 
friend, through familiarity, that change can come about. In the same way, although we may think 
now, “It is difficult to see myself as other,” it is  possible, through practice and familiarity, for our 
attitude to change.  The point here is not to tu rn away from the practice because we deem it to 
be too difficult. When we do nothing, then nothing positive can be accomplished.  
e”   One should meditate on exchanging self and others with the desire to free both self and 
others from suffering 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
256  
(8,120) 
Thus whoever wishes to qu ickly afford protection 
To both the self and other beings 
Should practice that holy secret: 
The exchanging of self for others. 
 
Since it is possible to develop the attitude of ex changing self and other through  familiarity, we 
who seek protection (i.e. to fr ee ourselves from the extremes of  nirvana and samsara), should 
practise this holy secret - the instru ction of exchanging self and other. 
 Why is this instruction secret? Because it shou ld not be revealed to someone whose Mahayana 
lineage has yet to  awaken. In other words, thos e who are of the Hearers and Solitary Realizers 
lineages are not suitable vessels for these instructions.   
Why is this instruction holy? Because the practice of exchanging self and other  is the ultimate 
practice of the Mahayana or the Great Vehicle. 
 
2” Making effort to abandon self-cherishing 
a” Self-cherishing is the cause of all fear 
b” Reflecting on how [self-cherishing] act to encourage one into all negative conduct  
a” Self-cherishing is the cause of all fear 
(8,121) 
Because of attachment to my body, 
Even a small object of  fear frightens me. 
So who would not revi le, as an enemy, 
This body that gives rise to fear? 
 
We develop strong attachment towards our bodies  due to our self-grasping. As a result, even 
small problems can cause great mental suffering for us. For example, we are afraid of tiny 
mosquitoes and insects, worrying about how they can harm us.  When a fly lands on our food, 
we fret over how we will get sick if we eat the food so we throw the food away. These worries and 
fears arise from our strong attachment to our bo dies. When we are afraid of small things, it 
stands to reason that we will be even more  afraid of  sickness, old age and death.  
Since it is our self-grasping that gives rise to this  strong attachment, self-grasping is the cause of all 
our fears and problems in this life and our future lives.   Due to our attachment to the body, all sorts of proble ms arise. If the body is the source of all our  
fears and suffering, then why would a wise person  want to remain attached to it? Instead of 
cherishing our bodies, we should  see them as our enemies.  
b” Reflecting on how [self-cherishing] act to encourage one into all negative conduct  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
257  
(8,122) 
By wishing for a means to remedy 
The hunger, thirst and sickness of the body, 
I might kill birds, fish and deer 
And loiter by the sides of roads (to rob others). 
 
(8,123) 
If, for the sake of its profit and comfort, 
I would kill even my father and mother, 
And steal the property of the Triple Gem, 
Then I would undoubtedly proceed to burn  in the flames of the deepest hell. 
 
(8,124) 
Therefore what adept would desire, 
Protect and venerate this body? 
Who would not scorn it 
And regard it as an enemy? 
 
In order to stop ourselves from experiencing the sufferings of hunger, thirst and sickness, we may 
do many negative things such as even killing our parents, stealing from the Triple Gem and other non-virtuous actions.   To satisfy the attachment to our bodies, we engage in negative actions that will lead us only to a 
rebirth in the Avici hell. If that is the case, what wise person would venerate this body, which is 
the source of all our difficulties?    
Who is the adept  mentioned here?  It is someone who want s to achieve the happiness of this and 
future lives, not someone who only focuses on temporal happiness.  
 We must work at stopping the self-cherishing attitu de, because it is the cause of all our faults and 
it will only bring us problems in this  life and our future lives to come.  
 
Question from student: How do we tell the difference between caring for and being attached to our 
bodies?  
 
Reply : We need to check whether taking care of ou r bodies brings us problems or leads to the 
creation of negative karma.  When we fuss over and are totally preoccupied with the care of our 
bodies, then that’s a sign that we are attached to our bodies.  
There is a need to take care of our bodies, espe cially when we are using  it to practise the 
Dharma. When we take care of th e body with that motivation, does  that increase our attachment 
or not? If it increases our attachment, it defeats the purpose of our Dharma practice.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
258  
 
When the attachment becomes stronger, the self-c herishing and self-grasping attitudes will also 
become stronger.  When self-cherishing increases,  we forget about exchanging self and other. 
That is the problem. 
 In examining the instructions of exchanging self and other, we have to see the faults of the self-cherishing attitude, the cause of all our problems . In order to stop this attitude, we have to 
reduce our attachment to ourselves and to our bodies. When our attachment is reduced, it 
becomes easier for us to embark on the pr actice of exchanging self and  other. 
 
3” Reflecting on the faults and benefits of primarily cherishing self and other [respectively] 
a” A list of the disadvantages and benefits of cherishing self and other [respectively] 
(1)  An extensive explanation 
(a) In terms of giving 
(b) In terms of harming (c) In terms of praise and renown 
(d) In terms of application to activities 
(e) In terms of benefit and bliss 
(a) In terms of giving 
(8,125) 
“If I give this, what shal l I (have left to) enjoy?” 
Such selfish thinking is the way of ghosts; 
“If I enjoy this, what shall I (have left to) give?” 
Such selfless thinking is a quality of the gods. 
 
We are unable to part with our wealth and posse ssions because of miserliness, thinking we will 
become poor or we won’t have anything left for ourselves. Miserliness is an affliction which can only lead to unpleasant fruitional results, such as  a rebirth in a fearful place and is therefore not 
beneficial.   Instead, we should think, “If I use up everything myself, I will have nothing to give others.” This way of thinking leads only to happiness from life to life, leading us to good rebirths, ultimately, 
to enlightenment and, is a quality of the gods . 
 Miserliness and generosity are mental attitudes an d do not depend on whether we actually give 
something away or not. If one is unable to part  with one's possessions, then even if one has 
plenty to spare, that is miserliness. If one has the mental willingness to give away everything one 
owns, that is generosity. So it does not depend on the actual giving.  
(b) In terms of harming 
(8,126) 
If, for my own sake, I cause harm to others, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
259  
I shall be tormented in hellish realms; 
But if, for the sake of othe rs, I cause harm to myself, 
I shall acquire all that is magnificent. 
 
We harm the bodies and possessions of others because of our self-importance, cherishing 
ourselves and putting our own intere sts first. These actions will only lead us to bad rebirths and 
suffering. 
 
On the other hand, it is not a problem if our bo dies or possessions are harmed in the course of 
our working to free others from suffering and placing them in happiness because such actions 
will only lead to good rebirths and, ultimately, enlightenment.  
(c) In terms of praise and renown 
(8,127) 
By holding myself in high esteem 
I shall find myself in unpleasant realms, ugly and stupid; 
But should this (attitude ) be shifted to others, 
I shall acquire honors in a joyful realm. 
 Cherishing ourselves and being interested solely in  our own welfare, desiring good looks, wealth 
and possessions, fame and high status – all these lead only to rebirth in the lower realms. Once 
there, we will be stupid and ugly and be  surrounded only by unpleasant things.  
 Shifting this attitude towards others – wishing th em to have wealth, fame and material comforts 
– will lead us to a good rebirth, endowed with wealth and     fame.  We can see from the above verses the different results of different states of mind. One is 
beneficial. The other is not. As we always have  our own interests at heart, thinking only of 
ourselves, we continue to circle in cyclic existence.   
(d) In terms of application to activities 
(8,128) 
If I employ others for my own purposes, 
I myself shall experience servitude; 
But if I use myself for the sake of others, 
I shall experience only lordliness. 
 When we treat others like slaves or servants to do our bidding, we are only creating the cause to 
experience the same fate ourselves in the future.   However, when we place ourselves in the service of  others, working only for their benefit, we will 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
260  
experience a good rebirth, be born in a family of good lineage and high status, be endowed with 
a pleasant appearance and wield power and influence as a king or minister.  
(e) In terms of benefit and bliss 
(8,129) 
Whatever joy there is in this world 
All comes from desiring others to be happy, 
And whatever suffering there is in this world 
All comes from desiring myself to be happy. 
 
All the happiness of this worldly life and all futu re lives come from our wish for others to be 
happy. Desiring happiness for ourselves leads only to problems, difficulties and suffering in this 
and future lives.  We should check and analyse whether what the text says makes sense or not. We need to 
investigate this at an individual level. When we think only of ourselves, does this generate profit or loss for us? When we think only of others, do we stand to lose or profit from this? We need to figure this out for ourselves.  As mentioned earlier, when we look at the meanin g of the adept or wise person, it does not refer 
to someone who concentrates on only a very sm all profit (or temporary happiness) but rather 
someone who focuses on the long-term view, the ha ppiness of all the future lives to come. Such a 
person is not someone who considers only the ha ppiness of a single hour, days or even months. 
A wise person is not like that. 
(2) Synopsis 
(8,130) 
What need is there to say much more? 
The childish work for their own benefit, 
The Buddhas work for the benefit of others. 
Just look at the diffe rence between them 
 Verse 130 is the summary of our discussion so fa r. Day and night, without break, even when we 
are sleeping, we have been harbouring this self-cherishing attitude. We think only of 
accomplishing our own purposes.   What have we achieved so far and what is the result? The outcome is that we only engage in 
negative thoughts that lead to negative actions,  the fruitional results of which are only suffering 
and problems. We go through life experiencing one problem after another, without break. Our 
selfish mental attitude leads to this kind of result.   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
261  
The buddhas and bodhisattvas, the great holy bein gs, spend their days and nights working only 
for the welfare of others. They do not experi ence any suffering or problems regardless of 
whatever form they may choose to manifest as or  whatever rebirth they take. They experience 
continuous happiness,  not only in that particular  life but also in all their future lives, because 
they only cherish others. 
 The advice here is for us to discard our self-int erest and  work solely for the benefit of others. 
The number of problems we experience depend s on whether we are narrow- or broad-minded. 
When we are narrow-minded, we will be unhappy  and have more problems. When the mind is 
vast and big, it will be more relaxed, happy and pe aceful. The advice in the above verses is to help 
us broaden our mental perspective beyond focusing  on just ourselves but to include all living 
beings, to working for their benefit.

Lesson 47   Chapter VIII - verse 131-139 
I was twelve years old when I went to Kopan mona stery. Lama Thubten Yeshe was still alive then. 
He taught us by writing out questions and their answers, pasting them on the wall and making us

Qualm : Since someone else's body is not your body, how can you develop the same attitude that you hove toward 
your own? 
Reply : Your body was produced from your father's semen and your mother's blood. Even though it was produced 
from portion of someone else's body, you conceive it to be your own by the influenc e of previous conditioning. 
Similarly, if you become accustomed to cherishing another' s body as you presently do yo ur own, you will view it 
with the same perspective that you now have toward your own body.  
Therefore just as you conceived ‘I’

Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION

memorise them. There were many questi ons. One I can still remember was, W hy do we need to 
practise the Dharma?  The questions were in Tibetan, and at that time, I was more familiar with my 
native dialect, Sherpa. Still, I memorised the question even though I did not understand its 
meaning. The answer to this question was: We all desire happiness and do not want suffering. The only 
way to abandon all suffering is the practice of the Dharma. Therefore, we have  to practise the Dharma. 
 
Another question was, Just beating the drum, ringing the bell and performing the rituals – are these 
actions Dharma?  The answer to that was, Beating the drum, ringing the be ll and reciting mantras alone 
are not necessarily Dharma. Why? Because you can also teach animals to do these things.  
 
At that age, we young monks were all preoccupi ed with games and playing but since we had to 
pass our examinations and memorisation tests, we had to memorise the questions and their 
answers even though we did not fully understand their content.    I am telling you this story to emphasise that Dh arma practice is performed primarily with our 
minds and not our bodies or speech. Reciting mantras, doing our daily commitments and prayers, knowing how to do some rituals -  these things are not necessarily Dharma.  
 Practising the Dharma means improving the mind and weakening our afflictions. The nature of 
afflictions is to disturb our minds, which le ads to suffering and unhappiness so, until the 
afflictions are eliminated, we will continue to ex perience problems and difficulties. The Dharma 
is the only way to get rid of  afflictions.   
 When we say, Dharma, we have to understand wh at that means. Dharma is that which enables 
us to transform our minds for the better.   There are many different kinds of instructions for training and developing our    minds. Here, we 
are focusing on the instructions for exchanging self and other through examining the faults of 
cherishing ourselves and the benefit of cherishing others. In order to practise these instructions, 
you must first listen to these teachings. Yo u should set this important motivation for 
participating in this session.    I have mentioned many times before the importance  of listening to the teachings with a correct 
motivation. It is very important to ad just our  motivation to en sure it is a proper one.  Our 
motivation should be: we are attending these te achings so that we can learn to transform our 
minds for the better. You will definitely benefit from  this session if you main tain this motivation.  
 Let us summarise the faults of cherishing ours elves and the benefits of cherishing others. 
Looking at ourselves, we can see that, day and night, we think only of ourselves and our own 
welfare. We spend our entire lives with this self -absorbed mental attitude. And what is the result? 
We are continually experiencing problems, suffering and unhappiness.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
263  
 
The buddhas, on the other hand, abide in perpet ual bliss and happiness. Why?  Because they 
work solely for the benefit of others.  
  
Since continuous happiness comes from continually  working for and  cher ishing others, day and 
night, we too should concern ourselves with the welfare and happiness of others. 
 
1’ Explaining the way to exchange self and other 
a’ An extensive explanation 
1” Familiarizing oneself with cherishing others 
2” Making effort to abandon self-cherishing 
3” Reflecting on the faults and benefits of primarily cherishing self and other [respectively] 
a” A list of the disadvantages and benefits of cherishing self and other [respectively] 
b” Thus, the reasonableness of discarding self-cherishing  
(1) The unseen disadvantages 
(2) The visible disadvantages 
(3) A presentation of the disadvantages in brief (4) The reasonableness of discarding self-cherishing 
b’ Synopsis 
1” The way to exchange self and other 2” Arresting misapplication [of the principle] once one has exchanged 
3” Practicing unerringly 
2’ The way to practice after having reflected on this exchange 
3’ The way to practice application [of this attitude] 
(1) The unseen disadvantages 
(8, 131) 
If I do not actually exchange my happiness 
For the sufferings of others, 
I shall not attain the state of Buddhahood 
And even in cyclic existence shall have no joy. 
 
This verse is reminding us that we should exch ange self and other and work for the benefit of 
others, by switching our focus from cherishing ou rselves to cherishing others.  When we do not 
engage in such an exchange and neglect the welfare of others,   we cannot achieve happiness for 
ourselves in our future lives.  Enlightenment is also not attainable. Without exchanging self and  
other, there is no way we can achieve the enlightened state.  
 
Qualm : But I do not mind not becoming a Buddha. I am happy to remain as I am in samsara.  
 Reply : The response to this qualm appear s in the last line of verse 131, Even in cyclic existence, you 
shall have no joy. 
 
It is impossible to experience lasting happiness as  long as we remain in cyclic existence. The 
temporal happiness experienced in samsara does no t last and is unstable and unreliable.  That 
being the case, it is then inappropriate to assert  remaining in samsara as an acceptable option.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
264  
 
Permanent, everlasting happiness comes only when we achieve the state of full enlightenment. In 
order to achieve such a state, we need to exchange self and  other. 
(2) The visible disadvantages 
(8, 132) 
Let alone what is beyond this world. 
Because my servants do no work 
And because my masters give me no pay, 
Even the needs of this life will not be fulfilled. 
 
Even the happiness of this life cannot be achieved  when we have a selfish attitude, paying no 
attention to the welfare of others. For example, due to miserliness of the employer, his workers 
will not perform their jobs well. From the workers’  side, they get no pay. Both sides lose out.  
(3) A presentation of the disadvantages in brief 
(8, 133) 
(By rejecting the method that) establishes both foreseeable and unforeseeable joy, 
I cast magnificent delight completely aside 
And then, because of inflicting misery on others, 
In confusion I seize hold of unbearable pain. 
 
Exchanging self and other is the cause for the happi ness of this life (which is foreseeable) and the 
happiness of future lives (which is unfore seeable). Choosing however to ignore these 
instructions, we harm other people instead.  As  a result, we accumulate negative karma, the 
consequences of which will have to be experienced in the future. 
(4) The reasonableness of discarding self-cherishing 
(8, 134) 
If all the injury, 
Fear and pain in this world 
Arise from grasping at a self, 
Then what use is that great ghost to me? 
 
(8, 135) 
If I do not completely forsake it 
I shall be unable to pu t an end to suffering, 
Just as I cannot avoid being burned 
If I do not cast aside the fire (I hold). 
 
We blame our enemies when problems and harm s befall us. “Harms” here encompasses harm 
from human as well as non-human sources, such as  spirits and animals. All these problems, fears 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
265  
and harms ultimately come from our  self-cherishing attitude. The problems and suffering 
associated with birth, aging and death have their  roots in the self-grasping conception and the 
self-cherishing attitude. 
 
The self-grasping conception and the self-cherishin g attitude cause our  downfall in this and all 
our future lives to come, preventing us from ac hieving liberation and enlightenment. This is the 
great ghost  who, until it is destroyed, brings on a ll the samsaric problems  and sufferings of the 
three realms.  Our hand is burnt when it is placed in fire. The only way to prevent this is to put out the fire 
which causes the burning. This analogy can be applied to the self-cherishing and self-grasping 
attitudes.   
Question from a student : If we refer to the self -cherishing attitude as the great ghost,  then have the 
hearers, the solitary realizers, arhats and foe destroyers abandoned self-cherishing? Have they 
abandoned cyclic existence? 
 
Answer :  
We are now looking specifically at the instruction of  exchanging self and other. To do this, we 
must get rid of the self-cherishing attitude and th e way to do this is to look at its faults.  
 In this context then, the self-che rishing attitude is described as the great ghost , the source of all our 
unhappiness and problems and is the root of cyclic  existence.  Accepting th is, we can then assert 
that until we get rid of this attitude, there is no way we can be freed from cyclic existence.  
 
In the twelve links of dependent origination, the fi rst link is ignorance, wh ich refers to the self-
grasping conception and this is clearly established as the root of cyclic ex istence. The two links of 
craving and grasping are essentially attachment of different intensities.    But here, we are examining the instructions on ho w to develop concern for the welfare of and to 
cherish others. The main obstacle to doing this is our self-cherishing attitude so to eliminate this 
attitude, we have to look at all its faults and disadvantages. Since it is the basis for all our 
samsaric difficulties, in this context, it is a sserted to be the root of  cyclic existence.   
 The hearers, solitary realizers and foe destroyers are liberated from suffering and cyclic existence. 
But it is questionable whether they have  abandoned the self-cherishing attitude.  
 
If I do not completely forsake it,/I shall be unable to put an end to suffering: 
Think if these two lines can be interpreted as: “I f I do not completely forsake my self-cherishing 
attitude, I will be unable to put an end to the suffering of all other sentient beings.” 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
266  
b’ Synopsis 
1” The way to exchange self and other 
2” Arresting misapplication [of the principle] once one has exchanged 
3” Practicing unerringly 
1” The way to exchange self and other71 
(8, 136) 
Therefore, in order to allay the harms inflicted upon me 
And in order to pacify the sufferings of others, 
I shall give myself up to others 
And cherish them as I do my very self. 
 
(8, 137) 
"I am under the control of others," 
Of this, mind, you must be certain; 
Now, except for benefiting every creature, 
You must not think of anything else. 
 
Therefore,  in Verse 136, refers to all the earlier reason s given for asserting that the self-cherishing 
attitude is the source of all undesirable things. In order to stop harming ourselves and others, we 
need to discard this attitude and cherish others instead. 
I shall give myself up to others:  T h i s  i s  d o n e  a t  a  m e n t a l  l e v e l ,  d e d i c a t i n g  o u r   e x i s t e n c e  t o  t h e  
happiness of others. This means that we belong to and are owned by others and we must always 
remember that, except for benefiting  othe rs, we are not to consider anything else. 
 
                                              
71 Thus, neither be self-centered nor support self-centered tendencies. You must train again and again in the 
attitude of freely giving to all beings your own body, resources, and roots of virtue, and you must work for the 
welfare of those to whom you give these things. It is wrong to do the opposite, so stop the attitude which sees your 
own body, resources, and roots of virtue as being for your own purposes: 
"I am controlled by others"; 
You, O mind, know this for certain. Now do not think of anything Other than the welfare of all beings.   It is wrong to achieve my own aims  With my eyes, etc. that are under others' control.  Therefore, it is incorrect to do wrong to others  With my eyes, etc. that are for their welfare. 
Similarly, when you repeatedly reflect on the benefits of cherishing others, you produce powerful enthusiasm from 
the depths of your heart. Discontinue your present atti tude of disregarding others and keep your potential 
disregard of others from arising. Through affectionately viewing others as dear and beloved as much as you can, 
develop an attitude that cherishes others to the degree to which you previously cherished yourself - "Cherish others 
as you do yourself.” ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, pg. 55-56) 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
267  
Once we have dedicated ourselves to others, we completely exchange self and other. Henceforth, 
whatever we do, we should do only for others. Whatever we think, we should only consider the interest of others. 
2” Arresting misapplication [of the principle] once one has exchanged 
(8,138) 
For my sake, I should not do anything 
With these eyes and so forth, which I have left at the disposal of others. 
It is quite incorrect to  do anything with them 
Which is contrary to the benefit (of others). 
 
Having exchanged self and other, other sentient  beings become our bosses and  we become  
servants at the service of all other living bein gs. Whatever we do with our bodies, speech and 
minds, we should never disturb or harm other li v i n g  b e i n g s .  W h e n  w e  f i n d  t h a t  w h a t  w e  a r e  
about to do contradicts this principle, we sh ould immediately put a stop to such  wrong 
behaviour. 
3” Practicing unerringly 
(8,139) 
Thus sentient beings should be my main (concern). 
Whatever I behold upon my body 
I should rob and use 
For the benefit of others. 
 
Having dedicated ourselves to others, we work on ly to benefit them. “Robbing” ourselves of our 
possessions means that, instead of being attached an d grasping at them with the attitude,  “This 
is mine,” we should replace that attitude with the thought: “I am now the servant of others. I 
shall henceforth use my wealth and possessi ons only for the benefit of others.”  
 Having dedicated our possessions, virtues, merit, et c. to the benefit of others, these things belong 
to them.  At the same time, we still need to us e our possessions. How do we go about doing this? 
We should do so like a servant using the belongin gs of the master e.g., staying in the master’s 
home, eating his food and so forth. The servant does this in order to be able to do his master’s work.  
 
When we dedicate sincerely our  possessions in this way, our sense of ownership of and our 
attachment to them will weaken as will our self -cherishing attitude. When this happens, there 
will less suffering for ourselves. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2

Lesson 48   Chapter VIII - verse 140-150 
The great Kadampa masters said: “All sentient be ings possess buddha nature, but when they do 
not make the effort to awaken it, there is no way th ey  can obtain a higher rebirth,  liberation or 
enlightenment.” 
 
Butter comes from milk but simply staring at the pot of milk will not turn it into butter. The 
milk must be churned. The same  applies to our aspiration to higher rebirth, liberation and 
enlightenment for the sake of sentient beings. Although we have the potential to achieve all 
these, we must put in the effort to awaken that potential by practising the Buddhadharma. 
Otherwise, nothing happens..   
1’ Explaining the way to exchange self and other 
2’ The way to practice after having reflected on this exchange 
a’ Presentation 
b’ Explanation 
1” The way jealousy for those higher than oneself is cultivated] 
a” The way it is cultivated b” How it is practiced once it has been cultivated 
2” The way competitiveness is cultivated between those equal to oneself 
a”   Competing over material gain and honor 
b”   Competing over the reputation of one’s qualities 
c”   Competing over the list of one’s goodnesses d”   Developing a like for dispensing harm 
3” The way pride over those lower than oneself is cultivated 4” The effects of cultivation 
3’ The way to practice application [of this attitude] 
a’ Presentation 
 (8,140) 
Considering lesser beings and so forth as myself, 
And considering myself as the other, 
(In the following way) I shou ld meditate upon envy,  
competitiveness and self-importance, 
With a mind free of distorted concepts:72 
 
After we have reflected on the exchange of self and other, how do we practise? 
 
There are three kinds of sentient beings: (1) thos e whom we deem inferior to us  (2) those who 
have equal status as us and (3) th ose who have superior qualities.  
                                              
72 From stanzas 141 to 154 inclusive, as a practice of exchanging  self for others, the role of ‘I’ and ‘other’ is literally 
reversed. Thus ‘I’ should be understood as all other sentie nt beings and ‘he’ should be understood as oneself. The 
meditation on envy is covered in stanzas 141-146, comp etitiveness in stanzas 147-150 and self –importance in 
stanzas 151-154. (Shantideva, Gu ide to the Bodhisattva’s Way of Life, Notes, pg. 175) 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
269  
W h e n  w e  e n c o u n t e r  p e o p l e  w e  c o n s i d e r  i n fe r i o r  t o  u s , w e  a r e  p r o u d  a n d  a r r o g a n t .  W h e n  w e  
meet those who are equal to us, we develop a sens e of competitiveness and we generate jealousy 
towards those who have superior qualities.  Th ese are our usual reactions when we come into 
contact with these three different groups of people.  
 When we exchange self and other, we reverse roles. We take them as “I,” and we become “other.” 
For example, we take our object of jealousy to  be “I” and that person to be ourselves.  
 Usually, we neglect the welfare of others. Not only  do we not work for their benefit, we do our 
best to ensure that we are protected from undesi rable things while wishing such things to befall 
others.   What we usually do is cherish ourselves, workin g only to accomplish happiness for ourselves and 
eliminating our own problems and suffering.  No matter how many good qualities we may 
cultivate, it does not stop us from developing jealousy towards others. Also, our acquisition of 
wealth and reputation leads to the generation of pride and a sense of competitiveness when 
others out-perform us, leading to more jealousy. As a result, we accumulate the different kinds of 
negative karma.   Verse 140 points out we need to cultivate a stable  realisation of the need to exchange self and 
other, With a mind free of distorted concepts .  O n  t h e  b a s i s  o f  t h a t  s t a b l e  r e c o g n i t i o n  o f  t h e  “ I ”  
becoming  “other” and the “other” becoming onesel f,  we meditate on jealously, competitiveness 
and pride. The benefit of this practice is that it  helps us to understand the suffering of others. 
 
Exchange between a student and Geshe-la : (Geshe-la’s remarks are in italics) 
 Is the mind that apprehends “I ” as “other” a valid cognizer? No.  
 
Is that mind a mistaken consciousness? Yes. 
 Can we accomplish anything positive by me ditating on exchanging self and others?   Yes.  
 Is the mind that views all sentient be ings as buddhas a wrong consciousness?    Yes. 
 Is there any benefit in seeing all sentient beings as buddhas?   Yes. 
 Geshe-la continues : The consciousnesses that apprehend sunlig ht as the sun or the light of a butter-
lamp as the sun are both wrong consciousnesses. When  we are looking for the sun, even though 
apprehending sunlight as the sun is a wrong cons ciousness,  depending on that, we will be able 
to find the sun. However, we will not  find th e sun by apprehending th e light from the butter 
lamp as the sun. Therefore, although both are wrong consciousnesses, one consciousness leads us 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
270  
to our goal while the other does not.   
 Similarly, we can apply this reasoning to the self  grasping conception and the attitude that holds 
“I” as “other.” Both are wrong consciousnesses bu t the former has been established as a mistaken 
consciousness and an object of abando nment while the latter benefits us.

1” The way jealousy for those higher than oneself is cultivated]

b” How it is practiced once it has been cultivated

a”   The way it is cultivated 
(8,141) 
"He is honoured, but I am not; 
I have not found wealth such as he. 
He is praised, but I am despised; 
He is happy, but I suffer. 
 
(8,142) 
"I have to do all the work, 
While he remains comfortably at rest. 
He is renowned as great in th is world, but I as inferior, 
With no good qualities at all 
 
(8, 143) 
"But what do you mean I have no good qualities? 
I have all good qualities. 
Compared to many, he is inferior, 
And compared to many, I am high. 
 
(8, 144)  
"The deteriorated state of my morals and views 
Is not due to me, but due to my disturbing conceptions, 
 
This particular meditation is done by switching  roles with someone whom  we see as lower than 
ourselves. We become that person, who is now re ferred to as “I” and the other party becomes us 
– the “other” (or “he”).   The “I” then cultivates jealousy towards the “oth er,” thinking, “He has everything while I have 
nothing. He is praised but I am despised. I wo rk like a dog but he just hangs around, doing 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
271  
nothing.  
 “He has all the qualities but I, being the inferior on e, has no qualities whatsoever. But surely, it is 
relative whether one has qualities or not. Yes, he has more qualities than me but compared to 
some other people, I am superior.  
What right does he have to look down on and behave so arrogantly towards me? 
 
“Yes, I may be a less ethical a person than he is. I am definitely poorer than him. On that basis, 
he feels justified in his contempt.  
“But this is not my fault. I didn’t choose to be  a bad person. I did not choose to be poor. These 
circumstances were caused by my strong nega tive emotions like anger, attachment and 
ignorance.”    Having cultivated this attitude by reflecting in this way, how do we put it into practice?

 The meditation continues with the “I” addressing th e “other”: “He is  rich and thinks of himself 
as a good person. He should then help and support me in overcoming my difficulties.

“Actually, he is the one with all the faults since he has no compa ssion and he does not help other 
people. He should recognise his faults and stop pretending to have good qualities.” 
 
Question from a  student: (inaudible) 
Answer:  We know that we are jealous of those who ha ve superior qualities. By exchanging self 
and other, we understand how those at the top look down on those of lower qualities. By 
generating jealousy in this way, we will come to  understand how we suffer when people look 
down on us. Knowing this, we will no longer be have in an arrogant and proud manner towards 
others, even if they have lesser qualities than ourselves.

d” Developing a like for dispensing harm 
(8,150) 
"For a long time I shall look with pleasure 
At his being made inferior; 
He will become the laughing-stock of all, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
273  
Regarded among everyone as an object of scorn and derision. 
 
When we associate with those of equal status or those who are only slightly better than ourselves, 
we develop a sense of competitiveness. Here, we switch roles with our ‘competitor,’ (now referred 
to as ‘I’)  and meditate on our competitiveness over material gain and honour, how we hide our 
faults and promote our own good qualities and how we gloat when unhappy things befall the 
others.  
 
This meditation helps us recognise our faults and is  the antidote to our engaging in such negative 
competitiveness. Instead, we will want to try to help our “competitor.”   
Lesson 49   Chapter VIII - verse 151-158 
There is a saying by the great Kadampa masters:  “The difference between cyclic existence and 
nirvana comes from whether we have realised the nature of our minds or not.” 
 
Liberation may seem external, like a distant place. But it is not like that. We can achieve it on the 
basis of our minds. In the same wa y, cyclic existence is not an ex ternal phenomenon. It abides in 
our minds. As long as our minds are   under the control and bondage of the afflictions, we 
remain in cyclic existence. We achieve liberation  at that very moment when our minds are freed 
from the control of our afflictions. So liberation  is not something far away or external and once 
liberated, we will experience everlasting bliss and happiness. 
 With reference to the paths and grounds - from th e path of accumulation through to the path of 
preparation, followed by the path of seeing, the ten bodhisattva grounds, the path of no more 
learning and, finally, enlightenment – the diffe rence between each level and each ground is 
primarily based on the qualities of the mind an d its development. We assert that someone has 
achieved and is abiding in a specific path on the basis of their mental development, not their 
physical transformation. How do we differentiate  between a bodhisattva and a non-bodhisattva? 
The difference lies not in their external appearan ces but on the basis on whether that person has 
developed bodhicitta or not.   Another way of interpreting the quotation is this: As  soon as we have realised the ultimate nature 
of the mind, its lack of true existence, then we have achieved liberation.  As long as we have not reached this realisation, we remain imprisoned by our afflictions.   Engaging in physical and verbal virtues (or positive actions) contributes to our mental 
development and this helps us one day, to realis e the emptiness of our minds. When we achieve 
the wisdom realising emptiness, we destroy cyclic existence. This is one of the benefits of realising 
emptiness. 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
274  
When our self-cherishing attitude is very strong, it  is very difficult for our actions to be virtuous. 
Furthermore, during the course of engaging in virtue, other afflictions such as competitiveness 
(in a negative sense), jealousy and pride arise.  
 
For example, arrogance and conceit may arise when we are doing retreat, “ I am in retreat and 
they are not.” Also, during the course of this 5-year Basic Pr ogram, we have acquired some 
knowledge and understanding of the Dharma. That  knowledge can be the condition for us to 
feel superior to others, thinking, “ I know more than you do.”  
 
It is important that our actions do not become the conditions for the development of jealousy, 
competitiveness and pride. These afflictions are harmful and therefore, we must learn how to 
apply the antidotes to overcome them.   In the context of exchanging self and other, we exchange roles with the other party and once we 
have done that,  we meditate on jealousy, competitiveness and pride. The purpose of the 
meditation is to reduce the self-cherishing attitu de, which we should always regard as the enemy. 
 
1’ Explaining the way to exchange self and other 
2’ The way to practice after having reflected on this exchange

b’ Explanation

a” The aspect of cultivated pride 
(8, 151) 
"It is said that this deluded one 
Is trying to compete with me, 
But how can he be equal with me 
In learning, intelligence, form, class or wealth? 
 
In this meditation, we switch roles with someon e who has more qualities than us and who looks 
down on us because of his pride. That person now becomes the “I” and the “other” is the party 
who is being looked down upon. 
  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
275  
The “I” then addresses the “other” in these term s, “How can you hope to compete with me who 
is superior to you in intelligence, knowledge, looks and wealth? There is no way you can even 
come close to what I have achieved.”   
b” The qualities of cultivation 
(8,152) 
"Thus, upon hearing of my good qualities 
That have been made well-known to all, 
I shall thoroughly enjoy the satisfaction 
Of the pleasant, tingling sensation that occurs. 
 
“I” continues, “Others rejoice and are happy to kn ow of my good qualities. They experience a 
special happiness and bliss through understanding how I am better than others in so many ways. 
You and other sentient beings should also feel happy in the same way.” 
c” The way to practice by means of application once cultivation has taken place 
 
(8, 153) 
"Even though he has some possessions, 
If he is working for me, 
I shall give him just enough to live on, 
And by force I'll take (the rest). 
 The “I” continues to address the “other,” “This pe rson will do what I want and I will give him 
only enough for him to survive.”   In summary, these meditations are done on the basi s of having exchanged self and other, without 
which the meditation cannot be pe rformed. As mentioned in verse 140, we need to meditate 
with, a mind free of distorted concepts,  with a stable attitude of havi ng exchanged self and other.  
 Once we understand this, we apply this  meditation in the following way:  
• For those we consider inferior or lower  than  us, we switch places with them and meditate 
on jealousy.  
• For those we consider equal to us, we swi tch places with them  and meditate on 
competitiveness.  
• For those we consider superior to us, we switc h places with them and meditate on pride.  
 Other commentaries use the terms: the old “I” and the new “I”. The old “I” is the self-cherishing 
“I,” who is totally absorbed with one’s own we lfare and completely disregards the welfare of 
others. The new “I” is the person who cherishe s others and disregards his own well-being.  
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
276  
In verse 153, the new “I” is telling the old “I,” “You ’d better listen to what I say. If you follow my 
orders, I will give you just enough food to keep you alive and I will  keep the rest for myself.” Why 
are we meditating in this way? So that  we can subdue our self-cherishing.  
d” Reflecting on the disadvantages of being separated from such pride 
(8, 154)73 
"His happiness and comfort will decline 
And I shall always cause him harm, 
 
The old “I” will suffer: His  happiness and comfort will decline. 
 
This meditation on exchanging self and other he lps us understand the difficulties and suffering 
experienced by other people. Knowing this, we wi ll no longer look down on others, compete 
with them or be jealous of them.  
 It is said in the commentary: 
• Through cultivating jealousy, it will act as an antidote to pride.   
• Through cultivating competitiven ess, it will act as an antidote to competitiveness. 
                                             
 
73 After you have eliminated the obstacles to exchanging self and other in this way, the actual way to meditate is as 
follows. Out of attachment to self, your self-cherishin g attitude has produced all sorts of undesirable things 
throughout the beginningless time of cyclic existence up to  now. Although you wanted to make things perfect for 
yourself, you emphasized your own welfare and engaged in  improper methods. You have spent countless eons at 
this, but have not at all accomplished your own or others ' aims. Not only have you not achieved these, you have 
been tormented only by suffering. If  you had replaced concern for your own welfare with concern for others' 
welfare, you would certainly have already become a budd ha long ago and would have completely and perfectly 
accomplished your own aims as well as those of others. As  you did not do this, you have spent your time uselessly 
and laboriously. 
 Make a firm determination by thinking, "Now I understa nd the faults of self-cherishing and the benefits of 
cherishing others. With great effort, relying on mindfu lness and vigilance, I will discontinue the present self-
cherishing, my greatest enemy, and I wi ll not allow any potential self-cherishin g to arise." In this way frequently 
stop self-cherishing. Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds says:  
Self-cherishing has harmed me in all My myriad lifetimes in cyclic existence. 
 
You, O mind, though you spent countless eons 
Wanting to accomplish your own welfare, Through such great hardship You have accomplished only suffering. 
 
And also: If formerly you had acted for others' welfare,  
This condition which lacks  
The perfect happiness of buddhahood  
Could not possibly have occurred.   ( Lamrim Chenmo , Vol. 2, Pg. 55)  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION

• Through cultivating pride, it will act as an antidote to jealousy.

a” Reflecting on the disadvantages of self-cherishing 
(8,154 cd) 
For hundreds of times in this cycle of rebirth 
He has caused harm to me." 
 
(8, 155) 
Because of desiring to benefit yourself, O mind, 
All weariness you have gone through 
Over countless past aeons 
Has only succeeded in achieving misery.  
 
All the problems and sufferings that we have been  experiencing since beginningless time till now 
can be attributed to our self-cherishing attitude.  Surely then, we should ask ourselves why are we 
still harbouring this self-cherishing attitude which only brings us problems and suffering?  
b” The proof that the benefits of cherishing others are limitless 
(8,156) 
Therefore, I shall definitely engage myself 
In working for the benefit of others, 
For since the words of the Mighty One are infallible, 
I shall behold its advantages in the future.  
 Since working for ourselves brought us only prob lems and suffering, henceforth we shall apply 
ourselves to working enthusiastica lly for the welfare of others. If we can do this, the result is 
happiness in this and all our future lives. The Bu ddha said this and his words are infallible. The 
b e n e f i t s  o f  c h e r i s h i n g  o t h e r s  b e c o m e  a p p a r e nt when we enter the paths and achieve the 
bodhisattva grounds. 
c” Explaining just the benefits and qualities 
(8, 157) 
If, in the past, I had practiced 
This act (of exchanging self for others), 
A situation such as this, devoid of th e magnificence and bliss of a Buddha, 
Could not possibly have come about. 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
278  
Qualm : We may work for others for a long time but we don’t see the results.  
 
Reply:  It is not like that. In the past, if we had exchanged self and other and worked for the 
benefit of others, we would definitely have achi eved everlasting happiness and the state of full 
enlightenment. It is because we had failed to meditate on bodhicitta and exchanging self and 
other that we remain stuck in sa msara and continue to experience all its attendant problems and  
difficulties. 
 
By understanding the faults of self cherishing, we generate regret for having such a harmful 
attitude and become determined to focus on exchanging self and other and meditate on 
bodhicitta. 
d” Instructions on making effort to cherish others 
(8,158) 
Therefore, just as I have come to hold as "I" 
These drops of sperm and blood of others, 
Likewise, through acquaintance 
I should also come to regard all others . 
 With familiarity, we can definitely exchange self and other, just as we grasp at our bodies, which 
actually comes from the egg and sperm of our pare nts, as “I.” We have become habituated to 
seeing our bodies as belonging to us.   That being the case then, with familiarity and with effort, it is conceivable that we can also come 
to regards others as “I” as well.  
Lesson 50   Chapter VIII - verse 159-166 
It is said, “One needs the lamp of hearing to remove the fog of the da rkness of ignorance.” Why 
is this so? Without listening to the teachings, we would not know what should be abandoned and 
cultivated and how to go about doin g  this.  It is imperative, therefore, for us to listen to and 
study the teachings.  Gungtang Rinpoche said, “In order to understand wh at it is that we should abandon and what it 
is that we need to cultivate, before we can begin to  engage in these activities, we need to listen to 
the teachings.”   We should not let whatever we have heard or le arnt in the teachings and our studies be carried 
away by the wind, going in one ear and out the ot her. When we do not re late the teachings and 
apply them to our minds, we will not experience any improvement, just as a rock sitting in a pond remains unchanged.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
279  
 
At the end of Gungtang Rinpoche’s collection of advice, he says: “It is ju st something that comes 
from the mouth when, without any experience of the Dharma, I ask others to study and practise 
when I have not done so myself. It becomes very  difficult to encourage others to practise.” 
 
He continues, “Without my practising, it is hard  to inspire others to do so and to follow my 
advice.  However, if there is any virtue that may have been accumulated by my repeating these 
words that came from the holy speech of the trul y qualified spiritual masters, may this virtue 
become the cause for all migrators to achieve enligh tenment.” This is his dedication at the end of 
the text.   I find this last verse applicable to myself. I te ach different subjects but since I do not have any 
experience, it is difficult for my teachings to in spire and to persuade you to practise the Dharma. 
Nonetheless, I am repeating what is in the text pertaining to the instructions on exchanging self 
and other. That is beneficial for me. 
  Likewise, if there is any virtue or merit that co mes from my teaching this  text, then I also pray 
and dedicate them that they may be of use an d benefit to others. For those of you who are 
listening to these teachings, if there is any virtue  that comes from your doing so, you should also 
dedicate that virtue to the enli ghtenment of all living beings. 
 
1’ Explaining the way to exchange self and other 
2’ The way to practice after having reflected on this exchange 
3’ The way to practice application [of this attitude] 
a’ The actual 
1” Applying all of one’s entire collection of goodness to others 
a” The actual

a” The actual 
(8,159) 
Having thoroughly examined myself (to see 
Whether I am really workin g for) others (or not), 
I shall steal (seize) whatever appears on my body and use it for the  benefit of others. 
 
The earlier outline, The way to practise after having reflected on this exchange , refers to the kind of 
mental perspective we should develop. In this se ction, having developed that mental attitude, we 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
280  
put exchanging self and other into actual practice. 
 This verse advises us to spy on ourselves to chec k what we are up to. Ar e we harming or are we 
benefiting others with our actions? We should  snatch away whatever we have – our bodies, 
enjoyments and possessions – and offer them to  others who are deprived of these things, 
reducing our sense of ownership and our conception that, “These are my things.” Imagining that 
we are bodhisattvas, we tell ourselves: "Whatever I have, I want to dedicate everything to others." 
When we need to use our possessions, we should al ways do so with the thought that we are using 
them for the benefit of others. 
 
I shall steal (seize ): With relation to our possessions, we al ways have the strong concept that, “This 
is mine and not yours. I am the owner.” By “sei zing” our possessions from ourselves, they no 
longer belong to us but to someone else and, in this way, we eliminate our sense of ownership of 
these objects. What makes it so difficult for us to dedicate our things to others? It is this grasping 
at those things as belonging to “me.” When this sense of ownershi p is weakened, we will be able 
to dedicate and give our possessions to others easily. 
b” The reasonableness of [others] being jealous of oneself 
(8,160) 
"I am happy but others are sad, 
I am high though others are low, 
I benefit myself but not others." 
Why am I not envious of myself? 
 
This outline should be amended to, The reasonableness of being jealous of oneself, referring to 
how we should be jealous of ourselves, rather  than other people being jealous of us.   
 
Here, we reflect on how much we have in term s of possessions, good things, reputation and 
status. Those who are in lower posi tions, have inferior qualities, are destitute and so forth have 
none of these things. 
 
I benefit myself but not others , can be  explained in different ways:  
(1) I have the opportunity to engage in virtue because I know how to do so and I know what 
good karma means. Other people, on the other hand , have no idea of how to engage in virtue. 
As a result, they only engage in negative ac tions that will ultimately harm them; or  
(2) There are so many people who are helping to  benefit me. But other people have no one to 
help them. 
 
The purpose of generating jealously towards oursel ves is to realise that we are not equal with 
others. We have many good things which others lack. Other people have many problems and are 
suffering. This becomes completely unacceptable to us and we realise we must do something 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
281  
practical to equalise the situation.  
 This meditation on the cultivation of jealousy is  different from the earlier one we had learnt. The 
earlier meditation was done on the basis of ou r having switched roles with someone else. Here, 
we are meditating on cultivating jealousy towards ourselves. 
 
We should relate verses 159 and 160 back to the broader outline of Applying all of one’s entire 
collection of goodness to others the idea that we should give all our good things to others. This is one 
of the great benefits of having an outline. Some times reading the root text alone may not give us 
a clear idea of its essential meaning. By relati ng the text to the outline, we get a clearer 
understanding of the main message it is trying to convey.  
 
2” Devoting oneself to [taking] the lower position for the sake of others 
a” Examining one’s own faults 
b” Confessing wrongs one has done to others 
c” Devoting oneself to [taking] the lower position 
a” Examining one’s own faults 
(8,161) 
I must separate myself from happiness 
And take upon myself th e sufferings of others. 
“Why am I doing this now?” 
In this way, I should examine myself for faults. 
 
In the past, we were happy while others were be set with problems and suffering. Henceforth, we 
will separate ourselves from the happiness that comes from the enjoyment of our possessions, 
giving them instead to others, fo r their enjoyment and happiness.   
 Others will then be happy. Bereft  of our possessions, we will no longer experience the happiness 
associated with their enjoyment and we would ha ve to endure whatever pain and suffering may 
arise from this separation. It is as if we had given away our happiness to others. We bring 
happiness to others and we take upon ourselves their suffering.   In the past, we had always scrutinised the faults  of others and not our own. Henceforth, having 
exchanged self and other and havi ng studied this text which is like a mirror for our own minds, 
we will see our own shortcomings instea d of looking for the faults of others. 
 All our studies and practices and whatever teaching s we have received, they  all act as mirrors for 
our minds, helping us to look at our own faults. They  are not tools to help us look at the faults of 
others. We should always examine the faults of our bodies, speech and minds. What are we 
thinking? What are we saying? How are we behavi ng physically? We need to always check up.  
b” Confessing wrongs one has done to others 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
282  
(8, 162) 
Although others may do something wrong, 
I should transform it into a fault of my own; 
But should I do something even slightly wrong, 
I shall openly admit it to many people. 
 
(8, 163) 
By further describing the renown of others, 
I should make it outshine my own. 
 
When we do something wrong, we usually push the blame to someone else, “He did it,” hiding 
w h a t e v e r  f a u l t s  w e  h a v e .  S o  n o w ,  w h e n  o t h e r s  m a k e  m i s t a k e s  o r  c a u s e  u s  s o m e  h a r m  o r  inconvenience, instead of blaming them, we shou ld see them as our faults and mistakes, as 
results of our own negative karma. Furthermore,  we voluntarily accept and even pray for the 
fruitional result of that mist ake to ripen on us alone.  
 When it comes to our own faults and mistakes, we should confess and admit them openly to 
many people even if the mistake we made was a very small one. The point is to own up to our 
mistakes, to admit and confess them. I am not su r e  w h e t h e r  w e  h a v e  t o  d o  t h i s  b e f o r e  m a n y  
people though.   Our usual behaviour is always to put down and cr iticise others, picking out their faults while we 
only praise ourselves. Rather than criticising othe rs and praising ourselves, we should now do the 
opposite. When we hear of others’ good qualities,  we should promote them, saying, "That is true. 
He is very good.”    When we are praised, we should make it known that there are many  others  who are far superior 
to ourselves in terms of qualities, renown and re putation, so that the good qualities of others 
outshine our own: I should make it outshine my own. 
 
Some commentaries on the verse 163 say, “We shou ld make the renown or qualities of others 
outshine my own," which means that we should hide our good qualities and not promote them 
in any way.  
 In summary, this verse is advising us to see the harm s, faults and mistakes done to us by others as 
our own faults and karma. But we are to confe ss openly and admit to whatever mistakes or 
wrongs we have done to others.   
c” Devoting oneself to [taking] the lower position 
(8, 163 cd) 
Just like the lowest kind of servant, 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
283  
I should employ myself for the benefit of all. 
 
(8, 164) 
I should not praise my naturally fault-ridden self 
For some temporary good quality it may have, 
I shall never let even a few people know 
Of any good qualities I may possess. 
 
In the past, we had always treated others as lowe r than ourselves, using them as servants for our 
benefit. Now, we do the reverse. We act humb ly as servants, working for their benefit and 
happiness.     We should always see our own faults. We should not think highly of ourselves even if we have 
some small good qualities (e.g., having heard some teachings, understood and studied a little 
Dharma and doing a few retreats).  We should not be arrogant or proud nor should we promote these qualities to others. We should keep them secret.    According to the outline, this is how we put ours elves in the lower position. We see ourselves as 
servants and others as our bosses or powerful peop le. As servants, we work for the benefit of our 
employers.  When we see ourselves as lower than ot hers, it makes it easier for us to work for and 
to serve them. The inability to see ourselves in th is way is one of the obstacles that prevents us 
from helping others. “This is too menial for me ,  f o r  s o m e o n e  o f  m y  h i g h  s t a t u s . ”  S u c h  a n  
attitude prevents us from helping others. 
3” A presentation of he way to practice application in brief 
(8, 165) 
In brief, for the sake of living creatures, 
May all the harms 
I have selfishly caused to others 
Descend upon me myself. 
 
(8, 166) 
I should not be dominating and aggressive, 
Acting in a self-righteous, arrogant way; 
Instead, like a newly married bride, 
I should be bashful, timid and restrained 
 From time without beginning, we had made use of and harming others to acquire possessions, 
wealth and respect. Henceforth, may all the harm s that I had caused descend upon myself alone 
in order that I may benefit others.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
284  
Verse 166 says that we should not be domineerin g and aggressive and behave in a bossy, arrogant 
way. An example of a newly married bride is used because it was customary for her to be bashful, 
timid and restraint.  
 
We are usually proud, arrogant and selfish. We sh ould be afraid of such attitudes and behaviour 
and learn to restrain our three doors (bodies, sp eech and minds) so that we do not think and act 
in these ways. 
 
We should not look for the faults  of others but rather at our ow n faults.  We should proclaim 
the qualities of others and hide our own qualitie s. We should not be je alous of those who are 
better off than us nor should we despise and disr egard those inferior to us. At all times, we 
should admit and proclaim our mistakes.   
Answer to a student’s question : 
In verse 159, we literally steal our own possession s and give and dedicate them to others. The 
purpose is to reduce our grasping at things as belonging to “me.”  
 
Verse 161 is similar to the practice of tongle n (giving and taking), where we dedicate our 
happiness to others and we voluntarily take on the suffering of others.   Giving away our enjoyments or possessions means we  will no longer be able to experience the 
bliss or happiness that comes from having or usin g them. The  absence of that bliss or happiness 
is a form of suffering that we would have to endure.  
Lesson 51   Chapter VIII - verse 167-176 
Gungtang Rinpoche advises, “Eve n if you owned mountain-high piles of gold, enough to cover 
the entire country, at the time of death, you will not be able to bring along a single atom of it 
with you. On the other hand, by reciting a mant ra like ‘Om Mani Padme Hum’ just once, that 
can open the door to a good rebirth in your fu ture life. Simply reciting ‘Om Mani Padme Hum’ 
is very beneficial. ”  
We can understand, when we analyse our situat ion more deeply, that material wealth cannot 
really benefit us, even in future lives. In fact, th e more we own, the more we grasp at these things. 
It increases our self-cherishing thoughts, atta chment and so forth which only create more 
negative karma that will not benefit us in our future lives.  
 Sometimes, we think, “I will definitely achi eve something and I will be happier and more 
satisfied if I am rich in this li fe.” But if we profess to attach greater importance to the happiness 
of our future lives, then having this kind of worldly goal is incorrect. If we are concerned with 
this life alone, then that is a different matter.  Otherwise, our goal should not be like that.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program               CHAPTER VIII - CONCENTRATION  
 
285  
 
Reciting “Om Mani Padme Hum” is just an ex ample. We should engage in our meditation 
practice and daily prayers or a single recitation  of “Om Mani Padme Hum”  with the conviction 
and single-pointed faith that we will definitely ac hieve happiness in our future lives. We need to 
generate that faith of conviction and be happy wi th whatever we are doing. Rejoice that we are 
doing this wonderful practice. It would be very good if we can do this.  
 
The whole point of practising the Dharma is to remove suffering and misery. Some people think 
like this: “I am just a nobody in this life. I am poor and will probably stay that way. I will never 
amount to anything.” Thinking like  that only brings unhappiness.  
 
Practising the Dharma is to increase whatever happy thoughts we may ha ve. We need to know 
h o w  t o  b e  h a p p y .  W e  s h o u l d  t h i n k :  “ E v e n  i f  I  do not become rich, at least now I have the 
opportunity to study and practise  the teachings and I am creating  the causes for happiness in my 
future lives.” We need to generate this belief, to  have this faith of conv iction and to feel happy 
doing our  practices by seeing the purpose in what we are doing. In his advice, Gungtang 
Rinpoche is telling us to practi se the Dharma because it creates the cause for our happiness. As 
the Lamrim says, at the time of death, only the Dharma helps.
