FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
314  
c) An extensive explanation of the reasoning establishing emptiness 
3) Instructions on making effort to de velop that (realization of emptiness) 
b The chapter’s name 
 
Whenever you deal with more challenging to pics, you must pay attention right from the 
beginning of the class. When you are distracted, you will probably get nothing from the session 
because the material is all inter-connected. Nothin g much can be done if you come to class and 
you are really tired.  But the point is the more you concentrate, the more  you pay attention, the 
more you will learn. At the very least, you should  try to extract the essence of what was taught. 
 
When you have been attending teachings for so me time, especially those who have attended 
many teachings, you may be present physically but th e mind is distracted.  It is like that, isn’t it? 
When you listen to songs, the mind is always co ncentrated but when you listen to the teachings, 
the mind is easily  distracted. Over time, your attitude towards listening to the teachings becomes 
more flippant and that is not good. Sometimes, yo u  may console yourself by thinking: “It is all 
right. It doesn’t matter whether I understand or no t. I will try to be better in the next lesson.” 
This may work but I think most of  the time, things do not really  work out this way. Whenever 
you are at any teachings, you have to make a pledge  to yourself: “I’ll try my best to listen and pay 
attention to what is being said.”   There are also students who have unrealistic expectations. They ex pect to understand everything 
they hear there and now. This is impossible.  Wh at is needed here is perseverance. With those 
expectations, when they do not  understand a fe w words, they get upset and  uptight. This is 
pointless. If you  really want to learn, then you must persevere.  
 I am not going to say this over and over again be cause it is not very pleasant for the ears. It 
probably burns your ears! The point is whenever you are dealing with a difficult topic, please try 
your best to listen and pay attention.  
 
Dependant Arising – A Praise of the Buddha  
4 
“Whatever depends on circumsta nces is empty of nature.” 
What greater teaching is there than this! 
The foolish, however, seize on it and only tighten chains of extreme views, 
While for the wise it cuts e ntangled nets of fabrication. 
 
For some, the teachings on dependent originatio n binds them to cyclic existence while for 
others, these  teachings liberate them from samsara.  The proponents of Vaibhasika and Sautrantika assert: “Phenomena arise from causes and conditions and because of this, they exist inherent ly.” This grasping at phenomena as inherently 
existent is the self grasping ignorance and, under its  power, we take rebirth in cyclic existence. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
315  
This grasping at inherently existent phenomena bi nds us to and prevents us from getting out of 
cyclic existence. 
 
For the proponents of Prasangika, with the same understanding that phenomena arise in 
dependence on causes and conditions, they conc lude that this means that phenomena do not 
exist inherently, because they are dependently aris en. This understanding of  the lack of inherent 
existence of phenomena (this view of emptiness) helps the proponents of Prasangika to cut the 
entangled nets of extreme views and the root of cyclic existence.  
 
In the previous lesson, we learnt that there we re two types of persons: the common people and 
the yogis. The yogis harmed (refuted) the common people and also harmed one another. We also 
saw how the Buddhist tenets harmed the non-Buddhis t tenets by employing logic and reasoning.  
We are not saying that all the assertions of the non-Buddhist tenets are incorrect. The Buddhist 
tenets seek only to refute thos e assertions which do not accord with reality and are mere mental 
fabrications.    We looked at how one Buddhist tenet system ca n refute another Buddhist tenet system. Again, 
not all the assertions of a tenet system are re futed. For example, Vaib hasika and Sautrantika 
assert that there are coarse objects formed out of  partless particles. This specific assertion is 
refuted by the next higher school,  Cittamatra , by the application of logic and reasoning. 
Cittamatra is not refuting everything that Sautrantik a asserts. In the same way, some assertions by 
Cittamatra, such as their positing that other-po wered phenomena are truly existent, are  refuted 
by the highest school, Prasangika which asse rts that phenomena do not exist truly.  
 The higher Buddhist tenets do no t look down on the lower Buddh ist tenets and try to refute 
every assertion they make. Rather, understanding th e assertions of the lowe r schools helps us to 
understand the assertions of the higher schools.   For example, with regards to the view of 
selflessness, Vaibhasika and Sautrantika refute the non-Buddhist assertion that there is such a 
thing as a permanent, unitary and independent se lf. By using logic and reasoning, they assert 
coarse selflessness as the emptiness of a perman ent, unitary and independent self and subtle 
selflessness as the emptiness of a self-sufficient, substantially existent person. These assertions are 
accepted by the higher tenets. But as long as there is a valid cognizer who realises the faults of an 
assertion, then the higher tenets woul d refute that particular assertion. 
 Regardless of whether one is a Buddhist or non-Buddhist or a proponent of a certain philosophical view or not, it is accepted there is  an “I” “me’’ or “self” that desires happiness and 
does not desire suffering. We do not need to empl oy logic or reasoning to prove the existence of 
such a “self.” Even an insect has a mind that wa nts to be happy and does not want to suffer.  
 Why are there so many varying vie ws of the nature of this “I” th at desires happiness? Basically, 
because there are two groups of people - those who are interested to analyse what is this “I” who  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
316  
wants happiness and not suffering. Then, there is another group who is completely disinterested 
in such analysis and investigation.  
 
When we look at the former grou p, this can be further sub-divi ded into Buddhist tenet holders 
(which again can be divided into different  levels) and non-Buddhist tenet holders.  
 
Let us look at the non-Buddhists who analyse this  “I” who wants to be happy and does not want 
to suffer. To them, applying their logic and reason ing, they conclude that  this “I”/”self”  who 
wants to be happy is permanent, unitary (i.e., do es not depend on parts) and independent (i.e., 
does not arise through the force of causes and conditions). They are unable to come up with 
something more profound than that.    When we look at our own beliefs, our own minds, we are in no position to debunk this assertion 
of the non-Buddhists. How do we look at ourselve s? We think that the “I” of this evening is 
exactly the same person as the “I” of this mo rning. We cannot see the difference between the 
two.   For the non-Buddhists and those of us  who believe that there is a next life, it is the “self” or “I” 
that moves on to the next life. Since this is the ca se, there must be some continuity, so it must be 
the same “I” that moves from one life to anothe r without any significant changes. Therefore, the 
“I” cannot be impermanent. It must be the same “I ” that moves from life to life and that “I” is 
therefore permanent. That is why proponents of non-Buddhist tenets assert that the person/self 
is permanent.  Proponents of Buddhist tenets find this asse rtion unacceptable. When we apply logic and 
reasoning, we can prove that the self/person is necessarily impermanent. The “I” we were this 
morning is different from the “I” we are no w. The “I” therefore is not permanent but 
impermanent. How else can we refute the existenc e of a permanent self/person? This is what we 
have to learn and analyse. This is how yogis ha rm the common people by refuting their beliefs  
through logic and reasoning.  
ii) Abandoning the objection that the need for emptiness has no power 
1’ The actual  
(9, 4 cd) 
By way of analogies accepted by both. 
Because unanalyzed, [one engages] for the sake of the result. 
 
(9, 5) 
Since the worldly see things and 
Conceive them to be perfectly real, 
Not illusion-like; it is here 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
317  
That yogis and worldly beings debate. 
 
What happens when Prasangika refutes the asse rtions of the schools below it (Vaibhasika, 
Sautrantika, Cittamatra and Svatantrika-Madhyam ika) through their logic and reasoning? The 
proponents of the tenets below Prasangika re spond with their counter-arguments against the 
logic used by Prasangika.  
 
Prasangika: Asserts that all phenomena do not exist inherently.  
 
The lower tenets: They have  not proven their case 
 
Prasangika: We have “ by way of analogies accepted by both.”  
 
“Both ” here refers to the proponents of Prasangi ka and the proponents of the lower tenets, 
who assert true existence. Both groups accept that analogies such as dreams and reflections 
are falsities (unreal). 
  
A vase does not exist inherently. It  is a falsity, like a reflection 
in a mirror.  
 The lower tenets:   If phenomena do not exist inhere ntly, there is no purpose to 
practising the perfections because there is no inherently existent Buddha, there is no inhe rently existent enlightenment. 
If something does not inherently exist, it does not exist at all 
and if it does exist, it must exist inherently.  
Prasangika:  Because unanalyzed, [one engages] for the sake of the result . 
When phenomena are left unanalys ed, they still exist  at the 
level of being merely imputed (or designated) by name. We should train in the causes of enlightenment by practising generosity and the other perfec tions in order to achieve the 
goal of enlightenment through understanding that all phenomena are merely labelled by the mind and are mental conceptions. All phenomena are like illusions.  
The lower tenets:  If something arises in dependence on causes and conditions, 
that phenomenon exists inherent ly. For example, fire has the 
ability to and its function is to burn naturally.  From the side of the fire, it has the potential to burn. This is 
not imputed or labelled by the mind. We can directly perceive 
this – that fire burns and has the potential to do so from its 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
318  
own side.     
 
Prasangika:  Since the worldly see things and/ Conceive them to be perfectly 
real,/Not illusion-like; it is here /That yogis and worldly beings debate . 
 
We both can directly perceive (o r cognise) in a valid way that 
functioning things (such as fire) have a potential to perform 
their function (i.e., to burn). This is the common position we 
share. However, you grasp at the fire’s potential to burn as real 
and existing inherently.   This is, in fact, mental fabrication. You are unable to comprehend that the fire’s potential to burn is illusory and does not exist inherently. We real ise that the fire’s potential to 
burn is merely labelled (or designated). From its own side, the fire is “empty” of its potential to burn and because of that, it 
exists like an illusion. We realise that but you do not.

The prayer, Dependent Arising – A Praise of the Buddha , points out that, for some people, the 
teachings on dependent arising become causes fo r them to tighten the ch ains of extreme views 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
319  
and to get stuck in cyclic existence.  For  th e wise, however, the same teachings become the 
causes for them to cut the root of cy clic existence and achieve liberation. 
 
Relating that verse to our root text, we saw ho w people can be divided into  yogis and common 
worldly people. For the latter, dependent arisin g becomes the cause for them to be bound to 
cyclic existence. But the same teachings on dependent arising be come the cause for the yogis to 
free themselves from cyclic existence. Only  the Buddha taught thes e pure teachings on 
dependent arising  and that is why the Buddha should be our only refuge.  
 
5 This teaching is not seen in the works of others,  
the title of Teacher, therefore, is yours alone. Given to others it is but the hollow flattery of a fox being hailed as a lion.   
 
6 Greatest of teacher! Greatest protector! Speaker supreme! Guide supreme! I bow to the teacher of dependent arising.  
 The presentation of dependent arising establishes the lack of inherent ex istence. These teachings 
cannot be found in non-Buddhist traditions. Lama Tsongkhapa has such strong stable faith in 
the Buddha because of his realisation that the Bu ddha is the only one who has taught correctly 
the nature of reality with his faultless presenta tion of dependent arising. That is why Lama 
Tsongkhapa calls the Buddha the perfect teache r and why he accepts the Buddha as his sole 
refuge.    
Just as there are people who may call a fox, a lion, likewise, there are those  who consider 
founders of non-Buddhist traditions to be valid objects of refuge, even though those founders do 
not possess the qualities of a spiritual guide or object of refuge.    The Buddha is the only valid teacher who can sh ow the path to those who desire to achieve 
liberation. Seeing this, Lama Tson gkhapa expresses his great joy: Greatest of teachers! Greatest 
protector! Speaker supreme! Guide supreme!  
 In this context, the Bu ddha is the greatest of teachers  because he is someone who shows the path 
to liberation.   The Buddha is our protector  because he has the power to prot ect us from the sufferings of the 
lower realms and cyclic existence.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
320  
The Buddha is the speaker supreme  because he has taught the meaning of dependent arising in 
an unmistaken way.    
 
The Buddha is the supreme guide  because he guides us not only in this life but also in the 
intermediate states and all our future lives, i.e., he guides us from life to life.  
 The way to establish that the Buddha is a faultless teacher is to see how his teachings are free of 
mistakes and faults. The more we critically anal yse the teachings, using logic and reasoning, the 
greater the ascertainment we will achieve of thes e teachings. Then, naturally, our faith in the 
teachings will  increase. In particular, we will  develop a very strong faith in the Buddha who 
taught them. Understanding this, you will see how important it is to study the teachings of the 
Buddha.   
 To summarise what we have covered so far in chap ter 9, Verse I points out that all the preceding 
chapters are preliminaries for us to develop the ve ry powerful  wisdom which realises emptiness. 
We have also seen how extremely important it is to develop this wisdom in our minds. In order 
to arrive at the definitive understanding of the me aning of emptiness, we have to understand first 
what is reality (or the objects of knowledge).   The objects of knowledge (or existent phenomena) are divided into conventional truth  or 
ultimate truth . These, in turn, are established by two groups of people: the common worldly 
people  and the yogis . We have seen how the yogis contradict  the assertions of the worldly people. 
The worldly people assert true existence, while th e yogis assert that things do not exist truly or 
inherently.    
In this text, the assertions of the first tw o Buddhist schools of tenets, Vaibhasika and 
Sautrantika, are first refuted. This is followed by  refutation of the assert ions of Cittamatra.  The 
proponents of Vaibhasika, Sautrantika and Cittama tra assert true existence, i.e.,  phenomena 
exist truly. To them, if phenomena did not exist trul y, they do not exist at all. They are unable to 
posit the existence of phenomena like persons wi thout positing true exis tence. However, there 
are complications because of their position. For ex ample, since they assert  true existence, with 
regards to the person or self, there must be some thing that can be posited ultimately to be the 
self (or person). To illustrate a person, some schools say that the person is the five aggregates  or 
continuity of the five aggregates.  Others say that the person is the mental consciousness . 
Ultimately, they assert that  the person is the mind. 
 
They meet with difficulties in positing what is the person. For exampl e, one sub-school of 
Vaibhasika, the Vasiputriyas have such difficulty in asserting whether a person is permanent or 
impermanent that they assert a person is ne ither permanent nor impermanent but is simply 
inexpressible. Only the followers of this sub-school assert that the self is in expressible, that it is 
neither permanent nor impermanent. They are the only exception. All the remaining proponents 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
321  
of Buddhist tenets assert that the person is ne cessarily impermanent. Only proponents of non-
Buddhist tenets will assert that a person is permanent. 
 
Even for proponents of Buddhist tenets who assert  that the illustration of the person is the 
mental consciousness , there is uneasiness with this positi on. There are proponents of Cittamatra 
who assert a collection of eight consciousnesses , as opposed to the usual six, and the “person” is 
posited to be   the mind-basis-of-all .  
 
In short, the proponents of  Vaibhasika , Sautrantika and Cittamatra assert that phenomena exist 
truly  whereas the proponents of Prasangika do not assert true existence . Because of these 
differences, this becomes the basis for further debate.   
2’ Abandoning the supposed harm to that 
a’ A general refutation of the proponents of true existence such as the Sautrantikas 
1” Refuting the harm by means of direct perception 
 
 (9,6) 
Forms and so forth, although directly perceived, 
Are [established] by renown , not by a valid cognizer. 
They are false, just like the unclean and so forth 
That are renowned to be  clean and so forth. 
 The proponents of Sautrantika asserts:  Objects of desire [such as form] are inherently existent.  For example, a visual form appears as inherently existent to the eye consciousness, which is the direct perceiver. 
Therefore the visual form is established by the eye consciousness.  The position of Prasangika is incorrect because the object appears to the eye consciousness as inherently existent and, 
at the same time, the inherently existent form is established by the eye consciousness. It is  wrong to assert that that 
object does not exist inherently.  
 
The rebuttal from 
Prasangika:  Forms and so forth, although direct ly perceived, /Are [established] 
by renown, not by a valid cognizer.  
 
Generally speaking, form, sound, smell, taste and touch are 
established by a valid cognizer. The objects of such establishment are not inherently  existent but are established 
by the valid cognizer as mere worldly conventions, i.e., as 
they are known in the world.   
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
322  
The valid cognizer that realises  form does not establish the 
final mode of abiding  (or the ultimate nature) of that 
object.  To suggest that any va lid cognizer can establish the 
ultimate nature  of the object would imply that ordinary 
beings can also directly realise emptiness. 
 In short, the valid cognizer that realises form are valid with 
respect to form but they are not valid with respect to the 
ultimate nature of form which is emptiness. This is because 
that same valid cognizer is unable to realise the ultimate 
nature of form.  (
Note: In this debate, form can be su bstituted with sound, smell, taste 
or touch ). 
 
The proponents of 
Sautrantika then argue:   If form does not exist inherently, how can it be established by renown? And if it is established by renown, how can it be said that it is false? 
 
Prasangika responds:  They are false, just like the unclean and so forth/ That are renowned to be clean and so forth.  
 
Although the body is unclean by nature, for those who are 
attached to it, the body is seen as (or renowned to be) clean 
and pleasant. In the same way, although the self is impermanent, the ordinary person grasps at it as permanent.  Although, in reality, contaminat ed feelings of pleasure are 
in the nature of suffering, the ordinary person apprehends those feelings to be pleasurable.  Although the self does not exist from its own side , the self is 
renowned to be existing from its own side.  In the same way, although phenomena appear to us to be inherently existent, in reality, it is not so. This is the fundamental disparity between appearance and reality.  
2” Refuting the harm by means of scripture 
a” Showing the scriptures that teach compounded phenomena and impermanence etc. to be 
[scriptures of] interpretive meaning 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
323  
 
(9, 7) 
For the sake of making worldly beings engage, 
The Protector taught things. 
 
The proponents of Sautrantika further argue that it is wrong for Prasangika to assert  that 
phenomena are not inherently exis tent since the Buddha taught in  some sutras that forms are 
inherently existent.  
 
For the sake of making worldly beings engage, The Protector taught things : For the sake of those who 
cling to phenomena as being truly existent, the Buddha taught that phenomena are truly existent 
with the intention of leading them slowly to the Mahayana path and the corre ct view of reality as 
expounded by Prasangika . 
 
When the Buddha was alive, he turned the wh eel of Dharma three times. During the 1st turning 
of the wheel of Dharma, he taught the main di sciples who were predisposed to the tenets of 
Vaibhasika and Sautrantika  that all existent ph enomena, from forms right up to the omniscient 
mind, exist by way of their  own character. Buddh a taught this in accord with the interests and 
dispositions of those disciples at the time. He did not teach that, in reality, things do not exist by 
way of their own character. The sutras which assert that phenom ena are established by way of 
their own character require interpretation and are known as interpretative  sutras. 
 We can relate this with the outline: 
a” Showing the scriptures that teach compounded phenomena 
and impermanence etc. to be [scriptures of] interpretive meaning.  
 
b” Refuting that they are [scriptures of] definitive meaning 
 
(9,7cd) 
In thusness 
They are not momentary. 
 Topics, such as impermanence and so forth, are no t ultimately established. Therefore, scriptures 
teaching these topics are not scriptures of definitive meaning. In the 1
st turning of the wheel of 
Dharma, although the Bu ddha taught that phenomena are trul y existent, in reality, phenomena 
do not exist truly. Therefore, those scriptures that teach that phenomena are truly existent are 
not scriptures of definitive meaning.  Prasangika assert that for a phenomenon to be truly existent, that phenomenon must be either 
inherently one with or inherently different from it. 
For example, a vase, according to Prasangika, does not exist truly. If the vase is truly existent, 
then the vase is either inherently one with or inherently different from it. 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
324  
All existent phenomena can be divided into two: It  i s  e i t h e r  o n e  o r  d i f f e r e n t   ( o r  s i n g u l a r  o r  
plural). Because of its dependence on parts, form is not inherently one (or singular).  Since form 
is established not to be inherently one, it cannot be established as inherently different (or plural). 
This is because we can only posit “different” in relation to “one.”  If there is no inherently one, 
we cannot posit inherently different.

Dependent Arising – A Praise of the Buddha  
7 
Benevolent teacher, you taught to help all living beings. 
Emptiness is the essence of those teachings, 
its highest proof dependent arising 
 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
325  
8 
Those claiming it proves the opposite, those denying its very existence, 
how will they grasp your teachings? 
 
The compassionate one, the Buddha, who works solely to benefit us, bestows upon us the 
medicinal Dharma, like the best of doctors ca ring for his patients. Amongst the Buddha’s 
teachings, the supreme teaching is his presentation  on the meaning of emptiness, the final nature 
of all phenomena. This is the essence of all of  Buddha’s teachings.  Dependent arising is the 
highest proof of and establishes the meaning of emptiness.    
Those claiming it proves the opposite  refers to the proponents of tr ue existence. For them, dependent 
arising cannot be used to establish emptiness as  they assert that if something is dependent 
arising, that phenomenon must exist truly. It is very difficult for them to understand the 
meaning of emptiness. 
 According to the teachings and the position of Prasangika, the best reasoning to establish the 
absence of inherent existence is dependent aris ing. Because phenomena are dependently arisen, 
they cannot exist inherently.   Dependent arising is one of the many different fo rms of reasoning used to establish  emptiness. 
We had looked at the four logical steps to esta blish whether a phenomenon is inherently one or 
inherently different. Form is not truly existent. Form does not exist by way of its own character 
because it is a dependent arising. How do we esta blish that form is not truly existent? This is 
because form is dependently arisen or we can say that it is neither inherently one  or inherently 
different .  
 
For example, we would apprehend a vase as a singular phenomenon and not different. On the 
other hand if we were to compare a vase and a pi llar, we  understand those two are different and 
not the same. 
 One and different or one and many are synonymo us. Anything that exists is either one or 
different. If something is inherently existent, it must be inherently one or inherently different. 
 If the subject matter is, say, form, it would be expressed as:  Form is not inherently existent 
because it is neither inherently one or inherently different.  Why is form not inherently one? This is becaus e form possesses parts. We need to understand 
why something that possesses parts cannot be in herently one. Why is form not inherently 
different? Form is not inherently different  because form is not inherently one. 
 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
326  
c" Abandoning contradictions to that 
(1) Abandoning [the notion] that is even relatively established 
 
(9,7cd) 
“Is it also contradictory conventionally?” 
 
(9,8) 
For yogis that it is conventional is without fault. 
Compared to worldly beings, they see thusness; 
Otherwise, their definite real ization of women as unclean 
Would be harmed by worldly beings. 
 
 
Qualm from Sautrantika: If it is asserted that composed phenomena are not 
impermanent ultimately, they cannot be established to be impermanent conventionally. Why? This is because 
something which existed in an earlier moment also exists at a 
later time. It is not conventionally known that composed phenomena are impermanent. In fact people believe that composed phenomena are permanent. 
 
Reply by Prasangika: We ordinary people think that things are permanent but, in 
reality, composed phenomena are not permanent. It is 
mentioned in the root text that ordinary beings believe and 
see impermanent things as permanent. However, there are 
minds, such as the conventional  valid perception of the yogis 
that can establish that composed phenomena are impermanent. There is therefore, no contradiction. 
 
Sautrantika then asserts: If composed ph enomenon is established as impermanent 
conventionally, this contradicts what is said in one of the 
sutras: Those who see composed phenomena as impermanent, they 
see emptiness. 
 
Prasangika responds: Verse 8 points out, Compared to worldly beings, they see thusness  
which means that those who see composed phenomena as 
impermanent see thusness (or emptiness).This does not 
mean that, in general, whoeve r sees composed phenomena as 
impermanent also sees the final nature of composed 
phenomena. In general, there are many misconceptions such 
as conceiving the unclean to be clean, suffering to be happiness and the impermanent to be permanent.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
327  
 
Compared to those adhering to such misconceptions, those who do realise that composed phenomena are impermanent 
are thus much better off in that they see the nature of 
phenomena. It is in this  sense that it is said, they see thusness.  
 
It is widely believed by ordinary people that  things, such as  
forms, are permanent in nature. What is the fallacy in holding such a belief? This sugge sts that the valid perception 
in the minds of ordinary bein gs would necessarily harm the 
valid perception of the yogis.  People who have achieved a certain level of concentration or realisation see phenomena as unclean and unpleasant by 
nature. This understanding harms the mind of a desire being 
who grasps at the body as being very clean.   One is an erroneous mind and the other is a valid mind. There is a difference between the two. If it is a valid perception, it cannot be harmed by its opponent.  The valid perception that realises unclean phenomena to be unclean acts as an antidote against attachment. When a mind of desire sees unclean phenomena as clean, such a mind cannot be a valid mind  that can harm the valid 
perception of the yogis.  
(2) Abandoning [the notion] that gathering the accumulations is invalid 
 
(9, 9) 
Merit comes from an illusory-like conqueror, 
Just as it does from an actually existent one. 
 Qualm from Vaibhasika and Sautrantika: Asserting things do not exist inherently suggests that the 
Buddha also does not exist inherently. If that is so, making offerings or prostrating to the Buddha will not lead to the generation of any merits.  
 
Response from Prasangika: Merit comes from an illusory-like conq ueror, just as it does from an 
actually existent one. There is no contradiction. Making 
offerings and prostrations to an illusion -like Buddha yields 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
328  
illusion-like merits.  
 
Just as it does from an actually existent one : The lower tenet 
systems assert that truly existent merits are acquired by 
making offerings and prostrations to the truly existent Buddha. In the same way, illusi on-like merits are gained by 
making offerings and prostrations to the illusion-like 
Buddha. 
 
(3) Abandoning [the notion] that conception is invalid 
 
Conception here means rebirth. 
(9, 9 cd) 
“If sentient beings are like an illusion, 
Having died, how are they reborn?” 
 
(9, 10) 
For as long as those conditions are assembled, 
For that long will illusions also last. 
Why should sentient beings be truly existent 
Due merely to their continua lasting long? 
 
Qualm from Vaibhasika 
and Sautrantika:    
If sentient beings are not inhe rently existent but exist like 
illusions, how can they take rebirth? The illusory elephant, 
created by the magician, does not take rebirth. In the same way, sentient being who do not exist inherently are unable to take rebirth too.  
 
Response from Prasangika: For as long as those conditions are assembled, for that long will 
illusions also last . 
 Sentient beings take rebirth due to their karma and afflictions. As long as these causes exist and come together, sentient beings will continue to take rebirth just as an illusionist creates his illusions in dependence on mantra 
recitations and special substances .  As long as these causes 
and conditions continue to exist, the illusion continues to 
appear.   When the conditions for taking future rebirth cease, rebirth will no longer happen. In the same way, when the causes and 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
329  
conditions for the creation of the illusion disappear, so will 
the illusion itself.  
 
Objections by Vaibhasika and Sautrantika to this argument:   How can sentient beings be compared with illusions? Sentient beings have existed since beginningless time and since they have been around fo r a very long time, they must 
exist truly whereas an illusion only exists for a short time.  
 
Response from the Prasangika:   Why should sentient beings be truly existent due merely to their continua lasting long?  Time is not the basis for esta blishing whether a phenomenon 
is truly existent or not. If we can use time as a determinant of 
this, it would imply that some longer-lasting dreams and illusions are truly existent whil e shorter ones are not. This 
means that there are some dreams and illusions that are truly existent. 
 
Q: If the Buddha did not teach on emptiness duri ng the 1st  turning of the wheel of Dharma, 
how did some of his disciples become arhats immediately after the teachings? 
A: The first five disciples of the Buddha are not pr oponents of Vaibhasika or Sautrantika. From 
the perspective of Prasangika, the Buddha did teach emptiness during the 1
st turning of the 
wheel of Dharma.  Because of those teachings, th ose five fortunate disciples realised emptiness 
and became arhats. 
 
Q: From the perspective of Prasangika, did the Buddha teach the Four Noble Truths during the 
1st turning of the wheel of Dharma? 
 
A: Yes. The Buddha taught the Fo ur Noble Truths during the 1st turning of the wheel of 
Dharma. The four characteristics or attributes of  the First Noble Truth (the truth of suffering) 
are: impermanent, suffering, empty and selfless.  
 
Therefore, from the perspective of Prasangika, Buddha did teach on selflessness and emptiness in 
the 1st  turning of the wheel of Dharma. But propon ents of the lower tene t systems, even the 
Svatantrika-Madhyamika School, as sert that the Buddha did not teac h the selfless of phenomena  
during the 1st turning of the wheel of Dharma.  
 
In this context, selflessness refers  to the lack of inherent existenc e of true suffering and of self 
and phenomena. Emptiness refers to the emptine ss of a self-sufficient substantially existent 
person. 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
330  
With reference to the object of negation, what is being negated? We need to posit the coarse 
object of negation and the subtle object of nega tion. There is a difference in the subtlety of the 
object of negation. Therefore, there is also a diffe rence in the subtlety of the level of emptiness or 
selflessness. 
 The 4 attributes of true suffering are in this or der:  Impermanence, suffering, empty, selfless. 
There are 16 aspects to the Four Noble Trut hs with 4 characteristics to each truth.

Dependent Arising – A Praise of the Buddha 
9 
For you, emptiness seen as dependent arising 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
331  
does not render as contradictory  
emptiness of self-nature an d ability to function.  
 
10 
To hold to the opposite, however –  that with emptiness there can be no function and with function, no emptiness –  
is to fall into a dangerous trap.  
 
As seen in the previous lesson, there are peop le who are unable to understand how dependent 
arising is the rationale for proving th at things do not exist inherently.  
 
For you  means the Buddha himself critically analysed  how phenomena  exist and how they finally 
abide. He discovered that the ultimate natu re of all things and events is emptiness. 
 
When the Buddha critically analysed how the pers on (“self” or “I”) exists, he discovered the 
inherently existent person cannot be found on th e basis of designation, the five aggregates. At 
the same time, that same realisation led him to th e ascertainment that the person does exist as a 
dependent arising. He understood immediately that  things and events do not exist inherently but 
exist as dependent arising, i.e., in dependence on other factors.   Based on the understanding that when somethin g is dependent arising, it does not exist 
inherently and because it does not exist inherently , it is a dependent arising, Buddha was able to 
establish cause and effect – that cause leads to effect and the existence of a functional 
agent/object/action.   For those who do not understand these realisat ions of the Buddha, they see emptiness and 
dependent arising as contradictory. When th ey talk about emptiness, they cannot posit 
dependent arising. When they talk about depend ent arising, they cannot establish the emptiness 
of inherent existence. 
 
For them, when things do not exist inherently, then cause and effect does not exist and their 
views fall into the extreme of existence or  th e extreme of non-existence. For the former, if 
something is dependent arising, it cannot be empt y of inherent existence.  For the latter, when 
something does not exist inherently, then that th ing does not exist at a ll. They are unable to 
establish dependent arising on the basis of things not existing inherently.  
 
For you, emptiness seen as dependent arising/ does not render as contradictory/ emptiness of self-nature and 
ability to function  is similar to the line, Form is empty, emptiness is form  in the Perfection of Wisdom 
Sutra . 
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
332  
(4) Abandoning [the notion] that the classifications of virtue and negativity is invalid 
 
(9,11) 
When a being who is an illusion kills and so forth, 
There is no negativity because it is without a mind. 
For one who possesses an illusory mind, 
Merit and negativity arise. 
 
(9,12) 
Mantras and so forth, because they lack the ability, 
Do not give rise to illusory mind. 
The illusions that arise from 
Diverse conditions are also various. 
 
(9,13) 
Nowhere does there exist 
One condition that is able [to give rise] to all. 
 
Qualm from Vaibhasika and Sautrantika :  
If sentient beings are like illusions (as asserted by  Prasangika), then when such a person kills, that 
person does not accumulate negative karma. This is akin to asserting that the action of killing 
will not have negative consequences.  
 
Response from Prasangika:  
This is because the illusory person, the vi ctim, does not possess mind and the karma is 
incomplete so the karma of killing is not incurred.  
 A complete karma has four factors:  the basis the intention the action the completion of the action.  For example, in the case of killing, th e life of the victim must be stopped. 
 
However, when we harm or benefit a human being with an illusion-like mind, we will create 
negative karma or accumulate virtue accordingly.   
The illusory person and the human bein g who has an illusion-like mind are similar  in that they 
do not exist inherently. However, they are not the same because the illusory person does not 
possess a mind.   
 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
333  
Qualm from Vaibhasika and Sautrantika :  
If they are similar because they do not exist inherently then why does the human being who 
possesses an illusion-like mind have a mind and the illusory person does not? 
 
Response from Prasangika :  
The answers appear in verses 12 and 13. 
 An illusory person  is an illusion created by the illusionist by reciting mantras and using special 
substances. These mantras and subs tances do not have the power to produce a person possessing 
a mind. The illusionist, the mantras and th e substances are necessary for producing an illusion  
of a person but they are not powerful enough to  produce a mind. Therefore, the illusory person 
does not possess a mind.  In the case of the human being with an illusory-like mind,  he arose from the combination of 
ignorance and compositional activities (or karma) . These two factors have the power to produce a 
human being with a mind and are referred to as “true origins”.   So, whether there is a mind or not depends on wh ether the causes have the potential to produce 
a mind. Different causes produce different effect s. Many causes are needed to produce a whole 
variety of effects. The conclusion then is that we will not be able to get a variety of effects from a single cause.   For example, the production of an illusory pers on requires an illusionist and the mantras and 
substances he uses. The illusion can only come about with the coming to gether of these three 
conditions.  To produce a different illusion, say,  an illusory horse, the illusionist will use 
different mantras and substances, i.e., different causes need to be used. 
 Applying this understanding to consciousness, the substantial (or main) cause of consciousness 
has to be consciousness. It cannot be something el se, like form, which do not have the ability to 
produce consciousness.  Based on this understand ing, we can establish past lives. When we can 
establish past lives, we can establish future live s. This is how we can establish reincarnation. 
 
(5) Abandoning [the notion] that there are separate and definite 
 
(9, 13 cd) 
“If ultimately they are nirvana and 
Conventionally, cyclic existence, 
 
(9,14) 
Since even a buddha would be a cyclic existence, 
What use would the bodhisattva conduct be?” 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
334  
If the conditions are not interrupted, 
Also illusions are not turned back. 
 
(9,15) 
Since the conditions are interrupted, 
[A buddha] does not aris e even conventionally. 
 
For Prasangika, there is no ultimate production  and ultimate cessation because neither exists 
inherently. The fact that events and phen omena do not exist inherently is called natural nirvana.  
If we take natural stain  to mean true existence, then the stat e, which is free of this natural stain, 
is called natural nirvana ( or the state beyond sorrow). All phenomena are, by nature, in the state 
beyond sorrow. 
 
What is samsara to Prasangika? Cyclic existence  refers to having to take rebirth in samsara under 
the power of karma and afflictions. 
 
Qualm from  Vaibhasika  and  Sautrantika:  
Asserting all phenomena are by nature in the st ate beyond sorrow means that all phenomena are 
nirvana. At the same time, asserting samsara is taking rebirth under the power of karma and 
afflictions means that both samsara and nirvana are equal and not different. It means we are in 
samsara and nirvana at the same time.   If that is the case, not only sentient beings but al so the buddhas are circling in samsara. If this is 
so, then the hard work the bodhis attvas put into their practice to stop birth and death in cyclic 
existence becomes meaningless.  
Response from Prasangika : 
This argument is based on an inability to differentiate between naturally abiding nirvana  and 
the nirvana which is the thorough purification of  all the adventitious defilements (or faults).   
 
Naturally abiding nirvana has existed since beginningless time and its existence does not depend 
on factors like generating a path or cult ivating antidotes. However, whatever is a naturally 
abiding nirvana  is not necessarily nirvana .  
 The nirvana  attained after the thorough purification of all the adventitious  defilements  comes 
from abandoning the objects of abandonment of the path of seeing and the objects of 
abandonment of the path of meditation. By doing that, cyclic existence is stopped and nirvana is 
achieved. This nirvana  comes about through the cultivation of antidotes and is achieved by 
meditating on the path, which is the antidote to  ignorance and karma. On  the path, when one is 
applying the antidotes to ignorance and karma, one will not achieve nirvana until one has put a 
stop to the continuance of ignorance and karma (e ven though things are, by nature, in the state 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
335  
beyond sorrow). Otherwise, one continues to take  rebirth again in the future under the power of 
ignorance and karma. 
 
Just as the illusion is produced as long as al l the conditions are present (the illusionist, the 
mantras and substances), in the same way, until we have put a stop to the causes of samsara 
(ignorance and karma), cyclic existence will cont inue to exist. When we  meditate on the path, 
which is the antidote to ignorance and karma, and we stop these causes, we will achieve the 
nirvana which is the thorough purification of all adventitious defilements. When that nirvana is 
achieved, cyclic existence at both the ultimate  and conventional levels ceases to exist.  
b’ A refutation of the Cittamatra position in particular 
Sautrantika asserts: 
That coarse objects are formed from the aggreg ation of partless particle s, which then act as 
observed object conditions realised by the sens e consciousness. The sense consciousness realising 
an object is produced in dependence on the prior existence of the external object, i.e., the object 
exists first followed by the co nsciousness that realises it.  
 
Cittamatra asserts : 
That the appearance of an object and the consci ousness to which it is appearing are established 
simultaneously.   There is no object which is of a differe nt substance from the mind, i.e., there are 
no external objects but everything  is in the entity of mind an d objects arise when the mental 
predisposition is awakened. 
 
Sautrantika asserts:  
External objects exist truly. 
 
Cittamatra asserts : 
There are no external objects and,  therefore, they do not exist tr uly. There are no truly existent 
external objects because they cannot be found when we look for them. This shows that, in reality, 
there is no such thing as external objects. 
 On the other hand, other-powered phenomenon, su ch as the mind (consciousness) exists and 
exists truly because it can be experienced.  As the mind is truly existent, there is another mind 
that realises it known as the self-knower. (T his is the peculiar assertion of Cittamatra). 
 Consciousness can be classified in different ways , one of which is: other knower and self-knower. 
The consciousness that realises form is an other knower  because it realises something other than 
itself. The consciousness that experiences itself is a self-knower . 
 Understanding the assertion of Cittamatra – that everyt hing is in the entity of  mind - is useful for 
understanding the assertions of  the higher school, Prasangika  that things exist by mere 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
336  
designation. 
 A Cittamatrin can be defined as the proponent of  Mahayana tenets who does not assert external 
objects and asserts self-knower as truly existent. 
 
1” Stating their position 
(9,15 cd) 
[Cittamatra ]: 
If even a mistaken [consciousness] does not exist, 
What is it that will observe the illusory? 
 Qualm by Cittamatra: 
Prasangika  asserts that both internal and external object s do not exist truly but are like illusions. 
If that is the case, then the consciousness that re alises those illusion-like phenomena must also be 
illusion-like. Since such a consciousness does not ex ist, illusion-like objects also do not exist, i.e., 
neither object nor subject exist.  
 To Cittamatra, mind (consciousness) truly exists. If  the mind does not exist truly, then the mind 
does not exist at all. So to Cittamatra, an illusi on-like mind does not exist. When that mind does 
not exist, then there cannot be  objects which are realised by  that mind. To Cittamatra, 
Prasangika is falling into the trap of asse rting that neither object nor subject exists. 
 
Answer to question from a  student: The definition of an existent is that which is established by a 
valid cognizer. Anything that is not establis hed by a valid cognizer  does not exist. 
 
Buddha turned the 1
st wheel of Dharma to the five disciples.  They were Hinayana arhats but that 
does not mean they were necessarily proponents of Hinayana tenets.  
 
Proponents of Hinayana tenets can never realis e emptiness because they cannot realise that 
things do not exist inherently. Until one is able to  get rid of the conception grasping at inherent 
existence, there is no way one can realise empt iness. Even to achieve liberation, one must 
definitely realise that phenomena do not exist inherently. 
 
According to the view of Cittamatra, Serlingpa wa s a bodhisattva who realised bodhicitta but he 
did not realise emptiness as po sited by the higher school. 
 According to the view of the higher school, As anga realised emptiness. Based on historical 
accounts, he was a bodhisattva abiding on the 3
rd ground. In reality, Asanga was not a proponent 
of  Cittamatra although he took on the  appear ance of being the trailblazer of Cittamatra.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
337  
Lesson 60   Chapter IX - verse 15-19 
D The way to train in wisdom- the nature of insight 
a. Explaining the chapter’s material 
1) Teaching that one who wants to attain liberation must develop the wisdom realizing suchness 
2) The methods by which one develops the wisdom realizing suchness 
a) A presentation of the two truths 
i) The actual ii) Abandoning the objection that the need for emptiness has no power 
1’ The actual 
2’ Abandoning the supposed harm to that 
a’ A general refutation of the proponents of true existence such as the 
Sautrantikas 
b’ A refutation of the Cittamatra position in particular 
1” Stating their position 
2” Refuting that 
a” The similarity [between their objection and ours] 
b” Refuting the response to that 
(1) Stating the position 
(2) Refuting that 
(a) It is not seen by any non-dualistic awareness 
(b) Refuting the self-cognizer posed in the response 
to the query 
(i) Scriptural refutation 
(ii) Refutation by reasoning 
(iii) Refuting the syllogism [that asserts] the existence of a self-cognizer 
(iv) Refuting the position that holds imputed 
existents to be possessors of truly established 
supports for functioning things 
c’ Abandoning the consequence that even the Madhyamaka’s need for the path realizing emptiness has no power 
b) Establishing that even someone who wants to attain mere liberation must realize 
emptiness 
c) An extensive explanation of the reasoning establishing emptiness 
3) Instructions on making effort to de velop that [realization of emptiness] 
 
According to Cittamatra, all phenomena are in th e entity of mind. Cittamatra does not assert 
external objects unlike Sautrantika, which asserts that on the basis of the presence of an object, 
that object is then perceived by a consciousness.   
 Cittamatra does not assert external objects becaus e to them, upon closer analysis, an external 
form cannot be found. This is not to say that fo rm does not exist. To Cittamatra, form does not 
exist externally but form is established in the entity  of mind, i.e., it exists  simultaneously with the 
consciousness apprehending it. They  posit that  consciousness is truly existent because its 
existence can be established through experience.  
 
An example is the way the eye consciousness a pprehending blue  is  generated. The sense 
consciousness is produced upon the aggregation of the three conditions:   the observed object condition 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
338  
the uncommon empowering condition 
the immediately preceding condition  
For Sautrantika, the eye consciousness apprehendi ng blue  comes about due to the object of 
observation of that consciousness, the external form, the colour blue. 
 
For Cittamatra, however, the eye consciousness apprehending blue arises not because of the 
external form, the colour blue but due to the po tential existing upon the immediately preceding 
condition of the eye consciousness.    Consciousness is a continuum. There is a prec eding moment of consciousness prior to the 
generation of  the eye consciousness apprehen ding blue that carries the potential for the 
production of the eye consciousness apprehending bl ue. It is the ripening of that potential which 
is asserted by Cittamatra to be the object of observation for the eye consciousness apprehending 
blue.   
1” Stating their position 
(9, 15 cd) 
[Cittamatra ]: 
If even a mistaken [consciousness] does not exist, 
What is it that will observe the illusory? 
 
Qualm from Cittamatra :  
Madhyamikas asserts that both in ternal and external objects do  not exist truly but are like 
illusions. Consciousness is also said to be empty of true existence. This implies that 
consciousness does not exist. If  consciousness does  not exist, then illusion-like objects cannot be 
posited. This means that neither object nor subject exists. 
 
2” Refuting that 
a” The similarity [between their objection and ours] 
(9,16) 
[Madhyamika ]: 
If the illusory itself does not exist for you, 
Then what is it that is observed? 
 
Response from Prasangika: 
Cittamatra does not assert external objects but they  do assert that consciousness is truly existent. 
What happens then when form a ppears to the consciousness apprehending it?  Does form exist 
as an external object like the way it is appearing  to the consciousness that is apprehending it?  
 
If Cittamatra asserts that form exists exactly in the way it appears  to the consciousness 
apprehending it, then that form has to be posited as an external object which means  external 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
339  
objects do exist. 
 If Cittamatra were to argue that although form  appears to the consciousness as an external 
object, it does not exist in the way it appears, that is akin to saying that form is not truly existent. 
If form does not exist truly, then, to Cittamatra, form does not exist at all. 
 
In both cases, the assertions of Cittamatra are refuted.  
 
b” Refuting the response to that 
(1) Stating the position  
(9, 16 cd) 
[Cittamatra ]:  
Since the other exists with respect to them, 
Those aspects are just mind .  
Response from Cittamatra : 
Form does not exist in the way it appears to the consciousness apprehending it. There are no 
external forms but this does not mean that form does not exist. It exists in another way. The 
appearance of form is established in the entity  of consciousness. The form appearing to the 
consciousness apprehending it is an appearance fa ctor of the consciousne ss and it exists due to 
the ripening of certain potential in the mental continuum. 
 
(2) Refuting that 
(a) It is not seen by any non-dualistic awareness 
 
(9,17) 
[Madhyamika ]: 
If that very mind is the illusory, 
What is seen by what? 
Response from Prasangika : 
To Cittamatra, form is asserted to be establishe d in the entity of mind. It does not exist as a 
different substance from the mind. If that is th e case, the appearance factor of form to the 
consciousness apprehending it would also be in the entity of mind.  What other mind is there 
then to observe that form? 
 
(b) Refuting the self-cognizer posed in the response to the query 
(i) Scriptural refutation 
(9,17 cd) 
The Protector of the World also 
Taught “Mind does not see mind.” 
 
(9,18) 
Just as the blade of a sword 
Does not cut itself, so too is the mind. 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
340  
 
Response from Prasangika (contd.): 
If Cittamatra asserts that form is established in  the entity of mind, this means both the form 
itself and the consciousness apprehending it ar e minds. How then do we differentiate between 
the apprehending consciousness and the apprehen ded object?  It is difficult to distinguish 
between the subject observing the object and the object itself.   
 
Response from Cittamatra : 
We resolve this by asserting a consciousness kn own as the self-knower. (Consciousness can be 
classified as other knower and self-knower. The other knower is a consciousness that apprehends 
an object other than itself while the self-knower is a consciousness that experiences itself.) The 
self-knower has the aspect of the apprehende r and experiences the consciousness itself. 
In the case of the eye consciousness apprehending  blue, there is a self-knower that experiences 
that eye consciousness in a non-du alistic way (which means  there is  a subsidence of that feeling 
of duality between subject and object and there is no longer a distinct separation between subject 
and object).  
 
The position of Prasangika : We do not assert self-knowers and their existence is refuted by 
scriptural authority. The Buddha says, Mind does not see mind . This means consciousness cannot 
experience itself in a non-dualistic way, thro ugh the subsidence of duality. The mind cannot 
experience itself, just as the bl ade of a sword cannot  cut itself. 
 
The fundamental assertion by Cittamatra, with resp ect to the consciousness, is that it is truly 
existent. If the consciousness is truly existent, th is means that it is independent, inherently one 
and does not possess parts. If it does not po ssess parts, then how does Cittamatra account for 
that consciousness which experiences itself. 
 
(ii) Refutation by reasoning 
 
(9,18 cd) 
[Cittamatra ]: 
It is just like a butter lamp 
That perfectly illuminates the thing that is itself. 
 
(9,19) 
[Madhyamika ]: 
A butter lamp is not an object to be illuminated 
Because it is not obscured by darkness. 
 
Rebuttal from Cittamatra :  
It is not true that the self-knowe r does not exist. It exists just like a butter lamp that perfectly 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
341  
illuminates the thing that is itself. In the same  way, the consciousness can experience itself.  
 
Response from Prasangika : 
The example of the butter lamp is  invalid because the butter lamp does not illuminate itself. 
There is neither a need for it to do so, nor does  it possess any ability to illuminate itself.  The 
butter lamp does not illuminate itself be cause it is not obscured by darkness.  
 
What is the fault in asserting that the butter la mp illuminates itself? This would be akin to 
asserting that darkness can be obscured by darkne ss. If darkness is obscured by darkness, then 
darkness can not be seen since it is  obscured.  
 
This line of reasoning appears in the commentary to Nagarjuna’s Fundamental Treatise of the 
Middle Way  called The Drop of Reasoning , where it is said that if we assert that the butter lamp 
illuminates itself, then we have to say that da rkness can obscure itself. If darkness can obscure 
itself, then darkness can never be seen. 
 
When we cover a vase with a piece of cloth, we would not be able to see the vase as the vase is 
covered by the cloth. In the same way, assert ing that  darkness can be obscured by darkness 
means that darkness can never be seen.  Cittamatra then puts forward anothe r example using a piece of glass. 
 
(9,19 cd) 
[Cittamatra ]: 
There is blue, like glass, that depends on 
Other for its blueness and that which does not.

c’ Abandoning the consequence that even the Madhyamaka’s need for the path realizing emptiness has

Dependent Arising – A Praise of the Buddha 
 
11 In your teachings, therefore, 
Knowledge of dependent aris ing is highly praised,  
But it will not be known 
to views of self or non-existence.  
 
The teaching on dependent arising is the essence of all the Buddha’s teachings.  He taught that 
emptiness and dependent arising are not contradict ory but are, in fact, complementary. Because 
of this revelation, the teachings on dependent arising are highly praised. 
 When we fail to realize this and believe that em ptiness and dependent arising  are contradictory, 
then we would have entered the wrong path an d not be able to achieve the state of full 
enlightenment. The Buddha taught many subjects su ch as how to achieve the different levels of 
clairvoyance, concentration and supernatural powe rs but they are not the most important of his 
teachings. This is because being able to accompli sh high levels of clairvoyance or concentration 
does not help us to free ourselves from cyclic exis tence. In order to achieve this, we must realise 
that emptiness and dependent arising are not contradictory.   If the idea of the absence of inhe rent existence makes us think that things do not exist at all, this 
is the extreme of nihilism or non-existence. On the other hand, if we believe that for something 
to exist, it must exist inherently, this is the extr eme of existence. For people holding on to either 
extreme view, it is impossible fo r them to realise dependent ar ising. The phenomena which are 
the objects of these two extreme views do not exis t in reality but the conceptions holding on to 
these two extremes do exist.  
 The lower tenets, with the exception of Prasangika , all assert that all phenomena are established 
by way of their own character (i.e., they truly ex ist). For them, if things exist, they must exist 
inherently. According to Prasangika , this is the extreme of existenc e and as long as we have this 
wrong conception holding on to th e extreme of existence, it is impossible to achieve liberation 
from samsaric suffering.    How should we study this presentation on emptin ess?  The main tool fo r  understanding these 
teachings is by applying our analytical minds,  using our logic and reasoning.  We learn by 
thinking about the subject matter, analysing it  and through debate and questioning. It is 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
343  
impossible to understand this topic by  listening to the teachings alone.  
 
We must use our mental ability. The more we us e our minds, the sharper they  become, the more 
stable the memory and the greater its analytical power. Our minds are not like our bodies. When 
we do not use our minds, they will become duller over time. It is the nature of our minds that 
the more they are used, the sharper they become.  
 
(1’) Refuting by example 
 (In this outline, there are two examples put forth by Cittamatra to support  their assertion of the 
existence of the self-knower – the analogies of the butter-lamp and a piece of glass. Both are 
refuted by Prasangika).  
 
We had discussed the Cittamatra assertion of the existence of a self-knower in the previous 
lesson. Cittamatra tries to prove the existence of the self-knower by using analogies. One analogy 
is the butter lamp illuminating itself: Just as a butter lamp illuminates itself, the mind can also 
experience itself. This is the argument put forth by Cittamatra.  
 Prasangika counters that this is a faulty exampl e because a butter lamp cannot illuminate itself. If 
a butter lamp can do this, then we have to posi t that darkness can be ob scured by darkness. If 
darkness can be obscured by darkne ss, then it follows that darkness can never be seen by anyone.  
Cittamatra then uses another example: 
(9,19 cd)  
[Cittamatra]: 
There is blue, like glass, that depends on 
Other for its blueness and that which does not. 
 
(9, 20) 
Likewise, some [things] are seen in dependence 
On other and some are also  seen without dependence. 
[Madhyamika]: 
It is not that that which was not blueness 
Made itself into blue by itself. 
 
The Cittamatra position:  
The mind is like a piece of glass, clear and tr ansparent. When the glass is placed on a blue 
surface, that surface becomes the condition for th e glass to take on a bluish hue. This does not 
mean that the glass is, by nature, blue in colo ur. The blue hue comes from the surface on which 
the glass is placed. However, if we had a piece of lapis lazuli wh ich, by its nature, is blue in 
colour, that colour does not depend on other factors but is established from its own side.  
 
Phenomena are established in dependence on bein g realised by something other than themselves 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
344  
i.e., form is realised by consciou sness. This is demonstrated by th e analogy of the clear glass being 
placed on a blue surface, taking on a bluish hu e. This symbolises the consciousness, the other 
knower.  
 
But for the consciousness itself, it does not need to depend on any other thing, other than itself, 
to be realised i.e., it realises itself. Here, Citta matra uses the example of the lapis lazuli which is 
blue by nature. Its colour does not depend on any other factors. To Cittamatra, this is analogous 
to the consciousness’ ability to realise or  expe rience itself. This symbolises the self-knower. 
 
The counter-argument of Prasangika:   
It is not that that which was not bluene ss/Made itself into blue by itself.  
 It is incorrect to assert that lapis lazuli is bl ue from its own side. Its colour came about due to 
causes and conditions.  As long as it is a co mposed phenomenon, its existence comes about in 
reliance on causes and conditions. Therefore, la pis lazuli is blue due to certain causes and 
conditions. Its blue colour is not established in herently without depending on other factors.  
 
(2’) Refuting by meaning 
(9, 21) 
(Cittamatra ): 
It can be said “A light illuminates [itself]” 
When it is known by consciousness. 
But through being known by what 
Can it be said “Awareness illuminates [itself]”? 
 
(9,22) 
[Madhyamika]: 
Since it is not seen by any, 
Whether it illuminates or does not illuminate 
Is like the grace of the daughter of a barren woman, 
In that it is meaningless even to discuss it. 
 
Prasangika position:  
 
Even if Cittamatra were to argue in the following  way: “The butter lamp cannot illuminate itself 
but the butter lamp nevertheless illuminates beca use there is a consciou sness, which is of a 
different substance from the butter lamp itself, that  realises this,” how can this argument support 
its assertion that consciousness illuminates?  A consciousness does not experience itself. It is  also not experienced by another consciousness 
other than itself. If consciousness is experienced by another consciousness other than itself, this 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
345  
leads to the fault of limitless regression as ther e has to be another consciousness experiencing the 
experiencer of the consciousness and another consciousness experiencing that consciousness and 
so forth.  
 
For Cittamatra, since the consciousness can neit her experience itself nor be experienced by 
another consciousness, then there is the fallac y of consciousness not existing. When the base, 
consciousness, no longer exists, then   the di scussion on whether consciousness illuminates or 
not is pointless. It is   like  talking about the son and daughter of a barren woman. 
 To reiterate, Cittamatra asserts self-knower, that  the mind is luminous and experiences itself. 
Prasangika asserts other knowers but not self-knowers.   The difficulty with the Cittamatra position is their assertion that the consciousness and its 
luminous (or illuminating) quality are inherently ex istent. When searched for, they can be found. 
With such a position, they are left with two option s - the consciousness either realises itself or it 
is realised by something else.  Prasangika sees problems with both options. Th ey refute the assertion that consciousness can 
illuminate itself (or that consciousness can realise itself). They also refute that consciousness can 
be realised by another consciousness other than itself since this will lead to limitless regression.  
(iii) Refuting the syllogism [that asse rts] the existence of a self-cognizer 
(1’) An example illustrating the production of me mories despite the lack of a self-cognizer 
 
(9,23) 
[Cittamatra]: 
If a self-cognizer did not exist 
How would consciousness be remembered? 
[Madhyamika ]:  
It is remembered through experience and 
In relation to other, like the poison of a rat. 
 Cittamatra asserts self-knowers to explai n how memories of the past occur.   
For example: We saw the colour blue today. Recall ing this the next day, to Cittamatra, the sign 
of memory is the thought: “(1) I remember having seen the colour blue and (2) I remember my 
eye consciousness saw blue.” In other words, there is recall of both the object (the colour blue) 
and the subject (our eye consciousness apprehending blue).    Cittamatra asserts that the fact we can recall seeing blue before is the sign that there was an eye 
consciousness that apprehended blue in the past . Without this eye consciousness apprehending 
blue, it would be  impossible for us to have any memory of the object seen, the colour blue.  
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
346  
  
Applying the same line of reasoning, they a ssert that we remember the eye consciousness 
apprehending blue because there was a consciousn ess experiencing that. This is the self-knower 
and therefore, at a later time, one recalls having seen blue.  
 This cannot be experienced by other consciousnesses.  Cittamatra assert that if we posit that other 
consciousnesses were experiencing the eye consci ousness apprehending blue, then this will lead 
to the fault of limitless regression of multiple consciousnesses experiencing the eye consciousness 
apprehending blue. Therefore, Cittamatra asserts that the eye consciousnes s apprehending blue  
experiences itself and there is such a thing as the  self knower.

Dependent Arising – A Praise of the Buddha  
12 
Nondependence, you have said, is like the sky flower. Nondependence, therefore, does not exist. 
Anything existent by its own nature 
contradicts existence by cause and circumstance. 
 
Nondependence, you have said, is like the sky fl ower,/Nondependence, therefore, does not exist:  This is 
because there is no conceptual mind that is able  to label something as being non-dependent. The 
existence of a phenomenon depends on a conceptu al thought designating it to be “this” or 
“that.” Nothing can exist without such design ation by a conceptual thought. Since no 
phenomena exist without depending on designat ion by a conceptual thought, therefore, Non-
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
347  
dependence is like the sky flower,  which does not exist.  All existent s must be dependent arising. If a 
phenomenon can exist from its own side, this cont radicts its coming into existence by depending 
on causes and conditions or by depending on the conceptual thought designating it to be “this” 
or “that.”  
 
13 
Nothing  is not dependently arising; 
nothing, therefore, is not empty of self-nature. 
 Therefore, there is nothing that  is not empty of self-nature (o r inherent existence). This was 
taught by the Buddha in the sutras where there are statements such as, Whatever is born from 
causes and conditions is unborn.  This means that whatever is created (born) from causes and 
conditions  is not inherently created (born). The fault in asserting that phenomena are inherently 
existent is addressed in the  next verse.  
14 Self-nature, you said, cannot be destroyed. Phenomena, therefore, possessed of nature  would render nirvana impossible. Samsara likewise would have no end. 
 
If phenomena are inherently existent, whatever ex isted at an earlier time cannot be destroyed or 
cease to exist at a later time. If one held on to th e view that cyclic existence is inherently existent, 
this would lead to the absurd consequence that  one will never be able to achieve liberation or 
nirvana.  
 
Phenomena, therefore,  possessed of nature:  These words are uttered by the Buddha himself in the 
sutra. If phenomena are inherently existent, th e Buddha and the hearers and solitary realizers 
would realise this. But, of course, they do no t. The Buddha points out that if phenomena are 
inherently existent, then nirvana would be impo ssible and there would be no way out of cyclic 
existence for all the scholars and  great practitioners.    According to Prasangika, all phenomena are depe ndent arising and are, therefore, empty of 
inherent existence. Because of this, the presentation of samsara and nirvana becomes feasible and possible.  As explained by Prasangika, whenever we see stat ements that things do not exist inherently, we 
must not understand this to mean that they are non-existent. Rather, it means things and events 
exist, but they are empty of inherent existence.  
(1’) An example illustrating the production of me mories despite the lack of a self-cognizer 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
348  
 
(9,23) 
[Cittamatra ]: 
If a self-cognizer did not exist 
How would consciousness be remembered? 
[Madhyamaka ]: 
It is remembered through experience and 
In relation to other, like the poison of a rat. 
 
Qualm from Cittamatra: 
If, as asserted by Prasangika, the self-knowe r does not exist, at a later time, how would 
consciousness be remembered? 
 Cittamatra asserts that there is an experiencer  of the eye consciousness apprehending blue that 
was generated at an earlier time. Later, we can re call   that eye consciousness apprehending blue 
that was generated earlier. When the thought, “I saw blue yesterday” arises, not only can we remember the colour blue that 
we saw yesterday, we can also remember the eye consciousness apprehending blue that was 
generated then. For Cittamatra, the reason we can remember there was an eye consciousness 
apprehending blue yesterday is because we “exp erienced” that eye consciousness apprehending 
blue yesterday. In other words, if something were  not experienced at an earlier time, it would be 
impossible to remember it later.   A fundamental assertion of Cittamatra is that ph enomena are inherently existent, so when they 
assert an experiencer  of consciousness, they have only tw o options: the consciousness either (1) 
experiences itself or (2) it is expe rienced by another  consciousness. 
 Cittamatra says that we cannot have another cons ciousness experiencing a consciousness (as in 
option 2) because this leads to th e fallacy of limitless regression. Therefore, they have to assert 
that there is a self-knower, a consciousness experiencing itself.  
Response from Prasangika: It is remembered through experience and/In relation to other, like the poison of a 
rat. 
 
Prasangika refutes the Cittamatra assertion of a se lf-knower because Prasangika asserts that it is 
possible to remember things wi thout positing a self-knower. 
 For Prasangika, the memory of the eye consciousn ess apprehending blue comes from the force of 
experiencing the object, the colo u r  b l u e .  T h a t  e x p e r i e n c e  o f  t h e  o b j e c t  i s  d i f f e r e n t  f r o m  t h e  
subject, the mind that apprehends blue. Wh en we remember blue, our memory does not 
selectively only remember blue and discard the memory of the eye consciousness apprehending 
Amitabha Buddhist Centre - Geshe Chonyi         Engaging in the Bodhisattva Deeds - Part 2 
FPMT Basic Program                           CHAPTER IX - WISDOM  
 
349  
blue. Through the force of memory of blue, we remember both the obje ct, blue (that we had 
apprehended with our eye consciousness before ) and also remember the subject, the eye 
consciousness apprehending blue. Therefore, we can remember both the object and the subject 
without asserting a self-knower.  
 We can remember something without experiencing it. For example, we are bitten by a mosquito. 
We are aware of being bitten but we are not awar e that the virus has entered our body. When the 
bite swells up a few days later, only then do we  recall that we had been  bitten by a mosquito and 
generate the conceptual thought that the virus from the mosquito must have entered our body at 
that time. We have this kind of remembering consciousness.   Does a memory of something have to be based on experience? Cittamatra maintains that we can 
only remember something that we have experien ced while Prasangika asserts that memory need 
not be preceded by experience.
